title: The Seventh Betrayal
content:
The pain came from seven directions at once.

Jin Tian, the Sword God of the Northern Wastes, felt each blade enter his body with surgical precision. His own disciples—the seven he had raised from children, taught to cultivate, gifted with techniques that would make emperors weep—had chosen their moment perfectly.

The first strike came from his left. Little Phoenix, his youngest, drove a short sword through his kidney. Her eyes, the same eyes that had once looked at him with pure adoration, now held only cold calculation.

"Why?" he managed, blood bubbling from his lips.

She didn't answer. But the second strike did.

"Because your era is over, Master."

Third Disciple Wei Chen twisted the blade in Jin Tian's back. The poison on its edge—Night Lotus Venom, rare enough to cost a kingdom—was already spreading through his meridians, freezing his qi pathways one by one.

The third strike severed his right hamstring. Fourth Disciple Li Ming, the one who had always been too eager to please, too desperate for validation. "The Heavenly Sword Sect has offered us protection," Li Ming said, his voice trembling. "They promised us resources. Position. A future."

"A future built on betrayal?" Jin Tian laughed, though it cost him blood. "I taught you better than this."

"You taught us to survive," said Fifth Disciple Shen Yue, stepping into his field of vision. Her beauty was wasted on cruelty. "And survival means knowing when to abandon a sinking ship."

Sixth and Seventh disciples—the twins, Hao and Jun—delivered the fourth and fifth strikes simultaneously. Twin sabers, their signature technique, practiced to perfection over twenty years. Jin Tian had designed that technique himself. He never imagined it would be used against him.

"Father," Hao said, still calling him by the title Jin Tian had never encouraged but never forbade, "please understand. This is not personal."

"Then what is it?" Jin Tian's vision was dimming. His cultivation base, three hundred years of accumulated power, was being drained by the Night Lotus Venom. He could feel his dantian cracking, his core shattering like a frozen lake under a hammer.

"It's opportunity," said the first disciple. The strongest. The one Jin Tian had trusted most.

Xiao Feng had not struck yet. He stood before his master, his long sword still sheathed, his face unreadable. Of all the seven, he had been with Jin Tian the longest. Forty years. A lifetime.

"Opportunity," Jin Tian repeated. The word tasted like ash.

"The Heavenly Sword Sect wants the Nine Heavens Sword Manual," Xiao Feng said. "They've wanted it for centuries. You possess the only complete copy in existence."

"I possess it," Jin Tian agreed. "And you want it."

"We want what it can give us." Xiao Feng finally drew his sword. The blade sang as it left the scabbard—Jin Tian had forged that sword himself, using Starfall Iron from a meteor that had crashed in the Eastern Wastes. He had given it to Xiao Feng on the day he accepted him as his first disciple.

"Give us the manual," Xiao Feng said, his sword point hovering over Jin Tian's throat, "and we will make your death painless."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then we will make it last for days."

Jin Tian looked at his seven disciples. His legacy. His greatest achievement and now his greatest failure. He had taken them from poverty, from obscurity, from families that had abandoned them. He had fed them, clothed them, taught them the Way of the Sword. He had loved them as a father loves his children.

And they had repaid him with steel and poison.

"Very well," Jin Tian said, his voice barely a whisper. "Come closer. I will give you what you want."

Xiao Feng leaned in, his eyes gleaming with anticipation. The other six disciples pressed closer, hungry for the secret that would elevate them to the peak of the martial world.

Jin Tian smiled.

In the instant before death, a cultivator's soul experiences something unique. A final burst of insight, if they are lucky. A moment of perfect clarity before the darkness claims them.

Jin Tian's insight came in the form of a question: If you could do it all again, knowing what you know now, what would you change?

The answer came immediately.

Everything.

And then the blade descended.

---

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open.

He was staring at a wooden ceiling. Rough-hewn beams, stained with age and smoke. The smell of cheap oil lamps and unwashed bodies filled his nostrils. Somewhere nearby, someone was snoring.

He sat up so fast his head spun.

His body—no, this was wrong. His hands were too small. His arms were too thin. His cultivation base, that vast ocean of power that had taken three centuries to build, was gone. Completely empty. Not even a single strand of qi remained in his dantian.

"What—"

The voice that came out of his mouth was not the voice of a seventy-year-old Sword God. It was the voice of a child. High-pitched. Untested.

Jin Tian looked down at himself. He was wearing rough gray robes, the kind issued to outer disciples of a minor sect. His body was small and underdeveloped, barely five feet tall. He had no calluses on his hands, no scars on his arms.

A memory surfaced. Not his memory—not the memory of the Sword God—but the memory of the boy whose body he now occupied.

The boy's name was also Jin Tian. He was twelve years old. An outer disciple of the Falling Cloud Sect, a minor cultivation sect in the Eastern Province. He had been born to a concubine of a minor noble house, sent to the sect at age eight when his potential for cultivation was deemed "mediocre at best."

Today was the day before the annual sect tournament.

Tomorrow, in the tournament, this boy would lose his first match. He would be humiliated. He would be assigned to the sect's herb garden, where he would spend the next six years doing manual labor before being expelled for failing to reach the second stage of qi condensation.

That was the boy's fate.

But the boy was no longer in control.

Jin Tian—the real Jin Tian, the Sword God who had lived three hundred years and died at the hands of his disciples—sat on the thin sleeping mat and took stock of his situation.

He was alive. Somehow. Impossible as it seemed, he had been given a second chance. He had been sent back in time, into the body of his twelve-year-old self, three hundred years before his death.

No. That wasn't quite right.

He examined the memories more carefully. This body's memories were different from his own. In his original life, he had been born a prodigy, the son of a sect elder, given every advantage from birth. But this boy—this alternate version of himself—had been born to nothing. A concubine's son. A nobody.

The timeline had diverged. This was not his past. This was a different world, a different fate.

But the knowledge remained.

Jin Tian closed his eyes and reached into his mind. The Nine Heavens Sword Manual was there, every character, every diagram, every secret technique he had spent a lifetime collecting and creating. The Falling Rain Sword Technique. The Heaven Sundering Strike. The Void Step. All of it, preserved in perfect detail.

He also remembered his disciples. Every face. Every word. Every betrayal.

And he remembered something else. Something he had never known in his previous life.

The Heavenly Sword Sect. The name Xiao Feng had mentioned before killing him. In his original life, Jin Tian had never encountered them. He had died before he could learn their secrets. But now... now he had time. Time to grow stronger. Time to discover who had orchestrated his death. Time to prepare.

Time to make them pay.

A bell rang somewhere in the compound. Morning assembly. The boy's memories told him that outer disciples were required to attend, to perform basic qi circulation exercises, and to receive their daily tasks.

Jin Tian rose. His body protested—it was weak, untrained, barely capable of the most basic movements. But he ignored the pain. Pain was a teacher. Pain was a friend.

He dressed in the gray robes, noting the coarse fabric against his skin. In his previous life, he had worn silk and spirit-threaded armor. Now he wore rags. It didn't matter. The sword did not care about the clothes of the one who wielded it.

The dormitory was a long building with rows of sleeping mats. Other boys his age were stirring, rubbing sleep from their eyes. They paid him no attention. In their world, Jin Tian was invisible. A background character in the story of their own lives.

He left the building and followed the stream of disciples toward the training ground. The Falling Cloud Sect was small, maybe five hundred disciples total, with only a handful of elders and a single Grand Elder who had reached the Nascent Soul stage. In Jin Tian's previous life, he would have considered such a sect beneath his notice. Now, it was his starting point.

The training ground was a flat expanse of packed earth, surrounded by wooden practice dummies and weapon racks. The outer disciples arranged themselves in rows, facing the platform where the sect elders would address them.

Jin Tian took his place among the other twelve-year-olds. He stood still, his hands at his sides, his breathing even. He observed everything. The way the elders moved. The qi signatures of the senior disciples. The layout of the compound. The defensive formations hidden in the walls.

Three hundred years of experience filtered through a twelve-year-old's eyes.

The head elder, a man named Feng Hao, stepped onto the platform. He was at the Core Formation stage, impressive for a sect of this size, but laughable compared to what Jin Tian had been. Feng Hao's qi was unstable, his foundation flawed. He had probably used pills to advance, sacrificing long-term potential for short-term gain.

"Disciples," Feng Hao announced, his voice carrying across the training ground, "tomorrow is the annual tournament. As you know, your performance will determine your placement for the coming year. Those who excel will be promoted to inner disciples. Those who fail..." He paused, letting the threat hang in the air. "Will be assigned to support duties."

A murmur rippled through the crowd. Jin Tian noticed several boys his age looking nervous. One of them, a chubby kid with a frightened face, was actually trembling.

The tournament. In the boy's memories, this was a source of terror. He knew he would lose. He knew he would be humiliated. He knew his future was already written.

But Jin Tian was not that boy anymore.

"Before we begin today's exercises," Feng Hao continued, "I want to introduce a special guest. Disciple Xiao Feng of the Heavenly Sword Sect has graciously agreed to observe our tournament."

Jin Tian's blood turned to ice.

Xiao Feng stepped onto the platform.

He was young. No more than twenty, with the arrogant bearing of someone who had never known true defeat. His robes were white, embroidered with golden clouds—the symbol of the Heavenly Sword Sect. His sword hung at his hip, a fine blade but unremarkable.

Jin Tian stared at the face of his murderer.

The same face. The same eyes. The same cruel mouth that had smiled as he drove a sword through his master's heart.

He was here. In this time. In this place.

The Heavenly Sword Sect had already sent someone to observe the Falling Cloud Sect tournament. Why? What were they looking for? What were they planning?

Xiao Feng stepped forward, his voice smooth and practiced. "Greetings, disciples of the Falling Cloud Sect. I am honored to be here. The Heavenly Sword Sect is

title: The Tournament That Never Was
content:
The morning sun painted the training grounds in shades of amber and gold, but Jin Tian saw only ghosts.

He stood at the edge of the Outer Disciple compound, his wooden practice sword feeling foreign in hands that remembered the weight of the Sky-Severing Blade. Around him, dozens of boys and girls his age stretched and warmed up, their breath misting in the cool air. None of them knew that in less than six hours, half of them would be dead.

*No*, Jin Tian corrected himself. *That was before. This time, I will change it.*

"Jin Tian! There you are!"

He turned to see a freckled boy with a crooked smile jogging toward him. The boy's robes were patched at the elbows, and his qi signature flickered weakly—barely at the third level of Qi Condensation.

Chen Wei.

Jin Tian's chest tightened. In his past life, Chen Wei had been his first friend at the Verdant Cloud Sect. They had trained together, eaten together, dreamed together. And then Chen Wei had died in the tournament, impaled by a rogue disciple's flying sword—a death that Jin Tian had always blamed on his own weakness.

But now he knew the truth. Chen Wei's death had been no accident. It had been a message. A warning for anyone who dared to rise too fast.

"You look like you've seen a ghost," Chen Wei said, punching him lightly on the shoulder. "Nervous about the tournament?"

Jin Tian forced a smile. "Something like that."

"Don't worry. We've been training for months. Just remember what Senior Brother Liu taught us—"

"Liu Feng is a traitor," Jin Tian said flatly.

Chen Wei's smile froze. "What?"

Jin Tian caught himself. He had spoken without thinking, letting the anger of three hundred years bleed through. In his past life, Liu Feng had been one of his seven disciples—the one who had driven the final sword through his heart. But that was centuries away. Right now, Liu Feng was merely a talented inner disciple, barely seventeen years old.

"I mean," Jin Tian said carefully, "I've heard rumors. That Senior Brother Liu might be trying to recruit disciples for his own faction. We should be careful who we trust."

Chen Wei's expression cleared, though his eyes remained troubled. "Oh. That. I've heard those rumors too. But Senior Brother Liu has always been good to us outer disciples. He even helped me with my sword stance last week."

*Of course he did*, Jin Tian thought bitterly. *He was identifying the weak ones. The ones who would die first.*

The tournament bell rang, a deep bronze note that echoed across the mountain. Around them, disciples began moving toward the arena—a massive circular platform of white jade that floated above a lake of crystalline water. The platform was large enough to hold a hundred combatants, and it was surrounded by viewing stands packed with elders, inner disciples, and visiting cultivators from neighboring sects.

Jin Tian's hand tightened on his wooden sword. He remembered this day with perfect clarity. The tournament was supposed to be a simple test of skill—a way for outer disciples to demonstrate their progress and perhaps catch the eye of a powerful elder. But in his past life, it had become a massacre.

The Demon Heart Sect had infiltrated the tournament. Disguised as outer disciples, they had activated a formation that turned the arena into a death trap. Fifty-three disciples had died before the elders could break the seal. And in the chaos, the Demon Heart Sect had stolen the sect's most treasured artifact: the Jade Phoenix Scroll.

But Jin Tian knew something now that he hadn't known then. The Demon Heart Sect hadn't acted alone. They had been aided by someone inside the Verdant Cloud Sect. Someone who had wanted the Jade Phoenix Scroll badly enough to sacrifice a generation of disciples.

Someone named Liu Feng.

"Jin Tian? Are you coming?" Chen Wei was already walking toward the arena, his face bright with anticipation.

Jin Tian followed, his mind racing. He had three hours before the tournament began. Three hours to prevent a massacre that had already happened in his memory. But how? He was only a twelve-year-old outer disciple at the third level of Qi Condensation. Even with the knowledge of a Sword God, his body could not yet channel the power he needed.

*Then I must be clever*, he thought. *I must use what I have.*

As they approached the arena, Jin Tian scanned the crowd. There were seventy-two outer disciples competing today, all between the ages of twelve and fifteen. Among them, he knew, were seven Demon Heart infiltrators. They would be the ones who activated the formation, sacrificing themselves to complete their mission.

But they were only pawns. The real mastermind was watching from somewhere above.

Jin Tian looked up at the viewing platform reserved for inner disciples. And there he was.

Liu Feng.

He was seventeen now, handsome and confident, his blue inner disciple robes immaculate. His cultivation had already reached the ninth level of Qi Condensation, and everyone said he would break through to Foundation Establishment before his twentieth birthday. He smiled at the outer disciples below with the benevolent warmth of a beloved senior brother.

Jin Tian wanted to kill him. Right here, right now. He could already see the weak points in Liu Feng's stance—the slight imbalance in his left foot, the way his qi pooled unevenly in his dantian. In his prime, Jin Tian could have killed Liu Feng with a single glance.

But he was not in his prime. He was a child with a wooden sword and a body that could barely handle basic qi circulation.

*Patience*, he told himself. *I have time. I know the future. I will use that knowledge.*

"Attention, outer disciples!"

Elder Wu, the tournament master, stepped onto the arena platform. He was an old man with a wispy white beard and robes embroidered with golden clouds. His cultivation was at the Nascent Soul stage—a power that Jin Tian had once surpassed but now could only dream of reaching again.

"The rules are simple," Elder Wu announced. "Matches will be random. First to disarm, knock out, or force a submission wins. No lethal techniques. No poison. No external aids. The top three disciples will receive resources to advance to the fourth level of Qi Condensation and will be considered for inner disciple selection."

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. For outer disciples, this was a chance to change their lives. Most of them had been stuck at the third level for months, unable to advance without better resources.

Jin Tian barely listened. He was watching the seven infiltrators, memorizing their faces, their stances, the way they moved. In his past life, he hadn't noticed them until it was too late. But now he could see the subtle signs—the way they stood slightly apart from the other disciples, the way their eyes kept flicking toward the formation nodes hidden in the arena's design.

They were nervous. Good.

The first matches began. Disciples paired up on the arena platform, their wooden swords clashing in controlled sparring. The crowd cheered, and elders took notes, evaluating potential.

Jin Tian's first match was against a girl named Zhao Lin, a quiet disciple with average talent. In his past life, he had lost to her by a narrow margin. But that was before he had three hundred years of sword experience.

When his name was called, Jin Tian stepped onto the platform. Zhao Lin faced him, her wooden sword raised in a standard guard stance.

"Begin!" Elder Wu commanded.

Zhao Lin attacked first, a basic thrust aimed at his chest. It was textbook perfect—but textbooks were written by mediocre swordsmen.

Jin Tian didn't block. He sidestepped, a movement so subtle it looked like he had barely moved. Zhao Lin's thrust missed by a hair's breadth, and before she could recover, Jin Tian's wooden sword tapped her wrist.

Her sword clattered to the ground.

"Match to Jin Tian!" Elder Wu announced, surprise flickering in his ancient eyes.

Zhao Lin stared at her empty hand, bewildered. "How did you—"

Jin Tian bowed politely and left the platform. Around him, disciples whispered. That had been too fast, too clean. An outer disciple at the third level shouldn't have been able to execute such a precise counter.

But Jin Tian didn't care about hiding his skill. He had bigger problems.

As he walked back to his position, he noticed one of the infiltrators watching him. The young man—disguised as an outer disciple named "Zhang Wei"—had a cold, calculating gaze. Their eyes met, and Jin Tian saw recognition flicker in the infiltrator's expression.

*He knows I'm not what I appear to be*, Jin Tian realized. *The Demon Heart Sect has methods of detecting anomalies.*

Zhang Wei smiled. It was not a friendly smile.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. If the infiltrators identified him as a threat, they might accelerate their timeline. The formation could be activated at any moment, and if it was triggered before he could act—

"Jin Tian! You were amazing!" Chen Wei grabbed his arm, bouncing with excitement. "Where did you learn that move? I've never seen anything like it!"

"Lucky guess," Jin Tian said, his eyes still locked on Zhang Wei.

The infiltrator was speaking to two others now, his words too quiet to hear. The three of them glanced at Jin Tian, then at the formation nodes hidden beneath the arena platform.

They were going to do it. Right now.

"Chen Wei," Jin Tian said urgently, "I need you to do something. Run to Elder Wu and tell him there's a demonic formation under the arena. Tell him the Demon Heart Sect is here."

Chen Wei's face went pale. "What? Jin Tian, that's—"

"Do it. Now. If you trust me at all, do it now."

For a moment, Chen Wei hesitated. Then he nodded and sprinted toward Elder Wu.

Jin Tian turned back to the infiltrators. They had noticed Chen Wei's sudden movement. Zhang Wei's eyes narrowed, and he raised his hand, a crimson sigil glowing on his palm.

*Too late.*

The ground beneath the arena trembled. A black light erupted from the formation nodes, and the white jade platform began to crack. Disciples screamed as the lake water boiled, turning to steam that reeked of sulfur and blood.

"DEMON HEART!" Elder Wu's voice thundered across the compound. "FORMATION ACTIVATED! ALL DISCIPLES—"

His words were cut off as a barrier of black qi slammed down around the arena, trapping everyone inside. The viewing stands erupted in chaos as elders tried to break the seal, but the formation was too strong, too well-prepared.

*This is it*, Jin Tian thought. *The massacre begins.*

But he was not the same man who had watched his friends die three hundred years ago.

He raised his wooden sword and faced the seven infiltrators. They were converging on him now, their disguises melting away to reveal black robes and crimson masks. Their cultivation levels were all at the fifth level of Qi Condensation—two levels above his current body's limit.

"Kill the anomaly," Zhang Wei commanded. "Then retrieve the scroll."

The seven Demon Heart disciples attacked as one.

Jin Tian's body moved before his mind could catch up. Three hundred years of sword instinct flowed through him, and even with a wooden sword and a child's body, he was still a Sword God.

He met the first attacker's blade with a deflection that sent the man stumbling. He spun, catching the second attacker's wrist and twisting until bone cracked. He ducked under a horizontal slash and drove his wooden sword into the third attacker's throat—not hard enough to kill, but enough to send him gasping to his knees.

Three down. Four

title: The Verdict That Changed Everything
content:
Jin Tian's eyes snapped open before dawn.

The familiar ache in his bones told him he was still twelve. Still weak. Still trapped in a body that had yet to taste true cultivation. But the memories remained sharp—every betrayal, every blade that pierced his flesh, every face of his seven disciples as they watched him fall.

He sat up in the cramped outer disciple quarters, the wooden cot creaking beneath him. Around him, twenty other boys his age lay in various states of sleep, their breathing shallow and untroubled. They had no idea what tomorrow would bring. No idea that the sect tournament would reshape their futures.

Jin Tian knew.

He had lived through it once. Watched as talents were discovered, rankings established, and the seeds of future greatness—or irrelevance—were planted. In his first life, he had placed thirty-seventh. Respectable for an outer disciple. Enough to earn him a mid-grade master and a decade of mediocrity before his rise truly began.

This time, he would claim first.

The memory of his regression burned in his chest like a second dantian. Three hundred years of sword cultivation compressed into a twelve-year-old vessel. His meridians were narrow, his foundation shallow, but his understanding remained intact. Every sword technique, every breathing method, every secret of qi manipulation that had taken him centuries to perfect—all of it was his to command.

He just needed the strength to execute it.

"The first step," he murmured, rising from his cot, "is to survive the tournament."

The tournament grounds stretched across the central plaza of the Verdant Cloud Sect, a sprawling complex of white stone platforms arranged in concentric circles. At the center stood the main arena, a raised dais of polished jade that gleamed under the morning sun. Senior disciples in blue robes manned the registration tables, their faces bored as they processed the endless stream of outer disciples.

Jin Tian stood in line, his simple gray robes marking him as nothing special. Around him, boys whispered nervously, their hands shaking as they clutched their wooden training swords. Some had already begun circulating qi through their meridians, a faint shimmer visible around their bodies.

He did none of that.

Instead, he observed. Counted the overseers. Noted the positions of the sect elders who would judge the matches. Located the seven figures that mattered most to him—the disciples who would one day betray him.

They were young now. Untainted. Three of them hadn't even joined the sect yet.

But one was already here.

Chen Wei.

Jin Tian's gaze found him across the plaza—a boy of fourteen, handsome and confident, surrounded by a cluster of admirers. Even now, he carried himself like a prince. His robes were of finer quality, his sword polished to a mirror shine. The son of a minor noble family, Chen Wei had entered the sect with connections and resources that most outer disciples could only dream of.

In Jin Tian's first life, Chen Wei had been his first disciple. The one who had stood beside him for two centuries. The one who had driven a sword through his back during the final betrayal.

The memory sent a spike of cold rage through Jin Tian's chest. He forced it down. Anger was a weapon, but only when controlled.

"Next!"

He stepped forward to the registration table. A senior disciple with tired eyes looked him over. "Name?"

"Jin Tian."

The senior checked a jade tablet, then frowned. "You're registered for the outer division. Bracket seven. Your first match is against—" he paused, scanning the list, "—Liang Feng. Arena Three. Don't be late."

Jin Tian nodded and took his token. Liang Feng. The name stirred nothing in his memories, which meant the boy was insignificant. A non-factor in the grand scheme of things.

Good. He could use the first match to assess his current physical limits.

The morning passed in a blur of matches. Jin Tian watched from the sidelines, cataloging every technique, every flaw, every moment of brilliance or failure. The other outer disciples moved like children playing with toys. Their footwork was sloppy. Their sword forms were incomplete. Their qi control was laughable.

But he couldn't afford arrogance. His body remembered techniques that it could no longer perform. His meridians burned when he tried to circulate too much qi. He was a master swordsman trapped in a vessel that had barely begun to cultivate.

When his name was called, he stepped onto Arena Three.

His opponent was already waiting—a stocky boy with a shaved head and a wooden sword held in a two-handed grip. Liang Feng. He looked nervous, sweat beading on his forehead despite the cool morning air.

"Begin!" the overseer announced.

Liang Feng charged immediately, a straightforward overhead strike that screamed inexperience. Jin Tian sidestepped. His body moved before his mind could catch up, instinct honed by centuries of combat. He brought his wooden sword up in a simple deflection, redirecting the blow past his shoulder.

Liang Feng stumbled, off-balance.

Jin Tian could have ended it there. A strike to the ribs. A sweep to the legs. A dozen different finishing moves flashed through his mind.

Instead, he pulled back.

Let them underestimate me.

He allowed Liang Feng to recover, trading a few more exchanges. He moved with deliberate clumsiness, letting his footwork falter just enough to look ordinary. He blocked rather than countered, defended rather than attacked.

After fifteen exchanges, he finally found an opening and tapped Liang Feng's wrist with his blade.

"Match to Jin Tian!" the overseer declared.

Liang Feng stared at his hand in disbelief, as if wondering how he'd lost. Jin Tian bowed and walked away, keeping his expression neutral.

From the corner of his eye, he saw Chen Wei watching him. Their gazes met for just a moment, and Jin Tian saw something flicker in the other boy's eyes—curiosity? Recognition? No, that was impossible. In this timeline, they had never spoken.

Jin Tian looked away first. Let the snake think he was nothing.

Three more matches. Three more victories. Each one carefully calibrated to look unremarkable. He won by narrow margins, never showcasing anything beyond basic sword forms. By the time the sun reached its zenith, he had advanced to the quarterfinals.

That was when the bracket changed.

"Attention all outer disciples," an elder announced, his voice amplified by qi. "Due to the unexpected withdrawal of Inner Disciple Zhao Ming, we have an opening in the inner division tournament. By the sect's ancient tradition, the highest-ranked outer disciple will be granted the opportunity to compete for an inner disciple position."

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

He remembered this. In his first life, a different outer disciple had been chosen—a boy named Xu Hao who had gone on to become a respected elder. But Jin Tian had never paid attention to the circumstances of his elevation.

"Based on the current standings, the outer disciple with the highest match score is... Chen Wei."

Of course.

Chen Wei stepped forward, a smug smile playing on his lips. He approached the elder with practiced humility, bowing deeply. "I am honored, Elder Zhou. I will do my best to represent the outer disciples."

Elder Zhou nodded approvingly. "Your performance has been impressive, young Chen. I expect great things from you."

Jin Tian watched the exchange, his mind racing. In his first life, Chen Wei had become an inner disciple at fourteen, then a core disciple at seventeen. The early advancement had accelerated his cultivation, giving him a foundation that most cultivators spent decades trying to achieve.

If Chen Wei advanced now, everything would change. The timeline would shift. Jin Tian's knowledge of future events would become unreliable.

He couldn't allow it.

But how could he stop it? He was a twelve-year-old outer disciple with no influence, no connections, and a body that could barely channel qi. Chen Wei had the backing of his noble family and the favor of the sect elders.

"Wait."

The word left his mouth before he could stop it. The crowd fell silent. Every eye turned to him.

Elder Zhou's brow furrowed. "You have something to say, boy?"

Jin Tian stepped forward, his heart hammering against his ribs. This was dangerous. Drawing attention to himself this early could ruin everything. But letting Chen Wei advance was worse.

"Forgive my impertinence, Elder Zhou," he said, keeping his voice humble. "But I believe there has been a mistake."

"A mistake?" The elder's eyes narrowed. "Explain yourself."

"The tradition states that the highest-ranked outer disciple earns the right to compete. But rankings are determined by match score, not by cultivation base or potential." Jin Tian paused, letting the words sink in. "By that metric, I believe my score is higher than Chen Wei's."

Murmurs erupted around him. Chen Wei's face flushed red. "Impossible! I've won all my matches with perfect scores!"

"As have I," Jin Tian replied calmly. "But the scoring system accounts for match duration and efficiency. My average match time is forty-three seconds faster than yours."

It was a gamble. He had been deliberately extending his matches to appear average, but he had also been tracking his opponents' patterns, learning their weaknesses, memorizing their tells. The overseers wouldn't have noticed—they only recorded results, not methods.

But Jin Tian had noticed everything.

Elder Zhou raised a hand, silencing the crowd. He turned to the scoring master, who was frantically consulting his jade records. After a long moment, the master nodded slowly.

"He's correct, Elder. Jin Tian's efficiency score is higher by a significant margin."

The air crackled with tension. Chen Wei's face cycled through disbelief, anger, and finally cold calculation. He turned to face Jin Tian fully, and for the first time, Jin Tian saw the mask slip.

Beneath the handsome features and noble bearing was something cold. Something hungry.

"Then by all means," Chen Wei said, his voice dripping with false courtesy, "let the boy have his chance. I'm sure he'll represent the outer disciples admirably."

The words were polite. The meaning was not.

Elder Zhou studied Jin Tian with new interest. "You're bold, boy. Boldness can be a virtue or a flaw, depending on the outcome." He paused. "Very well. You will take the open slot in the inner division tournament. Your first match will be against Inner Disciple Liu Feng."

The name hit Jin Tian like a physical blow.

Inner Disciple Liu Feng. Ranked seventh among the inner disciples. A prodigy known for his fire-aspected techniques and aggressive combat style.

And in Jin Tian's first life, Liu Feng had been the one who delivered the killing blow.

Not Chen Wei. Not the other disciples who had plotted his downfall. It was Liu Feng's blade that had pierced his heart, Liu Feng's face that had been the last thing he saw before darkness claimed him.

Jin Tian had never understood why. Liu Feng had been a rival, not an enemy. They had competed for resources, clashed over territories, but never with the kind of hatred that led to murder.

Now he had a chance to find out.

"Thank you, Elder," he said, bowing deeply. "I will not disappoint."

As he straightened, he caught Chen Wei's eye one more time. The other boy's smile had frozen into something brittle, his eyes promising retribution.

Jin Tian didn't care.

He had three hours until his match against Liu Feng. Three hours to prepare for a battle against a disciple who had killed him once before.

This time, he would be ready.

This time, he would find out why.

And this time, when the blades crossed, he would be the one walking away.

title: The Shadow Before the Storm
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The sun hung low in the sky, casting long shadows across the stone platforms where inner disciples usually sparred. He had chosen this place deliberately—far from prying eyes, close to the sect's weapon vault, and most importantly, the site of his death in his previous life.

He closed his eyes and let the memories wash over him.

The tournament had been a spectacle. Thousands of disciples packed the grand arena, elders observing from raised platforms, the sect master himself presiding over the final matches. Jin Tian had been at his peak then, having cultivated the Azure Sky Sword Art to its seventh layer, his sword qi capable of splitting clouds.

Liu Feng had challenged him in the semi-finals.

The fight had been brutal. Liu Feng's fire-aspected techniques had pushed Jin Tian to his limits, forcing him to reveal techniques he had been saving for the finals. But in the end, Jin Tian had won. He had shown mercy, sparing Liu Feng's life when he could have ended it.

That mercy had cost him everything.

Three years later, when the betrayal came, Liu Feng had been there. His blade had been the first to draw blood, the one that had pierced Jin Tian's heart while he was still reeling from the poison his disciples had administered. The fire in Liu Feng's eyes that day had been different—not the fire of battle, but something colder. Something calculated.

*Why?* Jin Tian had never understood. Liu Feng had no reason to hate him. They had been rivals, yes, but rivals who respected each other. They had shared drinks after tournaments, exchanged sword techniques, even fought side by side against demon beasts during the northern campaign.

There had to be something he was missing.

Jin Tian opened his eyes and drew his sword. The blade was simple—standard issue for outer disciples, forged from common iron, unremarkable in every way. It would have to do.

He began his forms slowly, letting his body remember the movements that had become instinct over three hundred years of cultivation. The Azure Sky Sword Art flowed through him like water, each strike precise, each transition seamless. He started with the first form, then the second, working his way through all nine layers of the technique.

But he didn't stop there.

In his previous life, he had spent decades refining his sword arts, creating variations, combining techniques from different schools. He had developed the Heavenly Sword Domain, a technique that allowed him to manifest sword qi across an entire battlefield. He had mastered the Void Severing Strike, a technique that could cut through space itself.

All of that knowledge was still in his mind, waiting to be unlocked.

The problem was his body. At twelve years old, with only the first layer of the sect's basic cultivation technique completed, his meridians were too narrow to channel the amount of qi required for advanced techniques. His bones were still growing, his muscles still developing. If he pushed too hard, he would shatter his foundation before it was fully formed.

*Patience,* he reminded himself. *You have time.*

But time was a luxury he might not have. If Liu Feng had killed him once, he would try again. The question was whether Liu Feng was acting alone or as part of something larger.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and sat cross-legged on the training platform. He needed to accelerate his cultivation without damaging his foundation. In his previous life, he had discovered a technique for doing exactly that—a method of cycling qi through pressure points that stimulated growth while strengthening the meridians.

He had called it the Thunderous Pulse Method.

The technique was dangerous. One wrong move could rupture a meridian, leaving him crippled for life. But Jin Tian had performed it thousands of times in his previous life. His body might be young, but his soul remembered every detail.

He began the cycle.

Qi surged through his channels, hotter than it should have been, more intense. He felt his meridians stretch, the walls thinning as the energy pushed through. Pain lanced through his arms, his chest, his legs. He gritted his teeth and continued.

*First pulse.*

The qi slammed into his dantian, expanding it by a fraction. He felt his cultivation base strengthen, the first layer solidifying.

*Second pulse.*

More pain. His vision blurred. He could taste blood in his mouth. But he didn't stop.

*Third pulse.*

Something cracked inside him. Not a meridian—thank the heavens—but a barrier. The second layer of the sect's basic cultivation technique opened before him like a gate swinging wide. Qi flooded through, filling his channels, strengthening his body.

He had done it. In three hours, he had advanced to the second layer—a feat that normally took months.

But the cost was high. His body trembled with exhaustion, sweat soaking his robes. His meridians ached, stretched thin from the forced expansion. He would need at least a week to recover fully.

*A week he didn't have.*

A bell rang in the distance, announcing the start of the tournament's afternoon matches. Jin Tian forced himself to his feet, ignoring the protest of his muscles. He had thirty minutes until his match against Liu Feng.

Thirty minutes to prepare for a battle against a disciple who had killed him once before.

He walked toward the main arena, his steps steady despite his fatigue. Disciples parted before him, whispering among themselves. Word of his confrontation with Chen Wei had spread quickly. Some looked at him with curiosity, others with pity. No one expected him to win.

*Good,* Jin Tian thought. *Let them underestimate me.*

The arena was massive, capable of holding ten thousand spectators. Disciples packed the stands, their voices rising in a constant hum of excitement. Elders sat in elevated platforms, their robes marking their ranks. And in the center of it all stood the combat platform, a circle of white jade thirty meters in diameter.

Liu Feng was already there.

He was older than Jin Tian remembered—sixteen, three years his senior—with sharp features and eyes that seemed to flicker with inner fire. His robes were red and gold, marking him as an inner disciple of the Blazing Flame Sect's main branch. In his hand, he held a sword that gleamed with heat distortion, its blade forged from fire-aspected materials.

Jin Tian climbed onto the platform, meeting Liu Feng's gaze. The inner disciple studied him with a mixture of curiosity and disdain.

"So you're the one who caused such a stir," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I expected someone... taller."

"Size isn't everything," Jin Tian replied calmly. "I've found that smaller targets are harder to hit."

A ripple of laughter ran through the crowd. Liu Feng's eyes narrowed.

"Clever," he said. "But cleverness won't save you from my blade."

"Then let's see if your blade is as sharp as your tongue."

The elder overseeing the match raised his hand. "Combatants, prepare. The match will end when one party yields, is unable to continue, or is thrown from the platform. Lethal force is forbidden. Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first.

He exploded forward, his sword trailing flames, the heat washing over Jin Tian like a wave. It was a simple opening strike, designed to test his opponent's reflexes. In his previous life, Jin Tian had blocked it, initiating a lengthy exchange of techniques.

This time, he did something different.

Instead of blocking, he sidestepped, his body flowing like water around the strike. At the same time, he extended his sword, not to attack, but to redirect. The tip of his blade caught Liu Feng's wrist, deflecting the strike just enough to throw him off balance.

Liu Feng stumbled, surprise flickering across his face. He recovered quickly, spinning to face Jin Tian with a wild slash.

Jin Tian ducked under it, his sword rising to meet Liu Feng's blade. But instead of clashing, he let his sword slide along Liu Feng's, guiding the force of the strike past him. The motion was subtle, almost invisible, but it left Liu Feng overextended, his guard open.

*Now.*

Jin Tian struck.

His sword lashed out, not at Liu Feng's body, but at his sword hand. The tip of the blade caught the inner disciple's wrist, drawing a thin line of blood. Liu Feng hissed, his grip on his sword faltering for a fraction of a second.

It was enough.

Jin Tian followed up with a kick to Liu Feng's knee, forcing him to drop to one knee. He brought his sword to rest against Liu Feng's throat, the blade cold against his skin.

The arena fell silent.

"I yield," Liu Feng said, his voice tight with barely contained rage.

Jin Tian stepped back, sheathing his sword. The match had lasted less than thirty seconds.

The elder's voice rang out, shocked. "Winner: Outer Disciple Jin Tian."

The crowd erupted. Some cheered, others booed. Most simply stared in disbelief. An outer disciple had defeated a ranked inner disciple in less than half a minute. It was unprecedented.

But Jin Tian wasn't watching the crowd. He was watching Liu Feng.

As the inner disciple rose to his feet, his eyes met Jin Tian's. And in that moment, Jin Tian saw something that made his blood run cold.

It wasn't anger. It wasn't humiliation.

It was recognition.

*He knows me,* Jin Tian realized. *Not as an outer disciple. He knows who I was.*

Liu Feng's lips curved into a smile that didn't reach his eyes. "Interesting," he said, his voice barely above a whisper. "The old man was right about you."

Then he turned and walked away, leaving Jin Tian standing alone on the platform, the weight of those words pressing down on him like a mountain.

*What old man?*

title: Chapter 5
content:
# CHAPTER 5: The Fire That Burns Within

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the stone courtyard, and the air smelled of dust and distant rain. He had exactly two hours and forty-three minutes before his match against Liu Feng.

He sat cross-legged in the center of the arena, closed his eyes, and began to breathe.

In his first life, he had prepared for this match with arrogance. He had been sixteen then, already confident in his talent, already certain of his victory. He had trained for three days, learned Liu Feng's techniques from watching his previous matches, and walked into the arena believing he understood everything there was to know about his opponent.

He had been wrong.

Liu Feng had done something unexpected that day. Something that had caught even the experienced Jin Tian off guard. A technique that shouldn't have existed, a power that defied the natural progression of fire cultivation.

Jin Tian had lost that match. Badly. Humiliatingly. It had taken him months to recover, and the defeat had set him back in ways he hadn't understood until much later.

But that defeat had also taught him something valuable.

Liu Feng was not a normal cultivator.

Now, as a twelve-year-old with three centuries of sword mastery burning in his soul, Jin Tian opened his eyes and began tracing patterns in the dust with his finger. The Qi flow patterns of the arena. The ley lines beneath the mountain. The subtle distortions that indicated hidden formations.

He found what he was looking for almost immediately.

There, beneath the eastern edge of the arena, was a faint pulse of energy that didn't belong. It was buried deep, hidden by layers of suppression formations, but to someone who had spent centuries studying the fabric of reality itself, it was as obvious as a bonfire in a dark room.

Someone had placed a formation under this arena. Someone powerful. And it was designed to do one thing: amplify fire-aspected techniques.

Jin Tian's jaw tightened. In his first life, he had never noticed this. He had assumed Liu Feng's power was natural talent, the result of lucky breakthroughs and superior cultivation. Now he understood.

The playing field had never been level. Someone had been rigging the game from the very beginning.

He stood slowly, brushing the dust from his robes. The question was no longer whether he could defeat Liu Feng. The question was who had placed the formation, and why.

The answer came to him with cold certainty.

Elder Zhou.

The same elder who had argued against Jin Tian's promotion. The same elder who had been so ready to dismiss him. The same elder who had conveniently been the one to announce Jin Tian's opponent.

Jin Tian had three centuries of experience with political maneuvering within the sect. He knew how these things worked. Elder Zhou wasn't acting alone—he was a pawn, or perhaps a willing participant, in something larger.

But that was a mystery for later. Right now, he had a match to win.

He spent the next hour studying the formation, memorizing its patterns, understanding its limitations. It was clever, he had to admit. The formation didn't boost Liu Feng's power directly—that would have been too obvious. Instead, it subtly altered the ambient Qi in the arena, making fire techniques more efficient and stable. The difference would be imperceptible to most observers, just a slight edge that could mean the difference between victory and defeat.

Jin Tian smiled. In his first life, he had been a master of formations. He had designed arrays that could level mountains, barriers that could withstand heavenly tribulation. This little trick was child's play.

He reached into his robes and pulled out three small jade tokens. They were common practice tools, given to outer disciples for basic meditation exercises. But in Jin Tian's hands, they became something else.

He pressed the first token into the ground, aligning it with the formation's primary node. The second he placed at the secondary node, and the third at the tertiary. To anyone watching, it would look like he was simply arranging meditation aids.

In truth, he had just planted the seeds of the formation's destruction.

When the match began, all he would need to do was channel a single thread of Qi through those tokens, and the formation would collapse in on itself. The backlash would be contained, invisible to observers, but Liu Feng would suddenly find himself fighting without his hidden advantage.

The shock alone might be enough to unsettle him.

---

The arena was packed when Jin Tian arrived for his match. News of his confrontation with Chen Wei had spread like wildfire through the sect, and every outer disciple, inner disciple, and wandering elder had come to see the boy who had dared to challenge the established order.

Chen Wei stood at the edge of the crowd, his arms crossed, his face carefully neutral. But Jin Tian could see the tension in his shoulders, the way his fingers drummed against his forearm. The boy was nervous. Good.

Liu Feng was already in the arena when Jin Tian entered. He was older than Jin Tian by three years, with broad shoulders and a scar that ran from his left eyebrow to his jaw. His cultivation base was solid—the fifth level of Qi Condensation, impressive for his age. His eyes were the color of dying embers, and they fixed on Jin Tian with predatory intensity.

"So," Liu Feng said, his voice rough as gravel, "you're the one who thinks you're better than the system."

"I think the system can be improved," Jin Tian replied, taking his position at the opposite end of the arena. "There's a difference."

"The difference is that you're about to learn humility." Liu Feng drew his blade—a curved saber that shimmered with heat haze. "I won't go easy on you just because you're a child."

"I wouldn't expect you to."

The referee, an inner sect elder named Wei, raised his hand. "The match will begin on my signal. Standard rules apply—no killing blows, no forbidden techniques, no external interference. Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. He was an aggressive fighter, relying on overwhelming pressure to break his opponents before they could establish their rhythm. His saber traced a burning arc through the air, and the temperature in the arena spiked dramatically.

In his first life, Jin Tian had tried to block that attack head-on. It had been a mistake.

This time, he stepped aside.

The movement was minimal, a shift of weight and a half-turn that brought him exactly one inch out of the saber's path. Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly as his strike cut through empty air, and he had to adjust his stance to avoid overextending.

"You're faster than I expected," Liu Feng admitted, circling to reset his position.

"You're slower than I remembered."

The words were a provocation, and they worked. Liu Feng's face flushed with anger, and he launched into a series of rapid strikes, each one hotter and faster than the last. Jin Tian dodged, weaved, and parried with minimal movements, conserving his energy while Liu Feng burned through his.

But Jin Tian was also watching. Waiting.

The formation beneath the arena was pulsing, feeding power to Liu Feng's techniques. Every time the saber swung, it left trails of fire that lingered in the air, creating a web of heat that slowly constricted Jin Tian's movement options.

Liu Feng was herding him. Using the residual flames to limit where he could step, channeling him toward a corner where the formation's power was strongest.

It was a good strategy. In his first life, Jin Tian had fallen for it completely.

This time, he saw the trap forming three moves before it closed.

He made his move.

As Liu Feng's saber came down in a blazing overhead strike, Jin Tian didn't dodge. Instead, he stepped forward, inside the strike's reach, and drove his palm into Liu Feng's chest.

The blow wasn't meant to injure. It was meant to transfer Qi.

A single thread of Jin Tian's energy flowed through his palm, into Liu Feng's body, and down through his feet into the ground. It found the three jade tokens Jin Tian had placed earlier, and activated them simultaneously.

The formation beneath the arena shuddered.

Liu Feng stumbled back, his eyes wide with confusion. The flames around his saber flickered and died, leaving nothing but cold steel in his hand. The ambient temperature in the arena dropped by ten degrees in the span of a heartbeat.

"What—" Liu Feng started, but Jin Tian didn't give him time to finish.

He drew his sword.

It was a simple practice blade, unremarkable in every way. But in Jin Tian's hands, it became something transcendent. He moved forward with the speed of wind and the precision of falling water, his blade tracing lines that seemed to exist in the spaces between reality.

Liu Feng tried to block. His saber came up, but Jin Tian's sword was already past it, the tip pressing against Liu Feng's throat.

The arena fell silent.

"I yield," Liu Feng said, his voice hollow.

Jin Tian stepped back and sheathed his sword. The match had lasted less than three minutes.

The crowd erupted. Disciples shouted in disbelief, elders leaned forward with renewed interest, and somewhere in the back, Chen Wei had gone pale.

But Jin Tian wasn't watching the crowd. He was watching Liu Feng.

The older boy's face was a mask of controlled fury, but beneath that, Jin Tian saw something else. Fear. Not the fear of defeat, but the fear of exposure.

"I don't know what you did," Liu Feng whispered, low enough that only Jin Tian could hear, "but you've made a powerful enemy today."

"I've made many enemies," Jin Tian replied. "You'll have to be more specific."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. "Elder Zhou sends his regards."

And then he was walking away, his shoulders rigid, his saber still cold in his hand.

Jin Tian stood alone in the center of the arena, surrounded by cheering disciples and curious elders, but his mind was elsewhere.

Elder Zhou. The hidden formation. The rigged matches. And somewhere in the shadows, the seven disciples who would one day betray him.

The pieces were starting to connect, but the picture they formed was still incomplete.

As he turned to leave the arena, he caught sight of a figure standing at the edge of the crowd. A woman, dressed in the white robes of a core disciple, her face half-hidden by a veil.

She was watching him with an intensity that made his blood run cold.

He knew those eyes.

In his first life, she had been his seventh disciple. The last one. The one who had held him down while Liu Feng drove the blade through his heart.

And she was already here. Already watching. Already part of whatever conspiracy was forming.

Jin Tian's hand drifted toward his sword.

She smiled, a thin crescent of white teeth beneath the veil, and then she was gone, swallowed by the crowd as if she had never existed.

The match was over. But the war was just beginning.

title: The Fire That Burns Within
content:
The training grounds stood empty, save for one figure.

Jin Tian sat in the lotus position, his practice sword resting across his knees. The afternoon sun beat down on the packed earth arena, but he barely noticed the heat. His mind was elsewhere, sifting through memories of a life he had lived before.

Liu Feng.

The name echoed in his thoughts like a bell that wouldn't stop ringing. In his first life, Liu Feng had been a worthy opponent. They had fought seven times over the years, each battle closer than the last. Jin Tian had won four, Liu Feng three. Their rivalry had been clean, honorable—or so Jin Tian had believed.

Then came the betrayal.

He closed his eyes, forcing himself to relive the moment. The banquet hall. The poisoned wine. The seven disciples rising as one, their faces twisted with greed and fear. And Liu Feng, stepping forward with a blade that dripped with killing intent.

*"Forgive me, Master,"* Liu Feng had said, his voice carrying no remorse. *"But your time has passed."*

The blade had pierced his heart. The world had gone dark.

And now, in this life, Jin Tian would face Liu Feng in less than three hours. The same Liu Feng, younger and less experienced, but still dangerous. Still carrying whatever secret had driven him to murder.

Jin Tian opened his eyes.

"Not this time," he whispered.

He rose to his feet, the practice sword feeling light in his hands. His body was still weak, still limited by its twelve-year-old frame. But his mind was sharp. His techniques were flawless. And he had something Liu Feng didn't have: the memory of every mistake his killer would ever make.

The arena gates creaked open.

Elder Zhou stepped through, followed by a procession of inner disciples. At their head walked a young man with fiery red hair and eyes like burning coals. Even at sixteen, Liu Feng carried himself with the confidence of a seasoned warrior. His robes were immaculate, his posture perfect, his gaze fixed on Jin Tian with predatory intensity.

The crowd that had gathered for the tournament spilled through the gates behind them, filling the stands that lined the arena. Word had spread quickly: an outer disciple had challenged Chen Wei's position and won. The anticipation was palpable.

Jin Tian met Liu Feng's gaze without flinching.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I've heard interesting things about you."

"Nothing but the truth, I'm sure," Jin Tian replied.

Liu Feng's lips curved into a smile that didn't reach his eyes. "Bold words. Let's see if your sword is equally sharp."

The overseer stepped forward, a jade token glowing in his hand. "The rules are simple: first to yield, first to fall. No lethal strikes." He paused, glancing between the two combatants. "Begin."

Liu Feng moved first.

Flames erupted around his hands as he formed a series of hand seals, and a sphere of fire the size of a human head shot toward Jin Tian. The heat was intense, the speed blinding.

Jin Tian didn't dodge.

He stepped forward, his sword tracing an arc that seemed impossibly precise. The blade caught the fire sphere and redirected it, sending it harmlessly into the ground beside him. The packed earth sizzled and cracked.

The crowd gasped.

Liu Feng's eyes widened for just a fraction of a second. Then his smile returned, wider this time. "Interesting. Very interesting."

He attacked again, this time with a blade of his own. The sword was wreathed in flames, and each strike left trails of fire in the air. He moved like a dancer, fluid and deadly, his attacks coming from angles that should have been impossible to predict.

Jin Tian met each strike with calm precision.

He had fought Liu Feng seven times in his first life. He knew every technique, every feint, every weakness. He knew that Liu Feng favored his right side when setting up a killing blow. He knew that his fire techniques left him vulnerable for exactly three seconds after execution. He knew that when Liu Feng's left hand twitched, he was about to use his signature move: the Blazing Sun Strike.

The left hand twitched.

Jin Tian moved before the attack could land. He sidestepped, his sword cutting upward in a diagonal slash that caught Liu Feng's wrist before the fire technique could fully form. The impact was clean, precise.

Liu Feng's sword clattered to the ground.

Silence.

The overseer raised his hand, ready to declare the match over. But Liu Feng laughed. He laughed long and loud, the sound echoing across the silent arena.

"Remarkable," Liu Feng said, flexing his injured wrist. "You read me like a book. How?"

Jin Tian didn't answer. He couldn't. How could he explain that he had studied this man's techniques for decades before they ever met? That he had learned Liu Feng's secrets through blood and betrayal?

"I asked you a question," Liu Feng said, his voice hardening. "How do you know my techniques?"

"I pay attention," Jin Tian replied.

"Liar."

The word hung in the air like a challenge. Liu Feng bent down and picked up his sword, his movements deliberate, controlled. When he straightened, his eyes had changed. The predatory gleam was gone, replaced by something colder. Something calculating.

"You move like someone who has faced me before," Liu Feng said. "But we've never met. So why does your stance remind me of someone I've only heard about in legends?"

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

"The Sword God," Liu Feng continued, his voice dropping to a whisper that somehow carried across the arena. "The legendary Jin Tian who rose from nothing to become the greatest swordsman in history. They say he had a stance just like yours. A stance that no one else could replicate."

The crowd murmured. Elder Zhou's eyes narrowed.

Jin Tian forced his expression to remain neutral. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't you?" Liu Feng's smile returned, but it was different now. Sharper. More dangerous. "I've studied the old texts. The historical records. The techniques of the Sword God were recorded before he disappeared. And your movements, your parries, your counters—they match perfectly."

The arena fell into absolute silence.

Elder Zhou stepped forward, his face unreadable. "Inner Disciple Liu Feng, explain yourself."

Liu Feng turned to face the elder, his expression triumphant. "I'm saying that this boy—this outer disciple who supposedly trained in obscurity—fights exactly like the greatest swordsman our sect has ever produced. The same swordsman who vanished three hundred years ago without a trace."

Every eye in the arena turned to Jin Tian.

He felt their gazes like physical weight. Suspicion. Curiosity. Fear. He had been careless. He had relied too heavily on his memories, on techniques that marked him as something other than a twelve-year-old prodigy.

But how could he have known? Liu Feng had never been a scholar in his first life. He had never studied the old texts. He had never shown any interest in history.

Unless something had changed. Unless someone had told him what to look for.

Jin Tian's gaze snapped to the stands, where Chen Wei sat with a satisfied smile on his face. Their eyes met, and Chen Wei nodded slowly, as if acknowledging a game well played.

Realization struck Jin Tian like a physical blow.

This wasn't a coincidence. Liu Feng's knowledge wasn't accidental. Chen Wei had arranged this—the challenge, the match, the revelation. He had planted the seed of suspicion, and Liu Feng had watered it with the poison of truth.

"I see," Jin Tian said, his voice carrying across the silent arena. "You've been well prepared."

Liu Feng's smile widened. "I have no idea what you mean. I'm simply stating facts. The overseers can verify my claims. The old texts are clear: the Sword God's techniques are unique, unmistakable, and impossible to replicate without years of training."

He paused, letting the implication sink in.

"So, Outer Disciple Jin Tian, I have a simple question. How does a twelve-year-old boy who has never left this sect fight exactly like a man who died three hundred years ago?"

The question hung in the air like a blade waiting to fall.

Elder Zhou's hand moved to his storage ring, and a jade slip appeared in his palm. He held it up, and a projection of text appeared in the air above the arena. Ancient characters. Diagrams of sword forms. And at the center of each diagram, a stance that matched Jin Tian's perfectly.

"The records don't lie," Elder Zhou said, his voice heavy. "These techniques are known only to those who have studied the Sword God's legacy. And according to our sect's history, no living disciple has mastered them."

He turned to face Jin Tian, his eyes hard.

"So I will ask you the same question. How do you know these techniques?"

Jin Tian stood alone in the center of the arena, surrounded by hundreds of eyes, all demanding answers he couldn't give.

His hand tightened on his sword.

And he smiled.

"Perhaps," he said, his voice carrying the weight of three hundred years of regret, "the Sword God isn't as dead as you believe."

The arena erupted.

Elder Zhou's face went pale. Liu Feng's triumphant expression faltered. In the stands, Chen Wei rose to his feet, his careful composure cracking for just a moment.

And in that moment of chaos, Jin Tian saw his chance.

He moved.

Not toward the exit, but toward the records hall. Toward the truth he had been seeking since the moment he woke in this life. If Chen Wei was already moving against him, if the conspiracy was already in motion, then he had no more time to waste.

He had to find the traitors before they found him.

And he had to do it before the entire sect turned against him.

Behind him, Elder Zhou's voice rang out: "Seal the arena! No one leaves!"

But Jin Tian was already gone, his twelve-year-old body carrying him through shadows and corridors, toward answers that would change everything.

The hunt had begun.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Blade That Remembers

The training grounds had emptied by the time Jin Tian finished his preparations. The midday sun beat down on the stone platforms, baking the dirt until it cracked like old skin. He stood alone in the center of the largest ring, his wooden practice sword resting across his knees.

Three hours until Liu Feng.

Three hours until he faced the man who had ended his first life.

He closed his eyes and let the memories wash over him. The cold steel. The shock of betrayal. The way Liu Feng's eyes had held no malice — only duty. As if killing the Sword God had been a task, nothing more.

*Why?*

That question had haunted him through three centuries of cultivation, through the slow rise to power, through the joy of taking disciples and the agony of their betrayal. Liu Feng had been the last blade. The one that stopped his heart. But he had never been the architect.

Someone had commanded him.

Someone had whispered the order that sent Liu Feng's sword through Jin Tian's chest.

The footsteps came softly, deliberately. Jin Tian didn't open his eyes.

"You're early, Outer Disciple Jin Tian."

The voice was smooth, cultured. A voice that belonged to poetry recitals and tea ceremonies, not battlefields.

"I prefer to familiarize myself with the terrain before a match," Jin Tian replied. "It gives me an advantage."

Liu Feng stepped onto the platform, his robes immaculate, his bearing that of a man who had never known defeat. He was younger than Jin Tian remembered — barely eighteen, with the soft edges of youth still clinging to his jaw. In Jin Tian's first life, Liu Feng had been a rising star, a fire cultivator whose techniques burned brighter than any other inner disciple's.

Now he was just a boy with a sword.

"Advantage," Liu Feng repeated, amusement coloring his voice. "Against me? You're an outer disciple who only secured this match through a loophole in the scoring system. I've been cultivating since before you could hold a blade."

"And yet here I am, sitting in your arena, preparing to fight you." Jin Tian opened his eyes. "That should tell you something about the value of assumptions."

Liu Feng's smile flickered. For just a moment, something dark passed behind his eyes — recognition, perhaps, or the first stirring of unease. Then it was gone, replaced by the polished mask of a confident inner disciple.

"You speak boldly for someone who's about to be humiliated."

"I speak honestly. There's a difference."

The tension between them crackled like static before a storm. Other disciples had begun to gather at the edges of the training ground, sensing the spectacle to come. Word had spread quickly — the arrogant outer disciple who had challenged the ranking system, who had claimed a slot that should have gone to Chen Wei, who now faced one of the strongest inner disciples in the sect.

They came for blood.

They came to watch a lesson being taught.

Liu Feng turned to leave, then paused. "One piece of advice, Outer Disciple. When the match begins, don't hold back. It would be a shame if you died before showing me what you're truly capable of."

He walked away without waiting for a response.

Jin Tian watched him go, his fingers tightening on the wooden sword.

*He knows.*

The realization hit like a thunderclap. Liu Feng's words hadn't been a threat — they had been a test. A probe. The inner disciple suspected something, even if he didn't know what. He had sensed that Jin Tian was more than he appeared.

Which meant that someone had warned him.

---

The hour of the match arrived faster than Jin Tian had anticipated. He had spent the time meditating, cycling his qi through pathways that had been closed to him in his first life at this age. His body was weaker than it should be — the malnutrition of his childhood years had stunted his growth, and his meridians were still adjusting to the sudden influx of power.

But his mind was sharp. His instincts were honed. And his hatred was a furnace that burned away all hesitation.

The arena was packed. Inner disciples lined the walls, their arms crossed, their expressions ranging from curiosity to contempt. Outer disciples clustered in the back, their presence tolerated but not welcomed. Elder Zhou sat on a raised platform, his face unreadable.

And in the center of it all stood Liu Feng, his cultivation blazing like a bonfire.

He had shed his robes, revealing a lean, muscular frame covered in the scars of countless battles. In his hand, he held a real sword — steel, not wood. The blade caught the sunlight and threw it back in sharp, angry points.

Jin Tian stepped into the arena with his wooden practice sword.

The crowd laughed.

"Ignorant fool," someone muttered. "He's going to die."

Jin Tian ignored them. He reached the center of the arena and stopped, his eyes locked on Liu Feng's.

"Rules," Elder Zhou announced, his voice carrying over the murmurs. "The match ends when one combatant yields, is incapacitated, or dies. No outside interference. Begin."

Liu Feng moved first.

He exploded forward, his sword trailing flames that left afterimages in the air. The strike was fast — faster than anything Jin Tian had faced since his regression. A killing blow, delivered without hesitation.

Jin Tian sidestepped.

The blade passed within a hair's breadth of his chest, close enough that he felt the heat of the flames. He didn't counter. He didn't need to. The first exchange was always about information — learning the opponent's rhythm, their tells, their weaknesses.

Liu Feng pressed the attack, his sword becoming a blur of fire and steel. Each strike was precise, each movement calculated. He fought like a man who had been trained to kill, not to compete.

*He's been taught by someone outside the sect.*

The realization crystallized as Jin Tian deflected a thrust with the flat of his wooden sword. The technique was too refined, too ruthless. The sect taught combat as a path to enlightenment. Liu Feng's style was designed for assassination.

"Running already?" Liu Feng taunted, his sword carving a crescent of flame through the air.

Jin Tian ducked under it, feeling the heat singe his hair. "Gathering information."

"Information won't save you."

"It already has."

He moved then, for the first time taking the offensive. His wooden sword became an extension of his will, striking at angles that shouldn't have been possible, flowing through gaps in Liu Feng's defense that didn't seem to exist.

The crowd fell silent.

Liu Feng's eyes widened as he was forced back, his perfect stance crumbling under the assault. Jin Tian's attacks were relentless, each one building on the last, creating a web of pressure that left no room for counterattack.

*He's strong,* Jin Tian admitted. *But he's predictable.*

He had fought Liu Feng a hundred times in his memories. He knew every technique, every feint, every moment of hesitation. The fire cultivator was a brilliant warrior, but he was still limited by his age and experience. He hadn't yet learned to hide his intentions.

Jin Tian had three centuries of practice seeing through masks.

He struck.

The wooden sword connected with Liu Feng's wrist, sending the steel blade spinning through the air. Before the clatter of metal on stone could fade, Jin Tian had his sword pressed against Liu Feng's throat.

The arena was silent.

"I yield," Liu Feng whispered, his voice hollow.

Jin Tian didn't lower his sword. "Who sent you?"

"What?"

"The assassination techniques. The way you fight. Who taught you?"

Liu Feng's face went pale. For a moment, Jin Tian saw fear in his eyes — genuine, soul-deep fear. Then it was replaced by something else. Resignation.

"I don't know their name," Liu Feng said. "They came to me in a dream. Showed me techniques I'd never seen before. Promised me power beyond the sect's teachings."

*A dream.*

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. He had heard of such things before — methods of mental intrusion that allowed cultivators to plant ideas, techniques, even commands in the minds of others. But those methods required a level of cultivation that should have been impossible in this era.

Unless someone else had regressed.

Unless he wasn't the only one who had returned.

"Who else?" Jin Tian demanded. "Who else did they contact?"

Liu Feng's lips curled into a bitter smile. "You think you've won, but you've only shown them your hand. They'll come for you now. They'll — "

He convulsed.

Blood erupted from his mouth, black and thick, spilling across the stone floor. His body twisted, bones cracking with sounds that made the crowd recoil. Jin Tian stepped back, his mind racing.

*Poison. A failsafe.*

Liu Feng's eyes met his one last time. There was no accusation in them. Only pity.

Then his body collapsed, dissolving into a pool of black liquid that evaporated before it could touch the ground.

The arena erupted into chaos.

Elder Zhou was shouting orders, disciples were screaming, and somewhere in the crowd, Jin Tian felt a gaze settle on him like a blade against his spine.

He turned.

Chen Wei stood at the edge of the arena, his face a mask of horror and confusion. But his eyes — his eyes were calm. Calculating. And they were fixed on Jin Tian with an intensity that spoke of knowledge.

*He knows,* Jin Tian realized. *He knows what just happened. He knows what Liu Feng was about to say.*

*And he knows what's coming for me next.*

The crowd surged around him, demanding answers, demanding explanations. But Jin Tian heard none of it. He was staring at Chen Wei, watching the boy's lips move silently, forming words that only he could read:

*"Welcome to the game, Sword God."*

title: The Jade Token's Secret
content:
The training grounds emptied quickly after Elder Zhou's announcement. Disciples scattered like startled birds, eager to spread news of the tournament's disruption. Jin Tian remained where he stood, feeling the weight of hundreds of stares burning into his back.

Chen Wei had already departed with his retinue, but not before casting one final glance over his shoulder. The message was clear: *This isn't over.*

Jin Tian ignored it. He had three hours until his match against Liu Feng. Three hours to prepare for the man who had ended his life in a previous existence.

He made his way toward the meditation pavilion at the sect's eastern edge—a secluded spot he remembered from his first life, where the qi flowed thick as honey and the elders rarely ventured. The path wound through bamboo groves and across stone bridges spanning crystal streams. In his youth, he had walked this path a thousand times, always rushing, always impatient.

Now he walked slowly, savoring every step.

The pavilion stood empty, as he'd expected. Its wooden pillars were carved with formation arrays meant to amplify concentration, though most disciples found them too uncomfortable for extended use. The stone floor was cold, the roof leaky in places. Perfect.

Jin Tian settled into a cross-legged position and closed his eyes.

He needed to assess his current strength honestly. In his first life, he had reached the ninth level of Qi Condensation by age fifteen, Foundation Establishment at seventeen, and Nascent Soul by thirty. His seven disciples had each surpassed him in their own ways—some through raw talent, others through cunning or luck.

But that was then. Now he was twelve years old, barely past the second level of Qi Condensation, with a body that had never tasted real combat.

The tournament would accelerate his growth, but it would also paint a target on his back. Chen Wei was already hostile. The other outer disciples would either fear him or seek to prove themselves against him. And now there was Liu Feng—an inner disciple with a direct connection to his past death.

*Why?* The question gnawed at him. *Why did Liu Feng kill me?*

In his memories, Liu Feng had been a straightforward cultivator. Brutal in combat, honorable in victory, never one for plots or schemes. He had risen through the ranks on pure talent, untainted by the politics that consumed so many others.

Yet it was Liu Feng's blade that had pierced Jin Tian's heart. Liu Feng's face that had been the last thing he'd seen.

Unless...

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open.

Unless Liu Feng hadn't acted of his own accord. In his first life, he had been so focused on his own power, his own cultivation, that he had failed to see the webs being woven around him. His seven disciples had manipulated events for years before striking. What if Liu Feng had been a pawn?

What if someone else had held the leash?

He reached into his robes and withdrew the jade token Elder Zhou had given him—the mark of his entry into the inner division tournament. It was a simple thing, rectangular and smooth, etched with formation characters that glowed faintly in the afternoon light.

In his first life, he had never received such a token. He had fought his way into the inner division through the standard channels, earning his place through years of accumulated merit. This shortcut was unprecedented.

Which meant it was also dangerous.

Jin Tian channeled a thread of qi into the token, tracing the formation characters with practiced precision. The glow intensified, and he felt a faint resistance—a seal of some kind, meant to prevent tampering.

He pushed harder.

The resistance shattered like glass, and the token's true nature revealed itself.

Beneath the surface layer of tournament registration data lay a complex web of tracking formations, memory arrays, and something else—something dark. Jin Tian's breath caught as he recognized the pattern.

Soul-binding poison.

Not enough to kill outright. Not even enough to weaken noticeably. But over time, the poison would seep into his meridians, corrupting his qi and making him susceptible to external control. In three months, he would be a puppet. In six, a slave.

In one year, he would be dead.

He crushed the token in his fist.

The jade shattered, and the dark formations dissolved into motes of black light that hung in the air like malevolent fireflies. They pulsed once, twice, then faded into nothing.

Jin Tian sat in the sudden silence, his heart pounding.

*Someone wants me controlled. Someone wants me dead.*
