But who? Elder Zhou had given him the token, but that meant nothing. The elder could have been tricked, or he could have been an unwitting pawn in a larger game. The sect was a labyrinth of factions and alliances, each pulling strings in invisible directions.

He thought back to his first life, to the moments before his death. Liu Feng's face had been expressionless, his movements mechanical. At the time, Jin Tian had assumed it was the cold efficiency of a killer. Now he wondered if it had been something else entirely.

*What if Liu Feng was controlled too?*

The possibility chilled him to the bone. If someone had the power to enslave inner disciples, to turn them into weapons against their will, then no one in the sect was safe. Not the elders. Not the patriarch. Not Jin Tian himself, even with all his past-life knowledge.

He had to find out more. And the only way to do that was to face Liu Feng in the tournament, to look into his eyes and see if there was anyone home behind them.

But first, he needed to purge any residual poison from his system.

He closed his eyes and began cycling his qi, pushing it through every meridian, every acupoint, every hidden channel he had learned to access in his previous existence. The process was agonizing—his young body wasn't accustomed to such rigorous internal cleansing—but he pushed through the pain, sweat beading on his forehead.

After an hour, he opened his eyes, exhausted but clean.

The poison was gone.

He stood, his legs shaky beneath him. The sun had begun its descent toward the mountains, casting long shadows across the pavilion floor. He had two hours until the match.

Time to prepare.

He retrieved a second jade from his storage pouch—a blank recording device he had taken from the sect library weeks ago, before the tournament had even begun. In his first life, he had used such devices to study his opponents' techniques. Now he would use them to study Liu Feng's.

He pressed the jade to his forehead and focused, drawing on memories that were decades old but still crystal clear. Liu Feng's fighting style. His signature techniques. His weaknesses.

The fire-aspected cultivator favored aggressive, overwhelming attacks. His signature move was the Blazing Sun Strike, a technique that condensed fire qi into a single devastating blow. He was fast, relentless, and ruthless.

But he had a tell.

In the moment before launching his strongest attacks, Liu Feng's left foot would shift slightly, adjusting his balance to accommodate the massive qi output. It was a tiny movement, almost imperceptible, but it gave away his intent a full second before the strike landed.

In his first life, Jin Tian had never exploited this weakness. He had been too proud, too confident in his own abilities. He had wanted to defeat Liu Feng at his best, to prove that his sword was superior to Liu Feng's flame.

That arrogance had cost him everything.

This time, he would fight differently. This time, he would use every advantage, exploit every weakness, and leave nothing to chance.

He was still reviewing his memories when a shadow fell across the pavilion entrance.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian."

The voice was cold, formal, and completely unexpected. Jin Tian looked up to see a young woman standing in the doorway, her robes the deep blue of an inner disciple. Her features were sharp and beautiful, her eyes the color of storm clouds, and her aura radiated the pressure of the fifth level of Qi Condensation—impressive for someone who looked no older than sixteen.

But what caught Jin Tian's attention wasn't her cultivation. It was her face.

He knew her. In his first life, she had been one of his seven disciples. The second to betray him. The one who had smiled as she plunged her dagger into his back.

*Shen Yue.*

She had been his most promising student, a genius of wind-aspected cultivation who had mastered techniques that took others decades to learn. She had called him "Master" with genuine affection, had laughed at his jokes and sought his advice on matters both martial and mundane.

And then she had killed him.

"Elder Zhou sent me," she said, her voice betraying no emotion. "He wants to ensure you're prepared for your match."

Jin Tian forced his face into a mask of calm. "That's... unexpected. I didn't think the elders concerned themselves with outer disciples."

"They don't." Shen Yue stepped into the pavilion, her movements fluid and graceful. "But you're no ordinary outer disciple, are you? The boy who calculated his own scores, who challenged the system, who humiliated Chen Wei in front of the entire sect."

"I didn't humiliate him. I simply proved my worth."

"Is that what you call it?" She circled him slowly, her eyes appraising. "Chen Wei has connections. His family has sponsored three generations of disciples. You've made an enemy of someone who can make your life very difficult."

"I'm aware."

"And yet you don't seem concerned."

Jin Tian met her gaze. In his first life, he had seen this same look—curiosity mixed with calculation, the assessing gaze of someone trying to decide if he was useful or dangerous.

"I have a match to win," he said. "Chen Wei is a distraction I can't afford."

Shen Yue's lips curved into a smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Then I'll be direct. Elder Zhou has his eye on you. He sees potential, but he also sees recklessness. The token he gave you was a test—a test you passed by destroying it."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "You knew about the poison?"

"We needed to know if you could detect it. If you couldn't, you would have been useful but disposable. If you could..." She paused, her smile widening. "Then you might be worth investing in."

"This is a recruitment," Jin Tian said slowly.

"This is an opportunity." Shen Yue reached into her robes and produced a second jade token, identical to the one he had destroyed. "This one is clean. Take it, and you'll have Elder Zhou's support in the tournament. Refuse, and you'll face Liu Feng alone."

Jin Tian stared at the token. The trap was obvious. Accept the token, and he would be indebted to Elder Zhou. Refuse, and he would make an enemy of a powerful elder.

But there was a third option.

"What if I want something in return?"

Shen Yue's eyebrows rose. "You're in no position to negotiate."

"Everyone is in a position to negotiate," Jin Tian said. "I want information. Specifically, I want to know why Liu Feng was chosen as my opponent."

The change in Shen Yue's expression was subtle but unmistakable. For just a fraction of a second, the mask slipped, and he saw something flicker in her eyes.

Surprise.

"That's a strange question," she said carefully.

"Is it? Or is it the question you were hoping I wouldn't ask?"

The silence stretched between them, heavy with unspoken truths. Jin Tian watched Shen Yue's face, reading the micro-expressions he had learned to recognize in his previous life. She was calculating, weighing options, deciding how much to reveal.

Finally, she spoke.

"Liu Feng requested you specifically. He said he heard about your performance in the outer division tournament and wanted to test himself against a rising talent."

"That's a lie."

"How do you know

title: The Weight of a Killing Blow
content:
The tournament grounds hummed with anticipation. Outer disciples lined the stone barriers, their voices a constant buzz of speculation and excitement. Jin Tian stood alone in the preparation chamber beneath the main arena, his eyes closed, his breathing steady.

Three hours. He had used them well.

His meridians pulsed with qi, freshly cycled through the Minor Circulation technique he had perfected over three centuries of cultivation. His body, still young and limited, hummed with borrowed strength. Not enough. Never enough against an inner disciple ranked seventh. But it would have to do.

The door slid open.

"Jin Tian." It wasn't a question. The voice belonged to Elder Zhou, whose presence filled the small chamber like smoke. "You have one minute before your match begins. I came to offer you advice."

Jin Tian opened his eyes. "I'm listening."

"Liu Feng specializes in the Blazing Sun Palm. His attacks are straightforward—powerful, direct, overwhelming. Most opponents try to outmaneuver him, use speed or trickery." Elder Zhou's eyes narrowed. "They fail."

"Then what would you suggest?"

"Don't let him dictate the rhythm. Liu Feng fights like a fire—he consumes space, controls range, forces you onto the defensive. If you let him, he will burn through your defenses in three exchanges." The old man paused. "But fires can be smothered. If you strike first, strike hard, and strike where he cannot defend..."

"His left side," Jin Tian finished. "He favors his right palm. His left guard drops when he winds up for a full-power strike."

Elder Zhou's eyebrows rose. "You've studied him."

"I've studied everyone."

A bell rang overhead. The match was about to begin.

Elder Zhou stepped aside, and Jin Tian walked past him into the corridor that led to the arena. The light grew brighter, the noise louder, and then he was there—standing on the raised stone platform, facing a young man who had killed him once before.

Liu Feng was exactly as Jin Tian remembered. Tall, broad-shouldered, with eyes that burned like coals set in a face of cold granite. He wore the inner disciple's silver robes, the symbol of the Blazing Sun Sect embroidered on his chest in threads of gold and red. In his right hand, he held a long blade—not drawn, but resting against his shoulder, casual and ready.

"So," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena, "the brat who talks his way into tournaments. I've heard of you."

"And I've heard of you," Jin Tian replied. "Ranked seventh among the inner disciples. Twenty-seven consecutive victories in tournament matches. Your Blazing Sun Palm has been known to melt stone."

Liu Feng's smile didn't reach his eyes. "You've done your homework."

"I believe in preparation."

"Preparation won't save you." Liu Feng drew his blade, and the air around it shimmered with heat. "I'll give you one chance to withdraw. No one will think less of you. You're an outer disciple. You've already exceeded expectations."

Jin Tian drew his own sword—a standard-issue outer disciple weapon, plain steel with a leather-wrapped hilt. It felt wrong in his hands, too light, too short, too ordinary. But it would have to do.

"I'm not here to exceed expectations," he said. "I'm here to win."

The referee, an overseer with a stern face and a jade token at his belt, raised his hand. "The match will begin on my signal. Both combatants will fight until one yields, is incapacitated, or is thrown from the platform. No lethal strikes are permitted."

The words were standard. The meaning wasn't. No lethal strikes meant that Liu Feng wouldn't kill him here. Not publicly. Not in front of the elders.

But if Jin Tian lost, if he was humiliated, if he was injured badly enough to miss the inner division tournament entirely...

That would be just as effective. And far easier to explain.

The overseer's hand fell.

Liu Feng moved first.

He was fast—faster than Jin Tian had expected, faster than the recordings showed. His blade cut through the air in a burning arc, forcing Jin Tian to leap backward, the heat singing his robes. The stone where he had stood cracked and blackened.

"You're quick," Liu Feng said, pressing forward. "But quick doesn't win battles."

He struck again, and again, each blow faster than the last. Jin Tian deflected, dodged, retreated. His sword sang against Liu Feng's blade, sparks flying, the metal heating with every impact.

This was wrong.

Liu Feng was fighting like he wanted to overwhelm, to crush, to end the match in seconds. But that wasn't his style. In Jin Tian's first life, Liu Feng had been a patient fighter, a strategist who wore his opponents down before striking the killing blow. This aggression was different. This was personal.

Why?

Jin Tian ducked under a horizontal slash, feeling the wind of its passage ruffle his hair. He countered low, aiming for Liu Feng's exposed ribs, but the inner disciple twisted aside with unnatural grace.

"You're holding back," Liu Feng snarled. "Fight me properly!"

"Why do you care how I fight?" Jin Tian replied, parrying another blow. "You're supposed to be testing me, not hunting me."

Something flickered in Liu Feng's eyes. Fear. Recognition. And then it was gone, replaced by the same burning intensity.

"I know who you are," Liu Feng said, his voice dropping so low only Jin Tian could hear. "I know what you're trying to do."

Jin Tian's blood went cold. "What are you talking about?"

"You think you're clever, hiding your strength, manipulating the tournament. But there are eyes on you, boy. Eyes that have been watching since the day you arrived." Liu Feng's blade blazed with qi, the heat becoming unbearable. "They sent me to stop you."

They.

The word echoed in Jin Tian's mind. Not Chen Wei. Not the seven disciples. Someone else. Someone who had known about him before he had even begun his regression.

Someone who had been waiting.

Liu Feng's next strike was different. It wasn't a tournament technique. It wasn't even a Blazing Sun Sect technique. It was something older, darker, a blade wrapped in shadows that drank the light and screamed with the voices of the damned.

Jin Tian recognized it.

He had seen it before. Three hundred years from now, in the hands of a demonic cultivator who had nearly destroyed the sect. A technique from the Abyss Sect, a forbidden school of cultivation that had been eradicated—or so he had thought—a thousand years ago.

Liu Feng was not what he appeared to be.

Jin Tian threw himself sideways, the shadow-blade grazing his shoulder. Pain exploded through his arm, not fire but ice, a cold that spread like poison through his meridians. He stumbled, nearly falling, and Liu Feng pressed his advantage.

"Surprised?" Liu Feng whispered, his eyes now fully black, no white visible. "You should be. The sect doesn't know. The elders don't know. Only a select few understand the truth about your master's plans."

Master. The word struck Jin Tian harder than any blade.

"What plans?" he gasped, deflecting another strike with desperate strength.

"You'll find out soon enough." Liu Feng's blade descended, and Jin Tian knew he couldn't block it. His arms were numb, his qi depleted, his body screaming for rest.

But he wasn't a boy anymore.

He was the Sword God.

Time seemed to slow. Jin Tian's mind, honed by centuries of combat, processed a thousand possibilities in a single heartbeat. He saw the angle of Liu Feng's strike, the weakness in his stance, the momentary imbalance as his weight shifted forward.

It was impossible. His body couldn't move fast enough. His qi couldn't respond in time.

But he didn't need to move. He didn't need to block.

He needed to redirect.

Jin Tian dropped his sword.

The blade clattered against the stone, and the crowd gasped. Liu Feng's eyes widened in confusion, but his strike continued its arc, aimed directly at Jin Tian's exposed chest.

In that instant, Jin Tian's hand moved. Not to his fallen sword. Not to block. His fingers found Liu Feng's wrist, found the pressure point where qi gathered before release, and pressed.

The shadow-blade flickered. The technique destabilized. And Liu Feng's own attack turned against him, the dark energy recoiling up his arm, into his shoulder, into his core.

He screamed.

It wasn't a human sound. It was a shriek of pure darkness, a cry that shook the arena and sent the disciples stumbling backward, clutching their ears. Liu Feng's body convulsed, his eyes rolling back, and then he collapsed, smoke rising from his skin.

The overseer rushed forward, his jade token glowing as he checked Liu Feng's pulse. "He's alive," he announced, his voice strained. "But his meridians are shattered. He won't cultivate again."

Silence.

Absolute, crushing silence.

Jin Tian stood over his fallen opponent, breathing hard, his hand still tingling from the contact. He had won. Against an inner disciple. Against a demonic infiltrator. Against a man who had killed him in another life.

But the victory felt hollow.

He looked down at Liu Feng's unconscious face, at the shadows still clinging to his skin, and remembered the words he had spoken.

*They sent me to stop you.*

Who were they? And what did they know about his regression?

A hand fell on his shoulder. Jin Tian spun, ready to fight, but it was only Elder Zhou, his face unreadable.

"The match is yours," the elder said. "You will advance to the next round."

"Elder—"

"Not now." Zhou's grip tightened. "Not here. When the tournament is over, we will speak. Until then, say nothing about what happened. Do you understand?"

Jin Tian met his eyes. There was something there—knowledge, perhaps, or suspicion. He couldn't tell which.

"I understand," he said.

But he didn't. Not yet.

As he walked off the platform, the crowd parting before him like water, he felt the weight of eyes upon him. Not just the disciples. Not just the elders.

Something else. Something watching from the shadows.

And for the first time since his regression, Jin Tian felt fear.

title: The Seventh Disciple's Shadow
content:
The training grounds were silent when Jin Tian arrived. Most disciples had gathered at the main arena to watch the inner division matches, leaving the peripheral training fields empty. Perfect. He needed solitude to prepare.

He found a secluded corner near the eastern wall, where ancient stone training posts stood weathered by centuries of sword strikes. Drawing his practice blade, he closed his eyes and let his breathing slow.

Liu Feng.

The name burned in his memory like a brand. In his first life, Liu Feng had been a worthy rival—someone he respected, someone he thought understood the pursuit of the sword. They had sparred dozens of times, each match pushing both of them to new heights. Jin Tian had considered him a friend.

Then came the ambush.

Seven disciples. Seven betrayals. And Liu Feng, the one who struck the final blow.

Why? The question had haunted Jin Tian through three hundred years of cultivation. What could possibly have driven a man of honor to commit such cowardice?

He began his forms. Slow, deliberate cuts that built muscle memory and cleared the mind. The weight of the sword felt natural in his hands, an extension of his will. But his thoughts churned beneath the calm surface.

In the original timeline, the inner division tournament had been a formality. Chen Wei had won, as expected. Jin Tian had placed second, earning enough recognition to qualify for the inner division entrance exam. Liu Feng had never been his opponent.

But this time was different. This time, Jin Tian had forced a change. And change created ripples.

"Training alone, little brother?"

Jin Tian's blade stopped mid-stroke. He hadn't heard anyone approach.

A young woman leaned against the training post nearest him, arms crossed, a faint smile on her lips. She wore the blue robes of an inner disciple, but her bearing spoke of something more. Her eyes were sharp, assessing, and her hand rested casually on the hilt of a sword that hummed with latent power.

"Sister Song," Jin Tian said, bowing. "I didn't expect to see you here."

Song Yuxi. Ranked third among the inner disciples. Known as the Jade Serpent for her fluid, unpredictable combat style. In his first life, she had been one of the few inner disciples who treated him fairly.

She raised an eyebrow. "You know my name?"

"You're famous, Sister. The Jade Serpent who defeated three inner disciples in a single match during last year's tournament."

Her smile widened. "Someone's done their homework." She pushed off the post and walked closer, circling him like a predator sizing up prey. "I heard you caused quite a stir at the selection ceremony. Called out Chen Wei in front of everyone."

"Not called out. Simply pointed out a discrepancy in the scoring."

"Mm-hmm." Her tone was skeptical. "And now you're facing Liu Feng in three hours. A bold move for an outer disciple."

"Bold or foolish?"

"Both." She stopped in front of him, her expression turning serious. "Liu Feng is not like the other inner disciples. He doesn't fight for glory or rank. He fights to win, by any means necessary. He will not hold back because you're younger or lower ranked."

"I understand."

"Do you?" She studied his face. "There's something different about you, Jin Tian. I've watched you in the outer division matches. You fight like someone who's been doing this for decades, not years. Your footwork is perfect. Your timing is impeccable. But there's something else—something in your eyes."

Jin Tian met her gaze without flinching. "What do you see?"

"Sorrow." Her voice was soft. "And anger. A lot of anger."

The words struck deeper than any blade. For a moment, Jin Tian felt exposed, as if she had peeled back the layers of his carefully constructed disguise and seen the three hundred years of rage and regret beneath.

"I have my reasons," he said finally.

"We all do." She reached into her robe and pulled out a small jade slip. "This is a recording of Liu Feng's matches from the past six months. His techniques, his patterns, his tells." She pressed it into his hand. "Consider it a gift from one sword to another."

"Why are you helping me?"

Song Yuxi's smile faded. "Because I don't like what's happening in this sect. The inner division has become a nest of vipers, and Liu Feng is one of the most venomous." She paused. "Also, I owe a debt to someone who believed in me when no one else did. She told me to pay it forward."

Jin Tian's heart stopped.

"Who?" he asked, his voice barely a whisper.

"A senior disciple. She left the sect years ago, but she taught me everything I know." Song Yuxi's eyes grew distant. "Her name was Lin Xue. Have you heard of her?"

Lin Xue. The name hit Jin Tian like a thunderbolt.

His fourth disciple. The one who had been quiet, loyal, and steadfast. The one who had wept when he announced his retirement from the sect's leadership. The one who had—

No. She wasn't there at the ambush.

In all the chaos of that day, Jin Tian had never seen Lin Xue among the seven who attacked him. He had assumed she was dead, killed in the fighting that preceded his betrayal. But if she had left the sect years before...

"Lin Xue," he repeated, tasting the name. "I've heard whispers."

"She was remarkable. The best sword I've ever seen." Song Yuxi's voice carried clear admiration. "She told me once that the true path of the sword is lonely, but it doesn't have to be walked alone. She said we should lift each other up, not cut each other down." She met his eyes. "I see that same fire in you, Jin Tian. Don't let them extinguish it."

Before he could respond, she turned and walked away, her robes billowing in the afternoon breeze.

Jin Tian clutched the jade slip, his mind racing. Lin Xue had left the sect. That meant she hadn't been among the seven traitors. But if she was alive, she might know something—might have seen something—that could help him understand what had happened.

The bell tower chimed. Two hours until his match.

He pressed the jade slip to his forehead, and the recorded matches flowed into his consciousness like a river of images. Liu Feng's movements played out in perfect detail—his aggressive openings, his reliance on fire techniques, the subtle shift in his stance before he launched his finishing move.

But as Jin Tian studied, something caught his attention.

In every match where Liu Feng was winning easily, he would glance toward the spectator stands. Once. Twice. Always in the same direction.

Jin Tian rewound the memory, focusing on that specific moment. In the shadows of the stands, barely visible, was a figure. A woman in black robes, her face obscured by a veil. She gave no signal, made no gesture. She simply watched.

And Liu Feng fought harder.

The pattern repeated in match after match. Whenever Liu Feng seemed to be struggling, he would look to the stands, and his strength would surge. His techniques would become sharper, faster, more brutal.

Someone was controlling him.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. If Liu Feng was being manipulated, then who else? The seven disciples who betrayed him—had they been puppets as well? Had someone been pulling the strings all along?

The jade slip crumbled to dust in his clenched fist.

He had two hours to prepare for a fight against a puppet. Two hours to face an enemy who might not be an enemy at all, but a victim of the same unseen hand that had orchestrated his death.

And somewhere in the sect, hidden in the shadows, that hand was still moving.

Jin Tian picked up his blade and began his forms again, but this time, his movements were different. Sharper. More deliberate. He wasn't just preparing for Liu Feng anymore.

He was preparing for war.

The arena was packed when Jin Tian arrived for his match. Word had spread through the sect like wildfire—an outer disciple had challenged Chen Wei's position, and now he was about to face one of the inner division's strongest fighters.

The stands buzzed with speculation. Most expected a quick defeat. A few whispered that perhaps the boy had potential after all. But no one expected what they were about to see.

Liu Feng stood in the center of the arena, his robes immaculate, his blade already drawn. He was handsome in a cold, angular way, with eyes that held no warmth. When he saw Jin Tian approach, he smiled.

"Impressive," Liu Feng said. "I didn't think you'd actually show up."

"I didn't think you'd be taking orders from someone in the shadows," Jin Tian replied, his voice carrying across the silent arena.

Liu Feng's smile vanished.

"What did you say?"

"I said," Jin Tian continued, stepping onto the arena floor, "that I wonder who you're fighting for today. Yourself? Or the woman in black who sits in the eastern stands?"

The reaction was immediate. Liu Feng's face went pale, then red. His hand tightened on his sword until his knuckles turned white.

"You know nothing."

"I know enough." Jin Tian drew his blade, the metal singing as it left the sheath. "And I know that when this match is over, I'm going to find out who's been pulling your strings."

Liu Feng's composure cracked. For a moment, Jin Tian saw fear in his eyes—not of the match, but of what would happen after.

"Enough talk," Liu Feng snarled, and launched himself forward.

The battle began.

Jin Tian met the attack head-on, his blade intercepting Liu Feng's with a shower of sparks. The force of the impact drove him back a step, but he held his ground.

Liu Feng pressed his advantage, unleashing a barrage of strikes that came faster and faster. Fire qi bloomed around his blade, leaving trails of heat in the air. Any normal outer disciple would have been overwhelmed in seconds.

But Jin Tian was not normal.

He flowed around the attacks like water around stone, his movements economical and precise. Every block, every dodge, was calculated to conserve energy while forcing Liu Feng to overextend.

The crowd watched in stunned silence as the outer disciple weathered the storm.

Liu Feng's attacks grew more desperate. He threw everything he had at Jin Tian—techniques that should have ended the match in moments—but nothing landed.

And then, in a flash of movement that no one saw coming, Jin Tian struck.

His blade slipped past Liu Feng's guard, tracing a line of red across the inner disciple's forearm. Liu Feng gasped and stumbled back, his rhythm broken.

"Who is she?" Jin Tian demanded, pressing forward.

"None of your business!"

Another exchange. Another cut. Jin Tian was bleeding Liu Feng slowly, methodically, like a butcher carving meat.

"She's using you," Jin Tian said, his voice cold. "Just like she used others before you."

Liu Feng's eyes widened. "You don't—you can't know—"

"I know more than you think." Jin Tian's blade found its mark again, this time opening a gash across Liu Feng's ribs. "And I know that if you keep serving her, you'll end up dead. Just like I did."

The words hung in the air.

Liu Feng froze.

"What did you say?"

But Jin Tian didn't answer. He saw it in that moment—the flicker of recognition in Liu Feng's eyes. The understanding that something was terribly wrong.

Before either of them could act, a scream tore through the arena.

Everyone turned.

In the eastern stands, a figure in black robes was fleeing, disappearing through a door that should have been locked. And behind her, lying crumpled on the ground,

title: The Serpent's Whispers
content:
The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the training grounds as Jin Tian made his way toward the inner disciple quarters. His victory over Liu Feng had sent ripples through the sect—an outer disciple defeating a ranked inner disciple was unprecedented, and the whispers followed him like a second shadow.

But the whispers he was listening for now were different.

He had sensed it during the match, beneath the roar of fire and the clash of steel: a presence watching him. Not Elder Zhou's appraising gaze, nor Chen Wei's venomous glare. Something older. Something that had been waiting.

The path to the inner disciple archives wound between ancient buildings, their roofs sagging with centuries of accumulated qi. Jin Tian had walked this path a thousand times in his first life, but now every stone felt significant, every shadow pregnant with meaning.

He stopped at a fork in the path, pretending to examine a crack in the paving stone. Behind his calm facade, his mind raced.

*Someone had been feeding Liu Feng information about his techniques. Someone who knew how Jin Tian fought—not as a twelve-year-old outer disciple, but as the Sword God he had once been.*

The realization had struck him during the third exchange of his match against Liu Feng. The inner disciple had countered his opening stance perfectly, anticipating the exact angle of his thrust. That wasn't luck. That was intelligence.

But from whom?

The only people who knew Jin Tian's combat patterns from his previous life were the seven disciples who had betrayed him. And in this timeline, none of them should have met him yet. He had been careful to avoid them, to stay beneath their notice.

Unless one of them had recognized him first.

"Beautiful day for training, isn't it?"

Jin Tian turned slowly. A woman stood at the edge of the path, her robes the deep blue of a core disciple. She was young—perhaps twenty—with sharp features and eyes that held too much knowing for someone her age.

"You're Lin Mei's senior sister," Jin Tian said, keeping his voice neutral. "I saw you at the tournament."

"Observant." She smiled, but the expression didn't reach her eyes. "My name is Xue Yan. I've been watching your matches with great interest."

"Why?"

"Because you fight like someone who's been doing this for much longer than twelve years." She stepped closer, and Jin Tian felt the air grow cold. "Because you knew exactly how to counter Liu Feng's fire techniques, despite never having faced an inner disciple before. And because..." She paused, tilting her head. "Because you looked at Elder Zhou like you've known him for centuries, not days."

Jin Tian's hand drifted toward his sword. "That's quite an imagination you have."

"Is it?" Xue Yan's smile widened. "Or is it that I've been where you are? Waking up one day with memories that don't belong to you, knowing things you shouldn't know, seeing the faces of people who will one day betray you?"

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow. He kept his expression neutral, but inside, chaos reigned.

*She knows. How does she know?*

"I don't know what you're talking about," he said carefully.

"Of course you don't." Xue Yan laughed, a sound like breaking ice. "But let me give you some advice anyway. The person who's been feeding information to your enemies? They're closer than you think. Much closer. And they're not working alone."

She turned to leave, then paused. "Oh, and Jin Tian? Watch your back during the inner division tournament tomorrow. The first match is always the most dangerous."

Before he could respond, she was gone, blending into the shadows as if she had never been there.

---

Jin Tian didn't sleep that night.

He lay in his cramped outer disciple quarters, staring at the ceiling, replaying Xue Yan's words in his mind. She had known things she shouldn't have known. She had spoken of betrayal as if she had experienced it herself.

*Could she be like me? Someone who lived through the future and came back?*

The possibility was terrifying. If there were others who remembered, then his advantage—his knowledge of future events—was no longer exclusive. His enemies could know his moves before he made them.

But there was another possibility, one that chilled him even more.

*What if she's one of the seven? What if they've already found me?*

He thought back to his first life. The seven disciples had been diverse in background and talent, united only by their ambition and their hatred of his success. None of them had been named Xue Yan. But names could be changed. Faces could be disguised.

At dawn, he rose and began his preparations. Today was the inner division tournament, where the top outer disciples competed against the lower-ranked inner disciples for advancement. His victory over Liu Feng had secured his place, but it had also painted a target on his back.

As he walked to the tournament grounds, he noticed something strange. The other disciples were avoiding him, giving him wide berth, whispering behind their hands. That wasn't unusual—he was used to being an outsider. But today, their fear seemed different. Sharper.

He found out why when he reached the arena.

The tournament board had been updated overnight. His name was listed for the first match, as expected. But his opponent had been changed.

*Jin Tian vs. Inner Disciple Xiao Yuan*

The name sent ice through his veins.

Xiao Yuan. Ranked second among the inner disciples. A genius of wind-aspected techniques, known for his speed and precision. And in Jin Tian's first life, Xiao Yuan had been the architect of his downfall.

Not the killer—that had been Liu Feng. But the planner, the strategist, the one who had orchestrated the betrayal, who had turned each of the seven disciples against him at the perfect moment.

*But Xiao Yuan shouldn't be here. He was supposed to be on a mission in the Eastern Wastes for another three months.*

Someone had changed the timeline. Someone had brought Xiao Yuan back early.

Jin Tian scanned the crowd, searching for Xue Yan's face. He didn't find her. But he did find Chen Wei, standing at the edge of the arena, talking to a figure in dark robes.

The figure turned, and Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

It was Xiao Yuan.

The inner disciple was older than Jin Tian remembered, his face harder, his eyes colder. But there was no mistaking those features, that predatory grace. Xiao Yuan met Jin Tian's gaze and smiled—a thin, knowing smile that promised pain.

*He recognizes me. How?*

The gong sounded, calling the competitors to the arena. Jin Tian walked forward on wooden legs, his mind churning through possibilities. Xiao Yuan's presence here, the changed bracket, Xue Yan's warning—it all pointed to a conspiracy he hadn't anticipated.

As he stepped onto the arena floor, Xiao Yuan waited for him, hands clasped behind his back.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Xiao Yuan said, his voice smooth as silk. "I've heard so much about you. They say you defeated Liu Feng in less than a minute. Impressive for someone of your... age."

"I had good teachers," Jin Tian replied, keeping his voice steady.

"Did you?" Xiao Yuan's smile widened. "Interesting. Because Liu Feng told me something fascinating before he was sent to the infirmary. He said you fought like someone who had studied his techniques for years. Like you knew every move before he made it."

"Liu Feng was predictable."

"Was he?" Xiao Yuan laughed. "Perhaps. Or perhaps you're not what you seem." He leaned closer, lowering his voice. "I know what you are, Jin Tian. I know where you come from. And I know what you're trying to do."

Jin Tian's hand tightened on his sword. "Then you know I'm not someone to be trifled with."

"Oh, I'm not trifling." Xiao Yuan's eyes glittered with malice. "I'm warning you. Stay out of matters that don't concern you. Forget what you think you know. Because if you don't..." He gestured to the crowd, where Jin Tian now noticed figures in dark robes scattered throughout the audience. "There are more of us than you think."

The gong sounded again, signaling the start of the match.

Jin Tian drew his sword, his mind racing. Xiao Yuan knew. Somehow, impossibly, the architect of his first betrayal knew about his regression. And if Xiao Yuan knew, then the other six might know as well.

*But how? And why warn me instead of killing me outright?*

The answer came to him as Xiao Yuan drew his own blade, a curved wind-aspected weapon that hummed with barely contained power.

*Because he wants me to be afraid. He wants me to doubt myself. He wants me to make mistakes.*

Jin Tian settled into his opening stance, the same stance he had used against Liu Feng. But this time, he modified it slightly, shifting his weight, adjusting his grip by a fraction.

If Xiao Yuan had intelligence on his fighting style, then Jin Tian had to change. Had to be unpredictable. Had to fight like the Sword God he had been, not the outer disciple he pretended to be.

"Come then," Jin Tian said, his voice carrying across the arena. "Show me what the second-ranked inner disciple can do."

Xiao Yuan's smile faded, replaced by something cold and calculating.

"With pleasure."

He vanished.

Jin Tian barely had time to raise his sword before Xiao Yuan appeared at his side, wind blade already descending. The impact sent shockwaves through Jin Tian's arms, forcing him to his knees.

*He's fast. Faster than I remember.*

But Jin Tian had been fighting for centuries, even if only in his memories. He rolled with the blow, coming up in a low stance, sweeping his blade toward Xiao Yuan's legs.

The inner disciple leaped over the strike, twisting in midair to launch a counterattack. Jin Tian parried, but the force of the blow sent him sliding backward, his feet carving grooves in the stone floor.

*He's not just fast. He's strong. Stronger than Liu Feng by a wide margin.*

The crowd roared as they exchanged blows, steel ringing against steel, qi exploding around them in bursts of light and sound. Jin Tian held his own, but he could feel the gap in their cultivation levels. Xiao Yuan was at the ninth level of Qi Condensation, while Jin Tian was barely at the fifth. The difference in power was immense.

But power wasn't everything. Technique mattered. Experience mattered. And in those areas, Jin Tian had the advantage.

He changed his strategy, abandoning direct confrontation for evasion and counter-attacks. He let Xiao Yuan press the attack, let him overextend, let him grow confident.

And then, when Xiao Yuan committed to a killing strike, Jin Tian was ready.

He sidestepped the wind blade, letting it pass a hair's breadth from his face. At the same time, he drove his sword upward, aiming for the gap in Xiao Yuan's defense—the space between his ribs where the qi flow was weakest.

The blade struck true.

Xiao Yuan gasped, stumbling backward, blood staining his robes. The crowd fell silent.

For a moment, Jin Tian thought he had won.

Then Xiao Yuan laughed.

"You're good," he said, pressing a hand to his wound. "Better than I expected. But you've made a mistake."

"What mistake?"

"You've shown me your hand." Xiao Yuan's eyes gleamed with triumph. "And now I know exactly who you are."

He raised his hand, and the dark-robed figures in the crowd rose as one.

"Sect rules," Xiao Yuan announced, his voice carrying across the arena, "dictate that any disciple found to be possessed by a demon spirit must be executed immediately. And I have proof that Outer Disciple Jin Tian is exactly that

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Snake Beneath the Lotus

Jin Tian stood alone in the preparation chamber beneath the main arena, his fingers tracing the edge of his practice sword. The steel was cheap, the balance mediocre—a far cry from the heavenly blades he had once wielded. But it would serve.

Three hours until Liu Feng.

Three hours until he faced the man who had ended his first life.

The chamber walls were lined with formation arrays that pulsed with weak qi, designed to help disciples recover stamina between matches. Jin Tian ignored them. He had no intention of wasting energy on recovery when there was knowledge to be gathered.

He closed his eyes and reached out with his spiritual sense, a technique that should have been impossible for a mere qi-condensation cultivator. But Jin Tian was no ordinary cultivator. The memories of three hundred years of sword cultivation had reshaped his soul, granting him abilities that transcended his current cultivation level.

The world expanded around him.

He could feel the crowd above, their qi signatures flickering like candle flames. The elders sat in their elevated positions, their flames brighter, steadier. And there, in the eastern corridor leading to the arena, was a flame that burned with unusual intensity.

Liu Feng.

Jin Tian focused his spiritual sense, pushing deeper than he ever had before. The technique was dangerous—if detected, the elders would assume he was spying. But he needed information. Needed to understand what made Liu Feng tick, what secrets he carried.

The flame resolved into a silhouette. Liu Feng was preparing for battle, his movements precise and efficient. He wore the standard inner disciple robes, but his body was wrapped in bands of formation-inscribed cloth that channeled fire qi through his meridians.

And he was not alone.

A shadow stood beside him, too indistinct for Jin Tian's spiritual sense to identify clearly. But he caught fragments of their conversation, carried on threads of qi that leaked through the formation arrays.

"...sure he's the one?" The voice was female, cold, familiar in a way that made Jin Tian's blood run cold.

"I'm certain." Liu Feng's voice was low, controlled. "The boy moves like a veteran. His forms are too perfect, his reactions too fast. No twelve-year-old outer disciple fights like that without extensive training. Someone's been teaching him."

"Or he's not what he appears."

The words hung in the air like poison.

Jin Tian pulled back his spiritual sense, his heart pounding. Someone was watching him. Someone had noticed the discrepancies in his behavior, the way he moved and fought like someone with decades of experience.

And they were discussing it with Liu Feng.

Before he could process the implications, a bell rang through the chamber. The match was starting.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and climbed the steps to the arena.

---

The main arena was a massive stone platform suspended above a pool of molten fire, the heat rising in waves that distorted the air. Thousands of disciples packed the surrounding stands, their voices a constant roar of excitement and anticipation.

At the center of the platform stood Liu Feng.

He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, his features sharp and angular, his black hair pulled back in a warrior's topknot. His eyes were the color of dying embers, flickering with barely suppressed power. In his hand, he held a blade of crimson crystal that seemed to drink the light from the arena.

"The match between Inner Disciple Liu Feng and Outer Disciple Jin Tian will now commence," Elder Zhou announced, his voice amplified by formation arrays. "Standard rules apply. No lethal techniques. Surrender or ring-out determines victory."

Jin Tian stepped onto the platform, and the temperature immediately rose. The molten fire below sent tendrils of heat crawling up his legs, testing his qi defense. Liu Feng was already radiating killing intent, pressing against Jin Tian's spirit like a physical weight.

"You're bold, boy," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I watched your match against Chen Wei. You have talent. But talent without cultivation is just potential wasted."

"Then I suppose I'll have to prove you wrong," Jin Tian replied.

Liu Feng's lips curved into a smile that didn't reach his eyes. "Indeed you will."

The gong sounded.

Liu Feng moved first, launching forward with explosive speed. His crimson blade traced an arc of fire through the air, the heat so intense that Jin Tian's robes began to smolder at the edges.

Jin Tian didn't block.

He sidestepped, his movement minimal but perfectly timed. The blade passed within inches of his chest, the heat leaving a red mark on his skin. He countered with a thrust to Liu Feng's exposed ribs, but the inner disciple twisted aside, his free hand releasing a palm strike of condensed fire.

The attack was meant to force Jin Tian back, to create distance. Instead, Jin Tian stepped into it, letting the fire wash over his qi barrier as he closed the gap. His sword came up in a diagonal slash that forced Liu Feng to parry.

Their blades met, and the impact sent shockwaves across the arena.

Liu Feng's eyes widened. "You're stronger than you look."

"I've been told," Jin Tian said, pressing forward.

For the next several exchanges, they danced across the platform. Liu Feng's style was aggressive, overwhelming, designed to crush opponents with raw power. He summoned pillars of flame, sent waves of heat screaming across the arena, and struck with the force of a falling star.

But Jin Tian had faced fire-aspected cultivators for three hundred years. He knew their weaknesses. He knew that fire was unstable, that it demanded constant fuel, that it burned brightest when it was about to die.

He let Liu Feng exhaust himself.

Every attack was deflected, dodged, or absorbed. Jin Tian never committed to a full counter, always retreating, always giving ground. The crowd began to murmur, confused by the outer disciple's passive strategy.

Liu Feng noticed too. "Fight me properly, coward!" he roared, summoning a massive fireball that he hurled at Jin Tian's position.

Jin Tian didn't dodge.

He raised his sword and cut through the fireball, splitting it in two. The flames parted around him, harmless, and he emerged from the inferno completely untouched.

"I've been watching you, Liu Feng," Jin Tian said quietly, his voice carrying through the sudden silence. "Your techniques are powerful, but they rely on momentum. Once you commit to an attack, you cannot change course. It's a flaw you've never bothered to correct because you've never faced an opponent who could exploit it."

Liu Feng's face went pale. "Impossible. You've never seen me fight before today."

"I've seen enough."

Jin Tian moved.

This time, he didn't retreat. He advanced with the speed of a striking viper, his sword becoming a blur of steel. Liu Feng barely managed to parry the first strike, the second, the third. But the fourth found its mark, slicing across his forearm and drawing blood.

Liu Feng stumbled back, clutching his wound. "Who are you?" he hissed. "No outer disciple fights like this. No twelve-year-old knows my techniques well enough to counter them."

Jin Tian didn't answer. He pressed his advantage, driving Liu Feng toward the edge of the platform. The inner disciple was panicking now, his attacks becoming wild and unfocused. He was throwing everything he had at Jin Tian, desperate to regain control.

But control was already lost.

In Jin Tian's mind, the battle was already decided. He had seen Liu Feng's patterns, understood his tells, memorized his rhythms. The man who had killed him in his first life was nothing more than a talented brute, relying on power to overcome skill.

And Jin Tian had more skill than anyone in this arena.

He disarmed Liu Feng with a twisting parry, sending the crimson blade spinning across the platform. A final sweep of his leg sent the inner disciple crashing to the ground, his back pressed against the edge of the arena.

The crowd fell silent.

"Yield," Jin Tian said.

Liu Feng stared up at him, his eyes burning with hatred. "You think this is over? You think you've won?"

"I've won this match," Jin Tian said. "That's all that matters."

"No." Liu Feng's voice dropped to a whisper, barely audible over the roaring flames below. "You've just signed your death warrant. They know about you now. The ones who sent me. They've been watching since the tournament began, waiting to see if you were the one."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "The ones who sent you? Who?"

Liu Feng laughed, a bitter, broken sound. "You'll find out soon enough. They're already here, in the crowd, watching. And now that you've beaten me, they'll come for you themselves."

Before Jin Tian could respond, Liu Feng rolled backward off the platform, falling toward the molten fire below. Jin Tian lunged forward, grabbing his wrist, but Liu Feng's weight pulled them both toward the edge.

"Tell me who sent you!" Jin Tian demanded.

Liu Feng's eyes met his, and for a moment, the hatred faded, replaced by something that looked almost like pity. "The Lotus," he said. "The Lotus sent me. And they're going to send someone much worse next time."

He wrenched his hand free and fell.

Jin Tian watched him disappear into the flames, his body consumed in an instant. The crowd erupted into chaos, disciples screaming, elders shouting orders. But Jin Tian heard none of it.

The Lotus.

He knew that name.

In his first life, the Lotus had been a shadow organization that operated in the underworld of the cultivation world. They traded in secrets, assassinations, and forbidden techniques. They had no allegiance to any sect, no loyalty to any master.

And they had been the ones who orchestrated his betrayal.

Jin Tian had always assumed his seven disciples had acted alone, driven by greed and ambition. But if the Lotus was involved, then everything he thought he knew was wrong.

He looked up at the crowd, searching for familiar faces. There, in the shadows of the eastern stands, he saw a figure watching him. Female. Cold. The same silhouette he had sensed in the preparation chamber.

She smiled at him, and the smile was sharp as a blade.

Then she turned and vanished into the crowd.

Jin Tian stood alone on the platform, surrounded by chaos, holding the sword that had just killed a man who couldn't die.

And for the first time since his rebirth, he felt genuine fear.

The Lotus was here.

And they knew who he was.

title: Chapter 13
content:
# Chapter 13: The Reaper's True Face

The tournament grounds had transformed.

What had been orderly rows of fighting platforms now blazed with torchlight, the flickering flames casting dancing shadows across thousands of faces. The inner division tournament was a different beast entirely from the outer disciple matches—grander, more dangerous, and far more deadly.

Jin Tian stood in the preparation chamber beneath the main arena, running his finger along the edge of his practice sword. The weapon was inadequate—standard iron, unaspected, the kind given to outer disciples who hadn't yet earned the right to carry spiritual weapons. Against Liu Feng's fire-aspected blade, it would shatter in three exchanges.

*Unless I don't let it touch.*

He closed his eyes, reaching into the depths of his spiritual sea. The qi within him had grown denser over the past week, compressing and refining with each meditation session. In his first life, he had reached the eighth level of Qi Condensation at this age. Now, at the fifth level, his foundation was stronger than it had ever been.

But foundation meant nothing against a seventh-level inner disciple with decades of combat experience.

A door creaked open behind him.

"You're the one who stole Chen Wei's spot."

Jin Tian didn't turn. He had sensed the newcomer's approach—heavy footsteps, deliberate breathing, the faint scent of burnt herbs clinging to robes. "I didn't steal anything. I earned it."

"Earned it." The voice chuckled, low and amused. "You have no idea what you've walked into, boy."

Now Jin Tian turned.

The man standing in the doorway was older than he'd expected—mid-twenties, with a scarred face and empty eyes that spoke of too many battles. He wore the crimson robes of an inner disciple, but there was nothing ceremonial about his bearing. This was a killer, through and through.

"And you are?"

"Your first opponent's older brother." The man stepped into the chamber, letting the door swing shut behind him. "Liu Feng sends his regards."

Jin Tian's hand moved to his sword. "The match isn't for another two hours. This is against the rules."

"The rules." The man laughed, a hollow sound. "The rules are for disciples who matter. You're a corpse walking, boy. I'm just here to make sure you arrive at your execution with the proper... humility."

He moved.

Jin Tian's blade came up—not to block, but to redirect. The man's palm strike slid past his guard, wind-aspected qi screaming past his ear. Jin Tian twisted, bringing his sword around in a horizontal arc that should have caught the attacker's exposed ribs.

The man's other hand caught the blade mid-swing.

"You're fast," he admitted, studying the iron edge pressed against his palm. A thin line of blood welled up, then vanished as his qi sealed the wound. "Fast enough to maybe land a hit on Liu Feng. Maybe."

He released the blade and stepped back.

"But he won't give you the chance." The man turned toward the door, pausing with his hand on the handle. "Consider this a warning. Withdraw from the tournament. Claim illness, injury, cowardice—I don't care. Live out your pathetic life as an outer disciple. It's more than most who cross Liu Feng get."

"Why?"

The man glanced back, something flickering in those dead eyes. "Why what?"

"Why does Liu Feng want me dead? We've never met. I'm an outer disciple. I shouldn't matter to someone of his rank."

For a long moment, the man said nothing. Then his lips curled into something that wasn't quite a smile.

"You're not the first. You won't be the last. Liu Feng has a... hobby. He likes collecting talented disciples. Breaking them. Finding out what makes them special before he snuffs them out." He tilted his head. "Word travels fast in the inner sect. They say you read the Sword Intent Inscription perfectly. They say your technique is flawless. They say you've been hiding your true strength."

He opened the door.

"Liu Feng doesn't like secrets he didn't uncover first."

The door slammed shut, leaving Jin Tian alone with his thoughts.

---

*Liu Feng doesn't like secrets.*

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the main arena, the roar of the crowd washing over him like a physical force. Thousands of disciples packed the viewing stands—outer disciples relegated to the highest tiers, inner disciples in the middle sections, and at the lowest level, closest to the action, the elders and sect masters in their raised pavilion.

And in the center of it all, waiting for him, stood Liu Feng.

He was younger than Jin Tian remembered. In his first life, Liu Feng had been a man in his prime—thirty-two years old, at the peak of the Nascent Soul realm, his fire cultivation blazing like a second sun. Now he was barely twenty, handsome and composed, his crimson robes immaculate, his posture radiating the confidence of someone who had never truly been challenged.

Their eyes met across the arena floor.

Liu Feng smiled.

It was a beautiful smile, warm and welcoming, the kind of smile that made people trust him instantly. Jin Tian had seen that smile a hundred times in his first life, had shared drinks and battle stories with the man wearing it. He had never once suspected that the same man would one day drive a blade through his heart.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," the announcer's voice boomed across the arena, "versus Inner Disciple Liu Feng. The match will continue until one combatant yields, is incapacitated, or—" the announcer paused, "—perishes."

A murmur rippled through the crowd. Death matches were rare in tournaments, reserved for grudges or disciplinary actions. For an outer disciple's first inner division match to carry such stakes...

Jin Tian's jaw tightened. Chen Wei's father was an inner elder. This was his doing.

"Begin!"

Liu Feng didn't move.

He stood in the center of the arena, arms loosely at his sides, his sword still sheathed. The posture was casual, almost bored, as if facing an outer disciple wasn't worth the effort of drawing steel.

"Come, little brother," he said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "Show me what made the elders choose you over Chen Wei."

Jin Tian's grip tightened on his sword. Every instinct screamed at him to attack—to end this quickly, to prove his worth, to silence the mocking smile on Liu Feng's face.

He ignored the instincts.

In his first life, he had been a Sword God. He had faced cultivators whose power dwarfed Liu Feng's a thousandfold. He had learned that the greatest weakness of the arrogant was their certainty that they could not lose.

He began to walk forward.

Not charging. Not sprinting. Walking, one slow step at a time, his sword held low and loose.

Liu Feng's smile flickered.

"What's this? Have you already given up?"

Jin Tian didn't answer. He kept walking, his footsteps measured, his breathing steady. The crowd's roar faded into background noise. The world narrowed to a single point—the space between himself and his opponent.

He was ten steps away.

Five.

Liu Feng's hand moved to his sword. The casual demeanor was gone, replaced by something sharper. "You're either very brave or very stupid."

"Neither," Jin Tian said, and struck.

The first blow was simple—a straight thrust, aimed at Liu Feng's throat. Liu Feng's sword came up to deflect, fire qi flaring along the blade.

Their weapons met.

And Jin Tian's sword shattered.

The crowd gasped. Liu Feng's smile returned, wider than before. "Is that all—"

Jin Tian's broken blade twisted, the jagged edge catching Liu Feng's wrist instead of his sword. Blood sprayed. Liu Feng's retaliation went wide, the fire-aspected strike carving a trench in the arena floor.

Before Liu Feng could recover, Jin Tian was inside his guard.

He had known his sword would break. He had counted on it. Against a properly aspected spiritual weapon, standard iron would last exactly one exchange. So he had planned for one exchange—and then a second that Liu Feng wouldn't expect.

His palm struck Liu Feng's chest, not with force, but with precision. A single thread of qi, needle-thin, pierced into the older disciple's dantian.

Liu Feng's eyes went wide.

He stumbled backward, one hand pressed to his chest, the other still holding his bloodied sword. "You... what did you..."

"Blocked your fire meridian," Jin Tian said calmly, letting the remnants of his shattered sword fall to the ground. "Just a temporary seal. It'll wear off in an hour."

The arena was silent. Thousands of disciples stared in disbelief. An outer disciple, weaponless, had just landed a crippling blow on a ranked inner disciple in the opening seconds of a death match.

Liu Feng's face cycled through shock, rage, and then—horrifyingly—delight.

"Interesting," he breathed. "You're not just talented. You're *special*."

He straightened, ignoring the blood still dripping from his wrist. His smile had changed—no longer warm, no longer mocking. It was hungry.

"I was going to kill you quickly," Liu Feng said, drawing his sword fully. The blade burst into flames, crimson qi writhing along its edge like living fire. "But I think I'll take my time instead. I want to see what else you're hiding."

He moved.

Jin Tian's instincts screamed. He threw himself sideways, a hair's breadth ahead of Liu Feng's blade. The fire-aspected sword passed so close he felt his robes begin to char.

*He's faster than I calculated.*

The seal on Liu Feng's fire meridian had slowed his qi circulation, but it hadn't stopped it. The older disciple was still a seventh-level cultivator, his body reinforced by years of spiritual refinement. Even slowed, even hampered, he was beyond what a fifth-level should be able to face.

But Jin Tian wasn't a typical fifth-level.

He ducked under a horizontal slash, feeling the heat sear his scalp. His hand shot out, fingers closing around Liu Feng's wrist. A sharp twist, a burst of qi, and—

Liu Feng's elbow caught him in the ribs.

The impact lifted Jin Tian off his feet, sending him crashing across the arena floor. He tasted blood, felt something crack inside his chest.

"You fight like a demon," Liu Feng said, advancing slowly. "Your technique is flawless. Your timing is perfect. You fight like someone who's been doing this for a hundred years."

He stopped, looking down at Jin Tian with those hungry eyes.

"You're not a prodigy, Jin Tian. Prodigies make mistakes. They learn. They grow." He tilted his head. "You already know everything. You fight like a master. Like someone who's already reached the peak and fallen."

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow.

*He knows.*

"You're wondering how I know," Liu Feng continued, his voice dropping to a whisper that somehow still carried across the arena. "I'll tell you a secret, little brother. I've been watching you since the day you entered the sect. I saw the way you moved during training. The way you read techniques. The way you looked at the elders like you were judging them, not the other way around."

He crouched down, bringing his face level with Jin Tian's.

"And then I heard about the Sword Intent Inscription. How you read it perfectly. How you didn't even seem surprised." His smile widened. "That's when I knew. You're like me."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

"You've lived before," Liu Feng said. "You've died before. And somehow, impossibly, you've come back."

He stood,

title: The Serpent's Hidden Fang
content:
The training grounds of the inner division were a world apart from the dusty courtyard where Jin Tian had spent years pretending to be ordinary. Polished obsidian tiles formed a perfect circle fifty meters across, etched with formation arrays that hummed with dormant power. Spectators lined the raised platforms—inner disciples, elders, even a few core members who had deigned to watch the first-round matches.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the arena, his outer disciple robes a stark contrast to the azure and gold silks around him. He could feel their eyes on him, measuring, dismissing, curious. A twelve-year-old boy who had somehow talked his way into a tournament meant for cultivators twice his age and ten times his cultivation base.

Let them underestimate him. He had spent three centuries learning the value of being overlooked.

Across the arena, Liu Feng stretched lazily, his fire-aspected qi causing the air around him to shimmer. He was tall for an inner disciple, with sharp features and hair the color of copper. His robes were open at the chest, revealing a network of scars that spoke of battles hard-won.

"You're the one who caused all that commotion," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I heard you humiliated Chen Wei in front of Elder Zhou. Bold move for someone who hasn't broken through to the second stage."

Jin Tian said nothing. He was reading Liu Feng's stance, the way he favored his right foot, the slight tension in his left shoulder. Old habits. Injuries that had never fully healed. In his first life, Jin Tian had never noticed these tells—he had been too focused on Liu Feng's reputation, his rank, his fire techniques.

Now he saw the man beneath the legend.

"Not much of a talker, are you?" Liu Feng grinned, but there was no warmth in it. "Good. I prefer my opponents quiet. Makes the screaming more satisfying."

The bell chimed. The formation arrays flared to life, creating a barrier around the arena that would contain stray attacks. The match had begun.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian knew he would. A spear of condensed flame materialized in his hand, and he hurled it with the speed of a striking viper. In his first life, this attack had caught Jin Tian off guard, forcing him onto the defensive. A mistake that had cost him the initiative.

This time, Jin Tian sidestepped before the spear was even fully formed. He read the intent in Liu Feng's qi flow, the way fire aspected techniques always required a moment of gathering before release. The spear passed harmlessly where he had been standing, exploding against the barrier in a shower of golden sparks.

Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly. "Lucky dodge."

Jin Tian drew his sword. It was a standard issue outer disciple blade, forged from common iron, unremarkable in every way. But in his hands, it became something else. He held it at an angle that suggested inexperience, his stance deliberately loose.

Another trick he had learned across centuries. Make them think you're weak. Make them overcommit.

Liu Feng obliged. He launched forward, both hands wreathed in flame, intending to overwhelm Jin Tian with raw power. It was a technique designed to break through defenses through sheer force—effective against opponents who tried to block or parry.

Jin Tian didn't block. He flowed.

His sword traced a path that seemed almost accidental, yet it intersected with Liu Feng's charge at the precise moment when the flame technique was between breaths. Not disrupting the attack—that would require more power than he had—but redirecting it. Guiding the flames past his body like water around a stone.

Liu Feng stumbled, his momentum carrying him past Jin Tian's position. Before he could recover, Jin Tian's sword flicked out, the flat of the blade slapping against the back of Liu Feng's knee.

The inner disciple went down hard.

Silence fell over the arena. Then whispers, growing into a murmur of disbelief. Liu Feng had been taken down in the first exchange. By an outer disciple. By a child.

"Stand up," Jin Tian said quietly.

Liu Feng's head snapped up, his eyes blazing with fury. "You think you've won, boy? That was a lucky—"

"You're favoring your right leg because of an old injury to your left hip," Jin Tian interrupted. "Your fire techniques are powerful, but you gather qi for a full second before releasing them—a tell that any competent fighter can read. Your shoulder tenses when you're about to throw a projectile attack, and you always, always lead with the same combination because you've never faced someone who survived the first exchange."

He paused, letting the words sink in.

"In your entire career as a cultivator, you've never lost a match that lasted more than thirty seconds. Do you know why?"

Liu Feng's face had gone pale. "Shut up."

"Because your opening attacks are designed to overwhelm. If they fail, you have no backup plan. No creativity. No ability to adapt." Jin Tian's voice was soft, almost gentle. "You're not a warrior, Liu Feng. You're a bully who learned one trick and never bothered to learn another."

The flames around Liu Feng's hands died. In their place, something cold flickered in his eyes. Something that made Jin Tian's instincts scream warning.

"You know a lot about me," Liu Feng said, rising slowly. His voice had changed—less bluster, more edge. "Things you shouldn't know. Things only someone who's studied me for years would know."

Jin Tian's grip tightened on his sword.

"You're not just some talented outer disciple, are you?" Liu Feng's smile returned, but it was different now. Knowing. "Who sent you? The Celestial Flame Sect? The Shadowed Blades?"

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Liar." Liu Feng laughed, and the sound was bitter. "You fight like someone who's been doing this for decades. You read my tells like a book. You know about my hip—an injury I received during a secret mission that only the sect elders and my fellow disciples should know about."

He took a step forward, and Jin Tian felt the temperature drop. The fire-aspected qi around Liu Feng had vanished completely, replaced by something else. Something dark.

"But here's the thing I can't figure out," Liu Feng continued. "If you're an assassin sent by another sect, why are you here? Why fight in a tournament that means nothing? Why expose yourself before you've completed your mission?"

"I'm not an assassin."

"Then explain it." Liu Feng's voice dropped to a whisper that somehow carried across the entire arena. "Explain how an outer disciple who's been average for years suddenly displays combat experience that rivals our best inner disciples. Explain how you know secrets about me that I've never spoken aloud."

The spectators had gone silent, sensing the shift in atmosphere. Elders were leaning forward, their eyes sharp with suspicion.

Jin Tian's mind raced. He had been careless. Too focused on winning, too confident in his reincarnated knowledge. He had revealed too much, and now Liu Feng was asking questions that had no good answers.

"I'm observant," he said finally. "I watch. I learn."

"Bullshit." Liu Feng spat the word. "You want to know the real reason I know you're lying? Because you didn't just know about my hip. You knew about my shoulder. You knew about my qi-gathering tell. You knew about my combination patterns."

He was close now, close enough that Jin Tian could see the veins standing out on his neck.

"Those are things I developed *after* my hip injury. They're adaptations. Changes I made to compensate." Liu Feng's eyes bored into Jin Tian's. "If you fought me before the injury, you wouldn't know about them. But you do. Which means you've studied me recently. Within the last year."

Jin Tian felt the ground shifting beneath his feet.

"So I'll ask you one more time, boy." Liu Feng's hand shot out, grabbing Jin Tian by the collar. "Who. Sent. You?"

The answer came not from Jin Tian, but from the stands.

"I did."

Every head turned. The crowd parted like water before a ship's prow, revealing a figure in pristine white robes. He was young—no older than sixteen—with features so perfect they seemed carved from jade. His eyes were pale blue, almost colorless, and his smile held the warmth of a winter sunrise.

He descended the stairs slowly, deliberately, his gaze fixed on Jin Tian.

"I sent him," the boy repeated. "Jin Tian is my agent. My spy. My weapon."

Liu Feng's grip slackened. "Senior Brother... I don't understand."

The boy laughed, and the sound was like breaking glass. "Of course you don't. None of you understand. That's the point."

He stopped at the edge of the arena, studying Jin Tian with an expression that made the Sword God's blood run cold.

Because he knew that face. He knew those eyes. He knew that smile.

In his first life, this boy had been Jin Tian's favorite disciple. The one he had trusted most. The one he had loved like a son.

The one who had driven the first blade into his back.

"Wei Chen," Jin Tian breathed.

The boy's smile widened. "Hello, Master. It's been a long time."

The world stopped.

Liu Feng looked between them, confusion warring with fear. "Senior Brother Wei Chen? You know this outer disciple?"

"Oh, I know him better than anyone." Wei Chen stepped into the arena, his white robes billowing despite the still air. "I know his techniques, his habits, his weaknesses. I know the way he moves when he thinks no one is watching, the way he holds his sword when he's about to kill."

He stopped before Jin Tian, close enough to touch.

"And I know that the boy standing before us is not a boy at all. He's a ghost. A remnant. A man who has already died once and somehow crawled back."

The whispers exploded into chaos. Elders rose from their seats, formation arrays flickering as they prepared to intervene. Liu Feng stumbled backward, his face pale.

But Jin Tian saw only Wei Chen. His most trusted disciple. His greatest betrayal.

"How?" he forced out.

Wei Chen tilted his head, still smiling. "You're not the only one who remembers, Master. You're not the only one who was given a second chance."

He leaned in, his lips brushing Jin Tian's ear.

"Welcome back to the game. This time, I win."

The formation arrays flared, and the world dissolved into white light.

title: The Eighth Disciple's Secret
content:
The tournament grounds had transformed since morning. What had been a simple sparring ring now blazed with formation arrays, their golden light pulsing in rhythm with the gathered crowd's anticipation. Three hundred inner disciples ringed the platform, their murmured conversations creating a wall of sound that pressed against Jin Tian's consciousness.

He stood in the waiting chamber beneath the main stage, alone except for the flickering lanterns that cast dancing shadows across the stone walls. Three hours. He had used them well, circulating his qi through meridians that most cultivators wouldn't discover for another fifty years of dedicated practice. His body hummed with borrowed power—not enough to match his former peak, but sufficient to surprise an opponent who underestimated him.

Liu Feng would underestimate him. The inner disciple had no reason to do otherwise. A twelve-year-old outer disciple who had never faced real combat? Against a ranked inner disciple with three years of formal training? The odds were laughable.

That was precisely what Jin Tian was counting on.

The chamber door slid open, and a servant bowed low. "Disciple Jin Tian. The match will begin in ten minutes. Elder Zhou requests your presence at the staging area."

Jin Tian nodded, rising with the fluid grace of a man who had spent centuries perfecting his body's relationship with gravity. The servant's eyes widened slightly—a movement so small that anyone else would have missed it. But Jin Tian saw everything now. He had trained himself to see everything.

As he followed the servant through the winding corridors, he began extending his spiritual sense outward. The skill was crude at his current cultivation level, barely more than a whisper against the world, but it was enough to taste the qi signatures around him. Most were familiar—the steady flame of fire-aspected cultivators, the flowing river of water artists, the immovable mountain of earth practitioners.

Then he felt something that made him stop mid-stride.

Cold. Absolute, absolute cold. Not the cold of ice cultivation, which was sharp and focused. This was the cold of absence. The cold of something that had been removed from the world and replaced with a void that ached to be filled.

Demonic qi.

Jin Tian's hand moved to his sword before his conscious mind caught up. He forced himself to relax, to breathe, to maintain the appearance of a confused child who had simply stumbled over nothing.

The servant turned back. "Is something wrong, Disciple?"

"No," Jin Tian said, forcing a smile. "I was just thinking about the match. Nerves, I suppose."

The servant nodded sympathetically and continued walking. But Jin Tian's mind was racing. Demonic qi at the Heavenly Sword Sect. In his first life, the corruption hadn't revealed itself until the war with the Demon Alliance, three years from now. Had it been here all along? Had he simply been blind to it?

Or had something changed?

He followed the source of the cold sensation with his spiritual sense, tracking it like a hound on a scent. It led him past the staging area, past the main corridor, to a small meditation chamber rarely used by anyone. The door was closed, but the qi seeped through the cracks like smoke through a failing dam.

Inside, someone was breathing heavily. Fighting something. Losing.

Jin Tian made a decision that would have horrified his younger self. He reached out and pushed the door open.

The scene inside was chaos. A young woman in inner disciple robes was pressed against the far wall, her hands clawing at her own throat as if trying to tear something out. Her eyes were solid black—no whites, no pupils, just pools of absolute darkness that seemed to drink the light around them.

And standing over her, a hand pressed to her forehead, was Elder Zhou.

The old man's face was serene, almost bored, as he channeled something into the struggling disciple. Gold light mixed with black tendrils, spiraling together in patterns that hurt to look at. When he sensed Jin Tian's presence, he didn't startle. He didn't even look up.

"Ah, young Jin Tian," Elder Zhou said, his voice carrying the same gentle warmth it had during the tournament. "You're early. How fortunate."

"What are you doing to her?" Jin Tian's voice came out flat. Controlled. The voice of a man who had learned that panic was a luxury he could no longer afford.

"Purifying a corruption," Elder Zhou replied. "The demonic taint has been spreading among the disciples for months now. I've been quietly removing it, one by one." He paused, finally meeting Jin Tian's gaze. "You understand why this must remain secret, don't you? If the sect learns that demonic cultivators have infiltrated our ranks, there would be panic. The tournament would be cancelled. The alliance negotiations would collapse."

"You're not purifying her." Jin Tian stepped into the room, his hand resting on his sword's hilt. "You're feeding it."

Elder Zhou's eyes widened—a genuine reaction, not the calculated performance he had shown earlier. "What did you say?"

"I've seen demonic corruption before." Jin Tian's mind flashed back to the war, to the battlefields littered with fallen cultivators whose bodies had been twisted into vessels for demonic will. "The purification technique requires the cultivator's qi to be stronger than the corruption. It burns the taint out through conflict. What you're doing is the opposite. You're forcing more in, binding it to her soul so deeply that it can never be removed."

Elder Zhou's serene expression cracked. For just a moment, Jin Tian saw something ancient and hungry looking out through the old man's eyes.

"You know far too much for an outer disciple," Elder Zhou said, his voice dropping to a register that made the air vibrate. "Who are you really?"

"I'm the one who's going to stop you."

Jin Tian drew his sword. The blade sang as it left the sheath, a sound that had been forged through a thousand battles in his previous life. The qi in the room responded to it, swirling toward the weapon like iron filings to a magnet.

Elder Zhou laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. "You think you can challenge me? I am an Elder of the Heavenly Sword Sect. I have been cultivating for two hundred years. You are a child who barely knows which end of the sword to hold."

"I know exactly which end to hold." Jin Tian settled into a stance that was ancient before Elder Zhou was born. "And I know that you're not an Elder of the Heavenly Sword Sect. You're something that replaced him."

The demonic qi in the room surged. The possessed disciple collapsed, unconscious, as Elder Zhou's form began to shift. His features blurred, running like wax, before settling into something else entirely. The face that emerged was young, beautiful, and utterly wrong. It was a face that had been carved by a sculptor who understood anatomy but had never seen a living person.

"You're sharper than I anticipated," the creature said, its voice now layered with harmonics that scraped against Jin Tian's soul. "I've been watching you since the tournament. The way you move, the way you fight. You're not a child. You're something else entirely."

"The feeling is mutual."

The creature tilted its head, studying him with predatory curiosity. "Tell me your secret, and I'll make your death painless. I'll even let you keep your soul intact. Most of it, anyway."

Jin Tian smiled. It was not a kind expression. "I've died before. It doesn't scare me anymore."
