He attacked.

The sword moved faster than any outer disciple should have been capable of, tracing a path through the air that ignored the distance between them entirely. The creature's eyes widened as it barely twisted aside, the blade grazing its cheek and drawing a line of black ichor.

"Impossible!" The creature leaped backward, landing in a crouch on the ceiling like a spider. "That speed, that accuracy—you've been hiding your cultivation level!"

"I've been hiding a lot of things."

Jin Tian pressed his advantage, his sword becoming a blur of silver light. Each strike was precise, economical, aimed at the pressure points that would disrupt a demonic cultivator's qi flow. The creature dodged and weaved, but Jin Tian had spent three centuries fighting demons. He knew their movements, their tells, the little twitches that preceded their attacks.

The creature realized it was losing. Its face contorted with rage, and it opened its mouth to—

The chamber door slammed open.

Liu Feng stood in the doorway, his fire-aspected blade already drawn. His eyes took in the scene—the unconscious disciple, the creature on the ceiling, Jin Tian with his sword raised—in less than a heartbeat.

"Demon," Liu Feng said, his voice flat. "I should have known."

The creature laughed. "Ah, the prodigal disciple arrives. Tell me, Liu Feng, does your master know what you've been doing in the forbidden archives? Does he know about the pact you made?"

Liu Feng's face went pale. His sword wavered.

"I don't know what you're talking about," he said, but his voice lacked conviction.

"Don't you?" The creature's smile widened. "Shall I tell everyone about the night you sold your allegiance for power? About the oath you swore to the one who will devour this world?"

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. He turned to look at Liu Feng, searching for the truth in the disciple's eyes.

What he found was guilt.

"Liu Feng," Jin Tian said slowly, "what is it talking about?"

Liu Feng's jaw tightened. His hand shook on his sword's hilt. And for a long, terrible moment, Jin Tian saw the disciple weighing his options, calculating his next move with the precision of a man who had something to hide.

"I can explain," Liu Feng said finally. "But not here. Not in front of that thing."

The creature howled with laughter. "Oh, this is beautiful! The betrayed becomes the betrayer! The victim becomes the villain! I couldn't have written it better myself."

Jin Tian's mind raced. Liu Feng had killed him in his first life. He had come here expecting answers, expecting to confront the disciple who had ended his existence. But this—this was something else entirely.

"Tell me now," Jin Tian said, his voice carrying the weight of a command. "What did you do?"

Liu Feng met his eyes. And in that gaze, Jin Tian saw a reflection of his own pain, his own desperation, his own willingness to do anything to survive.

"I made a deal," Liu Feng whispered. "To save someone I loved. And I've been paying for it ever since."

The creature's laughter cut off abruptly. Its head swiveled toward the main corridor, where alarm bells had begun to ring.

"Ah," it said, almost cheerfully. "It seems our time is up. The other Elders have noticed the disturbance." It fixed its gaze on Jin Tian, and its eyes burned with hunger. "We'll continue this conversation later, little sword god. I look forward to seeing what else you've been hiding."

The creature's form dissolved into shadow, slipping through the cracks in the stone floor and vanishing into the earth.

Jin Tian stood in the sudden silence, his sword still raised, his heart pounding. Liu Feng had collapsed against the wall, his face buried in his hands.

And in the distance, the alarm bells grew louder, summoning the sect to a threat they didn't understand.

The tournament was over.

The real war had just begun.

title: The Blade That Remembers
content:
Jin Tian stood alone in the preparation chamber beneath the tournament arena, his wooden practice sword resting across his knees. The stone walls hummed with the distant roar of the crowd above, thousands of disciples packed into the spectator galleries to witness the first round of the inner division tournament.

Three hours. He had used them well.

His breathing cycled through seven different meditation patterns, each one designed to awaken a different meridian pathway. In his first life, he had spent decades perfecting these techniques. Now, with a twelve-year-old body and a fraction of his former cultivation base, they felt like trying to fill an ocean with a cup.

But the ocean remembered what it was to be full.

A knock at the door. "Jin Tian. Your match begins in ten minutes."

He rose, rolling his shoulders, feeling the familiar weight of the wooden blade in his hand. The tournament rules forbade real weapons for the preliminary rounds—too dangerous for disciples who hadn't yet learned to control their killing intent. But Jin Tian knew what Liu Feng would be carrying. A mid-grade spirit sword called Emberfang, forged from volcanic iron and quenched in the blood of a fire salamander. It could cut through ordinary steel like paper and released waves of heat with every swing.

In his first life, that sword had pierced his chest. He remembered the sensation perfectly—the searing heat as it burned through his ribs, the shock of fire qi flooding his meridians, the strange clarity that came in the final moments before darkness.

He would not let it happen again.

The corridor leading to the arena was narrow and dark, designed to disorient combatants before they emerged into the blinding light of the tournament floor. Jin Tian walked slowly, counting his steps, memorizing the slight incline of the stone beneath his feet.

As he approached the entrance, a figure stepped out of the shadows.

Liu Feng.

The inner disciple was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader in the shoulders, his face carrying the confident arrogance of a prodigy who had never known defeat. His robes were deep crimson, the color of the Blazing Flame Sect, and his hair was bound in a warrior's topknot secured by a jade pin.

"Little outer disciple," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying a mocking warmth. "I heard you caused quite a stir at the selection. Cheated your way past Chen Wei, did you?"

Jin Tian said nothing. He simply watched, cataloging every detail. The way Liu Feng stood—weight slightly forward on his left foot, favoring his right hand. The slight twitch in his left shoulder when he spoke, suggesting a hidden blade strapped to his forearm. The way his eyes moved, never quite meeting Jin Tian's gaze directly, always scanning, always calculating.

"I asked you a question, boy."

"I heard you," Jin Tian replied. "I simply didn't find it worthy of a response."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. The mocking smile remained, but something colder flickered beneath it. "Bold words. Let's see if your sword can back them up."

He pushed past Jin Tian, deliberately shouldering him as he passed. The contact was brief, but Jin Tian felt it—a thread of killing intent, carefully controlled but unmistakably present.

Liu Feng wanted to hurt him. Maybe even kill him.

The question was why.

Ten years from now, in Jin Tian's first life, Liu Feng would be the one to strike the final blow. But that was supposed to be a betrayal born of opportunity, not premeditation. Liu Feng had been Chen Wei's ally, his blade, his executioner. He shouldn't have any reason to hate a twelve-year-old outer disciple.

Unless something had changed.

Unless someone had told him to.

The arena erupted in light as Jin Tian stepped through the entrance. Thousands of faces blurred together in the stands, a sea of white and blue robes punctuated by the occasional flash of elder's gold. The tournament floor was a perfect circle of white jade, thirty meters across, etched with formation arrays that would absorb stray qi and prevent collateral damage.

Liu Feng stood at the opposite end, already drawing his sword. Emberfang gleamed in the artificial light, its blade rippling with heat that made the air shimmer around it.

"Disciples," Elder Zhou's voice boomed from the elevated judge's platform, "you will fight until one of you yields, is unable to continue, or is thrown from the arena. Killing is forbidden. Maiming is discouraged but not prohibited. Begin."

The gong rang out.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. He came in fast, Emberfang trailing a crescent of fire, aiming for Jin Tian's throat with surgical precision. It was a killing stroke disguised as a tournament attack, fast enough that most opponents would have no time to react.

Jin Tian sidestepped.

Not a grand movement, not a flashy technique. Just a single step to the left, exactly ninety degrees from the line of Liu Feng's charge. His wooden sword came up, not to block, but to guide—tapping the flat of Emberfang and redirecting its momentum past his shoulder.

Liu Feng's eyes widened. He recovered instantly, spinning into a reverse grip, bringing the blade around in a horizontal arc that would have bisected Jin Tian at the waist.

Jin Tian dropped.

His knees bent, his body folded, and he passed beneath the blade so close that he felt the heat of it singe his outer robe. His wooden sword thrust upward, catching Liu Feng in the ribs with a crack that echoed through the arena.

The crowd gasped.

Liu Feng staggered back, one hand pressed to his side, his face a mask of disbelief. Blood seeped through his crimson robes, dark and thick. Jin Tian had broken at least two of his ribs.

"How?" Liu Feng hissed. "You're an outer disciple. You shouldn't be able to—"

"You're thinking too much," Jin Tian said quietly. "And swinging too wide. Your fire techniques are impressive, but you rely on them too heavily. When your opponent survives the first exchange, you don't have a backup plan."

Liu Feng's face twisted with rage. "You know nothing about my techniques!"

He attacked again, faster this time, Emberfang becoming a blur of fire and steel. The heat in the arena intensified until Jin Tian could feel it pressing against his skin, drying his eyes, making it hard to breathe.

This was the real Liu Feng. The one who had killed him in his first life. The one whose fire-aspected blade had burned through his defenses and pierced his heart.

But Jin Tian had spent three hundred years learning every aspect of sword combat. He had faced fire cultivators who could melt steel with a glance, who could turn the very air into an inferno. Compared to them, Liu Feng was a candle trying to compete with the sun.

Jin Tian moved.

He flowed around Liu Feng's attacks like water around stones, his wooden sword never meeting Emberfang directly but always deflecting, redirecting, creating openings where none should exist. Each exchange cost Liu Feng something—a cut on his arm, a bruise on his thigh, a shallow gash across his cheek.

The crowd had gone silent.

On the judge's platform, Elder Zhou leaned forward, his ancient eyes sharp with interest. Beside him, the other elders were whispering behind their sleeves, their faces unreadable.

After three minutes of continuous combat, Liu Feng was bleeding from a dozen wounds. His robes were torn, his breathing ragged, his perfect technique reduced to desperate flailing. Emberfang's flames had dimmed, the sword's qi depleted from his reckless expenditure.

"Yield," Jin Tian said. "You can't win."

Liu Feng laughed, a broken sound filled with bitter fury. "You think this is about winning? You think I care about the tournament?"

He dropped his guard. For a single, impossible moment, he stood completely open, Emberfang hanging loosely at his side.

Jin Tian didn't take the opening.

Something was wrong.

"I was told to test you," Liu Feng said, his voice dropping to a whisper that somehow carried across the arena. "To see if you were the one."

"The one what?"

"The one who would destroy everything." Liu Feng's eyes met Jin Tian's, and for the first time, Jin Tian saw something other than arrogance in them. He saw fear. "The one from the prophecy."

Before Jin Tian could respond, Liu Feng lunged.

But not at him.

Liu Feng lunged at the formation array at the edge of the arena, Emberfang raised high. The blade came down, not in an attack, but in a strike against the jade floor itself. The white stone shattered, and the formation array beneath it flared once, twice, then died.

The barriers around the arena flickered and failed.

The crowd screamed.

And in that moment of chaos, Liu Feng turned to Jin Tian with desperate eyes. "Run," he said. "They're coming for you. All of them. The elders, the sect masters, everyone who serves the—"

A blade erupted from Liu Feng's chest.

Black. Serrated. Dripping with a darkness that seemed to drink the light around it.

Liu Feng looked down at the steel protruding from his sternum, then back at Jin Tian. His lips moved, forming a single word that Jin Tian couldn't hear over the screaming crowd.

But he read it clearly enough.

*Run.*

Liu Feng fell. And behind him, standing in the shadows of the broken arena wall, was a figure Jin Tian had never expected to see again in this lifetime.

Elder Zhou.

The ancient elder's face was serene, his hands clasped behind his back, as if he hadn't just murdered an inner disciple with a forbidden blade. His eyes found Jin Tian, and he smiled.

"Well done, little sword," Elder Zhou said, his voice carrying clear and cold across the chaos. "You've passed the first test."

The world went white.

title: The Eighth Disciple's Shadow
content:
The training grounds hummed with anticipation as Jin Tian made his way toward the inner disciple arena. Three hours. He had three hours to prepare for a match that would determine far more than his advancement through the tournament bracket.

He found an isolated clearing behind the main hall, where the qi was thin and the shadows long. Perfect. He needed privacy for what came next.

Jin Tian sat cross-legged on the weathered stone, closing his eyes. The memories of his first life surged forward unbidden—Liu Feng's face, twisted with something that wasn't hatred. It had been fear. The realization struck him now with the force of a physical blow. In those final moments, when Liu Feng's blade had pierced his heart, the man had looked terrified.

*Why would a killer be afraid of his victim?*

The question had haunted Jin Tian across three centuries of death and rebirth. He had always assumed Liu Feng was simply another tool of Chen Wei's ambition, a weapon pointed at his master's back. But fear suggested something else entirely. It suggested coercion. It suggested *threats*.

"Brother Jin Tian."

The voice came from behind him, soft and familiar. Jin Tian's hand moved to his sword before his mind registered who it belonged to.

Mei Ling stood at the edge of the clearing, her pale robes stained with dirt and what looked like dried blood. Her face was calm, but her eyes held a wildness that Jin Tian recognized all too well—the look of someone who had seen too much and understood too little.

"Junior Sister," he said, keeping his voice neutral. "You should be resting."

"I should be dead," she corrected, stepping closer. "Chen Wei's men found me in the eastern pavilion an hour ago. They said they wanted to 'congratulate' me on my performance in the qualifiers."

Jin Tian's jaw tightened. "What happened?"

"I happened." Mei Ling's smile was thin and sharp. "Two of them won't be walking properly for a week. The third... well, he won't be walking at all."

She had killed someone. An outer disciple. The punishment for that was expulsion at best, death at worst.

"You shouldn't have come here," Jin Tian said, rising to his feet. "If anyone saw you—"

"Everyone saw me," she interrupted. "That's why I came. Chen Wei is moving faster than we anticipated. He doesn't just want to beat you in the tournament anymore. He wants to destroy everything you care about first."

The words hit harder than any blade. In his first life, Jin Tian had kept his distance from everyone, believing that isolation was strength. He had learned the hard way that isolation was simply death delayed. But now, having allowed himself to form connections, he had created vulnerabilities.

"Who else?" he asked, the question barely a whisper.

Mei Ling's composure cracked for just a moment. "Liang. They took him during the second round of qualifiers. No one saw it happen. He simply... vanished."

Liang. The boy who had shared his meals. The one who had covered for him during the night watches, who had asked no questions and offered nothing but loyalty. A boy who had no part in the schemes of ambitious disciples or the conspiracies of fallen masters.

"Why?" Jin Tian asked, though he already knew the answer.

"Because he's weak," Mei Ling replied. "Because hurting him sends a message. Because Chen Wei wants you distracted, angry, and making mistakes when you face Liu Feng."

She was right. Of course she was right. This was the same strategy Chen Wei had employed in their first life—strike at the foundations, weaken the supports, and watch the tower crumble. But there was a difference this time.

This time, Jin Tian saw the pieces moving.

"Tell me everything you saw in the eastern pavilion," he said, his voice hardening. "Every face. Every word. Every shadow."

Mei Ling hesitated, then nodded. She described the three disciples who had confronted her—their names, their fighting styles, their tells. She recounted their threats, their boasts, their casual mentions of Chen Wei's plans. And she described one detail that made Jin Tian's blood run cold.

"There was someone watching from the balcony," she said. "I only caught a glimpse before they retreated inside. White robes. A jade pendant shaped like a crescent moon."

The Eighth Disciple's symbol.

Jin Tian's mind raced. In his first life, his seven disciples had been the architects of his downfall. But there had been rumors—fragments of memories that didn't quite fit—of an eighth figure who had orchestrated events from the shadows. A figure who had never revealed themselves, who had pulled strings and whispered poison into ears, who had vanished when the deed was done.

He had dismissed those rumors as paranoia. Now he wondered if he had been a fool.

"The Eighth Disciple," he breathed.

Mei Ling's eyes widened. "What?"

"It's nothing." Jin Tian shook his head. "A ghost story. But if that ghost is real..." He trailed off, the implications spiraling outward. If there had been an eighth disciple, someone who had turned his own students against him without ever showing their face, then everything he thought he knew about his betrayal was wrong.

Chen Wei might not have been the architect. He might have been a tool, just like Liu Feng. Just like all of them.

"Brother Jin Tian, you're scaring me," Mei Ling said softly.

"Good." He met her gaze. "Fear keeps you alive. Now listen carefully. I need you to find Liang. Don't confront anyone. Don't fight. Just find out where they're holding him."

"And then?"

Jin Tian's hand drifted to his sword. "Then I'll handle it. After I win my match."

"Win? Brother, Liu Feng is ranked seventh among the inner disciples. He's been cultivating for years. You've been training for weeks."

"I've been training for three hundred years," Jin Tian corrected. "Liu Feng is a prodigy. But I've killed gods. I've shattered heavens. I've walked through the gates of death and come back laughing." He paused, letting the weight of his words settle. "One inner disciple is not going to stop me."

Mei Ling stared at him for a long moment, searching his face for any sign of madness. She found none.

"What are you?" she whispered.

"Someone who made a mistake," Jin Tian replied. "And someone who intends to fix it."

He turned and walked toward the arena, leaving Mei Ling standing alone in the clearing. The sun was beginning to set, painting the sky in shades of blood and fire. Perfect weather for a battle.

The inner disciple arena was a massive structure of white jade and black iron, carved with formations that reinforced the walls and amplified the qi of those within. Crowds had already gathered—inner disciples, elders, servants. Everyone wanted to see the outer disciple who had dared to challenge the ranking system.

Jin Tian stepped onto the platform, and a hush fell over the crowd.

Across from him, Liu Feng emerged from the opposite entrance. He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader through the shoulders, with a face that might have been handsome if not for the cold emptiness behind his eyes. He wore the crimson robes of an inner disciple of the Fire Lotus Sect, and flames licked at his fingertips as he walked.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the arena. "I've heard interesting things about you."

"I've heard interesting things about you as well," Jin Tian replied. "Though I suspect mine are more accurate."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. "Is that so?"

"Seventh among the inner disciples. Fire-aspected techniques. A reputation for finishing matches quickly and brutally." Jin Tian paused. "And a weakness for gambling debts that you've never quite managed to pay off."

The crowd's murmurs grew louder. Liu Feng's composure flickered.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't you?" Jin Tian smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I wonder what Elder Zhou would say if he knew that his prized inner disciple has been borrowing from the Shadow Coin Consortium. Or what the sect would think if they discovered that Liu Feng—the proud, the invincible—has been fighting for someone else's money."

Liu Feng's face went pale, then red. His flames surged, licking at the air around him.

"You're lying."

"Am I?" Jin Tian drew his sword, the blade catching the dying sunlight. "Then prove it. Beat me here, in front of everyone. Show them that you're still the man they think you are."

The challenge hung in the air like a drawn breath. Liu Feng's eyes darted to the balcony where the elders sat, then back to Jin Tian. Something passed across his face—fear, recognition, understanding.

He knew. Somehow, he knew that Jin Tian was telling the truth.

"What do you want?" Liu Feng hissed, his voice barely audible.

Jin Tian stepped closer, lowering his own voice to match. "I want to know who you're working for. I want to know why you killed me in another life. And I want to know where the Eighth Disciple is hiding."

Liu Feng's reaction was immediate and visceral—his eyes went wide, his breath caught, and for one perfect moment, Jin Tian saw absolute terror flash across his face.

"I don't know what you're talking about," he repeated, but this time the words rang hollow.

"Yes, you do." Jin Tian raised his sword. "And after I defeat you, you're going to tell me everything."

The bell rang. The match began.

Liu Feng attacked first, a torrent of flame erupting from his palms. Jin Tian moved, not to block, but to flow—his body becoming water against fire, shadow against light. He had fought a hundred fire cultivators in his previous life. He knew their patterns, their weaknesses, their inevitable exhaustion.

Liu Feng pressed the attack, his flames growing hotter, faster, more desperate. He was fighting like a man with nothing to lose, which meant he had everything to lose.

*Good,* Jin Tian thought. *Fear makes them predictable.*

He waited for the opening, the tell that Liu Feng had never managed to correct. A slight hesitation before his left-handed strikes. A tendency to overcommit when frustrated. A reliance on qi that left him vulnerable to physical attacks.

There.

Liu Feng launched another wave of fire, and Jin Tian stepped *into* it, not away. His sword cut through the flames, redirecting them, scattering them. He emerged on the other side with his blade already moving, already striking.

The tip of his sword stopped a hair's breadth from Liu Feng's throat.

"Yield," Jin Tian said.

The crowd erupted. Elders rose from their seats. Disciples shouted in disbelief. The match had lasted less than two minutes.

Liu Feng stood frozen, his flames extinguished, his eyes locked on the blade that could end his life with a single push.

"How?" he whispered.

"I told you," Jin Tian replied. "I've been training for three hundred years."

He lowered his sword, and Liu Feng collapsed to his knees. The victory was his. The tournament would continue.

But as Jin Tian turned to leave, Liu Feng grabbed his ankle.

"Wait," the inner disciple said, his voice cracking. "Please. There's something you need to know."

Jin Tian paused. "What?"

Liu Feng looked up, and his eyes were wet with tears. "The Eighth Disciple isn't one person. It's a title. A rank. And the person who holds it now..."

He swallowed hard.

"It's Elder Zhou."

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Blade That Remembers

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The sun hung low, casting long shadows across the stone tiles, and the air smelled of rain that hadn't yet fallen. He had exactly two hours and forty-seven minutes until his match against Liu Feng.

He didn't plan to waste a single second.

Jin Tian closed his eyes and let his memory drift back three hundred years. The moment of his death. Liu Feng's blade piercing his heart. The fire in his chest as his cultivation base collapsed. The confusion that had plagued his final moments.

*Why?*

In his first life, Liu Feng had been a rival, yes, but one he respected. They had fought seven times over the years. Jin Tian had won four, Liu Feng three. Each battle had pushed them both to new heights. When the inner sect tournament came, Jin Tian had expected Liu Feng to be his greatest challenge, the one who would force him to reveal his true power.

Instead, Liu Feng had been the one to end him.

But not with honor. Not in a duel. Liu Feng had struck from behind, between one breath and the next, while Jin Tian was recovering from a battle against another disciple. It was an assassination, not a victory.

Jin Tian opened his eyes.

"I need to understand," he muttered to the empty grounds. "What made you turn?"

He drew his sword—the same basic iron blade he'd had since becoming an outer disciple. In his first life, he had used this sword for three years before upgrading to a spirit-grade weapon. Now, he had only a few hours to prepare for a battle against an inner disciple with a fire-aspected cultivation base and years of advantage.

But he had something Liu Feng didn't expect: three centuries of combat experience.

Jin Tian began to move.

The first form was simple—a basic thrust he had learned on his first day as a disciple. But as he executed it, he poured qi into the blade, not enough to be visible, but enough to test the weapon's resonance. The sword hummed, a faint vibration that only he could feel.

"Good," he said. "You'll hold."

He moved through the forms faster, letting muscle memory take over while his mind worked on strategy. Liu Feng's fire techniques were powerful but predictable. He favored wide, sweeping attacks designed to overwhelm opponents with sheer force. In a tournament setting, that aggression was an advantage—most disciples couldn't handle the pressure.

But Jin Tian knew something Liu Feng didn't.

Fire techniques had a weakness. They required constant qi circulation to maintain their temperature. If that circulation was disrupted, even for a fraction of a second, the technique collapsed. The key was timing. Striking at the precise moment between cycles.

Jin Tian had done it before, against a fire-aspected demon lord in his previous life. The battle had lasted three days, but in the end, he had found the pattern and exploited it.

Liu Feng was not a demon lord. His technique would be less refined, his cycles longer, his openings wider.

"One flaw," Jin Tian said, stopping mid-form. "That's all I need."

He sheathed his sword and sat down cross-legged, closing his eyes. He needed to prepare his body, but more importantly, he needed to prepare his mind. The last time he had faced Liu Feng, he had been arrogant. He had assumed his talent would carry him through, that his natural genius was enough.

It wasn't.

This time, he would fight like he was facing a demon lord. With respect for his opponent's strength. With no room for error.

---

An hour passed. Then another.

Jin Tian rose as the gong sounded, signaling one hour until the tournament. He stretched, feeling the qi flowing smoothly through his meridians. His body was still weak compared to his previous life, but it was improving. The foundation he had built in the past few weeks was solid.

"You're early."

Jin Tian turned to find Elder Zhou standing at the edge of the training grounds, arms folded behind his back. The old man's eyes were sharp, missing nothing.

"Preparation is the foundation of victory, Elder," Jin Tian replied.

"Hmph. A cliché spoken by every disciple who thinks they're clever." Elder Zhou stepped closer, his robes rustling. "But you're not like the others, are you, boy?"

Jin Tian said nothing.

"I've been watching you since the tournament announcement. You move like someone who has been training for decades, not months. Your forms are precise, your breathing controlled. You knew about the scoring system in detail, which I find... interesting, given that outer disciples aren't given access to those records."

Jin Tian's heart rate didn't change. He had expected questions eventually. "I studied, Elder. The library is open to all disciples."

"The library contains basic technique manuals and sect history. It does not contain the inner workings of tournament scoring." Elder Zhou's gaze sharpened. "So I'll ask you directly. How did you know?"

The moment hung between them. Jin Tian could lie, claim he had overheard something, or that he had deduced the system through observation. But Elder Zhou was not a fool. He had lived for centuries, had seen countless schemes and conspiracies. A lie would only make him more suspicious.

"Because I've seen it before," Jin Tian said.

Elder Zhou's eyebrow rose. "Seen it before? The tournament has not yet begun."

"Not this tournament. Another one." Jin Tian met the old man's eyes. "In a different life."

Silence.

Elder Zhou studied him for a long moment, then let out a bark of laughter. "Interesting. Very interesting. You claim to have lived a previous life?"

"I claim nothing, Elder. I'm only answering your question."

"And if I were to report this to the sect elders? That one of our outer disciples claims to have memories of another life?"

Jin Tian didn't flinch. "Then I would be tested, questioned, and probably executed for heresy. But you won't do that."

"Won't I?"

"Because you're curious." Jin Tian allowed a small smile. "You've been sect elder for two hundred years, Elder Zhou. You've seen every type of disciple—the talented, the lazy, the ambitious, the cowardly. But you've never seen someone like me. And you want to know what I'll become."

Elder Zhou's expression was unreadable. Then, slowly, he nodded. "You're right. I am curious." He turned to leave, then paused. "One piece of advice, boy. Liu Feng has been purchased."

Jin Tian's blood went cold. "Purchased?"

"Chen Wei's family is wealthy. They have connections in the inner sect. A week ago, a set of high-grade spirit stones was transferred to Liu Feng's account. The amount was enough to buy his loyalty for a single match."

The pieces clicked into place. In his first life, Liu Feng had killed him not out of rivalry or hatred, but because he had been paid to do so. The assassination had been a transaction, nothing more.

"Why are you telling me this?" Jin Tian asked.

"Because I hate corruption," Elder Zhou said simply. "And because I want to see if you're as clever as you think you are."

He walked away, leaving Jin Tian alone with the weight of the revelation.

---

The tournament grounds were packed.

Inner disciples filled the stands, their robes marking their status. Outer disciples crowded the edges, watching with a mixture of awe and envy. At the center of the arena, a raised platform glowed with formation arrays, ready to contain the battle.

Jin Tian stood at the edge, waiting.

Liu Feng arrived exactly on time, his fire-aspected aura blazing like a furnace. He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, his arms thick with muscle, his face set in a confident smirk. Behind him, Chen Wei watched from the stands, his expression carefully neutral.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," the announcer called. "You face Inner Disciple Liu Feng. The rules are simple: no killing, no crippling techniques. Everything else is permitted."

Jin Tian stepped onto the platform.

Liu Feng smiled, a predatory expression. "I have to admit, I didn't expect to face an outer disciple in my first match. I thought they'd give me someone worth my time."

"Disappointed?" Jin Tian asked.

"Surprised." Liu Feng drew his blade—a crimson sword that seemed to glow with heat. "But I won't hold back. Chen Wei paid me well to make sure you don't advance."

The crowd gasped. Chen Wei's face went pale.

Jin Tian didn't react. He had expected this. Liu Feng was arrogant, secure in his position. He didn't need to hide his corruption because he didn't think anyone could stop him.

"Good," Jin Tian said, drawing his iron blade. "Then neither will I."

The gong sounded.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had predicted. A wave of fire erupted from his sword, sweeping across the platform in a wide arc. The heat was intense, warping the air, forcing Jin Tian to retreat.

But Jin Tian had been watching. Had been counting.

*One. Two. Three.*

He saw the moment Liu Feng's qi cycle hit its apex, the moment before the fire technique would need to be refreshed. It was a fraction of a second, barely visible to anyone watching.

But Jin Tian had spent three centuries learning to see the invisible.

He moved.

Not away from the fire, but into it. His iron blade sliced through the gap in Liu Feng's technique, striking the crimson sword at its weakest point. The impact sent a shockwave through the platform, and Liu Feng's eyes went wide with shock.

"What—"

Jin Tian didn't stop. He pressed the attack, his blade a blur of motion. Form after form, each one targeting the gaps in Liu Feng's defense. The inner disciple was stronger, faster, his qi more refined. But he had never faced someone who knew exactly where to strike.

The match lasted three minutes.

When it was over, Liu Feng was on his knees, his crimson sword lying broken on the ground. Jin Tian's iron blade rested against his throat, the tip drawing a single drop of blood.

"I yield," Liu Feng whispered.

The crowd erupted.

But Jin Tian didn't hear them. He was staring at Liu Feng's face, watching the shock and confusion turn into something else. Something darker.

Recognition.

"You knew," Liu Feng said, his voice barely audible. "You knew where to strike. You knew my technique's weakness. How?"

Jin Tian leaned closer, his voice cold. "Because you killed me once, Liu Feng. This time, I returned the favor."

Liu Feng's eyes went wide with terror.

And then Chen Wei was there, pushing through the crowd, his face a mask of fury. "What did you do to him? What did you say?"

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and turned away. "Nothing that concerns you, Chen Wei. But your turn is coming."

He walked off the platform, leaving chaos in his wake.

The first match was over. But the war had only just begun.

title: The Serpent Beneath the Silk
content:
The tournament grounds had transformed since morning.

Where simple wooden platforms had stood, now rose three grand arenas, each carved from white jade and inscribed with formation arrays that hummed with barely contained power. Banners bearing the sect's golden sword emblem snapped in the mountain wind, and disciples filled the surrounding terraces in a sea of blue and white robes.

Jin Tian stood in the preparation chamber beneath the central arena, alone save for the flickering torchlight and the weight of his memories.

Three hours until Liu Feng.

He had used the time wisely. His meridians had been cycled through three complete purification rotations, pushing his qi reserves to their current maximum. His sword—a standard-issue outer disciple blade—had been sharpened, balanced, and tested against his palm a dozen times until the weight felt natural in his grip.

It wasn't enough. It would never be enough against Liu Feng's burning techniques and rank-three cultivation base.

But it would have to do.

The chamber door slid open, and a young servant girl entered carrying a tray with tea and spirit-replenishing pills. She was perhaps fourteen, with plain features and nervous eyes that darted everywhere except his face.

"The overseers send their regards, Senior Brother," she said, her voice barely above a whisper. "They bid you rest and prepare. Your match begins when the sun reaches its zenith."

Jin Tian nodded, reaching for the tea. His fingers stopped an inch from the cup.

The qi signature was wrong.

He had spent three centuries studying the subtle arts of poison—both as a wielder and a victim. The tea smelled correct. It looked correct. But the qi within it vibrated at a frequency that pure spirit tea never would. A frequency designed to suppress cultivation, dull the senses, and induce a slow, creeping weakness that would manifest at the worst possible moment.

He lifted the cup anyway, bringing it to his lips.

The servant girl's eyes flickered—a micro-expression of anticipation that she masked almost instantly.

Jin Tian set the cup down without drinking.

"Tell Chen Wei his hospitality is appreciated," he said softly, "but I prefer my tea without Nightbloom Essence."

The girl's face went white. She stumbled backward, the tray clattering to the stone floor. "I—I don't know what you're talking about, Senior Brother—"

"Leave."

She fled.

Jin Tian stared at the poisoned tea, watching the faint iridescent sheen that only trained eyes could detect. Chen Wei was playing the game early. Good. That meant he was worried.

But the attempt told Jin Tian something more important than Chen Wei's fear. It told him that Chen Wei had access to Nightbloom Essence—a rare and expensive poison derived from a flower that only grew in the demon territories beyond the empire's borders. An outer disciple of a minor sect shouldn't have such connections.

Unless those connections had already been forged.

In his first life, Chen Wei's betrayal had been a surprise. In this one, Jin Tian was collecting the breadcrumbs that led to the same destination.

He crushed the tea cup with his palm, letting the liquid sizzle and evaporate on the stone floor. Then he closed his eyes and entered meditation, his consciousness sinking into the depths of his cultivation base.

He had three hours.

He would need every second.

---

The crowd's roar was deafening.

Jin Tian emerged from the tunnel into blinding sunlight, the central arena spreading before him like a lake of polished jade. Thousands of faces watched from the terraces—outer disciples, inner disciples, elders, and guests from allied sects. The weight of their attention pressed against his skin like a physical force.

Across the arena, Liu Feng descended from the opposite tunnel.

He was everything Jin Tian remembered. Tall, broad-shouldered, with flame-red hair that seemed to burn in the sunlight. His cultivation base blazed at the peak of the third realm—three levels above Jin Tian's current advancement. And his sword, a curved blade named Ember's Kiss, was already drawn and dripping with fire-aspected qi that made the air shimmer around him.

Liu Feng's reputation preceded him. Ranked seventh among the inner disciples. Victor in forty-three consecutive matches. His fire techniques were said to be so refined that he could reduce a man to ash without touching him.

In Jin Tian's first life, Liu Feng's blade had ended his.

"Disciples!" Elder Zhou's voice boomed across the arena, amplified by formation arrays. "You stand today at the threshold of greatness! This tournament will determine who among you has the strength, the will, and the spirit to represent our sect in the coming trials!"

Jin Tian tuned out the speech. He was reading Liu Feng's stance, his breathing, the subtle shifts of weight that betrayed his fighting style. In the first life, he had been distracted by the enormity of the moment, by the crowd, by the pressure. He had fought well but reactively, always responding to Liu Feng's attacks rather than dictating the pace.

This time would be different.

"—let the match begin!"

The formation array flared to life, creating a translucent barrier around the arena. The temperature dropped as energy shields hummed into existence, protecting the spectators from any stray techniques.

Liu Feng didn't wait. He exploded forward, Ember's Kiss trailing a comet's tail of fire.

Jin Tian met him head-on.

Their blades clashed with a sound like thunder. The shockwave rippled outward, cracking the jade beneath their feet. Jin Tian's arm went numb from the impact—Liu Feng's strength was monstrous, amplified by his higher cultivation base and fire-aspected reinforcement.

But Jin Tian hadn't come to win a battle of strength.

He let the impact carry him backward, using the momentum to create distance. Liu Feng pressed the advantage, his sword becoming a whirlwind of flame and steel. Strike, feint, kick, pursuit—his technique was flawless, honed by years of training and countless victories.

In the first life, this onslaught had overwhelmed Jin Tian. He had parried, dodged, retreated, and eventually fallen.

Now he watched.

Liu Feng's patterns were as clear as written text. A slight dip in his left shoulder before his signature flame lash. A half-second pause after his fourth combo, where he reset his stance. The way his eyes tracked Jin Tian's feet rather than his blade—a habit of someone trained to read intent through body positioning.

Jin Tian deflected a high slash, sidestepped a fire bolt, and for the first time, attacked.

His blade moved not with speed, but with purpose. A thrust aimed at Liu Feng's left knee—the joint he favored for pivoting. A horizontal cut that forced Liu Feng to block with his blade's flat rather than the edge. A feint toward the throat that made Liu Feng raise his guard, leaving his ribs exposed.

The crowd gasped as Jin Tian's fist connected with Liu Feng's side.

It wasn't a powerful blow. It wouldn't break bones or damage organs. But it was a strike that should never have landed against a cultivator of Liu Feng's caliber.

Liu Feng's eyes widened. For the first time, he looked at Jin Tian not as an opponent but as a threat.

"Lucky hit," he growled.

Jin Tian smiled. "Was it?"

They circled each other, the jade arena cracked and scorched around them. The crowd's roar had faded to a hushed murmur as even the casual observers realized they were witnessing something unprecedented.

Liu Feng attacked again, faster this time. His fire techniques blazed hotter, turning the air inside the barrier into a furnace. Sweat poured down Jin Tian's face, but his hands remained steady.

*Dodge. Parry. Counter. Move.*

He was reading Liu Feng like a book he had read a hundred times. Every attack had a tell. Every technique had a weakness. Liu Feng's cultivation was higher, his qi denser, his techniques flashier—but he fought with patterns, with habits, with the predictability of someone who had never faced an equal.

Jin Tian had faced his superiors for three centuries.

He had learned to cheat.

The match stretched into its tenth minute, then its fifteenth. Liu Feng's attacks grew wilder, more desperate. He was used to ending fights quickly, to overwhelming his opponents before they could adapt. Jin Tian refused to be overwhelmed.

And then, in the seventeenth minute, Jin Tian saw his opening.

Liu Feng overextended on a flame lash, his weight shifting too far forward. It was the same mistake he had made in their first life, the same moment Jin Tian had tried to exploit and failed because his cultivation was too weak.

This time, he didn't try to match Liu Feng's power.

He changed the angle.

Instead of meeting the flame lash head-on, Jin Tian dropped to one knee, letting the fire pass over his head. His blade swept low, not at Liu Feng's body, but at his footing. The jade beneath Liu Feng's back foot cracked, shifted, and crumbled.

Liu Feng stumbled.

Jin Tian rose like a serpent striking. His sword didn't aim for a killing blow—tournament rules forbade lethal strikes—but for the one place Liu Feng couldn't defend. The inside of his wrist, where the tendons controlling his grip lay exposed.

The blade bit shallowly, drawing a line of blood.

Ember's Kiss clattered to the jade floor.

Silence.

Liu Feng stared at his empty hand, his face a mask of disbelief. The fire around him guttered and died, his concentration shattered. He looked at Jin Tian—really looked at him—and something flickered in his eyes.

Recognition.

Not of Jin Tian as he was, but of something older. Something that shouldn't exist in a twelve-year-old outer disciple.

"Who are you?" Liu Feng whispered.

Jin Tian lowered his blade. "Someone who wants answers."

The crowd erupted. The sound was deafening—cheers, shouts, arguments. An outer disciple had just defeated a ranked inner disciple. The impossible had happened.

But Jin Tian only had eyes for Liu Feng.

And Liu Feng's face held a terror that had nothing to do with losing a match.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Liu Feng said, but his voice cracked. "I've never met you before today."

Jin Tian stepped closer, lowering his voice so only Liu Feng could hear. "You have. In another life. You put your sword through my heart, Liu Feng. I want to know why."

Liu Feng's face went gray.

And then, impossibly, he laughed.

It was not a happy sound. It was the laugh of a man who has seen the walls of reality crack and something terrible peering through.

"You remember," Liu Feng whispered. "Gods above, you *remember*." He shook his head slowly. "Then you know it's already begun. The game was set in motion before either of us drew our first breath. I was just a piece. We all are."

"What game?" Jin Tian demanded.

But before Liu Feng could answer, a bell tolled across the arena. The sound was wrong—discordant, jarring, nothing like the harmonious chimes that marked tournament transitions.

Elder Zhou's voice cut through the chaos, stripped of its earlier warmth.

"All matches are suspended. The sect is under immediate lockdown. No one leaves the arena grounds until the source of the disturbance is identified."

A disturbance.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

In his first life, there had been no lockdown during the tournament. No disturbance. No interruption.

Something had changed.

He turned to look at the eastern sky, where a column of black smoke rose from the sect's inner sanctum.

The Sword Library.

Someone was burning the Sword Library.

And in that moment, Jin Tian understood. The poisoned tea. The rushed tournament. The whispers he had dismissed as paranoia.

Chen Wei's

title: The Seventh Disciple's Betrayal
content:
The morning sun cast golden light across the training grounds, but Jin Tian felt no warmth. Three months had passed since the inner division tournament—three months of careful observation, quiet cultivation, and the slow, patient work of rebuilding his foundation. He had reached the fifth level of Qi Condensation, far faster than anyone expected, but still painfully slow compared to his first life.

Today, everything changed.

He stood at the edge of the Misty Peak training grounds, watching the inner disciples practice their techniques. Liu Feng was there, his fire techniques blazing across the courtyard, his movements precise and deadly. They had not spoken since their match—a match Jin Tian had won through superior technique and battlefield experience, but only just. Liu Feng was stronger than he remembered, faster, more ruthless.

But Jin Tian's attention was elsewhere.

A figure emerged from the inner sect's main hall—tall, elegant, wearing robes of pure white silk embroidered with silver clouds. The Seventh Disciple. His seventh disciple. The one who had been the first to turn her blade against him.

Mei Ling.

She was younger now, barely eighteen, her beauty already legendary among the disciples. In his first life, she had been his final student, the one he had trusted most, the one who had held his hand as he died.

And she had been the one to whisper the location of his secret cultivation cave to his enemies.

Jin Tian's hand moved to his sword before he caught himself. Not yet. He could not reveal his knowledge, could not tip his hand. Not until he understood the full scope of the conspiracy.

"You're staring."

The voice came from behind him—soft, amused, carrying the faint scent of jasmine. Jin Tian turned to find a young woman leaning against a training post, her arms crossed, her eyes sharp with intelligence.

She was plain by sect standards—ordinary features, simple robes, no obvious cultivation talent. But Jin Tian recognized her instantly.

Su Yin. The sect's information broker. In his first life, she had been executed for selling secrets to the demon cults. But that had been a lie, a convenient scapegoat to cover the real traitor's tracks.

She had died protecting Jin Tian's name, even as his disciples destroyed everything he had built.

"Who are you?" Jin Tian asked, keeping his voice neutral.

"Someone who notices things," Su Yin replied, pushing off from the post. "And I notice you've been watching the Seventh Disciple for seven consecutive days. That's either admiration or suspicion. Given your history with the inner disciples, I'm leaning toward the latter."

Jin Tian's eyes narrowed. "You've been watching me."

"Someone has to." She stepped closer, lowering her voice. "The inner sect is preparing for something. Alliances are shifting. Resources are being redirected. And three outer disciples have disappeared in the last month."

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow. In his first life, the disappearances had begun six months before his death, not three. The timeline was accelerating.

"Disappeared how?"

"Vanished from their quarters. No signs of struggle, no traces of Qi. The sect claims they deserted, but deserters don't leave their cultivation manuals behind." She held his gaze. "I think someone is harvesting them. Extracting their cultivation bases."

The Blood Lotus technique. A forbidden method that allowed a cultivator to absorb another's cultivation, but at the cost of twisting their own Qi, corrupting their foundation. It was crude, inefficient, and left traces.

Unless the user had access to a cleansing artifact.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. In his first life, he had possessed such an artifact—the Jade Purification Mirror, a treasure capable of removing any impurity from one's Qi circulation. It had been a gift from the sect master, a symbol of trust.

And it had been stolen three months before his death.

"What do you want?" Jin Tian asked.

Su Yin smiled—a predator's smile, sharp and knowing. "I want to know why a twelve-year-old outer disciple fights like a veteran of a hundred battles. I want to know why you look at the Seventh Disciple like she's a ghost. And I want to know what you're planning to do when the inner sect tournament begins tomorrow."

Tomorrow. The tournament. In his first life, the tournament had been the catalyst—the moment when the conspiracy had begun to move in earnest. His disciples had used the chaos to approach him, to earn his trust, to begin their long game of betrayal.

"I'm not sure what you mean," Jin Tian said carefully.

"You're a terrible liar." Su Yin reached into her sleeve and produced a small jade slip. "But I'm not your enemy. Take this. It contains the locations where the disciples disappeared, the times, and the Qi signatures I managed to record before the sect sealed the records."

She pressed the slip into his hand. Her fingers were cold, her grip surprisingly strong.

"Why help me?"

"Because someone needs to stop what's coming." She glanced toward the inner sect hall, where Mei Ling was disappearing through the ornate doors. "And I have a feeling you're the only one who can."

She turned and walked away, leaving Jin Tian alone with the jade slip and a thousand questions.

---

Jin Tian spent the next hour in his quarters, studying the information Su Yin had provided. The disappearances followed a pattern—always at night, always during specific celestial alignments, always from disciples with above-average talent but no powerful patrons.

Someone was systematically weakening the sect.

But why? The Azure Cloud Sect was one of the five great sects of the Eastern Continent. Its destruction would leave a power vacuum that would take decades to fill. Unless the goal wasn't destruction, but control.

Jin Tian's mind raced through possibilities. In his first life, the demon cults had risen three years from now, sweeping across the continent like a plague. But they had been led by a figure called the Void Emperor—a mysterious cultivator who had emerged from nowhere, wielding techniques that had never been seen before.

What if the Void Emperor had planted agents within the sects years before his appearance? What if his disciples, his betrayers, were part of something larger?

The thought made his blood boil.

He was still studying the records when a knock came at his door. He opened it to find a young boy—maybe ten years old, with dirt on his face and fear in his eyes.

"You're Jin Tian?" the boy asked, his voice trembling.

"Yes."

The boy thrust a folded piece of paper into his hands. "A woman gave me this. She said to give it to you and no one else. She said... she said the Seventh Disciple is meeting with someone in the Western Bamboo Grove tonight. And if you want to know the truth, you'll be there."

Before Jin Tian could ask more, the boy turned and ran, vanishing into the evening mist.

Jin Tian unfolded the paper. The handwriting was elegant, refined—unmistakably Mei Ling's.

*Come alone. Bring no weapons. I know what you are.*

The words hit him like a punch to the gut. She knew. Somehow, she knew he had regressed, knew he carried memories of a future that hadn't happened yet. How? Unless...

Unless this wasn't his first regression.

The thought was terrifying. What if this was just one cycle in an endless loop? What if he had lived this life a hundred times, a thousand times, always failing, always dying, always starting over?

He crushed the paper in his fist, feeling the familiar burn of anger rising in his chest.

No. He would not be trapped. He would break the cycle, destroy the conspiracy, and this time, he would live.

He grabbed his sword—despite the note's warning—and stepped into the night.

---

The Western Bamboo Grove was a maze of silent green stalks, their leaves rustling in the cold wind. Jin Tian moved through them like a ghost, his steps silent, his Qi suppressed to almost nothing.

He found them in a clearing at the center of the grove.

Mei Ling stood beneath a gnarled old tree, her white robes glowing in the moonlight. She was not alone. A figure stood opposite her, cloaked in shadows, their features impossible to distinguish.

"...the timeline is accelerating," Mei Ling was saying, her voice carrying a note of urgency Jin Tian had never heard from her. "He's already reached the fifth level. At this rate, he'll surpass his previous cultivation base within a year."

"Then we move faster." The cloaked figure's voice was distorted, layered with an unnatural resonance that made Jin Tian's ears ache. "The artifact is almost ready. Another week, and the convergence will be complete."

"And the disciples?"

"Dispose of them. We have enough cultivation bases to fuel the ritual. The rest are expendable."

Mei Ling nodded, her face unreadable. "And Jin Tian? What of him?"

There was a long pause. The cloaked figure seemed to consider the question carefully.

"The Sword God has returned too early. His regression was not supposed to happen for another three cycles. Someone interfered." The figure's voice dropped, becoming almost a whisper. "Find out who. And when you do... eliminate them."

Jin Tian's hand tightened on his sword. Three cycles. He had lived this life at least twice before, and he didn't remember. The knowledge was lost, buried beneath the trauma of regression.

But Mei Ling knew. And the cloaked figure knew even more.

He stepped forward, the bamboo crackling beneath his feet. Both figures turned, their eyes locking onto him.

"So," the cloaked figure said, their voice carrying a hint of amusement, "the Sword God reveals himself."

"I'm not a god," Jin Tian replied, drawing his blade. "But I am your death."

The figure laughed—a hollow, echoing sound that seemed to come from everywhere at once. "Bold words for a man who doesn't remember his own failures."

"Enlighten me."

The figure stepped forward, the shadows falling away to reveal a face Jin Tian had never expected to see again.

Elder Zhou.

The same Elder Zhou who had presided over his tournament match. The same Elder Zhou who had seemed so fair, so impartial, so trustworthy.

"I am the Void Emperor," Elder Zhou said, his eyes burning with dark light. "And you, Sword God, are my greatest creation."

Jin Tian's world shattered.

The Void Emperor. The architect of the demon cults. The man who had destroyed the Eastern Continent, who had killed millions, who had ended an era of peace.

And he was standing in the Azure Cloud Sect, wearing the face of a trusted elder, orchestrating a conspiracy that had already claimed dozens of lives.

"You planted them," Jin Tian said, his voice cold. "My disciples. You made them betray me."

"I didn't make them do anything." Elder Zhou smiled, and there was nothing human in it. "I merely showed them what they could become. The Seventh Disciple wanted power. The Sixth wanted revenge. The Fifth wanted love. I gave them all what they desired, and in return, they gave me you."

"Me?"

"Your regression was my greatest experiment. A soul, thrown back through time, carrying memories of a future that never happened. Do you know how many attempts it took? How many lives were sacrificed to perfect the technique?"

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow. He wasn't a reborn hero, a second chance given by fate. He was a test subject. An experiment. A tool.

"You've been alive for three cycles?" Jin Tian asked, his voice barely a whisper.

"Four, actually. But who's counting?" Elder Zhou spread his arms. "Each time, I learn. Each time, I refine. And this time, I will succeed. The convergence will be complete, the Void Gate will open, and the demon cults

title: The Fire That Burns Below
content:
The announcement of his match against Liu Feng sent ripples through the gathered disciples. Jin Tian ignored the whispers, the stares, the barely concealed glee on Chen Wei's face. Three hours. He had three hours to prepare for a man who had killed him in his past life.

He turned and walked away before anyone could speak to him. Let them think him arrogant. Let them think him scared. Their assumptions were weapons he could use later.

The training grounds were empty. Everyone else had gathered at the tournament platform to watch the inner division matches. Good. Jin Tian needed solitude.

He found a shadowed corner near the outer disciple dormitories, where the old training dummies stood weathered and cracked. He had trained here as a boy in his first life, swinging a wooden sword until his hands bled. The familiarity steadied him.

Liu Feng.

The name burned in his mind. He remembered the man's face—sharp features, cold eyes, a scar running from his left eyebrow to his cheekbone. In his first life, Liu Feng had been a rising star, known for his mastery of the Blazing Sun Technique, a fire-aspected cultivation method that turned his qi into molten fury.

But Jin Tian had never fought him seriously. They had been allies, not enemies. Brothers-in-arms who had shared meals and strategies and victories.

Until the betrayal.

He closed his eyes and let the memories surface. The day of his death. The ambush in the Celestial Peak. Seven figures surrounding him, their blades drawn, their faces twisted with something he had mistaken for loyalty.

Liu Feng had been the last to strike. He had watched the others fall first, watched Jin Tian cut down three of his attackers before exhaustion and poison took their toll. Then, when Jin Tian could barely raise his sword, Liu Feng had stepped forward.

"Why?" Jin Tian had asked, blood filling his mouth.

Liu Feng had not answered. He had simply driven his sword through Jin Tian's heart.

Jin Tian opened his eyes. The memory still hurt, but the pain was a tool now. A reminder.

He drew his sword. The blade caught the afternoon light, gleaming with a faint edge of frost. His Frost Lotus technique was his greatest weapon—a counter to fire-aspected cultivation. In his first life, he had developed it specifically to counter the Blazing Sun Technique.

But that development had come years from now. He was twelve years old, with the qi reserves of an outer disciple and the physical limitations of a child's body. His techniques were shadows of what they would become.

He needed a different approach.

*Think*, he told himself. *You have the knowledge of a Sword God. Use it.*

Liu Feng's fighting style was aggressive, overwhelming. He would open with a burst of fire qi, forcing his opponent onto the defensive, then press the attack until they broke. His weakness was stamina—the Blazing Sun Technique consumed qi rapidly, and if the fight extended beyond ten minutes, his power would wane.

But Jin Tian couldn't last ten minutes against a fully powered inner disciple. Not at his current cultivation level.

He needed to end the fight quickly.

He began to move through the forms, practicing the Frost Lotus technique at a fraction of its full power. Each strike was precise, economical, conserving qi while maintaining lethality. He adjusted the angles, shortened the ranges, modified the footwork to account for his smaller frame.

Three hours passed like minutes.

A bell tolled from the tournament platform, signaling the start of his match. Jin Tian sheathed his sword and walked toward the arena.

The crowd parted as he approached. Whispers followed him like shadows.

"Is he really going to fight Liu Feng?"

"He'll be dead in minutes."

"Chen Wei must be laughing."

Jin Tian ignored them all. He stepped onto the platform and faced his opponent.

Liu Feng was already there.

The inner disciple stood at the center of the arena, arms crossed, a lazy smile on his face. He was tall for his age—fifteen, Jin Tian remembered—with broad shoulders and the confident stance of someone who had never known defeat. His qi burned around him like a heat haze, distorting the air.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the silent crowd. "I've heard interesting things about you."

"Nothing good, I'm sure."

"On the contrary." Liu Feng's smile widened. "They say you're clever. Bold. That you have a talent for making powerful enemies."

Jin Tian met his gaze. "Some enemies are worth making."

The smile faded. Liu Feng's eyes hardened. "Are you sure I'm one of them?"

"I guess we'll find out."

Elder Zhou stepped between them, raising a hand. "This match will follow standard tournament rules. No lethal strikes. Surrender or incapacitation signals victory. Begin."

The elder stepped back.

Liu Feng moved first.

He exploded forward, a trail of fire following his blade. The heat was intense, suffocating, pressing against Jin Tian's skin like a physical weight. In his first life, he would have met this attack head-on, matching fire with frost, testing his opponent's strength.

Not this time.

Jin Tian stepped sideways. Not back, not forward—sideways. He moved at an angle that disrupted Liu Feng's attack trajectory, forcing the older boy to adjust mid-strike. The blade passed inches from his face, close enough to singe his hair.

Liu Feng recovered quickly, spinning into a follow-up strike. Jin Tian ducked under it, his sword sweeping low, aiming for Liu Feng's knee. The older boy jumped the strike, his fire qi flaring as he launched himself into the air.

"Running already?" Liu Feng called down, launching a wave of flame.

Jin Tian rolled, feeling the heat scorch the ground where he had stood. "Learning," he corrected.

He was reading Liu Feng's patterns. The way he shifted his weight before each strike. The slight hesitation in his left stance—a weakness from an old injury, probably. The tendency to overcommit on his third attack, leaving his right side exposed for half a heartbeat.

These were details Jin Tian had never noticed in his first life, because he had been too strong, too confident, too arrogant to study his opponent. He had relied on overwhelming power instead of precision.

Never again.

Liu Feng landed, his blade wreathed in flames. "You're faster than I expected."

"You're slower."

The taunt worked. Liu Feng's face flushed red—with anger or exertion, Jin Tian couldn't tell. He charged again, faster this time, his strikes coming in a furious barrage. Fire and steel blurred together, creating a wall of destruction.

Jin Tian retreated. He let the attacks drive him back, let Liu Feng believe he was winning. Every step was calculated. Every dodge was a measurement. He was drawing the older boy deeper into his rhythm, forcing him to burn through his qi.

"Stand still and fight!" Liu Feng roared, his strikes becoming sloppy.

Jin Tian saw the opening.

Liu Feng's left stance faltered as he overextended on a horizontal slash. The fire around his blade dimmed for a fraction of a second—the moment between qi expenditure and recovery.

Jin Tian struck.

His sword moved like water, flowing through the gap in Liu Feng's defense. Frost qi crystallized along the blade, forming a razor edge of ice. The strike was not powerful. It was not flashy. It was precise.

The tip of his sword touched Liu Feng's throat.

The arena went silent.

Liu Feng froze, his blade mid-swing, fire flickering uselessly around his hands. He looked down at the blade against his skin, then up at Jin Tian's face.

"How?" he whispered.

Jin Tian held his gaze. "You're predictable."

Elder Zhou's voice cut through the silence. "Match concluded. Victory to Outer Disciple Jin Tian."

The crowd erupted.

Jin Tian withdrew his sword and stepped back. He had won. He had defeated an inner disciple ranked seventh in the sect. But the victory felt hollow. He had not learned anything new. He had not discovered why Liu Feng had killed him.

"Wait."

Liu Feng's voice stopped him. The older boy had sheathed his sword, but his eyes were burning with something more dangerous than fire.

"That was not luck," Liu Feng said. "You knew my technique. You knew my patterns. You fought like you've faced me a hundred times before."

Jin Tian said nothing.

Liu Feng stepped closer, lowering his voice so only Jin Tian could hear. "There's a rumor among the inner disciples. They say you appeared three days ago, a nobody from the outer sect, and suddenly you're beating everyone. They say you know things you shouldn't."

"I know a lot of things."

"Do you know what happens to boys who know too much?" Liu Feng's smile returned, but it was cold now. "They disappear."

"Is that a threat?"

"It's a promise." Liu Feng turned and walked away, his robes billowing behind him.

Jin Tian watched him go. The crowd was still cheering, still buzzing with excitement over his victory. But he felt no joy. Something was wrong. In his first life, Liu Feng had never spoken to him like this. They had been friends. Brothers.

What had changed?

He left the platform before anyone could congratulate him. He needed to think. He needed to understand.

As he walked through the empty training grounds, a voice called out from the shadows.

"Impressive victory."

Jin Tian turned, his hand on his sword. A figure emerged from the darkness—a woman in inner disciple robes, her face hidden beneath a hood.

"Who are you?"

She pushed back her hood, revealing sharp features and eyes that gleamed with an unsettling intelligence. "My name is Lin Mei. I'm an inner disciple, like your friend Liu Feng."

"He's not my friend."

"He will be." Lin Mei smiled. "Or he'll be your enemy. The line is thinner than you think."

"What do you want?"

"I want to offer you a warning." She stepped closer, and Jin Tian noticed the insignia on her robes—the symbol of the sect's intelligence division. "There are people in this sect who are watching you. People who are very interested in your... sudden rise."

"Should I be flattered?"

"You should be careful." Her smile faded. "The tournament continues tomorrow. Your next opponent will not be as easy as Liu Feng."

"Who is it?"

Lin Mei met his eyes. "Inner Disciple Feng Xian. Ranked fourth. The strongest fire cultivator in the sect."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

Feng Xian.

In his first life, Feng Xian had been his senior, his mentor, his closest ally. The man who had taught him the Frost Lotus technique. The man who had stood beside him through countless battles.

And the man who had been the first to betray him.

Feng Xian had not struck the killing blow. He had done something worse. He had given the order.

Jin Tian had never known why.

"Thank you for the warning," he said, his voice steady despite the storm inside him.

Lin Mei studied him for a long moment. "You're not surprised."

"Should I be?"

"Most people would be terrified of facing Feng Xian."

"I'm not most people." Jin Tian turned and walked away, leaving her standing in the shadows.

Tomorrow, he would face his mentor. Tomorrow, he might get answers.

But as he approached his dormitory, he noticed something. A figure standing in the doorway. Waiting.

Chen Wei.

The other boy stepped into the moonlight, his face a mask of cold fury. "I don't know how you did it," he said, "but you've made a powerful enemy tonight."

"Liu Feng?"

"No." Chen Wei's smile was sharp. "Me."

He threw something at Jin Tian's feet. A jade

title: Chapter 5
content:
# CHAPTER 5: The First Cut

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. Sunlight filtered through the ancient pines surrounding the practice yard, casting long shadows across the worn stone tiles. He had exactly two hours before his match against Liu Feng.

Two hours to prepare for a man who had killed him in his previous life.

Jin Tian closed his eyes, letting the memories surface. The fight had been swift—brutally so. Liu Feng's fire techniques had overwhelmed him, forcing him onto the defensive from the first exchange. He remembered the heat, the suffocating pressure, the way his own blade had felt heavy and useless against the onslaught.

But that was then. He had been a different man—stronger in cultivation, but weaker in understanding. He had relied on raw power, on techniques learned from manuals and perfected through repetition. He had never questioned why those techniques worked. He had never looked beneath the surface.

Now he knew better.

He drew his sword. The blade caught the sunlight, throwing a brief flash across the training ground. It was a standard-issue weapon, forged from common steel and reinforced with a single qi-conducting core. Nothing special. Nothing that would give him an advantage.

But Jin Tian didn't need advantages. He needed understanding.

*Liu Feng's fire comes from his core, not his techniques,* he thought, beginning the first form. *He channels it through his meridians like breath, like blood. It's not something he learned—it's something he is.*

The memory of that fire burned in his mind. The way it had twisted and writhed, seeking gaps in his defense, reaching for his throat. It wasn't just destructive. It was alive.

*He's not a cultivator who uses fire. He's fire that learned to cultivate.*

That distinction mattered. It explained why Liu Feng fought the way he did—aggressive, relentless, never retreating. Fire didn't know how to retreat. Fire only knew how to spread, to consume, to grow until there was nothing left.

Jin Tian's blade traced a perfect arc through the air. The motion was precise, economical, wasted no energy. In his first life, he would have been practicing the fourth form of the sect's basic sword style, building foundation through repetition. Now he was practicing something else entirely.

He was practicing emptiness.

The sword style he was developing had no name yet. It was born from the space between techniques, the moments of transition that most cultivators ignored. His disciples had called it many things in his previous life—The Flowing Void, The Empty Blade, The Silence Between Worlds—but none of those names captured what it truly was.

It was the art of being where your opponent wasn't.

He moved through the forms again, faster this time. His body remembered the motions even if his current cultivation couldn't support them. Each strike ended where the next began. Each step flowed into the next. There was no beginning, no end, only the endless dance of blade and breath.

*Liu Feng will come at me with everything he has from the first moment,* Jin Tian reasoned. *He'll try to overwhelm me before I can find my rhythm. He'll use his fire to create distance, to control the pace of the fight, to force me into a defensive posture.*

It was the same strategy he had used in their first encounter. It had worked then. Liu Feng had no reason to believe it wouldn't work now.

*But I'm not the same person I was. And he doesn't know that.*

The realization settled into Jin Tian's bones like cold water. He had an advantage that no one in this world could predict: he had already fought this battle. He had already lost. He knew exactly what Liu Feng would do, because he had watched it happen once before.

The question was whether knowing was enough.

He sheathed his sword and sat cross-legged on the training ground's central platform. Around him, the morning air was thick with qi—the ambient energy that permeated everything in the sect. Most cultivators drew on this energy to fuel their techniques, absorbing it through their pores and channels like a sponge drawing water.

But that was inefficient. Jin Tian had spent three hundred years learning better methods.

He reached out with his awareness, not trying to absorb the qi but to understand its patterns. The energy moved in currents, flowing along lines of least resistance, pooling in places of concentrated life force. The ancient pines that surrounded the training ground were natural wells of qi, their roots drawing power from the earth itself.

*If I can use their energy instead of my own, I can fight longer,* he thought. *If I can move with the currents instead of against them, I can fight faster.*

It was a technique for foundational cultivation—one he had developed in his previous life when his core had been damaged in a battle against a demon lord. He had needed to learn to fight without relying on his own reserves. He had needed to become a conduit rather than a vessel.

The technique had saved his life then. It would save it again now.

He opened his eyes. The sun had climbed higher, burning away the morning mist. He had less than an hour before the match.

A shadow fell across him.

"I thought I'd find you here."

Jin Tian didn't turn around. He recognized the voice—reedy, uncertain, tinged with the awkwardness of youth. "Xu Ming. You shouldn't be here."

"Why not? You're my friend." The boy stepped into view, his round face creased with worry. "I heard about what happened at the announcement. Everyone's talking about it. They say you challenged Chen Wei in front of Elder Zhou, that you proved him wrong, that you—"

"That I made an enemy of someone powerful," Jin Tian interrupted. "Yes. I know."

Xu Ming's face fell. "Then why did you do it? The inner division tournament is dangerous. People die in those matches. And Liu Feng is one of the strongest inner disciples. He's killed before—in matches, I mean. Accidentally. They say his fire can't be controlled once it's unleashed."

*Accidentally,* Jin Tian thought. *Is that what they call it when a blade finds your heart?*

"Xu Ming." He stood, brushing dust from his robes. "You should go back to your quarters. Stay away from me for the next few days. If anyone asks, you don't know me. You didn't see me today."

"But—"

"This isn't a game." Jin Tian's voice was flat, cold, carrying an authority that didn't belong to a twelve-year-old boy. "Chen Wei is not the only threat. The inner disciples have their own agendas. Liu Feng is not just a fighter—he's a piece on a board I don't yet understand. If you're seen with me, you become a target."

Xu Ming's eyes widened. He opened his mouth to argue, but something in Jin Tian's expression stopped him. He nodded slowly, turned, and walked away.

Jin Tian watched him go. In his previous life, Xu Ming had been one of the few people who had stayed loyal until the end. He had died defending Jin Tian's retreat during the betrayal, cut down by disciples he had trained alongside. His last words had been a plea for forgiveness—for failing to protect his master, for not being strong enough, for being born too weak to matter.

*I won't let that happen again,* Jin Tian vowed silently. *This time, you'll live.*

He gathered his sword and walked toward the tournament arena.

---

The inner division tournament was held in the Grand Arena, a massive structure carved from the mountain itself. Thousands of disciples filled the surrounding seats, their voices creating a constant roar that echoed off the stone walls. Elder Zhou sat in the highest position, flanked by the other elders of the sect. Their eyes swept the arena with the detached interest of judges at an execution.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the arena floor, waiting for his opponent. The stone beneath his feet was scarred with the marks of countless battles—blackened by fire, etched by blades, stained by blood that had long since been washed away.

He could feel the weight of thousands of gazes on him. Most were curious, wondering who this unknown outer disciple was and how he had managed to earn a spot in the tournament. A few were hostile—followers of Chen Wei, no doubt, sent to watch him fail. A handful were calculating, taking his measure, filing away information for future use.

*They're all the same,* he thought. *In every life, they watch. They judge. They wait to see who will rise and who will fall.*

A gong sounded.

Liu Feng stepped onto the arena floor.

He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader in the shoulders, with a face that seemed carved from stone. His robes were red, the color of blood and fire, and his eyes held a heat that had nothing to do with temperature. He carried no visible weapon—his cultivation was his weapon, his body the vessel for destruction.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I must admit, I was surprised when I heard my opponent's name. The outer division rarely produces disciples willing to challenge their betters."

"I'm not here to challenge anyone," Jin Tian replied. "I'm here to learn."

Liu Feng's lips curved into a smile that didn't reach his eyes. "Learn? You'll learn more than you bargained for, boy. When the match begins, don't expect me to hold back."

"I wouldn't dream of it."

The gong sounded again. The match had begun.
