Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. Fire erupted from his palms, forming twin crescents that streaked across the arena floor. The heat was immense, distorting the air, turning the stone beneath Jin Tian's feet into a furnace.

But Jin Tian was already gone.

He moved sideways, not backward, slipping between the crescents with inches to spare. The fire passed so close to his face that he could feel his skin blister, but he didn't flinch. He had expected this. He had planned for it.

Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly—a fraction of a second of surprise. Then he recovered, spinning, unleashing another wave of fire that spread outward like a blooming flower.

Jin Tian ducked under the arc, rolled, came up inside Liu Feng's guard. His sword was in his hand, the blade aimed at the inner disciple's throat.

But Liu Feng was fast. Faster than Jin Tian had anticipated.

*He's improved,* Jin Tian realized, pulling his strike at the last moment to avoid a counterattack. *In my first life, he wasn't this skilled. He wasn't this precise.*

The thought sent a chill down his spine. If Liu Feng had been holding back in their first encounter, if he had been hiding his true strength, then everything Jin Tian knew about the man was wrong.

And if he was wrong about Liu Feng, what else was he wrong about?

Fire exploded around them, forcing Jin Tian to retreat. Liu Feng pressed his advantage, his attacks growing more intense, more focused. Each strike was aimed at a vital point—the throat, the heart, the dantian. He wasn't trying to win. He was trying to kill.

"Why?" Jin Tian asked, deflecting a blade of fire with his sword. "Why do you want me dead?"

Liu Feng's smile widened. "You ask the wrong question, boy. You should be asking who."

*Who.* The word echoed in Jin Tian's mind. Not why, but who.

Someone had ordered Liu Feng to kill him. In this life and the last.

Before he could press further, Liu Feng's fire changed. The red flames turned white, the temperature spiking to impossible levels. The disciples in the front rows screamed, scrambling backward. Even the elders leaned forward, their faces showing genuine surprise.

"This is a gift from someone who remembers you," Liu Feng said

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Flames of Betrayal

The training grounds of the Azure Cloud Sect stretched beneath a sky streaked with the amber hues of late afternoon. Jin Tian stood alone in a secluded courtyard, his practice sword tracing lazy arcs through the air. Each movement was deliberate, economical—a sword style he had perfected over three centuries of cultivation, now filtered through the limited body of a twelve-year-old.

Three hours until the match.

Three hours until he faced the man who had killed him.

He closed his eyes and let the memories wash over him. The blade sliding between his ribs. The shock of betrayal numbing him faster than the pain. Liu Feng's cold eyes as he twisted the weapon, ensuring the wound was fatal. And behind Liu Feng, the silhouette of Chen Wei, watching with that same hungry expression Jin Tian had seen today.

*Why?*

The question had haunted him through death and rebirth. Liu Feng had been one of the few inner disciples Jin Tian had respected. A fellow orphan who had clawed his way up through sheer determination. They had drunk together, trained together, bled together. There was no grudge between them, no rivalry that warranted murder.

But Liu Feng had killed him anyway.

"Your form is wrong."

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open. A figure stood at the courtyard entrance, silhouetted against the setting sun. Tall. Lean. Familiar.

"You're holding your shoulder too stiff," the figure continued, stepping forward. "Your sparring partner will exploit that in the third exchange." The sunlight revealed his face—sharp features, a small scar above his right eyebrow, eyes the color of burnt amber.

Liu Feng.

Jin Tian's grip tightened on his practice sword. Every instinct screamed at him to attack, to demand answers, to end this before it could begin. But he forced calm into his breathing, forced his muscles to relax.

"I appreciate the advice, Senior Brother," he said, his voice steady. "But I wasn't aware inner disciples monitored outer disciple training."

Liu Feng smiled, and the expression was almost warm. Almost genuine. "I don't usually. But news travels fast in the sect. The outer disciple who challenged Chen Wei and won a spot in the inner tournament? Everyone's talking about you." He leaned against a stone pillar, crossing his arms. "I wanted to see what all the fuss was about."

"And?"

"And you have good instincts. Your stance is solid, your footwork is above average, and you read your opponents well." Liu Feng's eyes narrowed slightly. "But you're hiding something. You fight like someone who's been training for decades, not years."

Jin Tian's heart skipped. In his previous life, no one had noticed. The other disciples had dismissed his skills as natural talent, the overseers had attributed it to rigorous training. But Liu Feng had always been observant—that was what made him dangerous.

"I practice a lot," Jin Tian said carefully.

"Practice doesn't teach you to read match efficiency scores at a glance. Practice doesn't give you the composure to challenge a senior disciple in front of the entire sect." Liu Feng pushed off the pillar, walking closer. "I've been watching you, Jin Tian. For the past three months, ever since you joined the sect. You've been holding back."

The words hit like a physical blow. *Three months.* Liu Feng had been watching him since the beginning. That meant—

"Don't look so surprised," Liu Feng said, stopping a few feet away. "I keep tabs on promising disciples. It's part of my duty as a senior brother." He tilted his head, studying Jin Tian with an intensity that bordered on unsettling. "You're interesting. Most outer disciples are content to coast through their training, hoping to scrape by into the inner division. But you have purpose. I can see it in your eyes."

He leaned in, lowering his voice. "What are you running from, Jin Tian? Or rather—what are you running toward?"

The question hung in the air between them. Jin Tian could feel the weight of it, the implication that Liu Feng knew more than he should. Could it be that Liu Feng was also caught in the web of betrayal, but from a different angle? Or was this a test, a way to gauge how much of a threat Jin Tian might become?

"I'm not running from anything," Jin Tian replied. "I'm just trying to get stronger."

"Strong enough to challenge Chen Wei?" Liu Feng laughed, but there was no humor in it. "You think Chen Wei is the one you need to worry about? He's a spoiled noble playing at cultivation. His family bought his way into the sect, bought his pills, bought his techniques. He's never had to truly struggle for anything." His eyes hardened. "Chen Wei is a puppet. The question is—who's pulling the strings?"

The words sent a chill down Jin Tian's spine. This was not the Liu Feng he remembered. The Liu Feng from his past life had been straightforward, almost simple in his approach to combat and life. He had never spoken in riddles, never hinted at conspiracies. But this Liu Feng was different. This Liu Feng *knew* something.

"What do you mean?" Jin Tian asked, keeping his voice neutral.

Liu Feng's smile faded, replaced by something darker. "I mean that the Azure Cloud Sect is not what it appears to be. There are factions within factions, plots within plots. The tournament isn't about finding the best disciples—it's about identifying those who can be controlled. Those who can be turned into weapons." He stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "You've already been noticed, Jin Tian. By people who would rather see you dead than free."

The confession hit Jin Tian like a thunderclap. This was the moment. The revelation that had been hidden from him in his previous life. Liu Feng wasn't just an assassin sent to kill him—he was a fellow pawn, trapped in a game he didn't fully understand.

"Then why warn me?" Jin Tian asked. "Why not let them use me like they used you?"

Liu Feng's face flickered with something—pain, regret, anger. It was gone before Jin Tian could identify it. "Because I've seen what happens to the ones who don't resist. I've seen the fire dim in their eyes, seen them become hollow shells of their former selves." He looked away. "I don't want that for you."

"Then help me."

The words slipped out before Jin Tian could stop them. Liu Feng's head snapped back, his eyes wide with shock. "What?"

"Help me," Jin Tian repeated, stepping forward. "You know the inner workings of the sect. You know who pulls the strings. Help me navigate this, and I'll help you break free."

Liu Feng stared at him for a long moment, his expression unreadable. Then he laughed—a bitter, hollow sound. "You don't even know what you're asking. Breaking free means death, Jin Tian. It means disappearing in the night, your body never found. I've seen it happen to three disciples who tried."

"Then we don't break free," Jin Tian said. "We break the system itself."

The words hung between them, heavy with implication. Liu Feng's eyes searched Jin Tian's face, looking for deception, looking for naivety. Whatever he found made him sigh.

"You're either the bravest person I've ever met," he said quietly, "or the most foolish." He reached into his robe and pulled out a jade slip, handing it to Jin Tian. "This contains information about the inner tournament's structure. Your opponents' strengths, weaknesses, patterns. Use it wisely."

Jin Tian took the slip, his fingers brushing against Liu Feng's. The contact sent a jolt through him—not of electricity, but of memory. In his past life, this same hand had held the blade that killed him. And now, that hand was offering help.

"Why?" Jin Tian asked, the question escaping unbidden.

Liu Feng's smile was sad. "Because I've been where you are. And no one helped me." He turned and walked toward the courtyard exit, pausing at the threshold. "One more thing, Jin Tian. Chen Wei isn't your enemy. He's a distraction. The real threat is someone you've never met, someone who pulls the strings from the shadows. Be careful who you trust."

He disappeared into the twilight, leaving Jin Tian alone with the jade slip and a thousand new questions.

---

The tournament arena was packed. Inner disciples lined the balconies, their robes pristine, their gazes condescending. Outer disciples crowded the lower tiers, their faces a mixture of hope and resentment. And at the center of it all stood the main platform, a circular stage of white jade inscribed with formation arrays that would contain the battle's energy.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the platform, his wooden practice sword replaced with a real blade—a gift from the sect armory, standard issue for tournament participants. It was unremarkable, but it would serve its purpose.

Across from him, Liu Feng stepped onto the platform, his movements fluid and confident. He had exchanged his casual robes for formal tournament attire, the Azure Cloud Sect's emblem gleaming on his chest. In his hand, he held a sword of crimson steel, flames licking along its edge.

"Disciples," Elder Zhou announced from the judges' platform, "this match will determine the final placement in the inner division rankings. The rules are simple: first to yield, or first to fall. No lethal techniques. Begin."

A gong sounded, and the match began.

Liu Feng moved first, his fire sword cutting through the air in a blazing arc. Jin Tian sidestepped, his own blade deflecting the strike with a controlled flick. The impact sent vibrations up his arm—Liu Feng was strong, stronger than he had been in Jin Tian's past life. Either he had been holding back then, or something had changed.

They exchanged a dozen blows in rapid succession, steel ringing against steel, fire flaring and dying. Jin Tian fought defensively, reading Liu Feng's patterns, looking for the opening that would end the match. But Liu Feng was relentless, his attacks coming faster and faster, forcing Jin Tian onto the back foot.

"You're holding back," Liu Feng hissed, his blade grazing Jin Tian's shoulder.

"So are you," Jin Tian replied, parrying a thrust aimed at his heart.

Their eyes met, and in that moment, Jin Tian understood. Liu Feng wasn't trying to win. He was trying to send a message. Each strike was a question, each feint a probe. He was testing Jin Tian, seeing if he was worthy of the trust he had been given.

Fine. Jin Tian would prove himself.

He abandoned his defensive stance and launched into a series of attacks, his sword becoming a blur of motion. The Flowing Water Sword Style, First Form: Cascade. The technique was basic, easily countered, but it served its purpose—forcing Liu Feng to commit to a block.

As Liu Feng raised his blade to parry, Jin Tian twisted his wrist, redirecting the strike into a feint. The Second Form: Ripple. Liu Feng's eyes widened as he realized his mistake, but it was too late. Jin Tian's blade slipped past his guard, the flat of the sword striking his ribs with controlled force.

Liu Feng stumbled back, gasping. The crowd erupted in murmurs.

"First blood to the outer disciple," someone shouted.

Jin Tian didn't relax. He watched Liu Feng carefully, waiting for the counterattack. But instead of pressing the advantage, Liu Feng lowered his sword and smiled.

"Well played," he said, his voice carrying across the arena. "You've earned the right to know the truth."

Before Jin Tian could ask what he meant, Liu Feng's expression shifted. His eyes went blank, his body stiffening as if seized by an invisible force. The flames on his sword extinguished, and when he spoke again

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Serpent in the Garden

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived.

Most disciples were still gathered at the tournament pavilion, dissecting the morning's matches and speculating about the afternoon's surprises. The sun hung directly overhead, casting short shadows that would grow long again by the time his match against Liu Feng began.

Three hours. He had three hours.

Jin Tian found a secluded corner near the practice dummies—weathered wooden figures scarred by countless strikes from disciples who had come before. He drew his sword slowly, deliberately, and closed his eyes.

In his first life, Liu Feng had been a puzzle he never solved.

The fire-aspected prodigy had risen through the ranks with terrifying speed, his techniques burning through defenses like dry grass. They had fought seven times over the years. Jin Tian had won four, Liu Feng three. Their rivalry had been legendary—balanced, respectful, the kind of competition that pushed both cultivators to greater heights.

And then, on the day of the betrayal, Liu Feng had driven a blade through his heart without a word of explanation. No gloating. No confession. Just steel and fire and silence.

*Why?*

Jin Tian opened his eyes and began his forms.

The first stance was slow, almost meditative. He let his qi flow through his meridians in a pattern he had perfected over three centuries of cultivation. But something was wrong. His energy caught at his dantian, stuttering like a candle in wind.

He tried again. Same result.

A cold knot formed in his stomach. He had been so focused on the tournament, on Chen Wei's schemes and the political games of the sect, that he had neglected something crucial: his foundation.

In his first life, Jin Tian had been a genius. He had broken through realms with absurd speed, mastering techniques in months that took others years. But that speed had come at a cost. He had built his cultivation on shortcuts—elixirs that boosted his qi, forbidden techniques that burned his meridians, meditation methods that sacrificed depth for breadth.

It had worked. He had reached the peak of power faster than anyone in history.

And then he had died, because his foundation was sand.

Now, with the memories of that failure burned into his soul, he understood the truth. He couldn't simply repeat his old path. He had to rebuild from the ground up, strengthening every layer, reinforcing every weakness. But that required time he didn't have.

Liu Feng would arrive in three hours.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and sat in the lotus position, closing his eyes. He needed to assess his current state honestly. At twelve years old, with a body that had barely begun to cultivate, his meridians were narrow and fragile. His dantian held only a trickle of qi. His physical strength was that of an ordinary child who had trained diligently for a few years.

Against an inner disciple like Liu Feng, who had been cultivating for at least a decade, he was outmatched in every measurable category.

Except one.

Experience.

Jin Tian smiled grimly. He might be weaker, slower, and less powerful, but he had something Liu Feng could never match: the memories of a Sword God. He knew techniques that hadn't been invented yet, understood the flow of battle in ways that transcended mere cultivation rank, and most importantly...

He knew Liu Feng's fighting style intimately.

The fire-aspected techniques, the tells, the habits, the moments of vulnerability that lasted only a fraction of a breath. In his first life, Jin Tian had learned those patterns over decades of combat. Now, he could exploit them from the very first exchange.

But there was another problem.

If he revealed too much of his skill, too much of his knowledge, questions would be asked. A twelve-year-old outer disciple shouldn't be able to predict the movements of a ranked inner disciple, shouldn't be able to counter techniques he had never seen before. The elders would investigate. Suspicion would follow.

Jin Tian needed to win, but he needed to win in a way that seemed plausible.

He was still pondering this dilemma when a shadow fell over him.

"Practicing alone, little brother?"

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open. A young man stood before him, dressed in the robes of an inner disciple. His features were sharp, almost handsome, but there was something off about his smile—a predatory quality that set Jin Tian's instincts screaming.

He had never seen this man before. In either life.

"Who are you?" Jin Tian asked, keeping his voice calm.

"Me?" The man laughed, a hollow sound that didn't reach his eyes. "I'm nobody important. Just a curious senior who heard about the bold outer disciple who challenged Chen Wei for a tournament slot."

"News travels fast."

"News about interesting things always does." The man circled around Jin Tian slowly, hands clasped behind his back. "I wanted to see you for myself. The boy who supposedly has the highest efficiency score in the outer division. The boy who dared to face Elder Zhou's judgment."

Jin Tian rose to his feet, keeping his hand near his sword. "And what do you see?"

The man stopped, tilting his head. "A child playing a dangerous game."

"Most games are dangerous if you play them well."

A pause. Then the man laughed again, and this time it sounded almost genuine. "I like you, little brother. You have fire. But fire can burn the one who holds it." He reached into his robe and produced a small jade vial, no larger than his thumb. "Take this. A gift."

Jin Tian didn't move. "What is it?"

"A qi restoration elixir. Refined from thousand-year spirit herbs. One drop will replenish your reserves ten times over during battle." The man held it out, his smile never wavering. "You'll need it against Liu Feng. He doesn't hold back."

"Why would you help me?"

"Because I find you interesting." The man tossed the vial, and Jin Tian caught it reflexively. "Consider it an investment. If you survive today, I may call on you for a small favor in the future."

He turned and walked away without another word, his robes billowing behind him.

Jin Tian stared at the vial in his hand. The jade was warm to the touch, and when he uncorked it, a faint floral scent drifted out. He recognized the fragrance—it was indeed a high-grade restoration elixir, the kind that cost more than an outer disciple's entire yearly allowance.

But something was wrong.

In his three centuries of cultivation, Jin Tian had learned one immutable truth: nothing in the cultivation world was free. Gifts always came with strings attached, and the ones that seemed too generous were usually poisoned.

He held the vial up to the light, examining it from every angle. The elixir was clear, with a faint golden shimmer that indicated its purity. If it was poisoned, the toxin was invisible to the naked eye.

Jin Tian corked the vial and tucked it into his robe.

He wouldn't use it. Not today, not ever. But keeping it might be useful. The mysterious inner disciple had shown his hand too quickly—a man who gave expensive gifts to strangers was a man with secrets, and secrets could be exploited.

The sun had begun its descent. Two hours remained.

Jin Tian returned to his meditation, but his mind was no longer focused on forms and techniques. He was thinking about the serpent he had just met, the one who slithered through the sect's shadows handing out favors like candy.

In his first life, he had never encountered this man. Either the man had died before Jin Tian rose to prominence, or he had hidden himself so well that even the Sword God never knew of his existence.

Either possibility was troubling.

But there was a third option, one that sent a chill down Jin Tian's spine: the man's existence was a change. A new variable introduced by Jin Tian's regression, a ripple in the river of fate caused by his return to the past.

If that was true, then the future he remembered was already crumbling. The seven disciples who betrayed him might not be the same ones this time. The alliances, the enmities, the pivotal moments—they could all shift, mutate, transform into something unrecognizable.

Jin Tian's hands trembled slightly.

He had been so focused on repeating his path, on identifying the traitors and preventing their betrayal, that he had forgotten the most dangerous truth of all: the past was not a script to be followed. It was a living thing, and his presence was an infection that would change everything it touched.

"Focus," he whispered to himself. "One battle at a time."

He rose and drew his sword again. The forms came easier now, his qi flowing smoother as his body remembered the patterns. He practiced for an hour, then another, until his robes were soaked with sweat and his muscles screamed for rest.

And then the bell tolled.

Three hours had passed. It was time.

The tournament pavilion had transformed while he was away. The scattered crowds of the morning had condensed into a dense mass around the central platform, where Elder Zhou stood with a scroll in his hands. Beside him, a young man in flame-red robes waited with obvious impatience.

Liu Feng.

He was younger than Jin Tian remembered—barely twenty, with a lean frame and eyes that burned with barely contained fire. His hair was pulled back in a warrior's knot, and his hands rested on the hilts of twin blades that hummed with latent heat.

When Liu Feng saw Jin Tian approach, he frowned.

"You're the one?" His voice was rough, dismissive. "I was expecting someone... bigger."

"Size isn't everything, Senior Brother," Jin Tian replied, stepping onto the platform. "Sometimes the sharpest blades come in small packages."

Liu Feng's frown deepened. "You talk too much for a boy who's about to get burned."

"Enough," Elder Zhou said, his voice carrying across the crowd. "This match follows standard tournament rules. No lethal strikes. Surrender is signaled by raising your hand. The match ends when one combatant yields or is incapacitated." He paused, looking between them. "Begin."

Liu Feng moved first.

Fire exploded from his blades as he launched forward, a wave of heat washing over the platform. His opening strike was a classic flame crescent—a sweeping arc of burning qi designed to force opponents back and set the tempo of the fight.

Jin Tian didn't move.

He stood perfectly still as the crescent of fire rushed toward him, counting the heartbeats, measuring the distance, watching the way Liu Feng's weight shifted just slightly to his left foot.

At the last possible moment, Jin Tian stepped forward.

Not back. Forward.

He ducked under the crescent, feeling the heat singe his hair, and drove his sword upward in a straight thrust aimed at Liu Feng's exposed armpit. It was a small target, a dishonorable strike, and completely unexpected.

Liu Feng's eyes widened. He twisted his body, barely deflecting the thrust with his off-hand blade, and leaped backward to create distance.

"Lucky," he snarled.

Jin Tian smiled. "Was it?"

They circled each other, and Jin Tian felt a familiar calm settle over him. The nervousness, the doubt, the fear of the unknown—all of it burned away in the heat of combat. Here, on the battlefield, he was home.

Liu Feng attacked again, this time with a flurry of strikes that came faster and faster, each one hotter than the last. Fire danced around his blades, leaving trails of smoke in the air. The crowd gasped and shouted, cheering for their favored inner disciple.

Jin Tian defended.

He didn't try to match Liu Feng's power. He couldn't. Instead, he moved like water, flowing around the attacks, deflecting them at angles that minimized their impact, redirecting the force rather than absorbing it. Every movement was precise,

title: The Serpent's Shadow
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The sun hung low in the afternoon sky, casting long shadows across the stone courtyard where outer disciples usually sparred. But today, the space belonged to him.

He needed it.

Three hours until he faced Liu Feng. Three hours to prepare for a battle against a man who had ended his life in another timeline.

Jin Tian closed his eyes and called upon the memories of that final moment. The cold steel sliding between his ribs. The shock of betrayal. And Liu Feng's face—not triumphant, not hateful, but *apologetic*. As if he had no choice.

*Why?*

The question had haunted Jin Tian through his first death and his second life. Liu Feng had never been one of his disciples. They had been peers, rivals, occasionally allies. There was no motive for murder.

Unless someone had ordered it.

Jin Tian drew his wooden sword and began the first form of the Verdant Willow technique. His movements were slow, deliberate, each position held with perfect precision. The technique was basic—foundation-level, taught to outer disciples as a way to build core strength and control.

But in Jin Tian's hands, it became something else.

He adjusted his stance by half an inch, tilted his wrist at a slightly different angle, and *breathed*. The qi in his dantian stirred, responding to his intent. Power flowed through his meridians, and the wooden sword hummed with energy.

*First form: Willow Bends to Wind.*

He swept the blade in an arc, and a whisper of sword light extended from the wood, carving a thin line across the stone floor ten feet away.

Satisfied, Jin Tian moved to the second form.

His body remembered what his cultivation level could not. At the seventh level of Qi Condensation, he could only channel a fraction of the power he once commanded. But technique was not about raw strength. Technique was about efficiency. About making every drop of qi count.

By the time he completed the tenth form, sweat was pouring down his face and his muscles screamed in protest. His cultivation base was too weak to sustain prolonged practice at this intensity. But he had learned what he needed.

The Verdant Willow technique had a hidden weakness. A moment of vulnerability between the sixth and seventh forms, when the practitioner's qi cycled back through their core. In a fight, that moment lasted less than a heartbeat.

Against a master, it was death.

But Liu Feng was not a master. Not yet. In his first life, Liu Feng had been a powerful inner disciple, but his true strength had come later, after years of cultivation. The Liu Feng Jin Tian would face today was still young, still growing, still making mistakes.

Jin Tian would find those mistakes. And he would exploit them.

"Impressive form."

The voice came from the shadows at the edge of the training ground. Jin Tian's hand tightened on his sword as he turned, every muscle coiled for combat.

A figure stepped into the light.

She was young—perhaps sixteen—with sharp features and eyes the color of autumn leaves. Her robes were those of an inner disciple, the silver trim marking her as a member of the Alchemy Pavilion. But there was nothing of the scholar or healer about her posture. She stood like a warrior, balanced and ready.

"You've been watching me," Jin Tian said. It was not a question.

"For the past hour." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I was curious about the outer disciple who dared challenge Chen Wei's position. And now I see why."

"I don't know you."

"No. But I know you, Jin Tian." She stepped closer, and Jin Tian noticed that her hands were empty. No weapons. No obvious threat. "I'm Lian Hua. Senior disciple of the Alchemy Pavilion. And I have a proposition for you."

"Speak quickly. I have a match to prepare for."

"Against Liu Feng." Lian Hua's smile widened. "Yes, I know. Everyone knows. The entire sect is buzzing about the audacious outer disciple who thinks he can compete with the inner circle." She paused. "Most think you'll die."

"And you?"

"I think you'll surprise them." She reached into her robe and produced a small jade bottle. "This is a Meridian Opening Pill. High-grade. It will temporarily boost your cultivation to the eighth level of Qi Condensation for the duration of a single fight."

Jin Tian's eyes narrowed. "What's the price?"

"The same thing everyone wants from you." Lian Hua tossed him the bottle, and he caught it reflexively. "Information. Specifically, how a twelve-year-old outer disciple learned to fight like a veteran of a hundred battles."

"I've trained hard—"

"Don't." Her voice sharpened. "I've been watching you for three days, Jin Tian. I saw you dismantle that Wei boy in the tournament. I saw you analyze your opponents' weaknesses before they even knew they had any. I saw you *know* things you shouldn't know." She stepped closer, close enough that he could smell the herbs on her breath. "You have a secret. I want to know what it is."

Jin Tian's mind raced. This was dangerous. Lian Hua was not like Chen Wei—she was observant, patient, and clearly intelligent. She had noticed inconsistencies that others had missed. If she dug deeper, she might uncover the truth.

But he needed the pill.

The Meridian Opening Pill was rare, even in his past life. It could mean the difference between winning and losing against Liu Feng. Between living and dying.

"I'll tell you one thing," Jin Tian said slowly. "I have a source of knowledge that no one else possesses. A spiritual inheritance from a forgotten master. That's how I know the techniques I know."

Lian Hua's eyes gleamed. "A inheritance? That's—"

"I won't tell you more until I survive my match." Jin Tian tucked the pill into his own robe. "But if I win, I'll consider sharing some of what I've learned."

"Consider sharing." She laughed, a brittle sound. "You drive a hard bargain for someone who might be dead in three hours."

"Then I'd better not die."

Lian Hua studied him for a long moment, then nodded. "Very well. But know this, Jin Tian: if you survive, I'll be watching. And I'm not the only one."

She turned and walked away, her footsteps echoing in the empty courtyard.

Jin Tian waited until she was gone before exhaling slowly. The Alchemy Pavilion. He had forgotten how political the sect was, even this early in its history. Every faction had its interests, its spies, its agents. And Lian Hua had just made it clear that he was now on their radar.

He pocketed the pill and began gathering his belongings.

*Time to find Liu Feng.*

---

The inner disciples' quarters were located on the eastern ridge of the mountain, overlooking the main training grounds. Jin Tian climbed the stone steps slowly, his body still aching from his earlier practice.

The Meridian Opening Pill sat heavy in his pocket. He could take it now, let its power surge through his meridians and prepare for battle. But that would waste its potency. Better to wait until just before the match.

A group of inner disciples passed him on the stairs, their conversation dying as they recognized him. He caught whispers of "the outer disciple" and "suicide mission" before they moved on.

He ignored them.

Liu Feng's quarters were easy to find. A simple wooden building at the end of a row of similar structures, marked by a small garden of fire-red flowers that seemed to pulse with ambient qi. Jin Tian approached the door and knocked.

Silence.

He knocked again. "Liu Feng. I'm Jin Tian. We have a match in—"

The door opened.

Liu Feng was younger than Jin Tian remembered. In his past life, Liu Feng had been a man in his prime, his face hardened by years of combat and cultivation. Now he was barely seventeen, his features still soft, his eyes lacking the coldness that would come with time.

But there was something else in those eyes. Something that made Jin Tian's hackles rise.

Fear.

"You're the outer disciple," Liu Feng said, his voice flat. "The one who took Chen Wei's slot."

"I am."

"Why are you here?"

Jin Tian met his gaze. "I wanted to see my opponent before the match. To understand who I'm fighting."

Liu Feng's jaw tightened. "You're fighting a dead man walking. That's who you're fighting." He stepped back, gesturing for Jin Tian to enter. "Come in. We shouldn't talk out here."

Jin Tian hesitated. Every instinct screamed at him to stay outside, to keep his distance. But he needed answers. He stepped through the doorway.

The interior of Liu Feng's quarters was sparse. A bed, a desk, a weapons rack holding a single steel sword. But Jin Tian's attention was drawn to the corner of the room, where a small altar had been set up.

On the altar was a portrait. A woman with kind eyes and a gentle smile. And beneath the portrait, a single word written in blood-red ink:

*Forgive.*

"You know what's going to happen tomorrow," Jin Tian said softly. "Don't you?"

Liu Feng's face went pale. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"Your mother." Jin Tian pointed at the portrait. "She's being held hostage. Someone is using her to control you."

Liu Feng's composure shattered. His hands began to tremble, and his voice cracked when he spoke. "How... how do you know that?"

"I know more than you think." Jin Tian's mind was racing, connecting dots he hadn't even known existed. In his past life, Liu Feng had killed him without explanation. But now, seeing the fear in the young man's eyes, he understood.

Liu Feng hadn't wanted to kill him. He had been forced.

"It's Chen Wei, isn't it?" Jin Tian pressed. "He's the one threatening your family."

"No." Liu Feng shook his head violently. "It's not him. It's someone above him. Someone in the inner circle." He grabbed Jin Tian's arm, his grip desperate. "Listen to me. You have to forfeit the match. If you don't, I'll have to—"

"Kill me?"

Liu Feng's silence was answer enough.

Jin Tian pulled his arm free. "I'm not forfeiting. And you're not going to kill me." He met the other boy's eyes. "I'm going to win this match. And then I'm going to find whoever is threatening your mother, and I'm going to end them."

"You can't—"

"I can." Jin Tian's voice carried the weight of three hundred years of experience. "I've faced worse than whatever puppet master is pulling your strings. Trust me, Liu Feng. I know what I'm doing."

For a long moment, Liu Feng just stared at him. Then, slowly, he nodded.

"One hour," he said. "The match is in one hour. If you survive, meet me at the Eastern Pavilion. I'll tell you everything I know."

Jin Tian turned and walked out of the quarters, his heart pounding.

The game had just gotten more complicated. Someone in the inner circle was pulling strings, manipulating disciples, and planning something that required Jin Tian's death. Chen Wei was involved, but he wasn't the mastermind. There was someone else. Someone with more power, more influence, and more to gain.

As Jin Tian descended the stairs toward the main arena, he caught a glimpse of white robes in the crowd below.

Elder Zhou stood at the center of the arena, his back to Jin Tian, speaking with another man in deep shadow. The conversation was tense, the elder's gestures sharp and angry.

title: The One Who Waited
content:
The tournament grounds hummed with anticipation as Jin Tian walked toward the inner division arena. The outer disciple compound had erupted after Elder Zhou's announcement, whispers following him like shadows as he passed. Some carried awe. Most carried doubt. A few—those who had wagered on Chen Wei—carried something far uglier.

He ignored them all.

His mind was fixed on Liu Feng. The seventh-ranked inner disciple. The man who had thrust a blade through his heart in a previous life, then vanished into the chaos of battle without explanation. Jin Tian had spent years assuming Liu Feng had been one of Chen Wei's pawns, a tool wielded from the shadows. But now, walking toward their first real confrontation, doubt clawed at that assumption.

Liu Feng had never been the scheming type. He was a fighter, pure and simple. Fire-aspected techniques that burned through defenses. A straightforward style that relied on overwhelming power rather than cunning. In Jin Tian's first life, they had fought seven times. Jin Tian had won four. Liu Feng had won three. Each battle had been brutal, honest, and without malice.

So why had Liu Feng killed him?

The inner division arena rose before him, a massive construct of white jade and reinforced formation arrays. Unlike the outer disciple platforms, this arena was enclosed by a barrier dome that could withstand attacks from Core Formation cultivators. The stands surrounding it held three thousand spectators, mostly inner disciples and elders, their murmuring voices creating a constant hum like disturbed beehives.

Jin Tian paused at the entrance.

Three hours. He had used them well. His meridians were primed, his qi circulating in patterns refined over three centuries of cultivation. The Four Season Sword Manual hummed beneath his skin, winter aspect dominant—the slow, patient cold that could freeze fire and temper steel.

He stepped through the gateway.

The noise died.

Three thousand faces turned toward him. Some showed curiosity. Some showed amusement. A few—the ones seated in the northern stands, where the senior inner disciples gathered—showed something that made Jin Tian's blood run cold.

Recognition.

Not of his name. Not of his face. But of his *presence*. The way certain cultivators straightened in their seats, their eyes narrowing as if they had been waiting for this moment. As if they knew something they shouldn't.

Jin Tian filed that observation away and walked to the center of the arena.

Liu Feng was already there.

He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader across the shoulders, his hair cropped short in the style of frontline fighters. A scar ran from his left temple to his jaw—a wound he hadn't possessed in Jin Tian's first life. That was new. That was *changed*.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice a low rumble. "I expected Chen Wei."

"Chen Wei was eliminated by scoring technicalities," Jin Tian replied. "I took his place."

Liu Feng's eyes flickered with something—amusement? Respect?—before settling into the flat, focused gaze of a warrior preparing for combat. "Technicalities. Right." He drew his blade, a crimson-edged saber that seemed to drink the light. "I suppose we'll see if you earned it."

The bell rang.

Liu Feng moved.

Fire erupted around him, not as techniques but as *breath*—flames that coiled around his arms and legs, propelling him forward with explosive speed. The saber came down in a diagonal arc, trailing heat so intense the air itself seemed to scream.

Jin Tian didn't block.

He sidestepped, the saber passing so close it singed his outer robe. His own sword—a standard-issue outer disciple blade—came up in a counter that forced Liu Feng to twist defensively.

*First exchange. Even.*

Liu Feng grinned. "Not bad."

He pressed forward, his style unfolding in a cascade of fire and steel. Each strike carried the weight of a cultivator who had trained for decades, who had refined his techniques through countless battles. The flames around him grew hotter, the saber faster, until the air shimmered and the barrier dome flickered from the heat.

Jin Tian weathered the storm.

He didn't fight back. He *observed*. Every movement, every shift in weight, every flicker of qi as Liu Feng cycled through his techniques. In his first life, Jin Tian had known Liu Feng's style intimately. But this version of Liu Feng was different—the scar, the slight hesitation in his left guard, the way he favored his right leg.

*Someone else has been training him*, Jin Tian realized. *Someone who changed his fundamentals.*

That raised a thousand questions. But now was not the time for questions. Now was the time for answers.

The battle continued for ten minutes. Twenty. The spectators grew restless, expecting a quick victory from the seventh-ranked inner disciple. Instead, they watched an outer disciple hold his ground, neither advancing nor retreating, simply *existing* in the space Liu Feng tried to claim.

Finally, Liu Feng stepped back.

"You're not attacking," he said, his voice flat.

"I'm learning."

"Learning what?"

Jin Tian met his eyes. "Why you killed me."

The words hung in the air. Liu Feng's expression didn't change, but his grip on the saber tightened—a micro-movement that only someone who had spent years studying him would notice.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Liu Feng said.

"Yes, you do."

The saber came up again, but slower this time. Liu Feng's eyes searched Jin Tian's face, looking for something. A trick. A hidden meaning. A trap.

"You're not him," Liu Feng finally said. "You're just a boy."

"I'm the boy you'll kill tomorrow," Jin Tian replied. "In another life. In this life, I'm the one who will stop you."

The words were a gamble. A dangerous one. If Liu Feng reported this conversation to the elders, Jin Tian would be questioned, possibly expelled, possibly executed for heresy. But Jin Tian had seen the recognition in the northern stands. He had seen the changed techniques. Something was happening beneath the surface of this tournament, something that connected to his death in the first life.

And Liu Feng was the thread he needed to pull.

The fire around Liu Feng guttered and died.

"Three hundred years," Liu Feng whispered, so quietly that only Jin Tian could hear. "You remember three hundred years."

Jin Tian's blood turned to ice.

"You know."

"I know what I was told." Liu Feng's voice was hollow now, stripped of all pretense. "The Seven Stars. The Betrayal. The *regression*." He shook his head slowly. "They said you would come back. They said you would remember. They said I would be the one to test you."

"Who said this?"

Liu Feng's eyes met his, and for the first time, Jin Tian saw something he had never seen in his old rival's face.

Fear.

"I can't tell you," Liu Feng said. "If I do, they'll kill me. Not quickly. Not cleanly." He raised his saber, the fire rekindling around it. "But I can show you."

He attacked.

This time, there was no hesitation. No testing. Liu Feng threw himself into the battle with the desperate fury of a cornered animal, his techniques wilder, faster, more unpredictable. The flames around him shifted from red to white, the temperature spiking so high that Jin Tian's outer robe began to smoke.

Jin Tian met the assault head-on.

His sword sang through the air, winter aspect fully unleashed. Cold against fire. Patience against fury. The clash sent shockwaves through the barrier dome, cracks spiderwebbing across its surface. The spectators gasped. The elders rose from their seats.

But Jin Tian saw only Liu Feng.

The saber came down. He blocked. The sword came up. He dodged. They circled each other, blades screaming, qi colliding, two warriors bound by a past that shouldn't exist and a future that hadn't happened yet.

In the chaos, Liu Feng spoke again.

"Forgive me," he whispered.

His saber shifted.

The blade that had been aiming for Jin Tian's chest suddenly redirected, plunging toward Liu Feng's own abdomen. The movement was too fast, too deliberate. Liu Feng's eyes were clear, his expression peaceful.

*He's trying to kill himself.*

Jin Tian's hand moved before his mind could catch up. His sword deflected the saber, the impact sending a shockwave through both their arms. The blade glanced off, carving a shallow gash across Liu Feng's side instead of plunging into his core.

"Stop!" Jin Tian shouted.

But Liu Feng was already falling to his knees, blood soaking his robes. His eyes found Jin Tian's one last time, and his lips formed three words that Jin Tian would carry with him forever.

*"They're already here."*

The barrier dome shattered.

A pressure descended on the arena, so vast and terrible that every cultivator—disciple and elder alike—froze in place. Jin Tian's knees buckled, his body screaming at him to submit, to kneel, to *die* rather than resist.

From the northern stands, a figure rose.

He was old. Ancient. His robes were the color of dried blood, his face a mask of wrinkles and scar tissue. His eyes—when they opened—held no pupils, only swirling darkness that seemed to drink the light itself.

Jin Tian knew that face.

In his first life, this man had been a legend. A ghost story told to frighten inner disciples. A demon cultivator who had terrorized the sect three hundred years before Jin Tian was born.

A man who should have been dead.

"Jin Tian," the ancient figure said, his voice echoing from everywhere and nowhere. "We've been waiting for you."

The darkness in his eyes swirled faster.

"Welcome back."

title: The Eleventh Disciple
content:
The preparation pavilion stood at the edge of the tournament grounds, its bamboo walls worn smooth by generations of nervous disciples. Jin Tian sat cross-legged on the wooden floor, his practice sword resting across his knees, breathing in the familiar scent of aged timber and dried blood.

Three hours.

In his first life, he had used those three hours to meditate on sword forms, to center his qi, to prepare his body for the match ahead. He had been confident then. Foolishly confident.

Now he used the time differently.

He closed his eyes and let his consciousness drift backward, past the memories of his death, past the centuries of cultivation, to the original tournament. The original match. Liu Feng had been ranked seventh among the inner disciples, a fire-aspected cultivator known for his ruthless aggression and complete lack of mercy.

But Jin Tian had never faced him in the outer division tournament. In his first life, Chen Wei had taken the slot, had won his first match against a lesser inner disciple, had advanced to face Liu Feng in the quarterfinals.

Chen Wei had lost. Badly.

The timeline had shifted. By taking the slot, Jin Tian had changed the sequence of events. Now he would face Liu Feng directly, three rounds earlier than their original encounter.

A knock on the door interrupted his meditation.

"Jin Tian." The voice was low, feminine, familiar.

He rose and opened the door. Standing before him was a girl of perhaps fourteen, her robes marking her as an outer disciple, her face plain but her eyes sharp with intelligence. She held a small leather pouch.

"You don't know me," she said, thrusting the pouch into his hands. "But I know you. Take this. Use it before your match."

Before he could respond, she turned and walked away, disappearing into the crowd of disciples preparing for their own matches.

Jin Tian opened the pouch. Inside was a single pill, pale blue and faintly luminous, etched with the symbol of a crescent moon.

He recognized it immediately. Moon Essence Pill. A rare alchemical treasure that temporarily enhanced qi circulation by thirty percent. Valuable enough that an outer disciple would never possess one legitimately.

And he recognized the symbol. The crescent moon was the personal mark of Elder Mei, the inner division's chief alchemist. A woman who, in his first life, had died three years from now under mysterious circumstances.

Why would an elder send him a pill? And why through a messenger who refused to be identified?

He tucked the pouch into his robes and returned to his meditation, his mind racing with possibilities. Was this a test? A trap? Or was someone else aware that the timeline had shifted?

---

The tournament arena was packed. Thousands of disciples lined the stone bleachers, their voices a constant roar that echoed off the surrounding mountains. At the center, the combat platform rose thirty feet above the ground, its surface inscribed with formation arrays that would absorb stray qi and prevent collateral damage.

Jin Tian climbed the stairs slowly, deliberately. He could feel the weight of countless eyes upon him—the outer disciples who saw him as an upstart, the inner disciples who viewed him as a curiosity, the elders who watched with calculating interest.

Across the platform, Liu Feng waited.

He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader in the shoulders, his face already hardened by years of combat training. His robes were the deep crimson of the inner division, and at his hip hung a sword that seemed to shimmer with heat, the blade itself appearing to drink the light.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I must admit, I was surprised when I heard my opponent had changed. I had prepared extensively for Chen Wei."

"Then you'll have to improvise," Jin Tian replied.

Liu Feng's lips quirked. "I enjoy improvisation."

The match began.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. A fire-aspected cultivator's greatest strength was initiation—controlling the flow of battle, forcing opponents onto the defensive, overwhelming them before they could establish their own rhythm.

A blade of pure flame erupted from Liu Feng's sword, arcing toward Jin Tian's chest with devastating speed.

Jin Tian didn't block.

He sidestepped, barely two inches to the left, letting the flame pass so close that he felt the heat singe his robes. In his first life, he had watched Liu Feng fight a dozen times. He knew every opening, every feint, every pattern of attack.

But knowing and surviving were different things.

Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly, but he recovered instantly, following with a diagonal slash that should have been impossible to dodge at this range.

Jin Tian caught it on his blade, but the force of the impact drove him back three steps, his feet sliding across the formation-inscribed stone. The difference in cultivation was immediately apparent. Liu Feng's qi was denser, more refined, his physical enhancements greater.

In a contest of pure strength, Jin Tian would lose.

But he had never fought with pure strength.

He shifted his stance, lowering his center of gravity, adjusting the angle of his blade by a fraction of a degree. In his mind, the Sword God's memories surfaced—techniques he had developed over centuries, movements that transcended mere martial skill and approached the realm of philosophy.

*The sword is not an extension of the arm. The arm is an extension of the sword.*

Liu Feng pressed forward, his attacks coming faster now, each strike carrying more heat, more force. The air around them shimmered with displaced qi, and the formation arrays on the platform began to glow brighter as they struggled to contain the energy.

Jin Tian gave ground, step by step, letting Liu Feng believe he was winning. He parried, dodged, deflected—never counterattacking, never committing to an offensive. He was learning. Memorizing. Every twitch of Liu Feng's shoulder told him when a strike was coming. Every shift of weight revealed the direction of the next attack.

And then he saw it.

A pattern. Subtle, almost invisible, but unmistakable once you knew what to look for. Every third combination ended with a feint to the left, followed by a thrust to the right. It was the kind of habit that would be punished at higher levels of cultivation, but at Liu Feng's current stage, it was effective.

Effective against anyone who hadn't been watching him for three hundred years.

Liu Feng completed his combination. The feint. The thrust.

Jin Tian was no longer there.

He had read the attack before it began, slipping inside Liu Feng's guard, his blade finding the gap between the inner disciple's ribs and his outstretched arm. The tip of his practice sword pressed against Liu Feng's side, exactly where the heart would be.

The arena fell silent.

Liu Feng froze, his eyes wide with disbelief. "How...?"

"Your pattern is predictable," Jin Tian said quietly, so only Liu Feng could hear. "Every third combination. You rely on it too heavily."

Liu Feng's face cycled through emotions—shock, anger, and finally, grudging respect. "You studied me."

"I studied everyone."

The match overseer raised his hand. "Winner: Outer Disciple Jin Tian!"

The crowd erupted. Not cheers, exactly, but a cacophony of surprise and speculation. An outer disciple had just defeated the seventh-ranked inner disciple in under three minutes. It was unprecedented.

But Jin Tian wasn't celebrating.

He was watching Liu Feng's face. Waiting for the moment of recognition, the flash of familiarity that would confirm what he suspected—that Liu Feng had known him before, in the future that no longer existed.

Instead, he saw something else.

Liu Feng's lips moved, forming words that the crowd couldn't hear, but that Jin Tian's enhanced senses caught perfectly.

"The eleventh disciple sends his regards."

A chill ran down Jin Tian's spine.

The eleventh disciple. In his first life, he had taken seven disciples. But there had been others—candidates who had been tested and rejected, students who had failed his trials, aspirants who had been deemed unworthy.

He had never thought about them again.

Until now.

"What do you know about the eleventh disciple?" Jin Tian demanded, grabbing Liu Feng's arm.

But Liu Feng only smiled, a cold, knowing expression that didn't reach his eyes. "Ask the alchemist who sent you the pill. If you survive that long."

The overseer stepped between them, separating the two disciples. "The match is over. Both of you, leave the platform."

Jin Tian descended the stairs in a daze, his mind racing. The eleventh disciple. Someone he had rejected, someone who held a grudge that had spanned three centuries. Someone who had found a way to reach across time, to influence events before they happened.

He reached the bottom of the stairs and found himself face to face with Chen Wei.

The young noble's face was pale, his hands trembling. "You... you actually won."

"Move aside," Jin Tian said flatly.

"Listen to me." Chen Wei's voice dropped to a whisper. "I don't know what game you're playing, but you've made powerful enemies today. Liu Feng was supposed to win that match. There were bets placed, arrangements made. You've cost people a lot of money."

"I don't care about your gambling schemes."

"Then care about this." Chen Wei grabbed Jin Tian's arm, his grip surprisingly strong. "After the tournament, there's a gathering. The inner disciples host it every year to welcome the new outer division champion. You'll be expected to attend."

"So?"

"So don't eat anything. Don't drink anything. And whatever you do, don't accept any gifts."

Chen Wei released his grip and walked away, disappearing into the crowd before Jin Tian could respond.

Jin Tian stood alone at the edge of the arena, the Moon Essence Pill heavy in his pocket, Liu Feng's warning echoing in his mind, and Chen Wei's cryptic words burning in his ears.

The eleventh disciple.

The alchemist.

The poisoned gathering.

Three threads, all converging on him at once. In his first life, he had walked through this tournament blind, unaware of the conspiracies forming around him. Now he saw them everywhere, a web of intrigue that stretched across decades and involved people he had never suspected.

He looked up at the mountain peak where the sect elder's hall stood, and for the first time since his regression, he felt something approaching genuine fear.

Not of his enemies.

But of the possibility that his enemies had been planning this for far longer than he had been alive to stop them.

title: Chapter 11
content:
# CHAPTER 11: The Truth Behind the Blades

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. Good. He needed silence, needed to think without the constant pressure of watching eyes and judging whispers.

He found a secluded corner near the meditation gardens, where ancient bamboo swayed in the afternoon breeze. Three hours until his match against Liu Feng. Three hours to prepare for a confrontation that would change everything.

But preparation wasn't about techniques or forms. He had mastered those centuries ago. What he needed now was information.

He sat cross-legged on the smooth stone, letting his consciousness drift back through the fog of his previous life. Liu Feng. Ranked seventh among inner disciples. Fire-aspected techniques. Aggressive combat style. Known for his explosive temper and unwavering loyalty to the sect.

*Loyalty.*

The word tasted bitter now. Liu Feng's blade had pierced his heart with surgical precision—one clean thrust between the ribs, angled upward to rupture the aorta. A killing strike delivered by someone who knew exactly where to place a sword.

Jin Tian had replayed that moment ten thousand times in the darkness between lives. The betrayal of his seven disciples had been expected, in a way. He had seen the cracks forming, the jealousy festering, the ambition growing like rot in a once-healthy tree.

But Liu Feng? The man had been a loyal inner disciple for thirty years. He had never sought advancement beyond his station, never complained about assignments, never shown even a hint of disloyalty.

*Why?*

The question gnawed at him as he opened his eyes. The bamboo swayed, casting dancing shadows across his face.

"I know you're there," he said quietly.

A moment of silence. Then footsteps, hesitant at first, then growing confident as they approached.

Elder Zhou emerged from behind a cluster of bamboo, his weathered face unreadable. He was alone, which was unusual. Elders rarely traveled without attendants.

"You have sharp senses, boy," Elder Zhou said, stopping a few paces away. "Most outer disciples wouldn't detect my presence until I was within arm's reach."

"Most outer disciples haven't spent their entire lives learning to read the flow of qi," Jin Tian replied, keeping his voice neutral. "What can I do for you, Elder?"

Elder Zhou studied him for a long moment, his eyes seeming to pierce through flesh and bone to the soul beneath. "You remind me of someone," he said finally. "Someone I knew a long time ago. He had the same stillness about him, the same sense of watching and waiting."

Jin Tian's heart rate didn't change. He had learned long ago to control his body's reactions. "I'm sure I don't know who you mean."

"Perhaps not." Elder Zhou walked closer, his robes brushing against the fallen leaves. "But I find it curious that a twelve-year-old outer disciple would know the inner workings of the sect's scoring system well enough to calculate efficiency ratings on the fly. I find it even more curious that he would know Liu Feng's combat style and weaknesses well enough to challenge him directly."

*Careful,* Jin Tian warned himself. *He's fishing.*

"Liu Feng is famous among the outer disciples," he said, keeping his voice light. "His matches are frequently discussed. It's not difficult to find patterns in his fighting style if one pays attention."

"And you pay attention." It wasn't a question.

"Always."

Elder Zhou nodded slowly. "Good. That's good." He paused, and when he spoke again, his voice had dropped to barely a whisper. "Because there's something you need to know about Liu Feng before your match."

Jin Tian's pulse finally quickened. "What?"

"Liu Feng has been acting strangely for the past three months. His behavior has changed. He's become secretive, meeting with people outside the sect, receiving correspondence from unknown sources." Elder Zhou's eyes hardened. "I've been watching him, but I haven't been able to determine who he's working with."

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow. Three months ago. That was when he had returned to this life. That was when everything had changed.

*Could Liu Feng have sensed something? Could someone else have sensed something?*

"He's my spy," Elder Zhou continued, and Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "I recruited him six months ago to investigate a corruption ring within the sect. But three months ago, he stopped reporting. He claimed his investigations had hit a dead end, but I know him better than that. He was afraid."

"Afraid of what?"

"Afraid of what he found." Elder Zhou's face was grim. "And now he's been assigned to fight you in a match that he cannot refuse, against an opponent he has no reason to harm. Unless someone is forcing his hand."

The pieces clicked into place with terrible clarity. Liu Feng hadn't been a traitor in Jin Tian's first life. He had been a pawn. A tool used by someone else to deliver a killing blow without revealing their own involvement.

*But who?*

"The match," Jin Tian said slowly. "It wasn't random, was it?"

Elder Zhou's silence was answer enough.

"I pulled strings," the elder admitted. "I needed someone in that match who could observe Liu Feng up close, someone who might be able to determine what's wrong with him. When you challenged Chen Wei, I saw an opportunity."

"You used me."

"I gave you an opportunity." Elder Zhou's voice hardened. "The same opportunity I've been trying to give you since I first noticed you. You're not an ordinary outer disciple, Jin Tian. I don't know what your secret is, and frankly, I don't care. But I know talent when I see it, and I know that you have the potential to become something greater than anyone in this sect expects."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then the corruption continues. The people behind it remain hidden. And Liu Feng remains a puppet dancing on strings that someone else controls." Elder Zhou met his eyes. "Or you can use this match to cut those strings."

Jin Tian's mind raced. This changed everything. Liu Feng wasn't the enemy—he was a victim, just as Jin Tian had been. And somewhere in the shadows of the sect, someone was pulling strings, manipulating events, preparing for something that Jin Tian still couldn't see.

The question was: who?

He thought back to his first life. The seven disciples who had betrayed him. The carefully orchestrated plan that had led to his death. The way everyone had been in exactly the right place at exactly the right time.

*It wasn't random. It was never random.*

"One condition," he said finally.

Elder Zhou raised an eyebrow. "Name it."

"After the match, regardless of the outcome, you tell me everything you know about the corruption ring. Names, dates, locations, everything." Jin Tian's voice was steel. "No more secrets. No more manipulation."

For a long moment, Elder Zhou said nothing. Then he nodded, a strange smile playing at the corners of his mouth. "Agreed."

He turned to leave, then paused. "One more thing, boy. Liu Feng has been poisoned. A slow-acting toxin that clouds the mind and heightens aggression. He doesn't know it, but it's been building in his system for weeks."

"He's being controlled."

"Exactly." Elder Zhou's voice faded as he walked away. "So when you face him in that ring, remember: you're not fighting Liu Feng. You're fighting whoever put that poison in his blood."

Jin Tian sat alone in the bamboo grove, the weight of new knowledge pressing down on him.

*Someone is moving pieces on a board I can't see. Someone planned this across two lifetimes.*

He rose, stretching his limbs, feeling the qi flow through his meridians like a river of molten gold.

*But they made one mistake. They put me on this board.*

He walked toward the arena, his footsteps steady, his mind clear.

*And this time, I'm not just a piece. I'm the one who flips the board.*

---

The crowd was enormous.

Word had spread quickly through the sect: an outer disciple had challenged Chen Wei for a spot in the inner division tournament, and now that same disciple was facing Inner Disciple Liu Feng in the opening match.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the arena, feeling the weight of hundreds of eyes upon him. The outer disciples watched with a mixture of hope and jealousy. The inner disciples watched with cold curiosity. The elders watched with calculating interest.

And somewhere in that crowd, the person responsible for poisoning Liu Feng was watching too.

The gong sounded.

Liu Feng entered from the opposite side of the arena, his face a mask of controlled fury. He moved with the predatory grace of a seasoned fighter, his robes billowing around him like flames given form.

Jin Tian studied him closely. There it was—a slight tremor in his left hand, a flicker of hesitation in his step. Signs that only someone who had spent centuries observing combat would notice.

*The poison is already affecting him.*

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the arena. "I must admit, I didn't expect to face someone so young in my first match."

"And I didn't expect to face someone so accomplished," Jin Tian replied, bowing respectfully. "I hope we can make this a match worthy of the sect's attention."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. For just a moment, Jin Tian saw something flicker in them—recognition? Desperation? It was gone before he could identify it.

"Let us begin," Liu Feng said.

The referee raised his hand. "Begin!"

Liu Feng exploded forward, flames wreathing his blade as he launched a devastating overhead strike. The crowd gasped at the speed and power of the attack.

Jin Tian didn't move.

He watched the blade descend, watched the flames twist and dance, watched the subtle shift in Liu Feng's stance that revealed the truth hiding beneath the aggressive facade.

*He's trying to lose.*

The realization struck Jin Tian like lightning. Liu Feng was pouring all his power into flashy, overwhelming attacks—attacks that should have been impossible to dodge. But his targeting was off. His strikes were landing exactly where an experienced fighter would move to avoid them.

*He's giving me an opening. He wants me to win.*

But why? If someone had poisoned him, why would he sabotage his own performance?

Unless he knew. Unless he had figured out what was happening to him and was trying to fight back in the only way he could.

Jin Tian sidestepped the blazing sword, feeling the heat singe his robes. He drew his own blade, a simple iron sword that felt like an extension of his soul.

"Impressive," he said, loud enough for the crowd to hear. "But I've seen this attack before. You always follow it with a horizontal sweep."

Liu Feng's eyes widened—whether in surprise at the prediction or realization that Jin Tian was playing along, he couldn't tell.

"Let's see if you can dodge both," Liu Feng growled, and launched into the exact combination Jin Tian had predicted.

It was a dance now. A carefully choreographed performance where both fighters knew the steps but neither could acknowledge it openly.

They exchanged blows, sparks flying, qi crackling through the air. Jin Tian matched Liu Feng strike for strike, never pushing too hard, never pulling too far back. He was giving the poisoner exactly what they wanted to see—a close match, an evenly contested battle.

*But I need more time. I need to see who in the crowd is watching too closely.*

He risked a glance at the elders' platform. Elder Zhou sat stone-faced, his eyes never leaving Liu Feng. But next to him, another elder was leaning forward, his fingers drumming against his knee in a steady rhythm.

*Elder Wu.* The head of the inner disciple training division. A man known for his strict

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Serpent's Other Fang

The match arena hummed with anticipation. Jin Tian stood at the edge of the raised platform, his wooden practice sword resting across his shoulders as he breathed slowly, deliberately, centering himself. The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the training grounds, and the crowd of disciples had grown three times larger than usual. Word had spread. The outer disciple who had dared to challenge Chen Wei's slot was about to face Inner Disciple Liu Feng.

*Three hours.* He had used them well.

His meridians still ached from the rapid circulation technique he had employed, forcing Qi through pathways that had been dormant for too long. In his first life, he had spent decades refining his foundation. Now, trapped in a twelve-year-old body with only a fraction of his former cultivation, he had to compensate with technique and memory.

He rolled his shoulders, feeling the subtle shift in his Qi flow. It was enough. It had to be.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," the announcer called, his voice carrying across the grounds. "Your opponent: Inner Disciple Liu Feng, ranked seventh in the inner division tournament."

The crowd parted.

Liu Feng walked forward with the casual confidence of a predator who had never known defeat. He was seventeen, tall for his age, with close-cropped black hair and eyes that burned like embers. His robes were the deep crimson of the inner disciples, embroidered with the fire crest of his clan. A real sword hung at his hip—not practice steel, but true forged iron, the blade dark with a faint red sheen.

In his first life, Jin Tian had admired that sword. Now he recognized it for what it was: a tool of execution.

"Little outer disciple," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying a lazy amusement. "You've got guts. I'll give you that. But guts don't win matches."

Jin Tian stepped onto the platform. "Neither does arrogance."

The crowd hissed. Liu Feng's eyes narrowed, the embers within them flickering with genuine interest. "Oh? You've got teeth too." He drew his sword, the blade catching the light and casting crimson reflections across the stone. "Let's see how many I have to pull before you learn your place."

Elder Zhou raised his hand. "The match begins when the bell sounds. Standard rules: no killing, no crippling techniques. Victory by submission, ring-out, or incapacitation." He paused, his eyes lingering on Jin Tian. "Begin."

The bell rang.

Liu Feng moved first, as expected. He exploded forward with a burst of fire-aspected Qi, his blade trailing a curtain of heat that made the air shimmer. It was a testing strike—fast, aggressive, but not his true power.

In his first life, Jin Tian had tried to block it. His practice sword had shattered, and the match had ended in seconds.

This time, he sidestepped.

The blade passed inches from his chest, the heat washing over him like a furnace breath. Jin Tian didn't counter. He flowed with the movement, circling to Liu Feng's flank, his practice sword held low and ready.

Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly. "Lucky dodge."

"It wasn't luck."

Liu Feng attacked again, faster this time. A combination strike—horizontal slash followed by a reverse grip thrust, the blade spinning in his hands with practiced ease. The fire Qi intensified, leaving trails of smoke in the air.

Jin Tian's body moved on instinct honed over centuries. He ducked under the horizontal slash, pivoted on his heel, and brought his practice sword up in a tight arc that deflected the thrust just enough to send it wide. The motion was minimal, efficient, using Liu Feng's own momentum against him.

The older disciple stumbled, caught off balance for just a fraction of a second.

Jin Tian struck.

His practice sword slammed into Liu Feng's ribs with a crack that echoed across the training grounds. Not enough to break bone, but enough to drive the air from his lungs.

Liu Feng staggered back, one hand clutching his side, his eyes burning with shock and fury. "You—"

"I know your style," Jin Tian said quietly. "Blazing Sun Sword Technique. Fourteen forms, each building on the last. You're strongest in the seventh form, where you combine fire Qi with a downward diagonal slash. Weakest in the eleventh, where the pivot leaves your right flank exposed for half a breath."

The crowd went silent.

Liu Feng's face drained of color, then flushed crimson. "How do you know that?"

*Because I watched you practice it for three years in my last life,* Jin Tian thought. *Because you used that very form to kill me.*

"I pay attention," he said instead.

Liu Feng snarled and lunged, abandoning technique for raw aggression. His sword became a blur of crimson light, each strike faster and more desperate than the last. Fire Qi erupted around him in waves, the temperature rising until the stones beneath their feet began to smoke.

Jin Tian retreated, deflecting and dodging, conserving his strength. He didn't need to win with power. He needed to win with patience.

Liu Feng's attacks grew wilder, his breathing ragged. The seventh form came—the downward diagonal slash—and Jin Tian was ready. He stepped into the attack instead of away from it, bringing his practice sword up to catch the blade at an angle, redirecting its force harmlessly past his shoulder.

Then he drove his knee into Liu Feng's stomach.

The older disciple doubled over, his sword clattering to the stone. Jin Tian followed with a strike to the wrist, then the elbow, each blow precise and economical, targeting pressure points that would numb the arm without permanent damage.

Liu Feng collapsed to his knees, his right arm hanging uselessly at his side.

"I yield," he gasped, the words tasting like ash.

The crowd erupted. Outer disciples cheered, inner disciples stared in disbelief, and somewhere in the back, Jin Tian could hear Chen Wei's voice, sharp and furious, demanding answers.

Jin Tian didn't celebrate. He stood over Liu Feng, watching the older disciple's face, waiting for the mask to slip.

It did.

Liu Feng looked up, and for just a moment, his eyes held something other than defeat. They held recognition. And fear.

"You're not supposed to be here," he whispered.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "What do you mean?"

Liu Feng's lips curled into a bitter smile. "You think you've won something today, little outer disciple? You've just painted a target on your back." He struggled to his feet, cradling his injured arm. "They'll come for you now. They'll all come."

"Who will?"

But Liu Feng was already walking away, his shoulders hunched, his defeat absolute.

Elder Zhou approached, his expression unreadable. "Impressive victory, Jin Tian. You've earned a place in the inner division tournament." He paused. "But you've also earned enemies. Be careful."

"I know."

As he stepped off the platform, the crowd parted around him like water around a stone. Some clapped his back. Others glared with undisguised hostility. He ignored them all, his mind racing.

*They'll come for you now.*

Who were "they"? Liu Feng was a tool, not a master. Someone had put him up to the murder in Jin Tian's first life, and someone was pulling strings now.

He needed answers. And he knew where to start looking.

The inner division archives were restricted to disciples who had passed the tournament, but Jin Tian had spent decades navigating restricted areas in his previous life. He knew the patrol schedules, the blind spots, the weaknesses in the formation arrays that protected the scrolls.

Three hours after his match, as the moon rose over the sect, he slipped through the shadows and into the archive building.

The scrolls were organized by cultivation technique, by clan affiliation, by historical significance. Jin Tian ignored all of them. He was looking for something specific: a name. A record. A clue.

He found it in a dusty corner, hidden behind a false wall that only someone who had lived three hundred years would think to check.

A list. Names of inner disciples, arranged by seniority. Beside each name, a mark—a serpent coiled around a blade.

The mark of the Shadow Serpent Sect.

Jin Tian's hands trembled as he counted. Twelve names. Seven he recognized as his future betrayers. Five he had never suspected.

And at the bottom, written in fresh ink, a name that made his blood freeze.

*Jin Tian.*

His own name, marked with a serpent coiled around a blade.

They already knew about him. They had already marked him. The tournament, the challenge, the victory over Liu Feng—it had all been watched, anticipated, planned for.

He was not hunting the Shadow Serpent Sect.

They were hunting him.

A sound behind him. Soft. Deliberate.

Jin Tian turned, his practice sword coming up, his heart hammering against his ribs.

A figure stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the moonlit corridor. Young. Familiar.

Chen Wei.

"I was wondering when you'd find it," Chen Wei said, his voice carrying none of the arrogance from earlier. It was cold, measured, *old*. "I left it here for you. A gift."

"Chen Wei." Jin Tian's grip tightened on his sword. "You're one of them."

"I'm more than that." Chen Wei stepped forward, and the moonlight caught his face. His eyes had changed. They were no longer the eyes of a seventeen-year-old outer disciple.

They were the eyes of someone who had lived just as long as Jin Tian.

"Hello, old friend," Chen Wei said, and his voice shifted, deepening, taking on a resonance that Jin Tian recognized with dawning horror. "Or should I say... *Old Master*."

The world tilted.

Jin Tian's mind raced, memories colliding, truths shattering. The betrayal. The murder. The seven disciples who had struck him down.

Chen Wei had not been among them.

But the voice. The voice was familiar.

"Zhang Chen," Jin Tian whispered.

Chen Wei smiled, and it was the smile of a serpent. "I wondered how long it would take you to remember. Three centuries of cultivation, and you never once suspected your most loyal disciple, did you?"

"How?" The word escaped before Jin Tian could stop it.

"How did I follow you through death? How did I end up in this body?" Chen Wei—*Zhang Chen*—spread his hands. "You're not the only one who was betrayed, Master. You're not the only one who was given a second chance."

He stepped closer, and Jin Tian saw the truth in his eyes. The same weariness. The same hunger. The same burning, endless hatred.

"I died too," Zhang Chen said softly. "They killed me three years after you. And I woke up in this body, with all my memories intact, just like you." He laughed, and the sound was broken glass. "The universe has a sense of humor, doesn't it?"

"Why are you here?" Jin Tian's voice steadied. He had faced worse. He had survived worse. "Why reveal yourself now?"

"Because the game is about to change." Zhang Chen's smile faded, replaced by something grim. "The Shadow Serpent Sect isn't just a faction within the sect, Master. It's the *entire sect*. The elders. The patriarch. Everyone above a certain rank has been corrupted for generations."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "All of them?"

"All of them." Zhang Chen met his eyes. "And they know you're here. They know who you are. They've known since the day you woke up in this body."

The archive felt like a tomb.
