"There's only one way out," Zhang Chen continued. "One way to survive. You need to win the tournament. Not just win

title: Chapter 13
content:
# Chapter 13: The Mask Begins to Crack

The arena stones still carried the echo of Jin Tian's victory over Liu Feng.

Three days had passed since that match, and the whispers hadn't died down. An outer disciple defeating a rank-seven inner disciple was unprecedented. An outer disciple *surviving* a deathmatch with Liu Feng was impossible. And yet Jin Tian had done both.

He sat cross-legged in his quarters, the evening light painting shadows across the walls. His body ached from the constant training, but his mind was sharper than ever. The pieces were beginning to fall into place.

Liu Feng's attack pattern had been exactly as Jin Tian remembered from his first life—the same opening sequence, the same feints, the same killing blow that had pierced his heart three hundred years ago. But there had been something different in Liu Feng's eyes. Not rage. Not rivalry.

*Fear.*

As if Liu Feng knew something he shouldn't. As if he was following orders.

A knock at the door shattered his concentration.

"Enter," Jin Tian said, rising to his feet.

The door slid open to reveal a young girl—perhaps fourteen, with sharp features and hair the color of autumn leaves. She wore the gray robes of an outer disciple, but her bearing was anything but common. Her eyes swept the room before settling on Jin Tian.

"You're the one who killed Liu Feng," she said. Not a question.

"I defeated him. He's recovering in the infirmary."

The girl snorted. "Recovering. Right. The healers say his core is shattered. He'll never cultivate again." She stepped inside without waiting for permission. "I'm Yue Lin. I've been watching you."

Jin Tian's hand drifted toward his sword. "Should I be concerned?"

"Depends. Are you planning to die in the next tournament match?" She tossed a scroll onto his bed. "Because if you are, I've wasted my time finding you."

He didn't touch the scroll. "Who sent you?"

"No one. I sent myself." Yue Lin crossed her arms. "I've been gathering information on the inner disciples for two years. Patterns. Alliances. Secret dealings. I know who's plotting against whom, who's skimming resources, who's making deals they shouldn't." She paused. "And I know that your match against Liu Feng wasn't random."

Jin Tian's blood went cold. "Explain."

"He was paid to eliminate you. Someone approached him three days before the tournament, offered him a high-grade spirit pill and a favor from a core disciple. All he had to do was make your death look like an accident during the match." Yue Lin's eyes never left his face. "But you weren't supposed to win. You were supposed to die."

"Who paid him?"

"That's where it gets interesting." She unrolled the scroll, revealing a complex web of names and connections. Lines stretched between disciples, elders, and factions within the sect. At the center sat a single name.

*Chen Wei.*

Jin Tian's jaw tightened. "Chen Wei is an outer disciple. He doesn't have the resources to bribe an inner disciple."

"Normally, no. But Chen Wei isn't working alone." Yue Lin pointed to a cluster of names branching from his. "He's connected to at least three core disciples, two elders, and someone outside the sect entirely. I don't know who that someone is yet, but the money trail leads beyond Cloud Peak Sect's borders."

This was new. In his first life, Chen Wei had been a schemer, but he'd worked within the sect's power structure. Outside connections meant something far more dangerous.

"Why are you telling me this?" Jin Tian asked.

"Because I want something in return. There's a competition next month—the Hidden Dragon Trials. Only inner disciples are eligible, but there's a loophole. If an outer disciple defeats three inner disciples in official matches, they can claim a spot." Her eyes glinted. "I've defeated two. I need a third. And I need someone who can teach me how to counter fire-aspected techniques. Liu Feng was fire-aspected, and you dismantled him like he was a child."

"You want a training partner."

"I want a *teacher*. There's a difference." Yue Lin rolled up the scroll. "Help me prepare for my third match, and I'll give you everything I know about Chen Wei's network. Deal?"

Jin Tian studied her. She was young, ambitious, and clearly intelligent. But she was also dangerous—the kind of person who gathered information as a weapon rather than a tool. In his first life, he'd trusted someone like her once.

It had ended with a knife in his back.

"One condition," he said. "You tell me everything now. The training comes after."

Yue Lin's smile was sharp. "You're a suspicious one, aren't you?" She tossed the scroll to him. "Fine. But I warn you—what I've found isn't pretty."

---

They met at midnight in the meditation grove, hidden from prying eyes by formation arrays Yue Lin had apparently set herself. The moonlight filtered through the leaves, casting silver patterns on the grass.

"There are three names you need to know," Yue Lin began, spreading a new scroll across a flat stone. "First: Elder Feng. He's the one who approved the tournament brackets. Your match against Liu Feng went through his office."

"Elder Feng is one of the Seven Elders. He's been with the sect for forty years."

"Exactly. Which means his corruption runs deep." She pointed to the second name. "Second: Core Disciple Zhao Ming. Ranked fourth among the inner disciples, but his reach extends far beyond the ranks. He's the one who provided the spirit pill to Liu Feng."

Jin Tian's mind raced. Zhao Ming. In his first life, Zhao Ming had been a neutral party—powerful, but uninvolved in sect politics. He'd survived the betrayal because he'd stayed out of it.

Or so Jin Tian had thought.

"And the third?"

Yue Lin hesitated. For the first time, she looked uncertain. "This is where it gets complicated. The third person is someone you know. Someone close to you."

Jin Tian's stomach dropped. "Who?"

"Your friend. Lin Chen."

The name hit him like a physical blow. Lin Chen had been his closest ally in the outer sect, the one person who had stood by him when others mocked his cultivation speed. They had trained together, eaten together, shared their dreams of becoming inner disciples together.

In his first life, Lin Chen had died in the betrayal. Killed by one of the seven disciples who had turned against Jin Tian.

But this time, things were different.

"Lin Chen doesn't have the resources to—"

"He doesn't need resources. He has leverage." Yue Lin's voice was grim. "Someone has something on him. I don't know what, but I've seen the meetings. Late at night, in the eastern training grounds. Lin Chen meets with Chen Wei's agents, passes information, receives instructions."

Jin Tian's hands clenched into fists. *No. Not Lin Chen. Anyone but him.*

"When does he meet them next?"

"Tomorrow. After the evening bell." Yue Lin met his eyes. "If you want proof, I can take you there. But once you see it, you can't unsee it."

---

The eastern training grounds were deserted at this hour. The moon hung low, casting long shadows across the stone floor. Jin Tian crouched behind a crumbling wall, Yue Lin pressed close beside him.

"Any moment now," she whispered.

They waited. The wind carried the scent of pine and distant rain. Jin Tian's heart hammered against his ribs, but his breathing remained steady.

Then he saw them.

Two figures emerged from the shadows—one tall and broad, the other slender and familiar. The tall figure wore an inner disciple's robes. The slender one wore gray, the same as Jin Tian.

Lin Chen.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold as he watched his friend approach the inner disciple. They spoke in low voices, too quiet to hear, but the body language was unmistakable. Lin Chen was deferential. Submissive. He nodded at something the inner disciple said, then reached into his sleeve and produced a small jade token.

The inner disciple took it, inspected it, then nodded. He said something that made Lin Chen's shoulders sag with relief.

Then the inner disciple turned, and Jin Tian saw his face clearly for the first time.

It was Zhao Ming.

"I told you," Yue Lin breathed. "Lin Chen is compromised."

But Jin Tian wasn't looking at Lin Chen anymore. He was looking at the way Zhao Ming held himself, the way his hand rested on his sword, the way his eyes swept the training grounds with practiced paranoia.

In his first life, Zhao Ming had been a neutral party who had survived the betrayal.

But now, watching him exchange tokens with his closest friend, Jin Tian realized the truth.

Zhao Ming hadn't survived the betrayal.

He had been *part of it*.

And Lin Chen—the friend who had died saving him, the brother who had bled out in his arms—Lin Chen had been working for the enemy all along.

The world tilted. Everything Jin Tian thought he knew crumbled like ash.

He stood up.

"What are you doing?" Yue Lin hissed, grabbing his arm. "They'll see you!"

"I know."

He stepped out from behind the wall.

Zhao Ming saw him first. His hand flew to his sword, his body dropping into a combat stance. Lin Chen spun around, his face going pale as death.

"Jin Tian," Lin Chen breathed. "I can explain—"

"You don't need to." Jin Tian's voice was hollow. "I saw everything."

Lin Chen's face crumbled. Tears welled in his eyes. "They have my family. My mother, my sister. They said they'd kill them if I didn't cooperate."

"And so you fed me to them."

"I never wanted to hurt you! I just—I had no choice—"

"There's always a choice." Jin Tian drew his sword. The blade caught the moonlight, gleaming like a shard of ice. "You chose them over me."

Zhao Ming laughed, a cold, ugly sound. "How touching. The betrayed friend discovers the truth." He drew his own blade. "But since you're here, I suppose we'll have to handle this differently."

He lunged.

Jin Tian met him halfway.

Their blades clashed, sending sparks flying into the night. Zhao Ming was strong—stronger than Liu Feng, stronger than any outer disciple should be able to match. But Jin Tian had three hundred years of experience, and he knew exactly where Zhao Ming's technique faltered.

*There.* A microsecond of hesitation in his left guard.

Jin Tian's blade flickered, bypassing Zhao Ming's defense entirely, and punched through his shoulder.

Zhao Ming screamed.

But before Jin Tian could press the advantage, a voice rang out from the darkness.

"Enough."

Elder Feng stepped into the light. His face was unreadable, his hands clasped behind his back. Behind him stood a dozen inner disciples, all armed, all watching Jin Tian with cold eyes.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Elder Feng said, his voice carrying the weight of judgment. "You are hereby charged with attacking a core disciple without provocation, conspiring with forbidden powers, and treason against Cloud Peak Sect."

Lin Chen's face went white. "No! Elder, please—he was defending himself—"

"Silence." Elder Feng's eyes never left Jin Tian. "You will be taken to the Judgment Hall. Your cultivation will be sealed. And then, you will tell us everything you know about the demon sect you serve."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

Demon sect.

They weren't just trying to kill him.

They were trying to make him the villain.

title: The Scorpion's Venom
content:
The training grounds emptied in a matter of breaths, leaving Jin Tian alone with the weight of Elder Zhou's announcement still pressing against his chest. Three hours. One hundred and eighty minutes to prepare for an opponent who had already killed him once.

He walked slowly toward the meditation pavilion at the edge of the outer disciple compound, his mind racing through every possible scenario. In his first life, Liu Feng had been ranked seventh among the inner disciples, but that ranking had been three years from now. The current Liu Feng would be weaker, less experienced, his techniques still in their formative stages.

But Jin Tian was also weaker. A twelve-year-old body with barely enough qi to sustain a single battle, let alone the prolonged exchanges that inner disciples specialized in.

He needed an advantage.

The pavilion stood empty, its stone floor worn smooth by generations of disciples seeking clarity. Jin Tian sat in the center, cross-legged, and closed his eyes. His consciousness drifted inward, tracing the meridians that had carried qi through his body for over three centuries.

In the past two months, he had reconstructed sixteen of the thirty-six fundamental meridians. That was enough for basic cultivation, enough for the outer disciple techniques he had been using. But against Liu Feng, it would not be enough.

He needed the eighteenth meridian.

The decision came with a weight that settled into his bones. The eighteenth meridian connected the heart to the dantian, allowing qi to flow freely between the body's core and its extremities. It was not a dangerous breakthrough—not by his old standards. But in a twelve-year-old body, forcing the connection would carry risks.

The memory of pain flashed through him. He had done this before, in his first life. It had taken him three days and left him bedridden for a week. Now he had three hours.

He began the process.

Qi gathered at his dantian, a warm pulse that grew with each breath. Jin Tian guided it upward, threading through the seventeenth meridian, feeling the familiar resistance at the junction where the eighteenth should open. The wall between them was thick, calcified by years of neglect.

*Push.*

The qi pressed against the barrier. Pain lanced through his chest, sharp and immediate. He ignored it.

*Push harder.*

Sweat beaded on his forehead. His heart stuttered, skipped, then resumed its rhythm with renewed force. The wall began to crack.

And then, without warning, the pressure vanished.

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open. His qi had stopped flowing, not because of failure, but because something had caught it, absorbed it, redirected it into nothingness.

He looked down at his chest.

A small black mark sat over his heart, no larger than a fingernail. It pulsed with a faint, oily sheen, and as he watched, it *moved*.

"Impossible," he whispered.

The mark was a seal. A qi-suppression seal, expertly crafted, placed directly over his heart meridian. It had been dormant, waiting for the moment he attempted to breakthrough the eighteenth meridian to activate.

He had been poisoned before he even started.

The seal was not meant to kill. It was meant to cripple. Anyone who triggered it would find their cultivation permanently stunted, their qi unable to flow past the heart meridian. They would never advance beyond the outer disciple stage.

And the seal had been placed inside him for at least two weeks.

Jin Tian's mind raced through every meal, every drink, every interaction he had had since awakening in this body. The seal could not have been placed by force—he would have noticed. It had to have been ingested, hidden in food or medicine, designed to activate upon contact with concentrated qi.

The morning tea. The bitter aftertaste he had dismissed as poor quality herbs. The kind smile of the serving disciple who had handed him the cup.

*Chen Wei's man.*

The realization crystallized with cold clarity. Chen Wei had not been content to let the tournament decision stand. He had been planning this for weeks, waiting for Jin Tian to push his cultivation far enough to trigger the seal. And Jin Tian had walked right into the trap.

He pressed his palm against the mark. The seal pulsed again, as if laughing at him.

In his first life, he had never encountered such a technique. The Heavenly Sword Sect had prided itself on honorable combat, on direct confrontation. Seals and poisons were the tools of demonic cultivators, of sects that had abandoned the righteous path.

But this seal was not demonic. The craftsmanship was too refined, the design too elegant. This was the work of someone trained in the sect's own formation arts.

Someone within the sect had given Chen Wei this seal.

The implications spread like ripples in still water. Chen Wei was not acting alone. He had an ally among the inner disciples, possibly among the elders themselves. The same network that would eventually betray Jin Tian in his first life was already forming, already moving against him.

And he had three hours until his match against Liu Feng.

Jin Tian stood slowly, his legs unsteady. The seal had weakened him, disrupted his qi flow, but it had not broken him. He still had the sixteenth and seventeenth meridians. He still had the techniques he had mastered over three centuries.

He would fight Liu Feng with one hand tied behind his back. It was not ideal, but he had fought in worse conditions. He had fought with a shattered arm, with a collapsed lung, with poison burning through his veins.

This was just another obstacle.

But as he walked out of the pavilion, a new thought settled into his mind, darker than the seal itself.

If Chen Wei had an ally among the inner disciples, then Liu Feng's betrayal in his first life took on new meaning. Liu Feng had not acted alone. He had been part of something larger, a conspiracy that stretched beyond the tournament, beyond the sect, into something Jin Tian had never fully understood.

The match against Liu Feng was no longer just a battle. It was the first thread of a tapestry he needed to unravel.

The arena was already filling when Jin Tian arrived. Inner disciples lined the elevated platforms, their robes marking their ranks, their eyes cold and assessing. Elder Zhou sat in the center, his face unreadable. And at the far end of the arena, Liu Feng stood waiting.

He was younger than Jin Tian remembered. Nineteen, maybe twenty, with a lean build and sharp features. His hair was the color of autumn leaves, pulled back into a tight ponytail. A sword hung at his hip, its sheath plain but its hilt ornately carved with fire-aspected formations.

Their eyes met across the arena floor.

Liu Feng smiled. It was not a friendly smile.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Elder Zhou announced, his voice carrying across the crowd. "You have chosen to face Inner Disciple Liu Feng in the first round of the inner division tournament. Do you accept the terms?"

"I accept," Jin Tian said, his voice steady.

"And do you understand the risks? Inner division matches are fought to submission, not to death. But accidents happen."

"I understand."

Liu Feng stepped forward, drawing his sword. The blade caught the light, gleaming with a reddish tint that spoke of fire-aspected refinement. "I've heard interesting things about you, junior brother," he said, his tone conversational. "They say you defeated Chen Wei's best man in three moves."

"The rumors exaggerate."

"Do they?" Liu Feng's smile widened. "Then I suppose I'll find out for myself."

The gong sounded.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. He was aggressive, confident, used to overwhelming opponents with speed and power. His sword lanced forward, trailing a ribbon of flame that split the air with a sharp hiss.

Jin Tian sidestepped. The flame passed within inches of his face, the heat searing against his skin. He did not draw his sword.

"Not going to fight?" Liu Feng asked, already pivoting for a second strike.

"I'm waiting."

"For what?"

"For you to show me something interesting."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. His third strike came faster, lower, aimed at Jin Tian's legs. The fire aspect had intensified, the blade now wreathed in actual flames that crackled and popped.

Jin Tian jumped, twisting in midair, and landed three steps to Liu Feng's left. His hand remained empty.

"You're fast," Liu Feng admitted. "But speed won't save you."

He changed his stance. The flames around his blade dimmed, condensed, and the sword itself began to glow with a deep, internal heat. The temperature in the arena rose noticeably.

*The Crimson Serpent technique. Rank four, first stage.*

Jin Tian remembered this technique. Liu Feng had used it in their first life, in the battle that had ended with a blade through Jin Tian's heart. It was powerful, destructive, and almost impossible to dodge at close range.

But Jin Tian knew its weakness. The technique required a full second of charge time, during which the user's qi was completely committed to the attack. In that second, the user could not defend.

Liu Feng's blade flared.

Jin Tian moved.

He closed the distance in a single step, his hand finally going to his sword. But he did not draw. Instead, he struck with the sheathed blade, driving the scabbard directly into Liu Feng's wrist, disrupting the flow of qi before the technique could complete.

The flames fizzled. Liu Feng's eyes widened in shock.

Jin Tian stepped back, his sheathed sword lowering. "Your stance gives away the charge," he said, his voice calm. "You shift your weight to your back foot a full second before you strike. Anyone watching your feet can predict the attack."

Liu Feng's face flushed. "You—"

"You rely on the flames to intimidate," Jin Tian continued. "They're effective against opponents who haven't faced fire-aspected techniques before. But fire is predictable. It follows the path of least resistance. It burns in a straight line."

He finally drew his sword. The blade was simple, unadorned, the standard issue for outer disciples. But in his hands, it seemed to hum with potential.

"Show me something I haven't seen before," Jin Tian said. "Or this match will end very quickly."

Liu Feng's composure cracked. The easy confidence vanished, replaced by something raw and violent. He lunged forward, abandoning technique for pure aggression, his blade carving wild arcs of flame that left afterimages in the air.

Jin Tian met him head-on.

The first exchange lasted three seconds. Their blades clashed four times, each impact sending sparks cascading across the arena floor. Jin Tian's movements were precise, economical, each deflection setting up his next position. Liu Feng's were wild, powerful, but uncontrolled.

The second exchange lasted two seconds. Jin Tian's blade found Liu Feng's shoulder, cutting through his robe and drawing a thin line of blood.

The third exchange lasted half a second.

Jin Tian's sword pressed against Liu Feng's throat.

The arena fell silent.

Liu Feng's hands dropped to his sides, his sword clattering against the stone floor. His face was pale, his breath ragged. "How?" he whispered.

"I told you," Jin Tian replied, holding his blade steady. "Fire is predictable."

He stepped back and sheathed his sword. The crowd erupted in murmurs, voices rising in disbelief. An outer disciple had just defeated a seventh-ranked inner disciple in three exchanges. It was impossible. It was unprecedented.

Elder Zhou rose from his seat, his face a mask of carefully controlled emotion. "The match is concluded. Jin Tian advances to the second round."

But Jin Tian was not looking at the elder. He was looking at Liu Feng, who had not moved from his kneeling position. The inner disciple's head was bowed, his shoulders shaking with barely contained rage.

"Liu Feng," Jin Tian said, lowering his voice

title: The Price of Victory
content:
The tournament grounds hummed with anticipation as Jin Tian stepped onto the elevated platform. The sun had begun its descent, casting long shadows across the stone arena where Inner Disciple Liu Feng already waited.

Liu Feng was exactly as Jin Tian remembered him. Tall, broad-shouldered, with a mane of red-tinged hair that matched the fire aspect of his cultivation. His eyes were like chips of amber, cold and calculating. He wore the crimson robes of an inner disciple with practiced ease, his posture relaxed, almost bored.

But Jin Tian knew better. Liu Feng was never bored. He was watching, waiting, cataloging every detail of his opponent with the patience of a predator.

"I expected someone taller," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "When Elder Zhou announced my opponent would be an outer disciple, I thought perhaps the sect had finally found a prodigy worthy of attention." He tilted his head. "Instead, I get a child who barely looks old enough to hold a sword."

Jin Tian said nothing. He had learned long ago that words before a battle were wasted breath. The sword would speak for him.

He drew his blade—a standard-issue outer disciple weapon, plain steel with a simple leather wrap. In his previous life, he would have been embarrassed by such a humble tool. Now, he knew better. A sword was only as good as the hand that wielded it.

The referee, an elder with graying temples and a scar across his throat, raised his hand. "The match will proceed until one combatant yields, is incapacitated, or is thrown from the platform. Killing is forbidden. Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first.

He exploded forward with a burst of qi, his blade igniting with crimson flames that left trails of smoke in the air. The strike was fast—faster than anything Jin Tian had faced in this life. It was aimed at his throat, a killing blow disguised as a disabling strike.

Jin Tian sidestepped.

Not by much. Just enough that Liu Feng's blade passed within a hair's breadth of his neck. The heat from the flames washed over his face, but he didn't flinch. Instead, he pivoted on his back foot and brought his own blade up in a rising arc aimed at Liu Feng's exposed ribs.

Liu Feng's eyes widened.

He twisted, barely managing to deflect the blow with the flat of his sword. Sparks flew as metal clashed, and Liu Feng leaped backward, creating distance between them.

"Interesting," Liu Feng said, his bored demeanor cracking. "You read my opening strike."

"You use the same footwork pattern as everyone who trains in the Blazing Sun style," Jin Tian replied calmly. "Left foot forward, weight on the back leg, blade held at a forty-five-degree angle. It's predictable."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. "You've studied the inner disciples' techniques?"

"I've studied everyone's techniques."

It was true. In his three hundred years of cultivation, Jin Tian had memorized the combat styles of thousands of cultivators. The Blazing Sun style was common among fire-aspect practitioners, and Liu Feng had never been particularly innovative with it. He relied on speed and aggression, overwhelming opponents before they could react.

Against a twelve-year-old outer disciple, it should have worked.

But Jin Tian was not a twelve-year-old outer disciple.

He attacked.

His blade moved like water, flowing around Liu Feng's guard with impossible grace. He didn't match strength with strength—that would be foolish against a cultivator three realms above him. Instead, he exploited gaps, feinted openings, and struck with surgical precision.

A cut here, a nick there. Small wounds that bled and burned, sapping Liu Feng's stamina.

Liu Feng roared in frustration, unleashing a wave of fire that forced Jin Tian back. "Stand still, you little insect!"

"I'm not an insect," Jin Tian said, wiping a bead of sweat from his brow. "I'm your opponent. And you're losing."

The crowd erupted. Murmurs of disbelief rippled through the spectators. An outer disciple, not just holding his own against a ranked inner disciple, but dominating the exchange?

Impossible.

Yet the evidence was there. Liu Feng's pristine robes were now stained with blood from a dozen small cuts. His breathing had grown ragged, his movements less precise. The fire around his blade had dimmed, a sign that his qi reserves were depleting faster than he could replenish them.

Jin Tian pressed his advantage.

He flowed into the third stance of the Falling Rain technique—a style he had developed in his eightieth year of cultivation, long after this tournament had ended. It was a technique that didn't exist yet, one that no one in this era had ever seen.

The blade became a blur, striking from every angle at once. Raindrops falling from a storm cloud, impossible to predict, impossible to block.

Liu Feng screamed.

His guard shattered. Jin Tian's blade pierced through, stopping a hair's breadth from Liu Feng's throat.

"I yield," Liu Feng gasped, his voice raw with humiliation. "I yield."

The referee's hand dropped. "Winner: Outer Disciple Jin Tian."

Silence.

Then thunderous applause.

Jin Tian lowered his blade and turned to leave. He had gotten what he wanted—a victory that would cement his place in the tournament, that would force the elders to take notice, that would set him on the path to—

"Wait."

Liu Feng's voice stopped him.

Jin Tian turned. Liu Feng was on his knees, his head bowed, his sword discarded at his side. But when he looked up, his eyes held no shame. They held something else entirely.

Fear.

"You're not an outer disciple," Liu Feng whispered, his voice barely audible over the crowd's noise. "You're not even a mortal cultivator. I've seen that technique before."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

"In my clan's records," Liu Feng continued, his voice trembling. "There's a description of a sword style that doesn't exist yet. A style developed by the Sword God in his later years. The Falling Rain technique."

The world seemed to stop.

Jin Tian stared at Liu Feng, his mind racing. How? The Liu family had been a minor clan in his first life, barely worth mentioning. They had no access to the Sword God's techniques, no connection to his inner circle.

Unless—

"You know something," Jin Tian said, his voice flat. "Tell me."

Liu Feng laughed bitterly. "I know that you shouldn't exist. I know that the technique you just used was developed three hundred years from now. I know that someone has been watching you, waiting for you to slip up." He met Jin Tian's gaze. "And I know that if you survive this tournament, they'll kill you themselves."

"Who?"

But before Liu Feng could answer, a bell tolled across the grounds. The tournament was resuming. The next match was about to begin.

Liu Feng rose unsteadily to his feet. "We can't talk here. Meet me at the Pavilion of Falling Stars tonight, after the third bell. I'll tell you everything."

He turned and walked away, leaving Jin Tian standing alone on the platform, the crowd's cheers fading into meaningless noise.

---

The Pavilion of Falling Stars was a crumbling structure at the edge of the sect grounds, long abandoned after a qi explosion had scarred the surrounding earth. It was the perfect place for a secret meeting—remote, forgotten, and easy to defend.

Jin Tian arrived as the third bell tolled, the moon hanging low and full in the sky. The pavilion's broken roof cast jagged shadows across the stone floor, and the wind carried the scent of decay.

Liu Feng was already there, standing in the center of the pavilion, his back to Jin Tian.

"You came," Liu Feng said without turning.

"I need answers."

"And I need to know if I can trust you." Liu Feng turned, and Jin Tian saw that his face was pale, his eyes hollow. "The technique you used—the Falling Rain. It was created in the year 847 of the Celestial Calendar, during the Sword God's campaign against the Shadow Sect. That year hasn't happened yet."

Jin Tian said nothing. He couldn't deny it. The evidence was too clear.

"I don't know how you know that technique," Liu Feng continued, "but I know why it matters. There's a faction within the sect—the Silent Council—that has been searching for someone like you. Someone who shouldn't exist. Someone who's been... changed."

"Changed how?"

Liu Feng's expression grew haunted. "The Silent Council believes that time is a river, and that certain people are stones that can alter its course. They've been collecting those stones for centuries, hoarding them, using them to..." He trailed off.

"To what?"

"To rewrite history."

The words hung in the air, heavy with implication.

Jin Tian felt a chill that had nothing to do with the night air. "The Silent Council. I've never heard of them."

"Because they don't exist in any official records. They're ghosts, shadows within the sect's hierarchy. Even the sect leader doesn't know they exist." Liu Feng's voice dropped to a whisper. "But I know because my family has served them for generations. We're their eyes, their ears, their..."

"Assassins," Jin Tian finished.

Liu Feng nodded slowly. "I was sent to kill you. Tonight. The Council ordered it the moment you defeated Chen Wei in the selection match. They said you were an anomaly that needed to be eliminated."

"Yet here you are, telling me this."

"Because I'm tired." Liu Feng's voice cracked. "I'm tired of serving masters I've never seen, tired of killing people who've done nothing wrong, tired of being a tool that gets discarded when it breaks." He looked at Jin Tian with desperate eyes. "I want out. And I think you're the only one who can help me."

Jin Tian studied him for a long moment. In his first life, Liu Feng had killed him. He had been the final betrayal, the blade that ended the Sword God's reign.

But now, standing in the ruins of the Pavilion of Falling Stars, Jin Tian saw something he hadn't seen before.

A pawn. Just like him.

"If I help you," Jin Tian said slowly, "I need to know everything. The Council's leaders, their methods, their goals. Everything."

Liu Feng nodded. "I'll tell you everything I know. But there's something you need to understand first."

"What?"

"The Council already knows you're here. They've known since the moment you used the Falling Rain technique. They have agents everywhere—inner disciples, outer disciples, even elders." Liu Feng's voice was barely a whisper now. "And they've already sent someone else to kill me, in case I failed."

As if on cue, a shadow detached itself from the darkness beyond the pavilion.

A figure stepped into the moonlight, clad in black robes, a blade gleaming in their hand.

"Well done, Liu Feng," the figure said, their voice distorted and inhuman. "You've delivered the anomaly straight to us."

Liu Feng's face went white. "You—you were here the whole time?"

"Of course. Did you think the Council would trust a traitor's son to complete such an important task alone?" The figure laughed, a sound like grinding stones. "You were always expendable, Liu Feng. Just like your father."

The blade flashed.

Liu Feng's head separated from his shoulders, his body crumpling to the ground before his eyes could even register shock.

Jin Tian's hand flew to his sword.

The figure turned to face him, and Jin Tian saw that beneath the hood, there was no face—only a swirling void of darkness, punctuated by two points of red light that might have been eyes.

"Hello, Sword God," the figure said, and there was mock

title: The Poison in the Porridge
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived, the evening mist curling around the stone pillars like spectral fingers. Three hours until his match against Liu Feng. Three hours to prepare for an enemy who had killed him once before.

But preparation required solitude, and solitude required trust—a commodity he had learned to hoard like a dying man hoards water.

He found a secluded corner near the eastern wall, where the qi suppression formations were weakest and the ambient energy thrummed with wild, untamed potential. The outer disciples avoided this place, claiming it felt "unsettling." Jin Tian knew better. The formations here were old, damaged by a rogue cultivator's attack thirty years ago, and the sect had never bothered to repair them properly. In his first life, he had discovered this spot during his fifth year as an outer disciple, using it to breakthrough to the third stage of Qi Condensation ahead of schedule.

Now he would use it for something far more dangerous.

He sat cross-legged on the cold stone, closing his eyes. The Azure Crane Sword rested across his knees, humming with a resonance that only he could hear. In his previous life, he had spent decades learning to listen to his blade. Now, with the memories of a Sword God, the connection came naturally—a thread of silver light that bound his soul to the steel.

*Focus,* he told himself. *The match against Liu Feng is a test, but it is not the true battle.*

The true battle was invisible. It was fought in whispers and poison, in alliances formed and broken, in the careful manipulation of fate itself. Someone had orchestrated his death three hundred years from now. Someone had turned his disciples against him. Someone had ensured that when he fell, there would be no one left to catch him.

And that someone was already moving.

A sound broke his meditation—footsteps, light and deliberate, approaching from the path behind him. Jin Tian's hand moved to his sword before his eyes opened.

"Easy, easy," a voice said, familiar and sharp. "I come bearing gifts, not blades."

Lin Mei stepped out of the shadows, her plain robes carrying a tray with two bowls of steaming porridge. She smiled, but there was something guarded in her eyes—a wariness that hadn't been there before.

"You shouldn't be here," Jin Tian said, not relaxing his guard. "If anyone sees us—"

"Then they'll see an outer disciple bringing food to another outer disciple. Scandalous, I know." She set the tray down between them, settling onto the ground with a grace that belied her apparent clumsiness. "You missed dinner. The hall was buzzing with rumors about your challenge. I figured you'd be out here, brooding."

"I don't brood."

"You brood constantly. It's your primary function as a mysterious prodigy." She pushed one of the bowls toward him. "Eat. You'll need your strength for Liu Feng."

Jin Tian stared at the porridge. It smelled of ginger and green onions, simple fare for outer disciples who couldn't afford spirit herbs. In his first life, he had eaten thousands of bowls just like it, never questioning the ingredients or the hands that prepared them.

He was no longer that naive.

"Who prepared this?" he asked.

Lin Mei's smile flickered. "The kitchen disciples. Why?"

"And who gave you the order to bring it to me?"

Her face went still—the particular stillness of someone who had been caught off guard and was scrambling to recover. "No one ordered me. I came because I wanted to help."

"Did you?"

The question hung between them like a drawn blade. Lin Mei's eyes widened, hurt flickering across her features before she masked it. "You think I would poison you? After everything?"

"I think that seven years from now, someone very close to me will drive a sword through my heart while smiling." Jin Tian's voice was flat, emotionless. "I think that trust is a luxury I can no longer afford."

Lin Mei stood abruptly, her hands trembling. "I saved your life. When Chen Wei's men cornered you, I—"

"You warned me. There's a difference." Jin Tian picked up the bowl, swirling the porridge. The liquid moved with an unnatural viscosity, clinging to the sides in a way that plain congee shouldn't. "And you were very convenient, Lin Mei. Always appearing at the right moment, always knowing the right thing to say. I was too grateful to question it before."

"You're paranoid."

"I'm alive."

She stared at him, and for a moment, he saw something raw beneath her mask—fear, perhaps, or guilt. Then she snatched the bowl from his hands, lifted it to her lips, and drank deeply.

The silence stretched as she lowered the empty bowl, porridge dripping from her chin. "Satisfied?" she demanded, her voice rough. "If I wanted you dead, I would have let Chen Wei's men finish the job. I wouldn't have—" She stopped, pressing a hand to her stomach.

Jin Tian watched her carefully. "What's wrong?"

"Nothing. I'm fine." But her face had gone pale, sweat beading on her forehead. "It's just—the porridge—I shouldn't have—"

She collapsed.

Jin Tian caught her before she hit the ground, his mind racing. The poison was subtle—something that worked slowly, designed to mimic a mundane illness. If he hadn't tested her, if she had simply watched him eat and waited...

"Lin Mei. Stay with me." He pressed two fingers to her throat, feeling her pulse flutter like a bird caught in a net. Her qi was unstable, lashing out in erratic bursts as her body fought the toxin.

"Didn't... didn't know," she gasped, her eyes finding his. "Swear it. Didn't know."

"I believe you."

He didn't. Not completely. But she had drunk from the same bowl, and that counted for something in the calculus of trust.

The poison was familiar—a blend of Nightshade Essence and Soul-Sapping Powder, rare ingredients that left no trace in the body after death. It was an assassin's tool, refined and elegant. The kind of poison that killed Sword Gods on mountain peaks three hundred years in the future.

Someone in this sect was using the same techniques.

Jin Tian closed his eyes, reaching into his memories. In his first life, he had studied medicine for three years under a healer who had since died of old age. The knowledge was there, buried beneath centuries of sword techniques and battle tactics.

*Nightshade Essence binds to qi meridians, blocking the flow of vital energy. Soul-Sapping Powder accelerates the process, creating the appearance of a sudden qi deviation. The antidote requires...*

His eyes snapped open. "I need seven ingredients. Can you hear me?"

Lin Mei nodded weakly.

"Seven ingredients. You're going to tell me where to find them. And then you're going to explain why you came here tonight, who sent you, and what you know about Chen Wei's plans."

"I'll... I'll tell you everything." Her voice was barely a whisper. "Just... don't let me die like this."

---

The infirmary was empty when Jin Tian carried her inside, the night healer having retired to his quarters. He laid Lin Mei on a cot and began preparing the antidote, his hands moving with the precision of a man who had performed this ritual a thousand times.

The ingredients were common enough—sage root, powdered jade, the tears of a morning glory vine. Any outer disciple with access to the herb gardens could gather them. But the combination, the proportions, the order of addition... those were secrets worth killing for.

As he worked, Lin Mei spoke.

"I was recruited six months ago," she said, her voice hoarse. "A woman approached me after the sect trials. She said she represented a patron who wanted to help me rise through the ranks. All I had to do was report on anything unusual."

"Did she give you a name?"

"No. Only a symbol—a crescent moon wrapped in chains."

Jin Tian's hands stilled. The Bound Moon. In his first life, it had been a minor sect that dissolved after its leader's death. But here, in this time, it was active. It was moving.

"What did you report?"

"Everything. Your fights, your training patterns, the people you spoke to. I didn't think... I didn't know they would try to kill you." Her voice cracked. "I thought they just wanted information. I thought I was being useful."

"You were being used." Jin Tian crushed the jade powder into the mixture, watching it dissolve into a pale green paste. "There's a difference."

"What happens now?" she asked. "After you save me?"

He looked at her—at the girl who had betrayed him, who might still be lying, who might be part of a conspiracy stretching across decades. He should kill her. It would be the safe choice, the smart choice.

But Jin Tian had learned, in his first life, that safety was an illusion.

"You're going to help me find the woman who recruited you," he said. "And then we're going to find out who she serves."

"After everything I did?"

"Because of everything you did." He lifted the bowl to her lips. "Drink. You have three hours to recover before my match. And then we both have a long night ahead of us."

Lin Mei drank.

And somewhere in the shadows of the sect, a woman with a crescent moon pendant smiled, knowing her trap had found its mark.

But the target was not Jin Tian.

It was never Jin Tian.

The porridge had been meant for someone else entirely.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Price of Memory

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The sun had begun its descent, casting long shadows across the stone platforms where outer disciples usually sparred until dusk. But tonight, word of the tournament had spread like wildfire through the sect, and everyone had retreated to their quarters to prepare, to scheme, to pray.

Jin Tian preferred the silence.

He drew his wooden practice sword—a crude thing compared to the weapons he had wielded in his past life—and began the first form of the Heavenly Sword Art. His movements were slow, deliberate, each strike carving invisible patterns in the cooling air. The sword knew no master, only the hand that guided it. The hand knew no fear, only the will that commanded it.

*Three hours.*

It was not enough time. Not nearly enough. In his past life, he had spent three years preparing for his first match against Liu Feng, and still he had barely won. Liu Feng was a monster disguised as a man, his fire-aspected techniques capable of turning the very air into a weapon. He fought with a ferocity that bordered on madness, his strikes carrying the weight of a man who had nothing to lose.

But Jin Tian had something Liu Feng did not: three hundred years of memories.

He stopped mid-strike, the sword hovering before him. The memory of Liu Feng's blade piercing his heart was still fresh, still raw, still bleeding. He had spent countless nights in his past life trying to understand why Liu Feng had killed him. They had been rivals, yes, but there had been respect between them. A grudging acknowledgment of each other's skill.

*Why?*

The question had haunted him through death and rebirth. And now he had a chance to ask it directly.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and sat cross-legged on the training platform. He closed his eyes and reached inward, feeling the flow of qi through his meridians. His cultivation base was still at the third level of Qi Condensation—pitiful by inner disciple standards, but he had been hiding his true strength. The tournament would require him to reveal more than he wanted, but some secrets were worth sacrificing.

He began cycling his qi, pushing it through pathways that had long since atrophied in this young body. Each rotation brought a familiar warmth, a sense of returning to something ancient and sacred. His sword had always been his truest companion, but his qi was the blood that gave it life.

*The first principle of the Heavenly Sword Art: the sword is an extension of the self. The second principle: the self is an extension of the heavens. The third principle: the heavens are empty, and so too must the swordsman be empty, for only in emptiness can the true strike find its home.*

Jin Tian had spent a lifetime mastering these principles. He had taught them to seven disciples, watched them grow and flourish, and then watched them turn their blades against him. Now he would teach himself again, not as a master but as a student, learning the lessons he had forgotten in his arrogance.

He opened his eyes.

The training grounds were no longer empty.

A figure stood at the edge of the platform, wrapped in shadows and silence. Jin Tian did not move. He had sensed the approach moments ago, but he had not recognized the qi signature. It was familiar and foreign at once, like a face seen in a dream.

"You're early," the figure said. The voice was a woman's, low and resonant, carrying the weight of command. "The tournament doesn't start until dawn."

"I'm preparing," Jin Tian replied. "And you?"

The figure stepped forward, and the dying light revealed her face. She was young—no older than sixteen—with sharp features and eyes that burned like embers. Her robes were black, unadorned, marking her as a disciple of the Shadow Pavilion. Jin Tian's breath caught.

*Elder Wu's disciple. The one who would later become the Shadow Blade, the sect's most feared assassin.*

He had never known her name. In his past life, she had been a ghost, appearing only in whispers and rumors. She had never spoken to him, never acknowledged his existence. And yet here she was, standing before him in the twilight, her eyes fixed on his with an intensity that made his skin crawl.

"You've been watching me," she said. It was not a question.

"I've been aware of your presence," Jin Tian replied carefully. "The Shadow Pavilion teaches its disciples to move unseen, but you've been... conspicuous."

A flicker of surprise crossed her face, gone as quickly as it appeared. "You can sense Shadow qi?"

"I can sense many things."

She studied him for a long moment, her expression unreadable. Then she sat down across from him, her movements fluid and precise, like a cat settling into a patch of sunlight. "My name is Mei. I've been assigned to observe the tournament and report any anomalies to Elder Wu."

"And I am an anomaly?"

"You are the boy who was supposed to fail the entrance exam but passed with the highest efficiency score in a decade. You are the boy who was supposed to lose to Chen Wei but instead humiliated him in front of the entire sect. You are the boy who should be nothing, but somehow you are becoming something." She tilted her head. "Yes. You are an anomaly."

Jin Tian said nothing. He had known that his actions would draw attention, but he had hoped to remain beneath notice for a few more months. The Shadow Pavilion's interest was dangerous. They were the sect's eyes and ears, its hidden hand, its conscience when necessary and its executioner when convenient.

"What do you want?" he asked.

"To warn you." Mei's voice dropped, losing its formal edge. "Liu Feng is not what he appears. He has been receiving instruction from someone outside the sect. Someone who teaches techniques that are not part of the Azure Flame Sect's inheritance."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "Outside the sect? From where?"

"I don't know. My master suspects a demonic cultivator, but there is no evidence." She paused, her eyes searching his face. "Liu Feng's fire techniques are too refined, too precise. They do not match the sect's lineage. And he has been growing stronger at an unnatural rate. In the past month, he has defeated three inner disciples who were ranked above him. Each victory was absolute."

*Demonic cultivation.* The words echoed in Jin Tian's mind like a death knell. He had seen what demonic techniques did to cultivators in his past life. They granted power at the cost of sanity, strength at the price of the soul. Those who walked that path rarely lived to see old age, and when they fell, they took everyone around them with them.

"Why are you telling me this?" he asked. "We've never spoken. You have no reason to trust me."

"I don't trust you," Mei replied flatly. "But I've been watching you since the entrance exams, and I've noticed something. You move like someone who has already lived a lifetime. Your sword forms are flawless, your qi control is impeccable, and your understanding of combat is far beyond what a twelve-year-old outer disciple should possess." She leaned forward. "I don't know who you really are, Jin Tian, but I know you're not what you appear to be. And I think that makes you the only person in this sect who might survive a direct confrontation with Liu Feng."

The weight of her words settled on his shoulders like a mantle of iron. She was right, of course. He was not what he appeared to be. He was a Sword God reborn, a man who had lived three centuries and died at the hands of his own disciples. But he was also a twelve-year-old boy with a cultivation base that barely qualified him for the tournament.

"I appreciate the warning," he said finally. "But I have nothing to offer in return."

"I'm not asking for anything." Mei stood, brushing the dust from her robes. "I'm merely ensuring that the sect's resources are not wasted on a corpse. If you survive the match against Liu Feng, we can discuss compensation." She turned to leave, then paused. "One more thing. Liu Feng's technique has a weakness. It relies on the heat of his own qi to fuel his flames. If you can make him expend too much too quickly, his furnace will cool, and his power will wane."

She vanished into the shadows without another word, leaving Jin Tian alone with the dying light and the weight of her revelation.

*Demonic cultivation.*

He had thought he understood his enemies. He had thought that by knowing their future, he could control the present. But this was different. This was a variable he had not anticipated. In his past life, Liu Feng had been a prodigy, yes, but he had never shown signs of demonic influence. He had fought with honor, with skill, with the fire of a true cultivator.

Until the day he had driven his blade through Jin Tian's heart.

Had the demonic cultivation been hidden even then? Had Liu Feng been a puppet, dancing on strings held by an unseen master? Or had he fallen to the dark path sometime in the decades after Jin Tian's death, corrupted by power and ambition?

There was only one way to find out.

Jin Tian rose, his muscles protesting after hours of meditation. The sun had set, and the training grounds were bathed in moonlight. He could sense the qi of the sect around him, thousands of cultivators preparing for the tournament, each with their own hopes and fears and secrets.

He would face Liu Feng in the morning. He would fight with everything he had, drawing on the knowledge of a lifetime spent mastering the sword. And when the battle was done, he would learn the truth.

One way or another.

The night stretched on, silent and watchful. Jin Tian did not sleep. He sat in meditation, cycling his qi, preparing his body and mind for the coming conflict. The familiar weight of his practice sword rested across his knees, a comfort and a promise.

Dawn came slowly, painting the sky in shades of gold and crimson. The tournament grounds filled with disciples, elders, and sect leaders, all eager to witness the matches that would determine the future of the Azure Flame Sect.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the arena, waiting. Across from him, Liu Feng stepped into the ring, his presence a furnace that made the air shimmer with heat. Their eyes met, and Jin Tian saw something in the older boy's gaze that made his heart freeze.

Recognition.

Liu Feng knew him. Not as an outer disciple, not as a rival, but as something more. Something that should not exist.

The bell rang. The match began.

And Liu Feng smiled.

"Welcome back, Sword God," he whispered, his voice carrying across the arena like a curse. "I've been waiting for you."

title: The Eighth Disciple Returns
content:
The tournament grounds buzzed with anticipation as Jin Tian stepped onto the arena platform. Three hours had passed since his confrontation with Chen Wei, three hours spent in meditation, refining his Qi, and preparing for what he knew would be the most dangerous fight of his second life.

Liu Feng stood across from him, already in stance. The inner disciple was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader in the shoulders, his fire-aspected Qi radiating like heat haze around his body. His eyes held no malice—only the cold focus of a warrior evaluating prey.

"The outer disciple who defied expectations," Liu Feng said, his voice flat. "I watched your matches. You're hiding your true level."

Jin Tian said nothing. He drew his sword—a standard-issue outer disciple blade, inferior to what Liu Feng wielded but adequate for what he planned.

"I don't know what game you're playing," Liu Feng continued, circling slowly, "but it doesn't matter. The gap between inner and outer disciples isn't something clever tactics can bridge."

"Then why are you still talking?" Jin Tian asked.

The crowd laughed. Liu Feng's eyes narrowed.

The bell rang.

Liu Feng exploded forward, his blade wreathed in orange flame. The technique was textbook—Blazing Sun Strike, a mid-grade fire art that turned Qi into concussive heat. In Jin Tian's first life, this same attack had nearly cost him an arm.

Now he saw it for what it was: predictable.

He sidestepped left instead of blocking. The flame blade carved air where his chest had been. Before Liu Feng could recover, Jin Tian's sword snapped upward, catching the inner disciple's wrist with the flat of his blade.

*Clang.*

Liu Feng hissed, his grip weakening for a fraction of a second. Jin Tian didn't press the advantage. Instead, he retreated, creating distance.

"That was a warning," Jin Tian said quietly. "Next time, I'll take the hand."

The arena fell silent. Inner disciples in the viewing stands leaned forward. Elders exchanged glances.

Liu Feng's face darkened. "You got lucky."

"Did I?"

They clashed again—this time, Liu Feng used a technique Jin Tian recognized from decades later: Ember Storm, a signature move that created afterimages of flame to confuse opponents. In his first life, Jin Tian had needed three encounters to figure out the counter.

Now, he simply closed his eyes.

The crowd gasped. Someone shouted, "Is he insane?"

But Jin Tian's other senses had been honed through centuries of combat. The flame afterimages were illusions, heat without substance. The real attack was the one that felt *cold* in comparison—the space where Liu Feng's sword displaced air.

He pivoted, brought his blade up in a perfect crescent arc, and met Liu Feng's sword exactly at the point of maximum force.

The impact sent shockwaves rippling across the arena. Both fighters skidded backward. Liu Feng's eyes widened.

"How did you—"

"Your tells are obvious," Jin Tian interrupted. "You shift your weight before every major technique. Your left foot points toward your intended target. Your breathing changes when you're about to use fire arts."

He was lying, of course. The tells were real, but he had only noticed them now because he already knew the techniques. Still, the explanation was plausible enough that Liu Feng's confidence visibly cracked.

"You're... you're not an ordinary outer disciple," Liu Feng said slowly. "Who trained you?"

"Someone who's dead now."

The answer seemed to satisfy something in Liu Feng. He sheathed his sword, surprising everyone.

"I concede."

The declaration rippled through the crowd like thunder. Elders rose from their seats. Disciples shouted in disbelief.

"Why?" Jin Tian asked, genuinely curious. In his first life, Liu Feng had never surrendered. He had fought until the very end, even when defeat was certain.

Liu Feng met his eyes. "Because you're not my real target. I was ordered to test you. To see if you were worth watching."

The words landed like stones in Jin Tian's gut. "Ordered by whom?"

Liu Feng glanced toward the elders' platform. His gaze lingered on one figure—a man Jin Tian had barely noticed before, seated in the shadows behind Elder Zhou. The man wore grey robes, nondescript, his face obscured by a wide-brimmed hat.

"The one who told me to kill you three hundred years from now," Liu Feng said, his voice dropping to barely a whisper.

Ice flooded Jin Tian's veins. "What did you say?"

But Liu Feng was already walking away. The crowd parted for him, their confusion mirroring Jin Tian's own horror. He knew—knew that Jin Tian was a reincarnator. How? The only people who could possibly know were—

*The other survivors.*

The ones who had also died and returned.

Jin Tian's hands trembled. He forced them still, bowing to the elders as was protocol, then stepping off the arena platform. His mind raced through possibilities. If Liu Feng knew, then others might know too. The traitors among his disciples. The enemies he had made in his first life. If even one of them had regressed alongside him—

"Jin Tian."

The voice came from behind him. He turned to find a young woman standing there, her features sharp and beautiful, her cultivation base hidden behind a veil of suppression techniques. She wore the robes of an inner disciple, but her bearing spoke of something far greater.

"I am Lian Hua," she said, bowing slightly. "Inner Disciple of the Violet Cloud Sect. I've been watching your matches with great interest."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. Lian Hua. He knew that name. In his first life, she had been a rising star, a woman whose talent rivaled his own. But she had vanished three years before his ascension to Sword God, her disappearance written off as a failed breakthrough.

Now she was here, alive, looking at him with recognition in her eyes.

"You know something," he said carefully.

"I know everything," she replied. "I know you're not twelve. I know you've lived before. I know you're trying to change what's already written."

The words hit him like a physical blow. "How?"

Lian Hua smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Because I'm like you, Jin Tian. I've walked this path before. And I've come to warn you: you're not the only one who remembers."

"Who else?"

"Chen Wei. Elder Zhou. The Grey-Robed Man who ordered Liu Feng to test you." She paused. "And someone you met a long time ago, in a life you thought was forgotten."

She reached into her robe and pulled out a jade pendant—a crescent moon wrapped in thorns. Jin Tian's breath caught in his throat. He knew that symbol. He had carved it himself, in another lifetime, for—

"No," he whispered. "She's dead. I watched her die."

Lian Hua's expression softened, just slightly. "Did you? Or did you watch what you were meant to see?"

Before he could respond, a bell tolled across the sect grounds—the signal for an emergency assembly. Disciples began streaming toward the main hall. Lian Hua pressed the pendant into his hand.

"Find me after the assembly. There's something you need to see. Something that will change everything you thought you knew about your first death."

She vanished into the crowd, leaving Jin Tian standing alone, clutching the pendant of a woman he had loved and lost three hundred years ago.

*Mei Ling.*

Her name surfaced from the depths of his memory, carrying with it a pain he had buried so deep he had convinced himself it had healed. She had been his first love, his first loss, the wound that had shaped him into the cold, distant Sword God he had become.

But if she was alive—if she had been alive this whole time—

He clenched the pendant until its edges bit into his palm. Blood dripped onto the stone floor.

The assembly bell tolled again, more urgent this time.

Jin Tian looked toward the main hall, then back at the direction Lian Hua had disappeared.

Two paths lay before him. The safe one—attend the assembly, maintain his cover, continue his careful reconstruction of power.

Or the dangerous one—follow the truth, even if it shattered everything he thought he knew.

He had never been good at choosing safety.

Jin Tian turned away from the assembly hall and walked into the shadows, the pendant warm against his skin, the past clawing at his heels with the promise of answers he wasn't sure he was ready to hear.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Serpent's True Scales

The morning sun cast long shadows across the training grounds as Jin Tian made his way toward the inner division arena. Three hours. Three hours to prepare for a match against the man who had killed him in his first life.

But preparation was not what he needed most.

He needed information.

Instead of heading directly to the arena, Jin Tian diverted toward the sect's administrative pavilion. The building stood three stories tall, its walls covered in formation arrays that pulsed with soft blue light. Records of every disciple, every mission, every transaction were stored within.

Access required authorization.

Jin Tian had none.

He circled to the eastern wall where a maintenance entrance sat hidden behind overgrown bamboo. In his first life, he had discovered this entrance during a game of hide-and-seek with fellow disciples. Three hundred years later, the sect had still never bothered to seal it.

The lock yielded to a thin sliver of qi, and he slipped inside.

The archives smelled of aged paper and ink. Scrolls lined the walls in seemingly infinite rows, each categorized by year and importance. Jin Tian moved silently, his steps memorized from decades of navigating this very building in his previous life.

He found what he was looking for in the restricted section—a cabinet marked with the seal of the inner division oversight committee.

Inside were the personnel files of every inner disciple.

Liu Feng's folder was thicker than most. Jin Tian unrolled it carefully, scanning the contents. Birth records. Talent assessments. Mission reports. Combat evaluations.

And then he found it.

A single page, tucked between two routine reports, written in a hand he recognized all too well.

*"Disciples Liu Feng and Chen Wei have been selected for Project Ember. Further instructions will follow. Do not deviate from the assigned timeline. The Sword God must fall when the stars align."*

The page bore no signature.

But the seal at the bottom belonged to the Azure Serpent Society.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

The Azure Serpent Society was a shadow organization that had operated in the shadows of the sect for centuries. In his first life, he had only learned of their existence in his final years—and by then, it was too late to root them out. They had been the ones who orchestrated his betrayal. The ones who had turned his disciples against him. The ones who had whispered promises of power and wealth until loyalty crumbled like dried clay.

But in his first life, the Azure Serpent Society had not begun their operations until decades from now.

They were already here.

They had already chosen their pawns.

And Jin Tian was walking straight into their trap.

He memorized the page's contents, then carefully replaced the folder. His mind raced as he slipped back out of the archives and into the morning light.

Project Ember. The Sword God must fall. Assigned timeline.

They had been planning his death before he had even become the Sword God.

But who was giving the orders? The Azure Serpent Society had always been led by a figure known only as the Grand Serpent. In his first life, Jin Tian had never learned their identity. Every lead had ended in dead bodies and burned records.

Now he had a chance to trace the serpent's trails back to its head.

He just had to survive the next three hours.

---

The inner division arena was a massive structure carved into the mountainside, its walls lined with observation platforms that could seat thousands. Today, only a few hundred had gathered—the match was considered a formality, a chance for the outer disciples to see the gap between their level and the inner division.

Jin Tian walked through the entrance tunnel, his footsteps echoing against the stone. The arena floor was made of reinforced granite, scarred by countless battles. At its center stood a figure wreathed in flame.

Liu Feng.

He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, his body lean and corded with muscle. Fire-aspected qi swirled around him like a living cloak, and his eyes—cold and calculating—locked onto Jin Tian the moment he entered.

"So, you're the one who stole Chen Wei's spot," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I expected someone more... impressive."

Jin Tian said nothing. He had learned long ago that words were useless against men like Liu Feng. They only served to reveal your intentions.

He drew his sword.

The blade was simple, unadorned, a standard-issue weapon for outer disciples. But in Jin Tian's hands, it hummed with suppressed power.

Liu Feng laughed. "Is that all you brought? A child's toy?" He extended his hand, and flames coalesced into a blade of pure fire. "Let me show you what a real weapon looks like."

The referee—an inner division elder with graying hair—raised his hand. "The match will proceed until one combatant yields or is unable to continue. No killing blows permitted." He paused, looking pointedly at Liu Feng. "Is that understood?"

"Perfectly," Liu Feng said, his smile never wavering.

"Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian expected. A burst of speed, a sweeping arc of flame—standard opening moves designed to overwhelm and intimidate.

Jin Tian sidestepped.

Not a dodge, but a calculated repositioning. He had seen this attack thousands of times in his first life, had dissected it, had taught his disciples how to counter it.

The flame blade passed inches from his chest.

Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly, but he recovered quickly, pivoting into a second strike. This one was faster, more precise—a thrust aimed at Jin Tian's throat.

Jin Tian tilted his head. The fire grazed his skin, leaving a thin line of heat that did not break flesh.

"What?" Liu Feng's composure cracked for just a moment.

Jin Tian saw his opening.

He stepped forward, closing the distance between them. Liu Feng's fire techniques were powerful, but they required space to reach full potency. In close quarters, they became unwieldy, dangerous even to their user.

It was a weakness Jin Tian had discovered after Liu Feng had killed him.

The lesson had cost him his life.

Now he would use it to save it.

He drove his sword upward, the blade aimed at the gap between Liu Feng's ribs. The strike was precise, economical—no wasted motion, no unnecessary power.

Liu Feng twisted, and the blade caught his shoulder instead of his heart.

Blood sprayed across the arena floor.

The crowd gasped.

Liu Feng stumbled back, one hand pressed to his wound. His flames flickered, destabilized by the injury. "You... how did you..."

"Your fire techniques require a stable qi circulation to maintain," Jin Tian said calmly. "A wound to the meridian cluster at your right shoulder disrupts that circulation. Without it, your flames become unstable. Dangerous to you. Useless against me."

Liu Feng's face contorted with rage. "You think knowing a few weaknesses makes you my equal?"

"I don't think I'm your equal," Jin Tian replied. "I think I'm your better."

The words hung in the air like a challenge.

Liu Feng roared and lunged forward, abandoning technique for raw power. Flames erupted from his body in a torrent, turning the arena into an inferno. The heat was suffocating, the light blinding.

Jin Tian closed his eyes.

He didn't need to see.

He had fought Liu Feng a hundred times in his mind since awakening. Had analyzed every movement, every pattern, every tell. The man was predictable in his rage—he always fell back on the same attacks when pushed.

The flame torrent would last twelve seconds. At the nine-second mark, Liu Feng would overextend, leaving his left side exposed.

Jin Tian counted.

*One. Two. Three.*

The heat intensified.

*Four. Five. Six.*

He could feel his skin blistering, his robes beginning to smoke.

*Seven. Eight. Nine.*

He moved.

His sword traced a perfect arc, cutting through the flames as if they were mist. The blade found Liu Feng's exposed side, biting deep into flesh.

The fire died.

Liu Feng collapsed to his knees, blood pooling beneath him. His face was a mask of disbelief. "How... how is this possible? You're just an outer disciple... a child..."

Jin Tian stood over him, sword still raised. "I am what you made me."

He didn't explain further. He didn't need to.

The referee rushed forward, checking Liu Feng's injuries. "The match is over! Jin Tian wins!"

The crowd erupted into chaos. Outer disciples were cheering. Inner disciples were silent, their faces unreadable. And somewhere in the observation platforms, hidden among the shadows, someone was watching.

Jin Tian felt their gaze like a weight on his soul.

He looked up.

A figure stood at the edge of the highest platform, cloaked in darkness. Even from this distance, Jin Tian could see the symbol embroidered on their collar.

A coiled serpent.

Azure.

The figure raised a hand, and a single message formed in the air before Jin Tian, written in fire:

*"Interesting. We will meet again, Sword God."*

Then the figure was gone, leaving only smoke and questions.

Jin Tian's hands trembled—not from exhaustion, but from the cold realization that had been creeping up his spine since he found that file.

The Azure Serpent Society knew who he was.

They knew he had regressed.

And they were watching his every move.

He sheathed his sword and walked off the arena floor, ignoring the congratulations and curious stares. His mind was already racing ahead, calculating, planning.

He had three years until the tournament where his first life's betrayal had begun.

But the serpent's coils were already tightening around him.

And this time, he couldn't afford to wait for them to strike.

He would have to strike first.

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Serpent's True Scales

The three hours passed like water through a sieve.

Jin Tian sat cross-legged in the practice courtyard, his eyes closed, his breathing steady. Around him, the other outer disciples gave him wide berth, their whispers a constant hum at the edge of his awareness. He ignored them. His mind was elsewhere, tracing the pathways of his meridians, feeling the flow of qi through his body like a river carving through stone.

He was still weak. Pathetically weak compared to what he had once been. But he was no longer the boy who had entered this tournament. Three weeks of combat, of pushing his body to its limits, of forcing his cultivation to grow through sheer will—it had transformed him. He had broken through to the fifth level of Qi Condensation, a feat that would have taken him three years in his first life.

It wasn't enough. It would never be enough. But it would have to do.

"Jin Tian."

He opened his eyes. Elder Zhou stood before him, his ancient face unreadable. Behind him, two inner disciples waited, their robes bearing the crimson sigil of the inner sect's enforcement division.

"Elder," Jin Tian said, rising and bowing.

"The arena is prepared. Your match against Liu Feng will commence in one hour." Elder Zhou's voice carried no warmth, no encouragement. "I trust you have used your time wisely."

"I have."

"Good." The old man turned to leave, then paused. "One piece of advice, boy. Liu Feng is known for his fire techniques, but that is not his true strength. He has trained in the Shadow Flame Art, a forbidden technique adapted from demonic cultivation. The sect permits it because he has proven useful. Do not let the fire fool you. The shadow is what kills."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. The Shadow Flame Art. In his first life, he had never known. Liu Feng had used standard fire techniques in all their matches, had built his reputation on them. The killing blow that had ended Jin Tian's life had been swift, precise, and utterly ordinary—a simple thrust through the heart. No shadow. No flame. Nothing that would have revealed the truth.

But now he knew. And knowledge was the sharpest blade of all.

"Thank you, Elder," Jin Tian said, meaning it.

Elder Zhou grunted and walked away, the enforcement disciples falling into step behind him.

Jin Tian turned back to the practice courtyard, his mind racing. The Shadow Flame Art was a hybrid technique, blending righteous qi with demonic corruption. It was unstable, dangerous, and incredibly powerful. More importantly, it had a weakness. The dual nature of the art created a natural conflict within the user's meridians, a point of fracture that could be exploited if one knew where to strike.

He knew where to strike.

The question was whether his sword could reach that point before Liu Feng's blade reached his heart.

---

The main arena was packed.

Thousands of disciples filled the stands, their voices a distant roar that vibrated through the stone beneath Jin Tian's feet. He stood in the entrance tunnel, his sword at his side, his body humming with barely contained energy. The tournament had reached its final stages, and the matches had grown increasingly brutal. Two inner disciples had already been carried out on stretchers, their cultivation crippled by their opponents' attacks.

This was not a game. This was survival.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," a voice announced, echoing through the arena. "Ranked sixteenth among outer disciples. Challenging for inner sect advancement."

The crowd's reaction was immediate and dismissive. Laughter. Boos. A few scattered cheers from the outer disciple section, quickly drowned out.

"His opponent," the announcer continued, "Inner Disciple Liu Feng. Ranked seventh among inner disciples. Holder of the Crimson Flame Token."

The laughter died.

Liu Feng stepped into the arena from the opposite tunnel, and the crowd erupted. He was tall, lean, with sharp features and eyes that burned with an inner fire. His robes were crimson, his hair the color of ash, and in his hand, he carried a blade that seemed to drink the light around it.

He moved like a predator. Like a man who had never known defeat.

Jin Tian walked forward, his steps measured, his breathing controlled. The arena floor was smooth stone, scarred by countless battles. The air smelled of blood and ozone and anticipation.

Liu Feng stopped ten paces away, his eyes sweeping over Jin Tian with obvious disdain. "So. You're the one who humiliated Chen Wei."

"I merely took what was rightfully earned," Jin Tian replied.

"Earned." Liu Feng's lips curled into a smile that didn't reach his eyes. "You're an outer disciple. You've earned nothing. The sect has been generous in allowing you to step onto this stage, but generosity has limits."

"Then show me those limits."

The crowd went silent. The challenge hung in the air like a drawn blade.

Liu Feng laughed, a cold, hollow sound. "You have spirit. I'll give you that. It will make breaking you more satisfying."

The overseer raised his hand. "The match will begin on my signal. No killing blows. A disciple may yield at any time. Victory is declared upon incapacitation, ring out, or submission."

Standard rules. They meant nothing. In matches like this, the only rule that mattered was survival.

The overseer's hand dropped.

Liu Feng moved.

He was fast—faster than any inner disciple Jin Tian had faced in his first life. The distance between them vanished in a heartbeat, his blade already swinging in a wide arc aimed at Jin Tian's throat.

Jin Tian didn't block. He stepped inside the arc, his own sword rising in a tight vertical slash that forced Liu Feng to redirect his attack or lose his arm.

Liu Feng chose to redirect, his blade spinning as he withdrew, creating distance. His eyes narrowed. "Fast reflexes. For an outer disciple."

"You're slower than I expected," Jin Tian replied. "For a seventh-rank inner disciple."

The barb struck home. Liu Feng's face tightened, and his next attack came with visible anger behind it—a flurry of strikes that drove Jin Tian back across the arena floor. Each blow was precise, powerful, meant to overwhelm through sheer force.

Jin Tian weathered the storm. His sword was a blur, deflecting, redirecting, absorbing the impact of each strike through controlled movement. He gave ground, but he did not break.

And all the while, he watched.

Liu Feng's technique was flawless. His footwork was perfect, his timing impeccable, his blade work refined through years of dedicated practice. But beneath the surface, Jin Tian could see the cracks. Small tells. A slight hesitation before certain strikes. A preference for his right side. A tendency to over-rotate on his follow-through.

And then there was the shadow.

It was barely visible, a faint darkness that clung to Liu Feng's blade like a second edge. Most cultivators wouldn't notice it. Most would see only the fire, the light, the brilliance of his techniques.

But Jin Tian had spent three centuries mastering the sword. He had seen through illusions, cut through darkness, walked the path of absolute truth.

He saw the shadow.

And he knew what it meant.
