Liu Feng pressed his advantage, driving Jin Tian to the edge of the arena. "Is this all?" he taunted. "I expected more from someone who dared to challenge the inner sect."

Jin Tian didn't answer. He was calculating, measuring, waiting for the moment when Liu Feng's shadow would reach its peak.

There.

Liu Feng launched another attack, his blade wreathed in crimson flame, the shadow beneath it pulsing with dark energy. It was a killing blow disguised as a standard technique, meant to overwhelm and destroy in a single strike.

Jin Tian dropped.

He fell to one knee, his body twisting as Liu Feng's blade passed over his head, close enough to singe his hair. The momentum carried Liu Feng forward, his balance compromised by the force of his own attack.

And Jin Tian struck.

His sword rose from below, a simple upward thrust aimed at a specific point on Liu Feng's chest. Not the heart. Not the lungs. A point where two meridians crossed, where the conflict between righteous qi and demonic shadow was at its most volatile.

The blade connected.

Liu Feng screamed.

The sound was not human. It was a shriek of pure agony, mixed with the hiss of burning shadow and the crackle of corrupt energy. Liu Feng's body convulsed, his blade falling from nerveless fingers as he clutched at his chest. Dark smoke poured from his mouth, his nose, his eyes, carrying the stench of decay.

The crowd screamed. The overseers rushed forward. Jin Tian stepped back, his sword dripping with dark energy, his heart pounding in his chest.

He had won.

But as Liu Feng fell to his knees, his face twisted in pain and rage, he looked up at Jin Tian with eyes that held no defeat.

Only recognition.

"You know," Liu Feng whispered, his voice a broken rasp. "You know what I am."

Jin Tian said nothing.

Liu Feng laughed, blood and shadow spilling from his lips. "Then you know nothing. I am not the serpent. I am only the first scale."

The overseers reached him, their hands glowing with healing energy. Liu Feng's eyes never left Jin Tian's face.

"He is coming," Liu Feng said, his voice dropping to a whisper that only Jin Tian could hear. "The one who set this all in motion. The one who has been waiting for you since before you were born."

The darkness in Liu Feng's eyes deepened, and for a moment, Jin Tian saw something ancient staring back at him. Something that had been watching. Waiting.

"Your first death was not the end," Liu Feng said, his voice no longer his own. "And your rebirth was not the beginning. You are a piece on a board you cannot see, moved by hands you cannot fathom."

Jin Tian's blood turned to ice.

"Who?" he demanded.

But Liu Feng's eyes rolled back in his head, and his body went limp.

The overseers carried him away, their faces grim. The crowd erupted in chaos, half celebrating Jin Tian's victory, half demanding answers for the darkness they had witnessed.

Jin Tian stood alone in the center of the arena, his sword still in his hand, his mind reeling.

He had come here seeking answers. He had found only more questions.

And somewhere, in the shadows beyond the sect walls, something was laughing.

title: The First Blood
content:
The training grounds were empty.

Jin Tian stood alone in the circular arena, his wooden practice sword resting across his shoulders. The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the packed earth, and somewhere in the distance, he could hear the clash of weapons from the inner disciple quarters. Three hours. He had three hours to prepare for a battle that, in his first life, he had lost before it even began.

Not because Liu Feng was stronger.

But because Jin Tian had been blind.

He closed his eyes, letting the memories surface. The inner division tournament, his first major competition. He had been fourteen, cocky, convinced that his natural talent would carry him through. Liu Feng had been seventeen, already a rising star, his fire techniques the talk of the sect. When they had drawn blades, Jin Tian had expected a duel of equals.

What he got was a massacre.

Liu Feng had moved like water, like wind, like something that shouldn't have been possible for a mere inner disciple. His flames had not been simple fire—they had been alive, seeking, hungry. They had wrapped around Jin Tian's sword, crawled up his arms, burned through his defenses as if they were paper. The match had ended in less than thirty seconds.

And then, years later, Liu Feng's blade had ended his life.

There was a connection there. Jin Tian could feel it, scratching at the edges of his consciousness like a memory just out of reach. Why would a rival become an assassin? Why would a prodigy throw away his future for a single killing blow? The questions haunted him, but they would have to wait.

First, he needed to survive.

He dropped into a fighting stance, his wooden sword extending in a perfect horizontal line. The Sun-Splitter Stance. Basic. Fundamental. The first stance every sword disciple learned. But like everything in cultivation, the basics were only as powerful as the foundation they rested upon.

Jin Tian breathed in. The Qi in his dantian stirred, responding to his call. It was pitifully weak—a trickle compared to the ocean he had commanded in his previous life—but it was enough. He began to move, flowing through the forms with a grace that belied his twelve-year-old body.

The first form: Dawn Cleaver. A simple upward diagonal slash, meant to catch an opponent off guard. Jin Tian executed it perfectly, his blade whistling through the air.

The second form: Solar Rupture. A wide horizontal sweep, designed to create distance. His feet shifted, his hips twisted, and the blade traced a perfect arc.

The third form: Eclipse Fall. The most difficult of the basic stances, requiring precise Qi control to execute properly. Jin Tian's sword dropped in a vertical line, and at the apex of the swing, he released a burst of Qi.

Nothing happened.

He froze, staring at his blade. In his previous life, that technique would have sent a crescent wave of sword Qi flying across the arena, capable of cutting through stone. Now, it was nothing. A puff of wind. A whisper of power that dissipated before it could form.

His cultivation base was too weak. His meridians were too narrow. His Qi reserves were too shallow. He was a child playing at swords, pretending to be the Sword God he had once been.

No. He shook his head, forcing the doubt aside. He was not pretending. He was rebuilding. There was a difference.

He resumed his training, this time focusing on the fundamentals. Footwork. Breathing. The subtle flow of Qi through his meridians. He had spent three hundred years perfecting these movements, and his body remembered even if his cultivation did not. Each step was precise. Each breath was measured. Each swing was deliberate.

By the time the hour bell rang, he had cycled through the basic forms a hundred times. Sweat dripped from his brow, and his muscles screamed in protest, but he felt something stirring within him. A connection. A resonance. His Qi was responding faster now, flowing more freely through channels that had been clogged for years.

He was not ready. Not even close. But he was less unready than he had been three hours ago.

"The match will begin in two hours," a voice said from behind him.

Jin Tian turned to find Elder Zhou standing at the edge of the training grounds, his arms folded behind his back. The old man's face was unreadable, but there was something in his eyes—curiosity, perhaps, or wariness.

"Elder Zhou," Jin Tian said, bowing. "I didn't hear you approach."

"Clearly." The Elder stepped into the arena, his robes brushing against the packed earth. "I watched your forms. They were... adequate."

Jin Tian said nothing. Adequate was high praise from a sect elder, especially one known for his exacting standards.

"You have good fundamentals," Elder Zhou continued, circling him slowly. "Better than most outer disciples, certainly. But fundamentals alone will not save you against Liu Feng. He has been training for fifteen years. He has mastered three fire techniques. He has killed."

The last word hung in the air like a blade.

"Killed?" Jin Tian asked, keeping his voice neutral.

"Beast hunting missions. He has taken down several Qi-condensation level beasts in the eastern forests. Alone." Elder Zhou stopped in front of him, his eyes boring into Jin Tian's. "You have never even drawn blood, boy. Are you sure you want to do this?"

Jin Tian met his gaze steadily. "I am sure."

"Then tell me your plan."

The question caught him off guard. Plans were not something outer disciples were expected to have. They were expected to follow instructions, to learn techniques, to advance through the ranks. Plans were for elders and strategists.

But Jin Tian was not an outer disciple. He was a Sword God wearing the skin of a child.

"Liu Feng relies on his fire techniques," he said slowly, the words forming as he spoke. "His flames are powerful, but they cost him Qi. Every spell, every attack, every defense—it drains him. If I can force him to expend his reserves, he will weaken. His techniques will slow. His flames will dim."

Elder Zhou's eyebrows rose slightly. "And how do you propose to do that?"

"By not engaging."

"Not engaging?"

"I will not fight him head-on. I will not meet his flames with my blade. I will dodge. I will evade. I will make him chase me across the arena until he runs out of breath and Qi alike." Jin Tian's voice hardened. "And when he is exhausted, when his flames are nothing but embers, I will strike."

A long silence followed. Elder Zhou studied him with renewed interest, his eyes narrowing as if seeing him for the first time.

"That is not a plan," he said finally. "That is a gamble. Liu Feng's fire techniques are area-of-effect. He can fill the entire arena with flames if he chooses. Where will you run then?"

Jin Tian smiled. It was not a pleasant smile. "I won't need to run. I'll be inside his guard."

Elder Zhou's face went pale. "You would—"

"It's the last place he would expect me to be. His techniques are designed for ranged combat, for controlling space, for keeping opponents at a distance. If I close the gap, if I get inside his reach, his flames become as dangerous to him as they are to me." Jin Tian's hand tightened on his wooden sword. "It's reckless. It's dangerous. It's probably insane."

"It is," Elder Zhou agreed.

"But it's also the only chance I have."

The Elder was silent for a long moment. Then, slowly, he nodded.

"You are either very brave or very foolish," he said. "I have not yet decided which." He turned and began walking toward the exit. "One hour. The arena is in the eastern courtyard. Do not be late."

He paused at the gate, his back still to Jin Tian.

"And boy? If you somehow survive this, come find me. I may have use for a disciple who thinks like a warrior."

He disappeared through the gate, leaving Jin Tian alone with his thoughts.

One hour.

He sat down cross-legged in the center of the arena, closing his eyes. His Qi was still weak, his cultivation still shallow, but he had something else. Something that Liu Feng did not have.

Three hundred years of combat experience.

He had faced fire cultivators before. Dozens of them. Hundreds. He knew their strengths, their weaknesses, their tells. He knew that fire techniques required constant Qi circulation to maintain, that the flames consumed the user's energy even as they consumed the enemy. He knew that fire cultivators were vulnerable to water techniques, to wind techniques, to anything that disrupted their flow.

But he had none of those. He had a wooden sword and a child's body and a plan that bordered on suicidal.

It would have to be enough.

He began to meditate, calming his breathing, centering his Qi. The world faded around him, replaced by the steady rhythm of his heartbeat and the flow of energy through his meridians. He was not trying to breakthrough to the next cultivation realm—that would take months, if not years. He was simply preparing his body for what was to come.

The minutes passed. The sun climbed higher. And then, finally, the hour bell rang.

Jin Tian opened his eyes.

It was time.

The eastern courtyard was packed.

Hundreds of disciples lined the edges of the arena, their voices a dull roar that filled the air. Jin Tian walked through the crowd, feeling their eyes on him, hearing their whispers. Most were skeptical. Some were amused. A few were openly hostile.

"The outer disciple who challenged Chen Wei."

"He won't last thirty seconds against Liu Feng."

"Look at him. He's just a child."

Jin Tian ignored them all. He stepped into the arena, his wooden sword held loosely at his side. The ground was stone, smooth and flat, perfect for combat. The boundaries were marked by glowing runes that would activate if anyone stepped outside. There was no escape.

Across from him, Liu Feng entered.

The inner disciple was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader, older. He wore the crimson robes of an inner disciple, and his hair was pulled back in a tight ponytail. In his hand, he held a real sword—steel, sharp, gleaming in the afternoon light.

He was smiling.

"An outer disciple," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the arena. "I was told I would have a worthy opponent. Instead, I get a child playing with a stick."

The crowd laughed. Jin Tian did not.

"Let's make this quick," Liu Feng continued, raising his sword. "I have better things to do than—"

The gong sounded.

Jin Tian moved.

He exploded forward, his wooden sword extended, his body low to the ground. It was not a technique. It was not a form. It was pure, raw aggression, designed to close the distance before Liu Feng could react.

The inner disciple's eyes widened. He raised his blade, flames flickering to life along its edge—

Too slow.

Jin Tian's wooden sword slammed into Liu Feng's guard, not with force, but with precision. The tip slipped past the fire-wreathed blade, tapping against Liu Feng's wrist. Once. Twice. Three times.

The flames sputtered. Liu Feng's grip loosened.

And then Jin Tian was inside his guard, too close for the fire sword to be effective, and the real battle began.

title: The Weight of Three Hours
content:
The training grounds of the Azure Cloud Sect stretched across three mountain terraces, each one reserved for disciples of different ranks. The outer disciples trained on the lowest terrace, their wooden swords clacking against practice dummies in monotonous rhythms. Inner disciples claimed the middle terrace, where spirit stones heated the ground and qi-enhanced strikes sent shockwaves through the air. And at the highest terrace, where mist coiled around ancient stone pillars, the core disciples trained in techniques that could shatter mountains.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the middle terrace, watching Liu Feng practice.

The inner disciple moved through a fire-aspected form, his blade leaving trails of orange light that hung in the air like afterimages. Each strike was precise, economical, brutal. He didn't waste movement. He didn't waste qi. Every action served a purpose, and every purpose was destruction.

*He's better than I remember.*

In his first life, Jin Tian had faced Liu Feng in the tournament finals. They had fought for seventy-three exchanges before Jin Tian's sword found an opening. He had won that match. He had been declared champion. And six months later, Liu Feng's blade had found his heart.

The discrepancy had haunted him for three hundred years. Why wait? Why let him rise to the peak of his power before striking? Why not kill him when he was weak, when the tournament ended and his guard was down?

*Because someone gave the order.*

Chen Wei stood at the edge of the training ground, watching Liu Feng with the same cold calculation Jin Tian had seen earlier. The noble-born outer disciple had recovered his composure, his mask of courtesy firmly in place. But his eyes never left the inner disciple's practice.

*They're connected,* Jin Tian realized. *They've always been connected.*

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian."

The voice came from behind him, smooth and measured. Jin Tian turned to find a young woman in inner disciple robes, her hair bound in a severe bun, her expression unreadable. She carried no weapon, but her hands bore the calluses of someone who trained with blades daily.

"I am Inner Disciple Mei Lin," she said. "Elder Zhou has assigned me to escort you to the tournament grounds."

Jin Tian bowed. "This one is honored."

Mei Lin's eyes flickered with something—amusement? Recognition?—before she turned and began walking. "You have two hours and forty-three minutes until your match. The walk to the arena takes fifteen minutes. That leaves you two hours and twenty-eight minutes to prepare."

"You've calculated precisely."

"I calculate everything precisely." She didn't look back. "It's why I'm still alive."

They walked in silence through the winding paths of the sect, past bamboo groves that whispered ancient secrets, past meditation pavilions where disciples sat in perfect stillness, past training halls where the sounds of combat echoed like thunder. Jin Tian memorized every detail, catalogued every face, noted every shift in the wind.

Three hundred years of regression had taught him one thing above all else: the world never stopped moving, and neither could he.

"You fought well in the outer tournament," Mei Lin said, breaking the silence. "Your efficiency score was impressive."

"Thank you."

"I didn't say it was a compliment." She stopped walking and turned to face him. "Efficiency means you studied your opponents. You learned their patterns. You exploited their weaknesses. That requires intelligence, observation, and patience." She paused. "It also requires deception. You deliberately extended your matches to appear average. Why?"

Jin Tian met her gaze. "Would you believe me if I said I was testing my opponents' endurance?"

"No."

"Then I won't insult you with lies." He stepped closer, lowering his voice. "I wanted to see who was watching. I wanted to see who would reveal themselves when they thought I was weak. The tournament was never about winning. It was about identifying threats."

Mei Lin's expression didn't change, but something shifted in her eyes. "And what did you find?"

"More than I expected."

She studied him for a long moment, then nodded once. "Interesting. Most outer disciples think only of advancement. You think of survival." She turned and resumed walking. "You'll need that against Liu Feng. He doesn't fight to win. He fights to break."

*I know,* Jin Tian thought. *I've felt his blade before.*

The tournament arena was carved into the side of the central mountain, a massive amphitheater that could hold ten thousand spectators. Stone seats rose in concentric circles, each level reserved for different ranks of disciples and elders. At the center, a circular platform of white jade gleamed under the afternoon sun, its surface etched with formation arrays that would absorb stray qi and prevent the battle from spilling into the crowd.

Jin Tian had stood on that platform a hundred times in his first life. He had bled on it, triumphed on it, received his rewards on it. But he had never faced Liu Feng there.

*The finals were held on the core disciple platform,* he remembered. *This is a preliminary match. Something changed.*

"Your opponent is already here," Mei Lin said, gesturing toward the arena.

Liu Feng stood at the edge of the platform, his arms crossed, his eyes closed. He wore the red-trimmed robes of an inner disciple, his longsword sheathed at his hip. Around him, the air shimmered with heat, distorting the light like a desert mirage.

He was cultivating. Even now, with a match approaching, he was refining his qi, preparing his body, sharpening his edge.

*That's why he killed me,* Jin Tian realized. *Not out of hatred. Not out of envy. Because he was ordered to, and he never questions orders. He's a weapon, not a man.*

"Two hours," Mei Lin said. "Use them wisely."

She left him at the edge of the arena, disappearing into the crowd of disciples who had gathered to watch. Jin Tian found a quiet corner near the eastern wall, sat cross-legged, and closed his eyes.

He didn't cultivate. He didn't meditate. He remembered.

Three hundred years of regression had given him something no other cultivator possessed: the memories of a lifetime spent mastering the sword. Every technique he had ever learned, every battle he had ever fought, every mistake he had ever made—they were all etched into his soul like scars on a blade.

But memories alone weren't enough. His body was still twelve years old. His qi reserves were still limited. His meridians were still narrow and untrained.

He had knowledge without power.

*Then I'll use knowledge as my weapon.*

In his first life, he had beaten Liu Feng through pure skill. He had been faster, more precise, more adaptable. But that Jin Tian had been twenty years old, at the peak of his physical prime, with decades of cultivation behind him.

This Jin Tian was a child.

*I can't beat him head-on. I need to find another way.*

He replayed their first fight in his mind, frame by frame. Liu Feng's opening stance. His preferred combinations. The tell in his shoulder before he unleashed his signature technique. The way his qi flared when he was about to use fire-aspected attacks.

*Seventy-three exchanges. I won because I exploited his weakness in the twelfth combination. His guard drops on the third strike of the sequence. I can use that.*

But that Jin Tian had been faster. This Jin Tian would need to create an opening, not find one.

*I need him to overextend. I need him to commit to an attack he can't pull back from. I need to make him angry.*

He opened his eyes and looked at Liu Feng again. The inner disciple had finished his cultivation, his body radiating heat like a furnace. His eyes were open now, fixed on the horizon, his expression utterly blank.

*He doesn't care about me. I'm just another opponent. Another obstacle to overcome.*

*Then I'll make him care.*

Jin Tian stood and walked toward the arena. The crowd parted around him, outer disciples and inner disciples alike watching with a mixture of curiosity and pity. They had seen this before—an outer disciple promoted early, thrown against a seasoned inner disciple, crushed in moments.

They expected a massacre.

They were about to be disappointed.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Elder Zhou's voice boomed across the arena. "Inner Disciple Liu Feng. Please take your positions."

Jin Tian stepped onto the jade platform. The formation arrays hummed beneath his feet, ready to absorb the impact of their battle. He drew his sword—a standard-issue outer disciple blade, iron-forged and unremarkable—and held it at his side.

Liu Feng drew his weapon. The longsword caught the light, revealing a blade of deep crimson, forged from fire-iron and quenched in the blood of a flame serpent. It was a weapon worthy of an inner disciple, capable of channeling fire qi with devastating efficiency.

*He's not holding back. He's going to end this quickly.*

"The match will proceed until one combatant yields, is incapacitated, or falls from the platform," Elder Zhou announced. "No killing blows. No permanent maiming. Begin."

Liu Feng moved first.

He exploded forward, his blade trailing fire, his body a blur of motion. The first strike came from above, a diagonal slash meant to split Jin Tian from shoulder to hip. Jin Tian sidestepped, his sword deflecting the blow at the last moment, the impact sending vibrations through his arm.

*Strong. Faster than I remember.*

Liu Feng didn't pause. His second strike came from the side, a horizontal cut aimed at Jin Tian's ribs. Jin Tian dropped low, sweeping his blade at Liu Feng's legs, forcing the inner disciple to leap back.

*He's testing me. Feeling me out.*

They circled each other, the crowd watching in silence. Liu Feng's expression remained blank, but his eyes had sharpened, studying Jin Tian with new interest.

"You're faster than expected," Liu Feng said, his voice flat.

"You're slower than I remember."

Liu Feng's eye twitched. *Good. He has emotions after all.*

The inner disciple attacked again, this time with a flurry of strikes—high, low, high, thrust, slash—each one faster than the last. Jin Tian deflected, dodged, parried, his movements precise but minimal. He conserved his energy, letting Liu Feng expend his.

*Fifty-three seconds. His twelfth combination is coming.*

Liu Feng's blade swept wide, leaving his left side exposed. Jin Tian saw the opening, felt the familiar pull of instinct urging him to strike. But he held back.

*Not yet. He's baiting me.*

Instead of attacking, Jin Tian stepped backward, creating distance. Liu Feng's eyes narrowed, and for the first time, something flickered in them—frustration.

"Why won't you engage?" Liu Feng demanded.

"Because you're waiting for me to commit to an attack so you can counter. Your guard drops on the third strike of your twelfth combination, but it's intentional. You've been fighting that way for years, training your opponents to expect the opening, then punishing them when they take it."

Liu Feng's expression shifted. The blank mask cracked, revealing surprise beneath.

*I've seen this technique before,* Jin Tian thought. *I taught it to you in my first life. You learned it from me.*

"How do you know my style?" Liu Feng asked, his voice low.

"I know many things, Inner Disciple." Jin Tian raised his sword, pointing it at Liu Feng's heart. "I know you've never lost a match. I know you've never been pushed to your limits. I know you're stronger, faster, and more skilled than me."

He paused.

"But I also know you're afraid. You're afraid because I'm an outer disciple who shouldn't

title: The Blade That Remembers
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. Good. He needed silence, needed to think, needed to understand why his hands were trembling.

He sat cross-legged beneath the ancient willow tree at the edge of the practice field, its branches swaying in the afternoon breeze. Three hours until his match against Liu Feng. Three hours until he faced the man who had killed him.

But that was the problem, wasn't it? Liu Feng hadn't killed him. Not really. Liu Feng's blade had pierced his heart, yes, but Liu Feng had been the instrument, not the architect. The true architects had been his seven disciples, and Jin Tian had been so consumed by rage at their betrayal that he had never stopped to ask the obvious question: why Liu Feng?

Why had a rising inner disciple, known for his honorable conduct and fiery temper, agreed to be an assassin's blade?

There was something wrong with the memory. Something he had been too blind to see through the haze of his death throes.

He closed his eyes and let the memory surface.

*The sky was red. No—the sky was burning. The Celestial Summit was in flames, his disciples scattered across the battlefield, fighting against enemies he couldn't see. He had been weakened, poisoned by a toxin that suppressed his qi circulation, but he had still managed to cut down twelve assassins before Liu Feng appeared.*

*"Senior Jin," Liu Feng had said, his voice strange. Hollow. "I'm sorry."*

*And then the blade had moved.*

In his memory, Liu Feng's eyes had been pleading. Not triumphant. Not cruel. Pleading. As if he was asking for forgiveness even as he committed the act.

Jin Tian had never understood that expression. He had been too focused on the pain, the betrayal, the faces of his disciples watching from the shadows. But now, sitting beneath the willow tree with three hundred years of wisdom and a second chance, he finally recognized what he had seen.

Liu Feng's eyes had been the eyes of a man with no choice.

"What did they have on you?" Jin Tian whispered to the wind. "What did they use to make you their weapon?"

The wind didn't answer. But the question burned in his chest like a brand.

He reached into his robes and pulled out the jade slip Elder Zhou had given him. It contained basic information on his opponent: Inner Disciple Liu Feng, age twenty-three, cultivation at the fourth level of Qi Condensation, specializing in fire-aspected techniques with a secondary focus on speed-based footwork. His combat record showed thirty-seven victories, three losses, and a style that favored overwhelming aggression over defensive positioning.

The information was useful, but it told him nothing about the man behind the style.

Jin Tian had three hours. He needed to prepare, but more than that, he needed to know. He needed to see Liu Feng's face when they met, to read the truth in his eyes before the battle began.

He stood and walked toward the inner disciple quarters.

---

The inner disciple compound was a sprawling complex of stone buildings and meditation gardens, separated from the outer sect by a wall of white jade. Jin Tian had once lived here, in a private courtyard with a waterfall and a thousand-year-old cherry tree. He knew every path, every shortcut, every hidden alcove where disciples gathered to share gossip and secrets.

He found Liu Feng exactly where he expected: at the edge of the training grounds overlooking the Spirit Lake, practicing his forms alone.

The man moved like fire. Each strike was a blazing arc, his wooden sword trailing heat shimmer through the air. His footwork was precise, aggressive, always pressing forward, always seeking the angle of attack. It was beautiful to watch. It was also predictable.

Jin Tian watched for a full minute before Liu Feng noticed him.

"You're the outer disciple," Liu Feng said, lowering his sword. His voice was flat, neutral. "The one who challenged Chen Wei."

"I didn't challenge him. I exposed him."

"Same thing in this sect." Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. "What do you want?"

Jin Tian stepped forward, keeping his hands visible, his posture relaxed. He had planned this approach carefully. Direct confrontation would trigger Liu Feng's combat instincts. Subservience would invite contempt. He needed something in between—a curiosity that couldn't be dismissed.

"I want to know why you're going to lose today," Jin Tian said.

Liu Feng's grip on his wooden sword tightened. "That's bold talk for an outer disciple who's never held a real blade in combat."

"I've held blades you can't imagine." Jin Tian smiled, letting just a fraction of his old self bleed through. "But that's not the point. The point is that you're going to lose because you're fighting the wrong battle."

"What are you talking about?"

"You think this match is about proving yourself. About advancing your rank among the inner disciples. About showing Elder Zhou that you deserve better resources." Jin Tian shook his head. "It's not. This match is about someone testing me, and they're using you as the instrument."

Liu Feng's face didn't change, but his shoulders tightened almost imperceptibly. "You're paranoid."

"Am I?" Jin Tian took another step forward. "Let me guess. Someone approached you before the draw. Told you that if you won against me, decisively and publicly, there would be a reward. A technique manual, perhaps. Or a favor from someone high in the sect hierarchy. Someone who could accelerate your advancement by years."

Silence.

The wind picked up, carrying the scent of cherry blossoms across the training ground. Liu Feng's wooden sword trembled in his grip.

"That's not—" Liu Feng started.

"It's the truth, and you know it." Jin Tian cut him off. "Let me tell you what's going to happen in our match. You're going to come at me with everything you have, expecting an easy victory. But I'm going to read every move before you make it. I'm going to counter your fire techniques with the exact water-aspected response they're weakest against. I'm going to use your aggression against you, baiting you into overextending, and when you realize you're losing, you're going to panic and make mistakes."

He paused, letting the words sink in.

"And then, when I win, the person who approached you is going to be very disappointed. And they're going to wonder why their promising instrument failed."

Liu Feng's face had gone pale. "You don't know what you're talking about."

"I know more than you think." Jin Tian met his eyes. "I know you're not a bad person, Liu Feng. I know you accepted this offer because you thought it was harmless, a way to advance without stepping on the wrong toes. But you're being used. The people you're working for don't care about you. They care about results."

"And you care about me?" Liu Feng's voice was bitter.

"No. I care about winning." Jin Tian turned and began walking away. "But I also care about the truth. And the truth is that you're not my enemy. You're just standing in my way."

He stopped at the edge of the training ground, not bothering to look back.

"Here's what I'm offering. Come to the match and fight honestly. Don't hold back, don't throw the match, don't do anything suspicious. Just fight me with everything you have, and let the outcome be what it is. If you win, you win. If you lose, you lose. But you'll have your integrity intact."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then I'll expose you." Jin Tian's voice was cold. "I'll tell Elder Zhou exactly who approached you and what they offered. I'll tell him about every meeting, every promise, every whispered word. And when he investigates, he'll find the truth. Because I know things, Liu Feng. Things I shouldn't know. And that will make him believe me."

He walked away without waiting for a response.

---

Three hours passed like water through fingers.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the tournament platform, feeling the weight of a thousand eyes upon him. The inner division arena was packed with disciples from every level of the sect, drawn by the novelty of an outer disciple challenging an inner elite. Chen Wei sat in the spectators' section, his face carefully neutral, but Jin Tian could feel the hatred radiating from him like heat from a forge.

And there, in the shadows of the eastern gallery, was a figure Jin Tian had hoped he wouldn't see.

Elder Shen.

The head of the Discipline Hall. A man whose reputation for cruelty was legendary even in Jin Tian's first life. A man who had personally overseen the execution of seventeen disciples for treason during the sect's last internal purge. A man whose presence at a simple tournament match meant only one thing.

Someone was expecting violence.

Liu Feng climbed onto the platform opposite him. His face was unreadable, his movements mechanical. He had taken Jin Tian's words to heart, but he had also made a decision. The question was which decision.

"Disciples," Elder Zhou announced, his voice carrying across the arena, "this match will determine the final slot in the inner division tournament. Standard rules apply. First to yield, incapacitation, or ring-out loses. No lethal techniques. Begin."

Liu Feng moved first.

He exploded forward with fire-aspected qi blazing from his blade, a technique designed to overwhelm and intimidate. It was beautiful, aggressive, and entirely telegraphed. Jin Tian sidestepped, his wooden sword tracing a precise arc that redirected Liu Feng's momentum past him.

"You made your choice," Jin Tian said quietly as they passed.

"I made my choice," Liu Feng replied, his voice barely audible. "But it's not the one you think."

He spun, his blade trailing flames, and Jin Tian realized with dawning horror what Liu Feng was about to do.

The attack was aimed not at Jin Tian, but at the platform itself. A fire technique designed to destabilize the arena, to create chaos, to give someone in the shadows an opportunity they weren't supposed to have.

Liu Feng wasn't trying to win.

He was creating a distraction.

The platform cracked. The crowd screamed. And in the chaos, Jin Tian saw a flash of movement from the eastern gallery—a figure in black, moving with killing intent, a blade aimed directly at his heart.

The trap had never been about the match.

It had been about creating an opportunity for assassination.

And Jin Tian had walked right into it.

title: The Truth Beneath the Blades
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the worn stone platforms, and the air carried the faint scent of incense from the main temple. He had exactly two hours and forty-seven minutes until his match against Liu Feng.

He did not waste a single second.

Jin Tian settled into a cross-legged position beneath the ancient willow tree at the edge of the grounds. Its branches hung like silver-green curtains, hiding him from casual view. Behind his closed eyes, the memory of Liu Feng's blade replayed with excruciating clarity.

In his first life, the match had been uneventful. Liu Feng had won, as expected. Jin Tian had been an outer disciple with potential but no real threat to the inner ranks. He remembered leaving the arena with a respectful nod, thinking nothing more of it.

But that was before Liu Feng's sword found his heart three hundred years later.

*Why?* The question burned in Jin Tian's mind. *What connection did we have that I never saw?*

He reached into his storage ring and retrieved a small jade slip. In his first life, he had never bothered to research Liu Feng's background—the man was simply a rival, nothing more. But now, with the eyes of a Sword God, Jin Tian began to piece together what he should have noticed centuries ago.

Liu Feng's fire-aspected techniques were unusual. The Azure Cloud Sect specialized in water and wind cultivation methods. Fire was rare, considered too volatile for their disciples. Yet Liu Feng had not only mastered fire techniques but had developed a unique fusion style that combined flame with sword intent.

*That style,* Jin Tian realized, his eyes snapping open. *I've seen it before.*

Not in this life. Not even in his first life as a disciple. He had seen it in the years before his betrayal, when he had traveled the continent and faced countless opponents. There was a fallen sect in the Southern Wastelands—the Crimson Flame Sect—that had been eradicated three hundred years before Jin Tian's birth. Their techniques had been lost, their lineage supposedly extinct.

But Liu Feng's fire-aspected sword style bore striking similarities to what Jin Tian had read in ancient texts.

"Interesting," he murmured. "Very interesting."

He rose to his feet and began to move through a series of forms, his wooden sword tracing patterns in the air. His body was still weak, his cultivation base barely at the third level of Qi Condensation. But his understanding of sword intent was absolute.

He did not need power. He needed precision.

The first form was defensive—a spiral of energy that could deflect fire-based attacks. The second was a counterstrike, designed to exploit the split-second opening after an opponent committed to an aggressive technique. The third was a killing blow, aimed at the meridian cluster just below the sternum.

Jin Tian practiced each form exactly seven times. In his first life, seven had been his lucky number. He had won seven consecutive tournaments, defeated seven peak masters, and taken seven disciples. The number had meaning.

Perhaps that was why he had been betrayed seven times over.

A rustle in the bushes drew his attention. Jin Tian's hand moved instinctively, his wooden sword whipping around to point at the source of the sound.

"Come out," he said flatly.

A girl emerged from the shadows. She was young—perhaps fourteen or fifteen—with sharp eyes and hair the color of autumn leaves. She wore the robes of an outer disciple, but her bearing suggested something more.

"You're Jin Tian," she said. It was not a question.

"And you are?"

"Lin Yue. I'm assigned to the archives." She stepped closer, her gaze studying him with uncomfortable intensity. "I heard what you did at the selection. Standing up to Chen Wei like that. Bold move."

"I don't recall asking for your opinion."

"Good, because I'm not giving one." Lin Yue reached into her sleeve and pulled out a rolled scroll. "I'm delivering a message. From Elder Zhou."

Jin Tian took the scroll, his expression carefully neutral. He unrolled it and read the contents in silence.

*Jin Tian,*

*Your match against Liu Feng has been moved. Report to the Inner Sanctum at dusk. The location has been changed to the Forbidden Courtyard.*

*Speak of this to no one.*

*—Elder Zhou*

The Forbidden Courtyard.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. In his first life, the Forbidden Courtyard had been the site of an execution—a traitor to the sect who had been put to death in secret. The courtyard was said to be cursed, its ground soaked with the blood of countless betrayers.

Why would Elder Zhou move their match there?

"What does it say?" Lin Yue asked, craning her neck to see.

Jin Tian rolled the scroll back up. "Nothing that concerns you."

"Fine." She shrugged, but her eyes lingered on him a moment too long. "Be careful, Jin Tian. The Forbidden Courtyard isn't a place for matches. It's a place for endings."

She turned and disappeared into the treeline before he could respond.

Jin Tian stood alone beneath the willow tree, the scroll heavy in his hands. The sun had begun its descent, painting the sky in shades of blood and gold.

*Something is wrong.*

In his first life, Liu Feng had killed him without warning or explanation. In this life, the circumstances were already diverging. The match location had changed. A mysterious girl had delivered a cryptic message. And Elder Zhou—a man who had never shown Jin Tian any particular interest—was suddenly pulling strings behind the scenes.

Jin Tian had planned to use this match to gather information. To understand why Liu Feng had betrayed him. But now he realized that the web of conspiracy extended far beyond a single disciple.

The question was: how deep did it go?

---

The Forbidden Courtyard lived up to its name.

It was hidden behind the main temple complex, accessible only through a narrow passage that wound between crumbling walls. The courtyard itself was circular, paved with black stone that seemed to absorb the fading light. At its center stood a single stone pillar, carved with characters that had been worn smooth by centuries of wind and rain.

Jin Tian arrived exactly at dusk. He found Elder Zhou waiting for him, his hands clasped behind his back, his face unreadable.

"You came," Elder Zhou said.

"You summoned me."

"I did." The elder turned to face him, and Jin Tian saw something in his eyes that he hadn't noticed before—a flicker of recognition, as if Elder Zhou saw someone else standing in Jin Tian's place. "Your match against Liu Feng will take place here, in private. No audience. No records."

"Why?"

"Because there are things that need to be settled in the shadows, not in the light." Elder Zhou gestured to the stone pillar. "Do you know what this place is?"

"A courtyard."

"It's an execution ground." Elder Zhou's voice was flat. "For three hundred years, this courtyard has been used to deal with traitors. Those who betray the sect. Those who conspire against their brothers. Those who walk the path of darkness."

Jin Tian's heart hammered against his ribs. *Three hundred years. The exact number.* "Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I need to know whose side you're on."

The words hung in the air like a blade poised to strike. Jin Tian met Elder Zhou's gaze without flinching.

"I'm on the side of the Azure Cloud Sect."

"Are you?" Elder Zhou took a step closer. "Then explain to me why your cultivation method doesn't match any known technique in our archives. Explain why your battle awareness far exceeds what a twelve-year-old outer disciple should possess. Explain why, when I look at you, I see a man wearing a child's face."

Jin Tian's mind raced. He had been careless. In his eagerness to change his fate, he had revealed too much. Elder Zhou was not a fool—he was a cultivator of the Nascent Soul realm, a man who had lived long enough to recognize the unnatural.

"I don't know what you mean," Jin Tian said carefully.

"Don't lie to me." Elder Zhou's aura flared, and suddenly the air in the courtyard became heavy, pressing down on Jin Tian from all sides. "I've lived eight hundred years, boy. I know the difference between talent and experience. Your movements, your strategies, your understanding of sword intent—these are not the marks of a child prodigy. These are the marks of a master."

Jin Tian said nothing.

"I should report you to the Sect Leader," Elder Zhou continued. "A spy. A reincarnator. A demon in disguise. There are a dozen explanations, and none of them are good."

"Then why haven't you?"

"Because I have my own secrets." Elder Zhou's aura receded, and he let out a long breath. "I know what it's like to carry knowledge you shouldn't have. To see patterns that others can't. To walk a path that isolates you from everyone around you."

He turned and walked toward the stone pillar, his robes trailing across the black stone.

"I brought you here because Liu Feng is not your enemy. He is a pawn, just like you. The real enemy is someone else—someone who has been planning your death for a very long time."

Jin Tian's blood froze. "Who?"

"I don't know their name." Elder Zhou reached the pillar and placed his palm against its surface. "But I know they're connected to your past. A past that you're not supposed to remember."

Before Jin Tian could respond, a figure stepped out of the shadows at the far end of the courtyard.

Liu Feng.

He was older than Jin Tian remembered—perhaps seventeen, with sharp features and a bearing that spoke of countless battles. His sword was already drawn, its blade gleaming with a faint orange glow.

"Elder Zhou," Liu Feng said, his voice flat. "You summoned me."

"I did." Elder Zhou stepped aside, leaving the center of the courtyard open. "Your opponent awaits."

Liu Feng's eyes met Jin Tian's, and for a moment, Jin Tian saw something unexpected in them. Not hatred. Not rivalry.

*Fear.*

"You don't belong here," Liu Feng said quietly. "None of us do."

"What do you mean?"

But Liu Feng didn't answer. Instead, he raised his sword, and the fire within it erupted into a blazing inferno.

"Let's end this quickly," he said. "Before they come."

Before Jin Tian could ask who *they* were, the ground beneath his feet began to tremble. The stone pillar cracked, and from within its depths, a voice echoed—ancient, malevolent, and familiar.

*"Jin Tian... you've returned..."*

The world went white.

title: The Poison in the Shadows
content:
The training grounds of the inner division stretched before Jin Tian like a frozen lake of polished stone, ringed by disciples who had come to witness the spectacle. Hundreds of pairs of eyes followed his approach—some curious, some dismissive, and a few sharp with calculation.

He walked alone.

Three hours had passed since Elder Zhou's announcement, three hours that Jin Tian had spent not preparing techniques or meditating on sword forms, but walking. Walking the paths of the sect, letting his feet carry him through familiar grounds while his mind raced through possibilities.

Liu Feng was not his enemy. That had been the first truth Jin Tian had accepted three hundred years ago, watching the fire-aspected disciple drive a blade through his chest. Liu Feng had been a rival, yes. A competitor. But never a conspirator.

Someone had put him up to it.

The question was who. And more importantly—who had the influence to convince a seventh-ranked inner disciple to assassinate a sect elder's favorite?

The answer had come to him as he stood before the pond where he had once taught Wei Chen the flowing water stance. A memory, sharp and clear: Liu Feng's younger sister, Mei Lin, had been diagnosed with a rare qi deviation three months before the tournament. The sect's healers had been unable to cure her. And then, suddenly, she had recovered.

The timing had always bothered him. Now it made sense.

Someone had offered Liu Feng a cure in exchange for a favor. And that favor had been Jin Tian's death.

The crowd parted as he reached the combat platform. Liu Feng already stood at its center, a broad-shouldered youth with flame-red hair and eyes that crackled with barely contained energy. His cultivation base was visible even to untrained observers—five levels higher than Jin Tian's, his qi pressure rolling off him in waves that made the weaker disciples step back.

But Jin Tian saw what others didn't. The slight tremor in Liu Feng's left hand. The way his gaze flickered to the eastern pavilion where the sect elders sat. The tension in his jaw that spoke not of battle readiness, but of dread.

He didn't want to be here.

"Begin!" Elder Zhou's voice rang out.

Liu Feng moved first, as everyone expected. Fire qi exploded from his palms, forming twin whips of condensed flame that lashed toward Jin Tian with killing intent. The attack was brutal, direct, and designed to end the fight in seconds.

Jin Tian sidestepped. Not with the graceful evasion of a master, but with the awkward stumble of a boy who had barely dodged. He let the flames singe his sleeve, let the heat wash over his face, let the crowd believe he was barely surviving.

Liu Feng pressed his advantage, his whips carving arcs of fire through the air. Each strike was precise, controlled, and—Jin Tian noted with cold certainty—aimed to maim, not kill. The attacks targeted shoulders, legs, ribs. Never the throat, never the heart.

Liu Feng was trying to injure him into submission. The killing blow would come later, once the crowd's attention wandered, once the elders looked away.

Jin Tian let the dance continue for three full minutes. He stumbled, dodged, nearly fell. The crowd murmured with disappointment—the outer disciple who had challenged Chen Wei was nothing special after all. Just lucky. Just bold. Just a fool who didn't know his place.

Then Liu Feng's left hand trembled again, and Jin Tian saw his opening.

He moved.

Not with the speed of a twelve-year-old outer disciple, but with the precision of a Sword God who had spent three centuries perfecting a single technique. His blade—borrowed from the sect's armory, nothing but common steel—slipped past Liu Feng's guard and tapped the inner disciple's wrist meridian.

The fire whips dissolved.

Liu Feng stumbled back, his eyes wide with shock. The crowd fell silent.

"Your left hand," Jin Tian said quietly, just loud enough for Liu Feng to hear. "The tremor. It's gotten worse since you started seeing the poison doctor."

Liu Feng's face went white.

"I don't know what they promised you," Jin Tian continued, his voice barely a whisper. "But I know about your sister. I know about the cure. And I know that whoever gave it to you is going to kill you once you've served your purpose."

"You don't—" Liu Feng started, his voice cracking.

"I know more than you think." Jin Tian stepped closer, his blade still raised in a guard position that looked defensive but was anything but. "The woman who visited your sister's room last month. Black hair. Silver eyes. Never speaks above a whisper. She's not a healer. She's a poison master from the Shadow Serpent Sect."

Liu Feng's sword dropped an inch. His face cycled through emotions too fast to track—fear, hope, desperation, and finally, a grim resignation.

"What do you want?" he asked, his voice hollow.

"Your cooperation," Jin Tian replied. "And the truth."

The crowd was growing restless. They had expected a slaughter, not a staredown between two motionless fighters. Elder Zhou was frowning, his hand raised to intercede.

"Three seconds," Jin Tian said. "I'm going to disarm you in three seconds. When you fall, I need you to whisper the name of the person who sent you."

Liu Feng's jaw tightened. "Why should I trust you?"

"Because I'm the only one who can save your sister."

The lie came easily. Jin Tian had no idea if the poison master's cure was real or if it was itself a slow-acting poison designed to ensure Liu Feng's compliance. But he had learned, in his first life, that desperate men didn't need certainty—they needed hope.

Liu Feng's shoulders sagged. "The Eastern—"

He never finished.

A scream ripped through the training grounds, sharp and terrible. The crowd turned as one toward the source—the eastern pavilion where the sect elders had been watching.

Elder Zhou was on his feet, his face ashen. Below him, crumpled on the stone floor, lay an elderly woman in white robes.

The sect's chief healer.

Blood pooled beneath her, black and thick, spreading across the stone like a curse given form.

"Poison!" someone shouted. "The healer has been poisoned!"

Pandemonium erupted. Disciples scattered, elders shouted, and in the chaos, Jin Tian saw Liu Feng's face transform from resignation to terror.

"They found out," Liu Feng whispered. "They knew I would talk. They're cleaning up."

He turned to run, but Jin Tian caught his arm. "Who?"

But Liu Feng's eyes were fixed on something beyond Jin Tian's shoulder. His face drained of all color, and his lips moved soundlessly, forming a single word.

Chen.

Jin Tian spun around, but the crowd was too thick, too chaotic. He saw faces—fearful, confused, panicked—but nothing that confirmed Liu Feng's accusation.

When he turned back, Liu Feng was gone.

And the chief healer was dead.

The tournament was canceled. The inner division was locked down. Sect elders swept through the training grounds like vengeful spirits, demanding answers that no one could give.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the chaos, his borrowed sword still in hand, his mind racing through possibilities.

Liu Feng had been ready to talk. He had been about to name his master. And then, at the exact moment of his surrender, the chief healer had been poisoned.

No. Not poisoned. Killed.

Someone had been watching. Someone had known exactly when to strike. Someone had access to the elders' pavilion, knowledge of the chief healer's schedule, and the ability to administer a poison that would kill instantly.

In his first life, Jin Tian had never discovered who orchestrated his death. He had assumed it was Chen Wei, acting alone, driven by ambition and resentment.

But Chen Wei was a twelve-year-old outer disciple. He didn't have the connections, the resources, or the knowledge to command a poison master from the Shadow Serpent Sect.

Someone else was pulling the strings. Someone who had been planning this for far longer than Jin Tian had realized.

And if they were willing to kill a sect elder's healer to protect their secrets, they were willing to kill anyone.

A hand landed on his shoulder. Jin Tian spun, his blade half-raised, before he recognized the face.

Chen Wei.

The other boy's expression was unreadable, his eyes cold and calculating. "Impressive performance against Liu Feng," he said, his voice flat. "I didn't think you had it in you."

"What do you want?"

Chen Wei smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "The elders are questioning everyone who was near the pavilion. They've already arrested three disciples. They're looking for a scapegoat."

"And you think that scapegoat should be me."

"I think," Chen Wei said, leaning close, "that you've made yourself very interesting to very dangerous people. And I think you should be more careful about whose secrets you try to uncover."

He stepped back, his smile widening. "Good luck, Jin Tian. I have a feeling you're going to need it."

Chen Wei disappeared into the crowd, leaving Jin Tian alone with the weight of his realization.

The poison master wasn't working for Chen Wei.

Chen Wei was working for the poison master.

And somewhere in the shadows of the sect, a conspiracy was unfolding that was far older, far deeper, and far more dangerous than a single assassination three hundred years in the future.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and walked toward the elders' pavilion, his heart cold with determination.

This time, he wouldn't just survive.

This time, he would uncover the truth.

Even if it killed him all over again.

title: The Shadows Beneath the Mask
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. Good. He needed solitude to prepare, to think, to plan. Three hours until he faced Liu Feng—the man who had killed him in his first life, though back then he had been a different person entirely. A Sword God, betrayed by seven disciples, struck down by a rival he had never truly understood.

Jin Tian settled into a cross-legged position beneath the ancient willow tree at the edge of the grounds. Its branches hung like weeping arms, brushing the earth with each gust of wind. He closed his eyes and let his consciousness drift inward, accessing the memories of three hundred years of sword cultivation.

Liu Feng's fighting style had been aggressive, almost reckless. Fire-aspected techniques that burned hot and fast, designed to overwhelm opponents before they could mount a defense. In his first life, Jin Tian had countered with water-aspected techniques, neutralizing Liu Feng's flames with superior elemental control. But that had not saved him.

*Why?*

The question gnawed at him. He had been stronger. Faster. More skilled. And yet Liu Feng's blade had found his heart. There was something he was missing.

"Training so soon after your victory?"

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open. A figure stood at the entrance to the training grounds, silhouetted against the setting sun. Tall. Slender. Female.

Inner Disciple Mei Lin.

She was the fourth-ranked inner disciple, known for her wind-aspected techniques and her reputation as a loner. In his first life, they had barely exchanged a dozen words. She had no allegiance to any faction, no ties to the seven disciples who would later betray him.

But here she was, watching him with an unreadable expression.

"Disciple Mei," Jin Tian said, rising to his feet. "To what do I owe this honor?"

"You're facing Liu Feng in three hours." She walked toward him, her robes billowing despite the absence of wind. "He's asked me to give you a message."

Jin Tian's hand drifted toward his sword. "And what message would that be?"

Mei Lin stopped ten feet away. Her face remained impassive, but her eyes held something. Warning? Concern? He couldn't tell.

"He wants you to know that your victory over Chen Wei means nothing," she said. "That the inner division tournament is not a place for children playing at swordsmanship."

"Children playing," Jin Tian repeated, letting the words hang in the air. "Is that what you think?"

"I think you're either very brave or very stupid." Mei Lin's lips curved into a faint smile. "Liu Feng killed his last opponent in the qualifying rounds. Broke his cultivation base. The boy will never wield a sword again."

"I know."

"You know, and yet you still accepted the match."

"I had no choice."

"There's always a choice." Mei Lin stepped closer, her voice dropping to a whisper. "But some choices lead to death, and some lead to something worse."

Jin Tian studied her. In his first life, Mei Lin had been a non-entity. She had died during a cultivation accident three years from now, her body never recovered from the ruins of a collapsed training cave. No one had mourned her. No one had even noticed.

But now, seeing the sharp intelligence in her eyes, the way her hand rested casually on her sword hilt, he wondered if he had misjudged her.

"What do you want?" he asked.

"To warn you." Mei Lin glanced over her shoulder, as if checking for eavesdroppers. "Liu Feng is not what he appears. The fire-aspected techniques are a facade. His true strength lies elsewhere."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "Explain."

"I've seen him train when he thinks no one is watching. He practices techniques that don't align with his known cultivation path. Shadow techniques. Movement techniques that leave no trace." Mei Lin's voice dropped even lower. "He's hiding something. And whatever it is, it's powerful enough that he's willing to kill to keep it secret."

The pieces clicked into place. Liu Feng's unexpected victory in their first life. The killing blow that should never have landed. The way Jin Tian's water techniques had failed to neutralize the flames.

*Because the flames were never the real threat.*

"Thank you," Jin Tian said, bowing deeply. "I am in your debt."

Mei Lin shook her head. "Don't be. I'm not doing this out of kindness." She turned to leave, then paused. "I'm doing this because I've seen what happens to those who cross Liu Feng. I don't want to see another corpse on the training grounds."

She vanished before he could respond, her figure dissolving into the evening shadows like mist.

Jin Tian stood alone beneath the willow tree, his mind racing. Shadow techniques. Hidden cultivation paths. A secret powerful enough to kill for. This changed everything.

He had planned to fight Liu Feng using the techniques he had mastered in his first life. Water-aspected sword forms, defensive stances, gradual pressure to force mistakes. A safe, predictable strategy that would ensure victory without revealing his true abilities.

But safe strategies were useless against unknown variables.

If Liu Feng had hidden techniques, then Jin Tian would need to adapt. He would need to fight not as the Sword God he had been, but as something else entirely. Something Liu Feng would never expect.

He reached into his spatial ring and withdrew a worn leather pouch. Inside was a single jade slip, chipped and faded with age. He had found it years ago, hidden in the sect library's restricted section. The techniques recorded within were considered lost arts—too dangerous, too unstable for modern cultivation.

Jin Tian had memorized them in his first life but never used them. They required too much qi control, too much precision. One mistake and the backlash would destroy him.

But he was no longer the man he had been. He was the Sword God, reborn with three hundred years of experience. If anyone could master the lost arts, it was him.

He pressed the jade slip to his forehead and let the knowledge flood into his mind.

---

Two hours and fifty-nine minutes later, Jin Tian stood at the entrance to the inner division arena.

The structure was massive, carved from white stone and reinforced with formation arrays that could withstand even Nascent Soul-level attacks. Thousands of seats ringed the central platform, each one filled with disciples, elders, and visiting cultivators who had come to witness the tournament.

Elder Zhou stood at the center of the platform, his voice amplified by qi. "Our next match pits Inner Disciple Liu Feng, ranked seventh, against Outer Disciple Jin Tian, who earned his place through an unexpected victory in the qualifying round."

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. Jin Tian could feel their eyes on him, judging him, dismissing him. An outer disciple challenging an inner disciple was unheard of. An outer disciple winning was impossible.

But Jin Tian had never cared about impossible.

He walked onto the platform, his footsteps steady, his breathing calm. Across from him, Liu Feng emerged from the opposite entrance, his robes blazing with fire-aspected qi.

Liu Feng was handsome in a sharp, angular way. His eyes were like chips of obsidian, dark and cold despite the flames that danced around his body. He smiled as he saw Jin Tian, a predator's smile that promised pain.

"So, the little outer disciple actually showed up," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the arena. "I thought you might run."

"I don't run from dogs," Jin Tian replied.

The crowd gasped. Liu Feng's smile faltered, replaced by a flicker of genuine anger.

"Bold words for a corpse."

"Bold words for a man whose true strength is hidden in shadows."

The change in Liu Feng's expression was subtle, almost imperceptible. But Jin Tian saw it. The tightening of his jaw. The slight widening of his eyes. The way his hand twitched toward his sword.

*He knows what I'm hinting at.*

Liu Feng recovered quickly, his smile returning. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"You will."

Elder Zhou raised his hand. "Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. A burst of flame, a blur of motion, a sword aimed at his throat. It was fast. Deadly. The kind of attack that would have killed any ordinary outer disciple.

But Jin Tian was not ordinary.

He sidestepped, his body flowing like water, and drew his sword in a single motion. The blade caught the light, reflecting Liu Feng's flames back at him. Liu Feng's eyes widened as he was forced to shield his face, his attack disrupted.

"Impressive," Liu Feng said, recovering quickly. "But that was my warm-up."

He attacked again, this time with a series of rapid strikes. Fire exploded from his sword with each swing, forcing Jin Tian to dodge, weave, block. The crowd roared with excitement, their cheers drowning out the clash of steel.

Jin Tian let himself be pushed back, let Liu Feng think he was winning. He parried and dodged, never committing to a counterattack, always staying just one step ahead of the flames.

*Show me your true technique.*

Liu Feng grew frustrated. His attacks became wilder, more desperate. He was used to winning quickly, to overwhelming his opponents with raw power. Jin Tian's refusal to fall was throwing him off balance.

"Stand still!" Liu Feng roared, unleashing a wave of fire that engulfed the entire platform.

Jin Tian leaped, using the flames as cover to launch himself into the air. He twisted mid-flight, his sword aimed at Liu Feng's exposed back.

But Liu Feng was ready.

He spun, his eyes burning with dark light. The flames around him flickered and died, replaced by something else. Shadows. They writhed and twisted around his body, forming a barrier of pure darkness.

Jin Tian's sword struck the barrier and stopped, unable to penetrate.

"You wanted to see my true strength?" Liu Feng's voice had changed. It was deeper now, more resonant, as if two voices spoke at once. "Then see it, and despair."

The shadows exploded outward, swallowing the arena in darkness.

Jin Tian could not see. Could not hear. Could not feel anything except the cold touch of shadow qi pressing against his skin.

*Shadow Domain.*

A lost technique. A forbidden art. One that required sacrificing a piece of one's soul to master.

Liu Feng had not just been hiding his strength. He had been hiding a deal with something far darker than any demon.

Jin Tian smiled in the darkness.

*Perfect.*

He had been waiting for this. The lost arts he had memorized, the techniques he had never used—they were designed specifically to counter shadow domains.

He sheathed his sword and closed his eyes.

The Shadow Domain was a prison of the senses. It fed on fear, on doubt, on the victim's inability to trust their own perception. To fight it, one had to abandon perception entirely.

Jin Tian's breathing slowed. His heart rate dropped. His qi settled into a rhythm that matched the pulse of the shadow itself.

He had spent three hundred years mastering the sword. But he had also spent those years learning to become the sword.

And swords do not see. Swords do not hear. Swords only cut.

Liu Feng's attack came from behind, silent and deadly. The shadow-blade pierced through where Jin Tian had been standing a moment before.

But Jin Tian was already moving.

He flowed through the darkness like water through cracks in stone, his body guided by instinct honed over centuries. He felt the ripples in Liu Feng's shadow qi, felt the spaces where the domain was weakest.

He found the gap.

His sword flashed, not with qi, not with light, but with pure intent. The blade struck the nexus point of the Shadow Domain, the place where Liu Feng's

title: The Serpent Beneath the Mask
content:
The arena was smaller than Jin Tian remembered.

Three hundred years of dust and decay had warped his memories, made the past grander than it truly was. The actual combat platform was a simple stone circle, thirty meters across, scarred by countless battles and stained with the blood of defeated disciples. Bleachers rose on three sides, packed with hundreds of students, their voices a constant hum of anticipation.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the platform, his wooden practice sword feeling foreign in his hands. The tournament rules prohibited killing weapons for preliminary matches—a formality that would be discarded in the later rounds. But rules had never stopped a determined killer.

Across the arena, Inner Disciple Liu Feng was stretching with practiced ease. He was older than Jin Tian remembered, perhaps eighteen or nineteen, with a lean build that spoke of speed over raw power. His cultivation base was at the seventh level of Qi Condensation—three levels above Jin Tian's current fourth level. In a fair fight, the gap should have been insurmountable.

But Jin Tian had never fought fair.

"The rules are simple," Elder Zhou announced from the elevated judging platform. "First to yield, ring out, or incapacitation. No killing. The match begins when the gong sounds and ends when I declare it over."

Liu Feng rolled his shoulders and drew his own wooden sword. His movements were fluid, unhurried, the confidence of someone who had never faced a real challenge from the outer division. He was ranked seventh among the inner disciples for a reason.

The gong rang.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. A burst of fire-aspected qi flared around his blade, turning the wooden sword into a streak of orange light. He closed the distance in three steps, his strike aimed at Jin Tian's shoulder—a test, not a killing blow.

Jin Tian sidestepped.

It was a simple movement, but executed with a precision that made the watching crowd gasp. He didn't block, didn't parry, didn't counter. He simply wasn't where the strike landed.

Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly. He recovered quickly, spinning into a follow-up slash that would have caught most opponents across the ribs. Jin Tian ducked under it, the wooden blade passing inches above his head, close enough that he felt the heat radiating from the qi-infused weapon.

"You're fast," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the arena. "But speed without power is meaningless."

He launched a flurry of attacks—five strikes in three seconds, each one aimed at a different vital point. The moves were textbook perfect, the kind of technique that had earned him his rank among the inner disciples.

Jin Tian weaved through them like smoke.

He didn't need to block because he never needed to be where the blade was. In his first life, he had studied Liu Feng's style extensively. They had sparred dozens of times, fought side by side in dungeon raids, even shared meals and conversations. Jin Tian knew Liu Feng's tells—the slight twitch of his left eye before a feint, the way his weight shifted a fraction of a second before a heavy strike, the rhythm of his breathing that accelerated when he was about to use his signature technique.

Liu Feng was predictable.

The crowd was growing restless. They had expected a quick victory for the inner disciple, a demonstration of the gap between the divisions. Instead, they were watching a twelve-year-old boy dance around one of the sect's rising stars.

Liu Feng's face reddened. He was being made a fool of, and he knew it. His attacks grew more aggressive, less controlled. He abandoned the measured approach and began throwing everything he had at Jin Tian—overhand chops, spinning slashes, even a kick aimed at Jin Tian's knee.

Jin Tian caught the kick.

The arena went silent.

He had grabbed Liu Feng's ankle with his free hand, stopping the attack cold. For a moment, the two of them stood frozen—Liu Feng balanced on one leg, his expression shifting from shock to fury, and Jin Tian holding him in place with an iron grip that shouldn't have been possible for a fourth-level disciple.

"Impressive," Liu Feng hissed. "But you've made a mistake."

Fire qi exploded from his leg, racing up Jin Tian's arm. The heat was intense, enough to blister skin and melt flesh. Jin Tian released him immediately, stepping back as the flames licked at his sleeve. The fabric blackened and curled, but his arm remained unharmed—he had withdrawn his qi to protect himself the instant he felt the surge.

Liu Feng landed gracefully, his composure restored. "You know my techniques. You've studied me. That means you've been preparing for this fight for a long time." He tilted his head, studying Jin Tian with new eyes. "Who are you really, outer disciple?"

Jin Tian didn't answer. He couldn't. The truth would sound insane, and even if he told it, no one would believe him. A man reborn from three hundred years in the future, betrayed by his disciples and killed by the very person standing before him? It was the stuff of legends and madness.

"I'm just someone who wants to understand," Jin Tian said instead.

"Understand what?"

"Why you killed him."

The words slipped out before Jin Tian could stop them. Liu Feng's expression flickered—confusion, then recognition, then something that looked almost like fear. It was there and gone in an instant, but Jin Tian saw it. He saw it clearly.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Liu Feng said, but his voice had lost its confident edge.

"Don't you?"

The gong sounded again—a signal for a temporary pause. Elder Zhou rose from his seat, his brow furrowed. "The match is not progressing as expected. Both disciples, approach."

Jin Tian and Liu Feng walked to the center of the platform, their weapons lowered but not sheathed. The crowd leaned forward, sensing that something unusual was happening.

"Elder," Liu Feng said, bowing respectfully. "This match has revealed certain irregularities. This outer disciple seems to possess knowledge of inner division techniques that should be beyond his station. I suspect he may have been receiving unauthorized instruction from an inner disciple, or worse—stealing techniques through espionage."

The accusation was clever. It shifted suspicion onto Jin Tian while casting Liu Feng as the wronged party, a victim of cheating rather than a fighter struggling against a weaker opponent.

Elder Zhou's eyes narrowed. "Is this true, Jin Tian? How do you know Liu Feng's combat patterns?"

Jin Tian had expected this question. He had prepared for it. But the truth was still a dangerous thing to reveal.

"Elder Zhou," Jin Tian said, keeping his voice steady, "I have been studying the inner division disciples since I joined the sect. I watch their matches, analyze their techniques, and memorize their patterns. There is nothing forbidden about observation. The sect's library contains records of every match fought in this arena for the past fifty years. I have read them all."

Liu Feng laughed. "Impossible. No one has read all those records. There are thousands of them."

"Four thousand, seven hundred and sixty-three," Jin Tian replied. "I started with the oldest and worked my way forward. I can recite the details of any match you choose to name."

The crowd erupted in whispers. Elder Zhou raised an eyebrow, clearly intrigued. "Name a match, Liu Feng."

Liu Feng's face contorted. He was trapped. If he named a match and Jin Tian recited it correctly, he would be humiliated. If he refused, he would look like a coward. He chose a middle path.

"Match 2,341," he said. "Inner Disciple Wei Chen versus Outer Disciple Zhang Lu."

Jin Tian didn't hesitate. "Year 43 of the Azure Calendar, autumn tournament. Wei Chen won by ring out in the third minute of the match. The decisive technique was a palm strike to Zhang Lu's chest, which sent him flying. However, Zhang Lu had already sustained an injury to his right leg from a previous match, which affected his mobility. Wei Chen exploited this weakness by attacking from Zhang Lu's right side repeatedly until he lost his balance and fell from the platform."

The silence that followed was absolute.

Elder Zhou's face was unreadable. Liu Feng looked like he had swallowed poison. The crowd held its breath, waiting for the verdict.

"You have been busy, haven't you?" Elder Zhou said finally. There was no anger in his voice, only curiosity. "This level of dedication is unusual for an outer disciple. Most are content to simply train and advance. You have chosen to study."

"I believe knowledge is as important as strength, Elder."

"An interesting philosophy." Elder Zhou stroked his chin. "Very well. The match will continue. But I warn you both—no more delays. The next exchange will determine the victor."

Liu Feng's eyes met Jin Tian's across the platform. There was something new in them now, something that hadn't been there before. Recognition, but not of the kind Jin Tian had expected. It was the look of a man who had just realized he was fighting a ghost.

"When this is over," Liu Feng said quietly, so only Jin Tian could hear, "we need to talk."

"Why?"

"Because I think I know who you really are." Liu Feng's voice dropped to barely a whisper. "And I think you're about to make the same mistake you made last time."

The gong rang.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

*Last time.*

Three words that shattered everything he thought he understood.

Before he could react, Liu Feng attacked—not with the fire-aspected techniques he had used before, but with something entirely different. His blade moved in a pattern that Jin Tian had never seen, a style that didn't exist in any of the records he had studied.

A style that looked ancient.

A style that looked familiar.

Jin Tian barely deflected the first strike. The impact jarred his arms, sending shockwaves through his body. The second strike came from an angle he couldn't predict, catching him across the ribs and sending him stumbling backward.

"You're not the only one who remembers," Liu Feng hissed, pressing his advantage. The wooden blade in his hands seemed to move of its own accord, dancing through the air in patterns that defied logic. "I was there too, Sword God. I was there when you fell."

Jin Tian's mind raced. If Liu Feng remembered the future, then everything he thought he knew about his death was wrong. Liu Feng hadn't been a pawn or a tool. He had been something else entirely.

Someone who had been waiting for this moment.

Someone who knew Jin Tian was coming.

Someone who had been preparing for this fight for just as long as Jin Tian had.

The blade caught him across the face, sending him sprawling to the ground. Blood dripped from a cut above his eye, staining the stone platform red.

Liu Feng stood over him, his wooden sword pointed at Jin Tian's throat. "Yield," he said. "Or I'll kill you right here, rules or no rules."

Jin Tian looked up at his killer, his former disciple, the man who had been waiting for his return.

And he smiled.

"I didn't come back to yield," he said.

The ground beneath him exploded.

title: The Puppet's Strings
content:
The tournament arena hummed with anticipation as Jin Tian stepped onto the jade platform. Three hours had passed like water through fingers—barely enough time to meditate, to center his qi, to prepare for the confrontation he had waited three centuries to understand.

Liu Feng stood across from him, already in stance. The inner disciple's robes were embroidered with flame motifs that seemed to flicker in the afternoon light. His blade was drawn—a curved saber with a crimson edge that pulsed with heat distortion.

"Outer disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying the practiced authority of someone who had never known defeat. "I expected Chen Wei. Instead, I get a child who doesn't know when to stay in his place."

Jin Tian drew his sword slowly, deliberately. The motion was fluid, unhurried, and it made Liu Feng's eyes narrow.
