"Funny," Jin Tian replied. "I expected someone taller. The man who killed me must have been a giant, surely. But you're just... average."

The words landed like stones in still water. Liu Feng's composure cracked for a fraction of a second—a flicker of confusion crossing his features before the mask of arrogance slammed back into place.

"You speak of things that haven't happened, boy. Delusions of grandeur are common among outer disciples who rise too fast."

"Am I delusional?" Jin Tian shifted his weight, settling into a stance that made Liu Feng's saber hand twitch. The stance was ancient. Forbidden. A style that wouldn't be invented for another two hundred years. "Or am I remembering things you'd rather forget?"

The gong sounded.

Liu Feng moved first—a blazing strike that left trails of fire in the air. In Jin Tian's first life, this attack had been overwhelming, a storm of qi and heat that had forced him onto the defensive from the opening exchange.

Now, he simply stepped left.

The fire strike passed inches from his shoulder, close enough to singe his robes but not close enough to wound. Jin Tian's counter came without thought—a precise thrust aimed at the meridian point just below Liu Feng's right armpit.

Liu Feng's eyes went wide. He twisted, barely avoiding the strike, and the tip of Jin Tian's sword drew a thin line of blood across his ribs.

"You—" Liu Feng stumbled back, hand pressed to the wound. "That's not possible. You can't know that stance. You can't know my weakness."

"I know a lot of things, Liu Feng." Jin Tian advanced, his sword becoming a blur of silver. "I know you favor your right side after your first three strikes. I know you overextend on your Flame Crescent technique. I know that when you're afraid, you clench your jaw and your left eye twitches."

With each word, he pressed harder. Liu Feng retreated, his perfect form dissolving into desperate blocks and parries. The crowd watched in stunned silence as the seventh-ranked inner disciple was driven back by a twelve-year-old boy.

"What do you want?" Liu Feng snarled, finally managing to create distance between them. His saber blazed with renewed fury, the heat becoming oppressive even from twenty feet away. "If you know so much, then you know I haven't done anything to you!"

"In this timeline, no." Jin Tian's voice dropped low, barely audible over the crackling flames. "But in another life, your blade found my heart. I want to know why."

Liu Feng froze. The fire around his saber flickered, dimmed, and for a moment he looked genuinely afraid.

"You remember," he whispered. "Sweet heavens, you actually remember."

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow. He had expected denial. Confusion. Perhaps even laughter at his "delusions." He had not expected recognition.

"You know what I'm talking about."

"I know nothing." Liu Feng's voice was too quick, too defensive. "I don't know what game you're playing, boy, but I want no part of it."

"Game?" Jin Tian's grip on his sword tightened until his knuckles went white. "You think this is a game? I died, Liu Feng. I felt your blade tear through my cultivation base. I watched my disciples—my disciples—stand over my corpse and divide my possessions like vultures."

The arena had gone completely silent. Even the overseers seemed frozen, uncertain whether to intervene or to let the drama unfold.

Liu Feng's face cycled through a dozen emotions in the span of a heartbeat. Fear. Anger. Desperation. And finally, something that looked almost like resignation.

"It wasn't supposed to happen that way," he said, his voice barely a whisper. "You were never supposed to find out."

"Find out what?"

But before Liu Feng could answer, a bell tolled three times from the main hall. Every head in the arena turned toward the sound. The Sect Master's bell. It only rang for matters of utmost importance.

Elder Zhou's face had gone pale. He raised a hand, signaling the match to halt. "All disciples, remain where you are. Sect Master Wei has summoned an emergency gathering."

Sect Master Wei.

The name crashed through Jin Tian's mind like thunder. In his first life, Sect Master Wei had been a distant figure, a benevolent leader who had entrusted him with the sect's future. He had been the one to promote Jin Tian to elder. The one who had praised his loyalty, his dedication, his purity of heart.

The one who had been conspicuously absent on the day of Jin Tian's death.

The crowd parted as Sect Master Wei walked into the arena. He was exactly as Jin Tian remembered—tall, silver-haired, with eyes that held the weight of centuries. But there was something different in his bearing now. Something that Jin Tian had been too blind, too trusting, to see in his first life.

The Sect Master's gaze swept across the gathered disciples before settling on Jin Tian. And in that moment, Jin Tian saw it.

Recognition.

Not the recognition of a master seeing a promising disciple. But the recognition of a hunter seeing prey that had somehow escaped the trap.

"Well done, outer disciple Jin Tian." Sect Master Wei's voice was warm, fatherly, the same voice that had praised Jin Tian's achievements in his first life. "Your performance today has exceeded all expectations. Truly, you are a diamond in the rough."

He approached the platform, and the disciples bowed. Jin Tian did not. He stood straight, sword still in hand, watching the man who had betrayed him.

"Your match will be rescheduled," the Sect Master continued, seemingly unbothered by Jin Tian's lack of deference. "There are matters we must discuss. Important matters. Matters of the sect's future."

"I'm sure there are," Jin Tian said flatly.

The Sect Master's smile didn't waver, but his eyes hardened. "Come. Walk with me."

It was not a request.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword, acutely aware of every pair of eyes on him. He could feel Chen Wei's glare boring into his back. He could sense Liu Feng's fear radiating like heat from a forge. And above all, he could taste the poison in the Sect Master's honeyed words.

They walked in silence through the sect's inner courtyards, past training grounds and meditation halls, until they reached a secluded garden that Jin Tian remembered well. It was here, in this very garden, that the Sect Master had first told Jin Tian he would be the sect's future leader.

"I've been watching you, Jin Tian." The Sect Master stopped beside a koi pond, his reflection rippling on the water's surface. "From the moment you defeated Chen Wei with that impossible technique. I knew then that you were special."

"Special," Jin Tian repeated. "Is that what you called me before you had me killed?"

The Sect Master's reflection smiled. "Ah. So you do remember. I had hoped it was merely a coincidence, a fluke of reincarnation. But you remember everything, don't you?"

"Why?"

"Because you were never meant to be the Sword God, Jin Tian." The Sect Master turned to face him, and his kindly mask had finally fallen away. Beneath it was something ancient, predatory, hungry. "You were meant to be a vessel. A perfect vessel for the Heavenly Demon's reincarnation. Your talent, your cultivation, your body—all of it was cultivated for a single purpose."

The words struck Jin Tian like a physical blow. "You were raising me like livestock."

"Like a sacred vessel," the Sect Master corrected. "The Heavenly Demon's soul was supposed to merge with yours at the peak of your power. Your disciples were instructed to deliver you alive, your cultivation base intact, so the transfer could be completed. But they failed. They killed you instead of subduing you."

"And now?" Jin Tian's hand drifted toward his sword. "You'll try again?"

"No." The Sect Master's smile was terrible. "Now I have a better idea. You've already proven you can retain memories across death. That means your soul is stronger than I anticipated. Strong enough, perhaps, to serve as the Heavenly Demon's vessel without needing to be broken first."

"You're insane."

"Perhaps. But I am also your Sect Master. And as your Sect Master, I command you to submit."

The pressure that descended on Jin Tian was unlike anything he had ever felt. It wasn't qi. It wasn't spiritual pressure. It was something older, darker, a presence that pressed against his soul like a black sun.

The Heavenly Demon.

It was already inside the Sect Master. It had always been inside him.

"You will kneel, vessel." The voice that spoke was not the Sect Master's. It was deeper, layered, resonating with the screams of a million tormented souls. "You will kneel, and you will accept your purpose."

Jin Tian's knees buckled. His body screamed at him to submit, to surrender, to let go of the exhausting burden of consciousness.

But he was the Sword God.

He had been betrayed, murdered, and reborn. He had watched his disciples, his sect, his entire life crumble to dust. He had clawed his way back from death itself.

He would not kneel.

"No."

The word came out as a snarl, raw and defiant. Jin Tian drew his sword, and for the first time, the Heavenly Demon's pressure faltered.

"You will not break me," Jin Tian said, his voice rising with each word. "You will not use me. And you will die—again—before I let you take another life."

The Heavenly Demon laughed. It was a sound like grinding bones, like collapsing mountains, like the death of hope itself.

"Bold words, little sword. But you have forgotten one crucial detail."

"What?"

"You are still in my sect. Surrounded by my disciples. Watched by my eyes." The Heavenly Demon's smile widened, splitting the Sect Master's face in ways that should have been impossible. "And I have already sent word to your family."

Jin Tian's blood turned to ice.

"Your mother. Your father. Your little sister, who just turned seven." The Heavenly Demon's voice dripped with mock sympathy. "They are being brought to the sect as we speak. For their safety, of course. The sect is so worried about the threats against its promising young disciples."

"You wouldn't."

"Wouldn't I?" The Heavenly Demon tilted its head, and in that motion, Jin Tian saw the fate of everyone he loved hanging in the balance. "Submit to me, Jin Tian. Become my vessel willingly, and your family lives. Resist, and they will suffer fates worse than death."

Jin Tian stood frozen, his sword trembling in his hand, the weight of three hundred years of hatred and loss pressing down on him.

And for the first time since his rebirth, he did not know what to do.

The Heavenly Demon

title: The Serpent's Fang
content:
The training grounds lay empty in the twilight hour, mist curling like ghostly fingers across the stone tiles. Jin Tian stood alone in the center ring, his borrowed practice sword held loosely at his side.

Three hours until the match.

He had used the first hour to meditate, to calm the storm that Liu Feng's name had unleashed within him. The second hour he spent reviewing everything he knew about his would-be killer—techniques, habits, weaknesses.

The third hour he would spend preparing for the unexpected.

"Impressive dedication."

Jin Tian didn't turn. He had felt the presence approaching from the moment it entered the grounds—light footsteps, controlled breathing, the subtle shift of qi being suppressed rather than unleashed.

Han Xiu stepped out of the shadows, her white robe stained with blood that wasn't her own.

"The infirmary?" Jin Tian asked.

"Three disciples attacked me on the way back from the archives." She said it like she was commenting on the weather. "They were waiting for me."

"Chen Wei's men?"

"One of them, yes. The other two wore inner disciple robes." She pulled a jade slip from her sleeve and tossed it to him. "But that's not important. I found something you need to see."

Jin Tian caught the slip and pressed his spiritual sense into it. The information hit him like a blade to the gut.

The sect's registry of life-binding contracts.

Every master-disciple relationship in the Heaven's Sword Sect was recorded here, bound by spiritual oaths that couldn't be broken without severe consequences. The contracts were sealed, accessible only to elders and the sect master.

Someone had broken the seal.

And within the records, Jin Tian found a name he recognized.

*Elder Zhou. Bound to Inner Disciple Liu Feng. Contract effective: seventeen years ago.*

Seventeen years. That meant Elder Zhou had taken Liu Feng as a disciple almost immediately after the boy joined the sect—an unprecedented move for a man who had refused every applicant for decades.

"Elder Zhou is Liu Feng's master," Han Xiu said, her voice flat. "The same Elder Zhou who just assigned you to face him. The same Elder Zhou who overturned decades of precedent to give you that opportunity."

Jin Tian's mind raced. In his first life, he had never known this. Liu Feng had always seemed independent, unaffiliated with any particular elder. He had risen through the ranks on his own merit—or so everyone believed.

"Why would they hide it?" he murmured.

"Because Elder Zhou isn't supposed to take disciples. He took a vow of non-interference after the Sun-Moon Rebellion. If the sect master knew he'd broken that vow, he'd be expelled."

"Then how did he hide it for seventeen years?"

"The contract is forged. Look at the binding patterns."

Jin Tian looked again, and this time he saw it—the subtle wrongness in the qi signatures, the way the spiritual threads twisted instead of flowing straight. It was masterful work, almost impossible to detect without knowing what to look for.

But it wasn't perfect.

"Someone in the registry office helped him," Jin Tian said.

"Dead. Three months ago. Qi deviation."

"How convenient."

"Indeed." Han Xiu's eyes met his. "There's more. I found references to other forged contracts. At least twelve, possibly more. All connected to Elder Zhou's inner circle."

Twelve disciples bound to a man who had sworn never to interfere in sect politics. Twelve knives waiting to be unleashed.

"Chen Wei's father is an elder," Jin Tian said slowly. "Is he connected?"

"Not directly. But his resources come from somewhere, and the Chen family has been unusually prosperous lately. New techniques, rare medicines, weapons that shouldn't exist outside the inner vault."

Jin Tian handed the jade slip back. "This is enough to destroy Elder Zhou. Why are you giving it to me instead of taking it to the sect master?"

"Because I would never reach him." Han Xiu's voice dropped. "The sect master has been 'indisposed' for three weeks. No one has seen him except Elder Zhou and the senior council. The story is that he's in seclusion, cultivating a new technique."

"But you don't believe that."

"I believe that when a man who has never missed a council meeting in forty years suddenly vanishes, and the man who hates him most takes control of the tournament schedule, something is very wrong."

The implications settled over Jin Tian like a shroud. The tournament wasn't just about choosing representatives for the celestial summit. It was about consolidating power. Elder Zhou was using the chaos to position his people, eliminate his enemies, and—

And Jin Tian had just walked into the center of his web.

"Why me?" he asked. "I'm an outer disciple. Barely worth noticing."

"You humiliated Chen Wei publicly. You demonstrated knowledge you shouldn't have. And you've been asking questions about the seven peaks that no outer disciple should know about."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. The seven peaks. In his first life, those were the territories his seven disciples had claimed after his death. In this life, they were just remote training grounds—but he had been visiting them, searching for clues, trying to understand when and how the betrayals had begun.

Someone had been watching.

"Elder Zhou knows," he said.

"He knows you've been to every peak in the last month. He knows you've been reading restricted archives. He knows you've been avoiding the surveillance formations that monitor outer disciples." Han Xiu paused. "He doesn't know how, but he knows you're dangerous."

"That's why he gave me the slot in the tournament. Not to test me. To put me in a place where he can control the outcome."

"And if you survive Liu Feng, there will be others. Stronger ones. Each match designed to eliminate you without anyone questioning it."

Jin Tian looked at his practice sword. The blade was chipped, the grip worn smooth from years of use. In his first life, he had wielded swords that could cut mountains. Now he was lucky to have a weapon that wouldn't shatter on impact.

"Then I'll have to make sure Liu Feng doesn't survive."

"That's not—" Han Xiu stopped. "You can't kill an inner disciple in a tournament match. The rules forbid lethal force."

"The rules forbid intentional killing. But accidents happen. Qi deviations. Technique failures. A blow that lands harder than expected."

"You're talking about murder."

"I'm talking about survival." Jin Tian met her eyes. "Liu Feng killed me once. I don't intend to give him a second chance."

Han Xiu was silent for a long moment. Then she reached into her robe and pulled out a small wooden box, lacquered black and bound with silver wire.

"Then you'll need this."

Jin Tian took the box carefully. The moment his fingers touched the wood, he felt it—a pulse of spiritual energy so dense it made his teeth ache.

"What is this?"

"Something I stole from the inner vault. A fragment of the Sun-Moon Rebellion's legacy." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Consider it payment for the jade slip you gave me last week. The one with the technique that saved my life."

Jin Tian opened the box.

Inside, nestled on silk the color of dried blood, lay a single finger bone. It was ancient, yellowed with age, and carved with characters so small they seemed to writhe when he looked at them directly.

"Sun-Moon Art. First Form: Eclipse," Han Xiu said. "The technique that allowed a mortal to wound a Nascent Soul cultivator. It will destroy your meridians to use it, but—"

"But it will kill Liu Feng."

"Without question."

Jin Tian closed the box. His hand was steady, but his heart pounded against his ribs like a caged beast. This was the kind of power he had sworn never to touch—forbidden, corrupted, born from the same rebellion that had nearly destroyed the sect.

But the rebel hadn't been wrong. He had simply been desperate.

Just like Jin Tian.

"How long to learn it?"

"Three hours, if you're willing to sacrifice your cultivation base."

Jin Tian's cultivation base. Years of painstaking work, rebuilding his meridians, gathering spiritual energy, slowly climbing back toward the power he had once commanded. If he used this technique, he would lose it all.

He would be a mortal again.

But he would be alive.

"Teach me," he said.

Han Xiu's expression flickered—something like respect, something like sorrow. "I was hoping you'd say that. And dreading it."

She began to explain the technique, her voice low and urgent. The characters on the bone glowed as she spoke, their light casting twisted shadows across the training ground.

Jin Tian listened with every fiber of his being, memorizing, understanding, preparing.

Three hours.

Three hours until he faced the man who had killed him.

Three hours until he discovered whether the first lesson of his second life would be revenge or ruin.

And somewhere in the shadows beyond the training grounds, someone was watching. Someone who had been watching since the moment Jin Tian woke up in his twelve-year-old body.

Someone who smiled as the Eclipse technique began to burn its way into Jin Tian's meridians.

*Perfect,* the watcher thought. *He's finally ready.*

title: The Shadow Beneath the Arena
content:
The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the training grounds as Jin Tian made his way toward the outer disciple dormitories. His victory over Chen Wei had sent ripples through the sect, but he knew better than to celebrate. The tournament was tomorrow, and Liu Feng awaited.

But as he rounded the corner near the bamboo grove, something stopped him cold.

A figure stood beneath the swaying stalks, half-hidden in shadow. Tall. Lean. Wearing the dark robes of an inner disciple, but with a crimson crest that Jin Tian had never seen before—a serpent coiled around a broken sword.

"You move well for an outer disciple," the figure said, stepping into the light. It was a woman, perhaps twenty years old, with sharp cheekbones and eyes like chips of obsidian. Her hair was cropped short, military style, and a thin scar ran from her temple to her jaw. "I watched your match against Chen Wei."

Jin Tian's hand drifted toward his sword. "Who are you?"

"Someone who recognizes talent when she sees it." She smiled, but there was nothing warm about it. "My name is Yin Xue. I represent interests beyond this sect's petty squabbles."

*Beyond the sect.* The words sent a chill down Jin Tian's spine. In his first life, he had learned of factions that operated in the shadows, pulling strings, manipulating cultivators like pieces on a board. He had never discovered who controlled them. By the time he reached the peak of his power, the shadow organizations had vanished, leaving behind only questions.

"You're wasting your time," Jin Tian said, keeping his voice flat. "I'm just an outer disciple trying to survive."

"Survive?" Yin Xue laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "Jin Tian, you defeated a Qi Condensation cultivator with nothing but sword techniques and raw willpower. You analyzed the scoring system in real-time and used it against your opponent. You knew Liu Feng's name before Elder Zhou announced it." She tilted her head, studying him like a curious predator. "That's not survival. That's calculation."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Of course you don't." She reached into her robe and pulled out a jade slip, tossing it to him. He caught it reflexively. "Consider that a gift. Information about tomorrow's tournament that the sect elders would prefer you not have. Including Liu Feng's true cultivation base."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "What do you mean, *true* cultivation base?"

"Ranked seventh among inner disciples at the Qi Condensation third layer." Yin Xue's smile widened. "That's what the sect records show. But Liu Feng broke through to the fifth layer three months ago. He's been hiding his advancement, waiting for the right moment to reveal his true power."

Impossible. The sect's assessment formations were supposed to detect any attempts at concealment. But if what she said was true—

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I want to see what happens when a true prodigy faces an impossible opponent." She turned, her robes billowing despite the lack of wind. "And because, Jin Tian, I believe you're going to win. When you do, I'll find you again. We have much to discuss."

She disappeared into the bamboo grove, leaving no trace of her passage. Jin Tian stared at the jade slip in his hand, his mind racing.

*Who is she? What faction does she represent? And why does she care about a twelve-year-old outer disciple?*

He crushed the jade slip in his fist, pulverizing it. If she wanted him to have that information, she had already ensured he would remember it. Carrying physical evidence was too dangerous.

He needed to prepare. If Liu Feng was truly at the Qi Condensation fifth layer, then Jin Tian's original plan—using his superior technique and experience to overcome a three-layer gap—was now dangerously optimistic. The gap between the third and fifth layers wasn't just numerical. It represented a fundamental difference in qi density, combat endurance, and technique power.

But Jin Tian had faced worse odds. In his first life, he had killed a Nascent Soul cultivator while still in the Core Formation realm. He had survived assassination attempts, spiritual venom, and dimensional collapses. A fifth-layer Qi Condensation disciple was barely a warm-up.

The problem was his current body. At twelve years old, his meridians were still developing. His qi reserves were shallow. He could sustain peak combat for perhaps three minutes before exhaustion set in. Against Liu Feng, he would need to end the fight quickly—or not at all.

He spent the next two hours in secluded meditation, reviewing his memories of Liu Feng's fighting style. In the original timeline, Liu Feng had been known for his Flame Serpent technique, a mid-grade martial art that conjured streams of fire-aspected qi. It was powerful but had a fatal weakness—a one-second delay between the third and fourth movements, when the qi needed to cycle through the lower dantian.

Jin Tian had exploited that weakness countless times during their sparring sessions in his first life. But that was before the betrayal. Before Liu Feng's blade had found his heart.

*Why did you do it, Liu Feng? What did they promise you?*

The questions burned in his chest, but he forced them down. Answers would come after the match. If he lost, they would never come at all.

---

The arena was packed the next morning. Word of the outer disciple who had challenged an inner disciple had spread through the sect like wildfire. Disciples lined the stone bleachers, their voices a constant roar of speculation and excitement.

Jin Tian stood at the eastern entrance, his sword at his side, his breathing steady. Across the arena, Liu Feng emerged from the western gate.

He was taller than Jin Tian remembered—but then, Jin Tian was remembering a grown man, not a seventeen-year-old prodigy. Liu Feng's face was still youthful, his features handsome in a rugged way, his black hair tied back in a warrior's knot. He wore the red-trimmed robes of an inner disciple, and a long saber hung at his hip.

When their eyes met, Liu Feng smiled. It was a confident smile, the expression of someone who knew he was about to crush an overconfident child.

*You don't know what's coming,* Jin Tian thought. *You have no idea who you're facing.*

Elder Zhou rose from his seat in the judges' pavilion. "The match between Inner Disciple Liu Feng and Outer Disciple Jin Tian will commence shortly. Both participants, step forward."

They walked to the center of the arena, stopping twenty feet apart. The crowd fell silent.

"Rules are simple," Elder Zhou continued. "First to yield, fall unconscious, or leave the arena loses. Lethal force is discouraged but not forbidden. Begin."

Liu Feng drew his saber, the blade gleaming with a faint red light as he channeled qi into it. "I'll give you one chance, boy. Yield now, and I'll let you walk away with your dignity intact."

Jin Tian drew his sword. "I decline."

"So be it."

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian knew he would. The inner disciple was aggressive, confident in his superior cultivation. He closed the distance in three strides, his saber cutting a horizontal arc toward Jin Tian's neck.

Jin Tian didn't block. He stepped inside the arc, his body twisting as the blade whistled past his ear, close enough to part a strand of his hair. In the same motion, he drove his elbow into Liu Feng's ribs.

The impact was solid, but Liu Feng's qi armor absorbed most of the force. He grunted, stepping back, his eyes widening slightly. "Fast."

"Not fast enough." Jin Tian pressed his advantage, his sword flashing in a series of strikes aimed at Liu Feng's pressure points. The older disciple parried each blow, but Jin Tian could see the growing recognition in his eyes—this was not the technique of a twelve-year-old outer disciple.

*Good. Keep him off-balance.*

For the first thirty seconds, Jin Tian dominated the exchange. His sword was everywhere at once, ghosting through Liu Feng's defenses, forcing the inner disciple to retreat step by step. The crowd gasped with every near-hit, their cheers growing louder.

But then Liu Feng's expression changed. The surprise faded, replaced by cold calculation. He stopped retreating.

"Impressive," he said, his voice carrying over the clash of weapons. "You're better than any outer disciple I've faced. But you're still limited by your body."

He exploded forward, his saber blazing with crimson qi. The Flame Serpent technique unleashed a torrent of fire, forcing Jin Tian to leap back. The heat was intense, searing his skin even through his qi protection.

*There it is. The fourth movement.*

Jin Tian counted silently. *One... two... three...*

The delay came exactly as he remembered. For a fraction of a second, Liu Feng's qi stuttered, the flames dimming as they cycled through his lower dantian.

Jin Tian moved.

He didn't attack directly. Instead, he dropped to one knee, his sword stabbing upward at a forty-five-degree angle—a thrust aimed not at Liu Feng's body, but at the gap between his qi armor's chest and abdomen plates.

The blade pierced through, biting into flesh.

Liu Feng screamed, stumbling back. Blood stained his robes, spreading from a wound just below his ribs. His eyes were wild now, no longer confident, no longer calculating.

"How?" he gasped. "How did you know?"

Jin Tian straightened, his sword dripping crimson. "I know many things, Liu Feng. I know about the Qi Condensation fifth layer you've been hiding. I know about the shadow faction that approached you last month. And I know that one day, you're going to try to kill me."

Liu Feng's face went pale. "You—who told you?"

"Does it matter?" Jin Tian advanced, his sword pointed at Liu Feng's throat. "Yield, and I'll let you live. Refuse, and I'll show you what happens when you face someone who's already killed you once."

For a long moment, Liu Feng stared at him. Then, slowly, he lowered his saber.

"I yield."

The crowd erupted. Cheers, boos, gasps of disbelief—the noise was deafening. But Jin Tian barely heard it. He was watching Liu Feng's face, watching the confusion and fear and something else—something that looked almost like recognition.

*He knows something. About me, about the future, about the betrayal. I need to question him.*

But as Elder Zhou raised his hand to announce the victor, a messenger burst into the arena, his face ashen.

"Elder Zhou! Urgent news from the sect headquarters!"

The elder frowned. "What is it?"

The messenger hesitated, his eyes sweeping across the crowd before settling on Jin Tian. "The Jade Council has issued an arrest warrant. Outer Disciple Jin Tian is to be detained immediately for questioning regarding the murder of Inner Disciple Chen Wei."

Silence fell like a hammer.

Chen Wei was dead.

And Jin Tian was being accused of his murder.

title: The Blood Lotus Seal
content:
Jin Tian did not waste time watching the celebration.

While the other outer disciples swarmed around the tournament grounds, buzzing with excitement over his upset victory, he slipped away through a side passage used by servants. His robes were still wet with blood—some his, most not. The cut on his arm pulsed with dull pain, the fire-aspected qi from Liu Feng's blade still lingering in the wound like a splinter of glass.

He needed answers.

The inner division's medical pavilion stood three levels above the outer court, accessible only through a spiraling staircase carved into the mountain's spine. Jin Tian had climbed these steps a thousand times in his past life, but never as a twelve-year-old with a body that screamed for rest at every landing.

*Pathetic,* he thought, forcing his legs to move. *A single cut and I'm gasping like a dying fish.*

But the thought carried no real venom. He knew the limitations of this vessel. Knew that every day of training was rebuilding what he had once possessed. The real question was how Liu Feng had possessed something he should not have.

Jin Tian reached the medical pavilion's entrance just as the evening bells began to toll. The building was a modest structure of white stone and jade tiles, its windows glowing with soft lantern light. He pushed through the bamboo door and found himself in a waiting room lined with scrolls and medicinal jars.

A young woman in healer's robes looked up from her desk. Her eyes widened when she saw him.

"You're—the outer disciple who defeated Liu Feng."

"Jin Tian," he said, bowing slightly. "I need to see the wounds on Liu Feng's body."

The healer blinked. "The wounds? But you inflicted them. Surely you—"

"Not mine. His previous wounds. The ones he had before our match."

She stared at him for a long moment, then sighed. "I cannot share patient information without authorization."

Jin Tian reached into his robe and produced the token Elder Zhou had given him after the victory—a small jade pendant marking him as a provisional inner disciple. "This grants me access to inner division facilities. The medical pavilion is included."

The healer examined the token, her expression shifting from reluctance to resignation. "Follow me."

They walked through a corridor lined with treatment rooms, each door marked with the elemental symbol of the patient within. The healer stopped at the fourth door, where a bronze plaque read: *Liu Feng — Severe Fire Qi Imbalance.*

"His injuries were... unusual," the healer said, her voice dropping to a whisper. "The burns on his body didn't match your ice-aspected techniques. They looked self-inflicted."

Jin Tian's pulse quickened. "Show me."

She pushed open the door.

Liu Feng lay on a stone bed, his body wrapped in bandages that seeped crimson at the edges. His face was pale, his breathing shallow, but his eyes snapped open the moment Jin Tian entered the room.

The hatred in them was absolute.

"You," Liu Feng hissed, his voice cracking. "You ruined everything."

Jin Tian walked to the bedside, ignoring the venom in the other boy's words. He studied the bandages, the patterns of discoloration bleeding through the fabric. Then he reached out and pulled back the wrap on Liu Feng's chest.

The healer gasped.

Beneath the bandages, Liu Feng's skin was covered in intricate burn patterns—not random marks of combat, but deliberate, geometric shapes that spiraled outward from his dantian like the petals of a flower. Each line was precise, almost artistic, as if carved by a master calligrapher using flame instead of ink.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

*The Blood Lotus Seal.*

He had seen this before. In his past life, he had found similar markings on the bodies of three elders who had died in suspicious circumstances. The seal was a forbidden technique that consumed the user's life force to temporarily amplify their cultivation base. It was painful, suicidal, and utterly illegal under the sect's laws.

But that wasn't what made Jin Tian's hands tremble.

The Blood Lotus Seal had been created by one person. Perfected by one person. Used by one person.

His second disciple, Shen Yuan.

"No," Jin Tian whispered, stepping back from the bed. "No, this can't be right."

Liu Feng laughed, a broken, wet sound. "Surprised? You should be. I was supposed to win. I was *chosen* to win."

"Chosen by whom?"

"The same person who chose you." Liu Feng's eyes glazed with something like madness. "Don't you understand yet? You were never supposed to be here. None of this was supposed to happen. But when you defeated Chen Wei, when you changed the tournament results, you broke the pattern. And now *he* knows."

"He who?"

Liu Feng's lips twisted into a smile that showed too many teeth. "The one who watches from the shadows. The one who sees all paths and walks the one he chooses. You think you're the only one who remembers the past, Jin Tian? You think you're special?"

The words hit like a physical blow.

Jin Tian grabbed Liu Feng by the collar, pulling him upright. "What do you know about the past? Speak clearly, or I'll—"

"You'll what? Kill me?" Liu Feng laughed again, blood spilling from his lips. "I'm already dead. The seal burns through my core as we speak. By morning, I'll be nothing but ash." His voice dropped to a whisper. "But before I go, I wanted to see your face. Wanted to see if you recognized the pattern."

"Pattern?"

Liu Feng raised a trembling hand and pointed at the burn marks on his chest. "Look closer. It's not just a seal. It's a message."

Jin Tian leaned in, forcing his eyes to trace the geometric lines. The spirals formed characters, ancient script that predated the current cultivation era. He read them once, twice, three times, each repetition driving a spike of cold terror deeper into his heart.

*The Sword God walks again. But the sword has a new master now.*

Jin Tian released Liu Feng, stepping back until his shoulders hit the wall.

Someone knew.

Someone in this timeline remembered what had happened three hundred years from now. Someone had seen Jin Tian's regression, understood its implications, and was already moving to counter him.

The question was who.

There were only three people who could have known about the Blood Lotus Seal. Shen Yuan, who had created it. Jin Tian himself, who had witnessed its effects. And the person who had killed Shen Yuan and stolen his research notes—the same person who had orchestrated Jin Tian's betrayal in his first life.

His eldest disciple, Zhao Ming.

"No," Jin Tian said again, but this time the word carried less certainty. "Zhao Ming was loyal. He died defending me."

"Did he?" Liu Feng's voice was barely a whisper now. "Did you see his body? Did you hold his hand as he passed?"

Jin Tian's mind raced back to that final battle. Zhao Ming had fallen early, cut down by a swarm of assassins while trying to buy Jin Tian time to escape. There had been no time to examine the body. No time to verify the death.

But Jin Tian had felt his disciple's presence fade from their mental link. Had sensed the exact moment the connection shattered.

Unless the connection had been severed deliberately.

Unless Zhao Ming had faked his death to escape suspicion.

The healer cleared her throat. "I... I think it's best if you leave now. He's becoming agitated, and his condition is deteriorating."

Jin Tian nodded mechanically, his mind still processing the implications. He turned to leave, but Liu Feng's hand shot out and grabbed his wrist.

"One more thing," the dying disciple rasped. "The tournament isn't over. Tomorrow, you face the inner division champion. And when you do..." His eyes glazed over, losing focus. "When you do, remember that not all blades are made of steel."

Liu Feng's hand fell limp.

The healer rushed forward, checking his pulse, his breathing. After a long moment, she looked up at Jin Tian with hollow eyes.

"He's gone."

Jin Tian left the medical pavilion without another word.

The night air hit his face like a bucket of cold water, but it did nothing to clear the fog in his mind. Someone knew. Someone was watching. Someone had access to the same forbidden knowledge that had shaped his first life.

He walked through the empty training grounds, his footsteps echoing in the silence. The stars above seemed distant, cold, indifferent to the revelations that had just shattered everything he thought he understood.

*Zhao Ming.*

The name burned in his mind like a brand. His eldest disciple, the one he had trusted above all others. The one who had taught the younger disciples, managed the sect's affairs, stood by Jin Tian's side through every trial.

The one who had been there when the poison was administered.

The one who had suggested the formation that trapped Jin Tian during the assassination.

The one who had held his hand as he died, whispering words of comfort that now sounded like mockery.

Jin Tian stopped walking and looked up at the moon.

"Three hundred years," he said to the empty sky. "Three hundred years of planning, and I still can't see the full board."

A shadow moved at the edge of his vision.

Jin Tian spun, his hand reaching for a sword he no longer carried. But there was no one there—only the rustling leaves of a wind-bent tree and the distant sound of night insects.

He stood motionless, straining his senses, feeling for any disturbance in the ambient qi.

Nothing.

But the hair on the back of his neck remained standing, a primal warning that his cultivation could not dismiss.

Someone was watching.

Someone was always watching now.

Jin Tian turned and walked back toward the outer disciple dormitory, his mind already planning his next move. Tomorrow, he would face the inner division champion, a prodigy named Yun Shu who specialized in water-aspected techniques. In his past life, Yun Shu had been one of the few inner disciples who had shown him kindness, who had warned him about the growing discontent among the elders.

Now, Jin Tian couldn't be sure if that kindness had been genuine or a carefully crafted performance.

He entered his room and barred the door, then sat in the center of the floor and began to meditate. He needed to recover his strength, to heal the wound on his arm, to prepare for tomorrow's battle.

But even as he closed his eyes, Liu Feng's final words echoed in his mind.

*Not all blades are made of steel.*

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open.

He looked down at his right hand and saw a faint mark on his palm—a geometric pattern that had not been there before, burning with a heat that no amount of qi could extinguish.

The Blood Lotus Seal.

It was already spreading.

title: Chapter 15
content:
# CHAPTER 15: The Weight of Memory

The training grounds stretched before Jin Tian like a battlefield of ghosts. Three hours until his match against Liu Feng. Three hours until he faced the man who had ended his first life.

He found an isolated corner near the western wall, where old practice dummies stood weathered and forgotten. The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the cracked stone floor. Dust motes danced in the golden light, undisturbed for what must have been years.

Jin Tian drew his practice sword. The wooden blade felt wrong in his hands—too light, too fragile. In his first life, he had wielded the Sword of Ten Thousand Annihilations, a blade forged from the heart of a dying star. Now he carried a piece of carved oak that a child could snap in two.

*It doesn't matter*, he told himself. *The sword is only an extension of the will.*

He began the first form of the Celestial Sword Art. His body moved through the motions with a precision that belied his twelve-year-old frame. Each strike carried the weight of three hundred years of cultivation, even if the spiritual power behind them was barely a whisper.

The first form: *Heaven's Judgment*.

His blade arced upward, tracing a curve of impossible grace. The air around him seemed to still. For just a moment, the dust motes hung frozen in the light.

*Too slow*, he critiqued himself. *The wrist needs to rotate three degrees more at the apex.*

He adjusted and struck again.

The second form: *Earth's Embrace*.

A downward diagonal slash, followed by a horizontal sweep. His feet shifted with practiced ease, weight transferring from heel to toe in perfect rhythm. The wooden blade whistled through the air.

*Better. But the power is still lacking. My foundation isn't strong enough.*

He had known this would be a problem. In his first life, he had spent decades building his qi reserves, tempering his meridians, strengthening his dantian. Now he had to compress that journey into days. The tournament was only the first step.

The third form: *Mortal's Defiance*.

A counter-slash, a pivot, a thrust. The combination flowed like water over stone, natural and inevitable. Jin Tian's breath came steady, his mind clear.

And then the memories came.

*Liu Feng's blade, burning with crimson fire. The heat searing through Jin Tian's chest. The shock of betrayal, the numbness spreading through his limbs as he fell from the peak of Mount Azure.*

*"Why?" Jin Tian had asked, blood bubbling from his lips.*

*Liu Feng had knelt beside him, his face unreadable. "Because you were already dead. You just didn't know it yet."*

Jin Tian's practice sword clattered to the ground.

He stood there, breathing hard, his hands shaking. The memory was so vivid he could still feel the phantom pain in his chest, the cold embrace of death creeping through his meridians.

*Focus*, he commanded himself. *You are not that man anymore. You are Jin Tian, outer disciple, twelve years old. You have time. You have knowledge. You have—*

"You look like you've seen a ghost."

Jin Tian spun around, his hand instinctively reaching for a sword that wasn't there.

A girl stood at the edge of the training ground, watching him with curious eyes. She was perhaps thirteen, with sharp features and hair the color of autumn leaves. Her robes marked her as an outer disciple, but there was something about her bearing that suggested she was more than she appeared.

"I'm fine," Jin Tian said, bending to retrieve his practice sword. "Just preparing for my match."

"The match against Liu Feng." It wasn't a question. "Everyone's talking about it. The little outer disciple who challenged Chen Wei and somehow won." She tilted her head. "Though I have to wonder... how did you know about the scoring system?"

Jin Tian's muscles tensed. "I paid attention."

"Mm-hmm." The girl walked closer, her footsteps silent on the stone floor. "The thing is, I've been watching you for the past few days. You move like someone who's been training for decades, not years. Your techniques are flawless, your footwork is perfect, and your understanding of combat theory is... advanced."

She stopped three feet away, close enough that Jin Tian could see the faint scars on her hands—calluses from sword practice, but also older marks, the kind that came from real combat.

"Most of the other disciples think you're just talented," she continued. "But I know talent. I've seen prodigies before. You're not a prodigy, Jin Tian."

"Then what am I?"

"I don't know yet." Her eyes met his, and there was something ancient in them, something that didn't belong in a thirteen-year-old's face. "But I intend to find out."

Before Jin Tian could respond, a bell tolled in the distance. The tournament grounds. His match was in two hours.

"I should go," the girl said, stepping back. "Oh, and Jin Tian? Be careful around Liu Feng. He's not what he appears to be."

"How do you know that?"

She smiled, and it was the saddest smile he had ever seen. "Because I've been where you are. Standing at the edge of a battle you don't fully understand, facing an enemy whose motives are hidden." She turned away. "Good luck. You'll need it."

She vanished around the corner, leaving Jin Tian alone with more questions than answers.

---

The tournament grounds were packed.

Disciples from every division had gathered to watch the matches. The inner division tournament was always a spectacle, but today's crowd was larger than usual. Word had spread about the twelve-year-old outer disciple who had dared to challenge the rankings.

Jin Tian stood in the waiting area, his wooden practice sword replaced with a proper steel blade. It was still a far cry from the weapons he was used to, but it would have to do.

Across the arena, Liu Feng was already in position.

The inner disciple was everything Jin Tian remembered: tall, broad-shouldered, with short-cropped hair and a face that seemed carved from granite. His cultivation base was at the fifth level of Qi Condensation—impressive for his age, but unremarkable by Jin Tian's standards. What made Liu Feng dangerous was his fire-aspected technique, the Blazing Sun Art, which allowed him to channel destructive heat through his blade.

*In my first life, I defeated him easily*, Jin Tian reminded himself. *We were both at the Foundation Establishment stage by then. But now...*

Now Jin Tian was at the first level of Qi Condensation. His body was weak, his meridians narrow, his dantian barely developed. He had the knowledge of a Sword God, but the power of a child.

The odds were not in his favor.

"Disciples!" Elder Zhou's voice boomed across the arena. "The first match of the inner division tournament will now commence! Representing the inner disciples—Liu Feng! Representing the outer disciples—Jin Tian!"

The crowd erupted. Most of the cheers were for Liu Feng. A few were for Jin Tian, born of curiosity rather than loyalty.

Jin Tian stepped onto the arena floor. The stone beneath his feet was smooth, worn by countless battles. Above him, the sky was clear and blue, a perfect day for combat.

*Focus*, he told himself. *You know his techniques. You know his patterns. You know how he moves, how he breathes, how he thinks. You have the advantage.*

"Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first.

He shot forward like an arrow, his blade already wreathed in crimson flame. The heat washed over Jin Tian in a wave, intense and suffocating. In his first life, he would have brushed it aside with a flicker of his own qi. Now he had to dodge.

He sidestepped, feeling the heat pass inches from his face. Liu Feng's sword carved through the air where Jin Tian had been standing, leaving a trail of smoke.

"You're fast," Liu Feng said, his voice flat. "But speed won't save you."

He pressed the attack, his blade becoming a blur of fire and steel. Jin Tian deflected, parried, dodged. Each movement was a hair's breadth from disaster. His body screamed in protest, muscles straining against limits they shouldn't have.

*I need to end this quickly*, Jin Tian realized. *I can't match his power. But I can outthink him.*

He retreated, drawing Liu Feng into a predictable rhythm. Block, dodge, retreat. Block, dodge, retreat. The inner disciple grew confident, his attacks becoming wider, more aggressive, less careful.

*There.*

Jin Tian saw the opening—a split-second gap in Liu Feng's guard, a moment of overextension that would leave him vulnerable.

He struck.

His blade slipped past Liu Feng's defenses, aimed at the meridian point just below the collarbone. A precise strike that would disable Liu Feng's sword arm without causing permanent damage.

But Liu Feng's eyes widened, and instead of completing his attack, he twisted violently. The strike that should have hit his shoulder caught him in the ribs instead. Blood sprayed across the arena floor.

The crowd gasped.

Liu Feng stumbled back, clutching his side. His face was pale, but his eyes burned with something that made Jin Tian's blood run cold.

Recognition.

"You..." Liu Feng whispered, his voice barely audible over the roar of the crowd. "You're him."

Jin Tian froze. "What are you talking about?"

"You move like him. You fight like him." Liu Feng's lips curled into a bloody smile. "I thought I was imagining it. But watching you, the way you calculated that strike, the way you exploited my pattern... it's exactly what he would have done."

"Who?" Jin Tian demanded, his heart pounding.

"The Sword God." Liu Feng laughed, a hollow, broken sound. "The legendary Jin Tian, the greatest cultivator of his generation. I saw him fight once, years ago. His movements were burned into my memory." He looked at Jin Tian with something approaching awe. "And you move exactly like him."

The arena seemed to tilt around Jin Tian. How? How could this outer disciple from three hundred years ago recognize his fighting style? Liu Feng should be dead, his soul scattered across the ages. Unless...

"You've been reborn too," Jin Tian breathed.

Liu Feng's smile widened. "No. Not reborn." He straightened, ignoring the blood flowing from his wound. "I've been waiting."

"For what?"

"For you." Liu Feng raised his sword, the flames flickering and dying. "The Heaven's Mandate Order sent me. Three hundred years ago, they foresaw your regression. They knew you would return to this moment, and they prepared."

The Heaven's Mandate Order. Jin Tian's blood turned to ice. In his first life, the Order had been a shadow organization, manipulating events from behind the scenes. He had never discovered their true purpose.

"They want me dead," Jin Tian said.

"No." Liu Feng shook his head. "They want you alive. They want to know how you did it. How you cheated death." His eyes gleamed with dark purpose. "And they sent me to capture you."

The crowd was still cheering, oblivious to the conversation happening in the center of the arena. The elders were watching, but they saw only two disciples locked in combat.

Jin Tian had three choices: surrender, escape, or fight.

But Liu Feng had just revealed the existence of an organization that had been planning for his return for three centuries. An organization that knew about his regression. An organization that wanted to unlock the secret of his power.

And if they knew about him, they knew about his disciples. They knew about the betrayal. They might even know about—

"Your disciple," Liu Feng

title: The Betrayal's First Echo
content:
The tournament grounds had transformed in the three hours since Jin Tian's victory over Chen Wei. Where once there had been orderly rows of training platforms, now stood a massive arena carved from white jade, its surface etched with formation arrays that pulsed with contained energy. Spectators filled the rising tiers of seats—inner disciples, elders, and even a few sect masters from allied schools who had come to witness the annual competition.

Jin Tian stood in the preparation chamber beneath the arena, his hand resting on the hilt of his sword. The blade was ordinary, forged from standard sect steel, but it felt heavier than it should. Perhaps because he knew what waited above.

Liu Feng.

The name echoed in his mind like a death knell. In his first life, Liu Feng had been a shadow that moved at the edges of his awareness—a rival, yes, but never a true threat. They had sparred dozens of times, trading victories and defeats with the easy rhythm of equals. Jin Tian had respected him, even liked him.

And then Liu Feng had killed him.

Not in battle. Not in honorable combat. It had been a betrayal as complete as any of his disciples', delivered with a blade that Jin Tian had never seen coming because he had never considered Liu Feng an enemy.

Why?

The question had haunted him through three hundred years of waiting, through the agony of rebirth, through every moment since he had opened his eyes as a twelve-year-old outer disciple. Now he would have his answer.

"You're the one who humiliated Chen Wei?"

Jin Tian turned. A young woman stood in the chamber's entrance, her robes marking her as an inner disciple. She was beautiful in the way that sharpened steel was beautiful—dangerous, precise, with eyes that missed nothing. Her cultivation base radiated at the fifth level of Qi Condensation, impressive for someone who appeared no older than sixteen.

"I didn't humiliate him," Jin Tian said calmly. "I simply proved I was more qualified."

"You proved you were willing to make enemies." She stepped closer, and Jin Tian caught the faint scent of night orchids. "Chen Wei has powerful friends. His father is an elder of the Alchemy Pavilion. His uncle sits on the Sect Council."

"I'm aware."

"And yet you challenged him anyway." Her lips curved into something that might have been approval. "Interesting."

"Who are you?"

"Lin Yue." She offered no title, no explanation. Just the name, as if it should mean something. "I'm Liu Feng's senior sister. He asked me to deliver a message."

Jin Tian's hand tightened on his sword. "What message?"

"He wants you to know that he holds no grudge against you. Whatever happens in the arena, he bears you no ill will." Her eyes searched his face. "He also asked me to tell you that he hopes you'll survive."

"Survive?"

"The inner division tournament is different from what you're used to, outer disciple." She said the title with a hint of mockery, but there was something else beneath it. Concern, perhaps. Or warning. "The formation arrays in the arena are designed to prevent fatal injuries, but accidents happen. They always have."

"You're warning me."

"I'm telling you that Liu Feng is not your enemy." She turned to leave, then paused. "But someone else is. Someone who has been watching you since you stepped onto the stage three hours ago. Someone who doesn't want you to reach the inner division."

Before Jin Tian could ask more, she was gone, swallowed by the shadows of the preparation chamber.

The announcement came moments later: "Outer Disciple Jin Tian versus Inner Disciple Liu Feng. Proceed to the arena."

Jin Tian ascended the stairs into blinding sunlight.

The crowd's roar hit him like a physical force. Thousands of faces blurred past as he walked to the center of the jade platform, his steps measured, his breathing controlled. Across from him, Liu Feng emerged from the opposite entrance.

He was exactly as Jin Tian remembered. Tall, broad-shouldered, with flame-red hair that seemed to flicker in the light. His eyes were the color of cooling embers, and his sword—a blade of crimson crystal—hummed with barely contained power. He moved with the economy of a true expert, each step carrying him forward with fluid grace.

When they met at the center of the arena, Liu Feng bowed. "I've heard much about you, Jin Tian. Your victory over Chen Wei was impressive."

"Chen Wei was not a worthy opponent," Jin Tian replied, returning the bow. "I expect better from you."

Liu Feng's smile didn't reach his eyes. "I'll do my best not to disappoint."

The overseer raised his hand. "The match will continue until one combatant yields, is incapacitated, or is unable to continue. Killing is forbidden. Lethal force will result in immediate disqualification and punishment."

The words were standard. The emphasis was not.

"Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first.

Fire exploded from his blade, a torrent of crimson energy that swept across the arena in a wave of killing intent. Jin Tian didn't block—he flowed around the attack, his body moving with instincts honed through countless lifetimes. The flame passed inches from his face, the heat singing the tips of his hair.

He closed the distance in three steps, his sword lashing out in a horizontal arc. Liu Feng parried, and the impact sent shockwaves rippling through the formation arrays. The crowd gasped.

They traded blows, each exchange faster than the last. Jin Tian's sword became a blur of silver light, his movements precise, economical, devastating. Liu Feng matched him blow for blow, his fire-aspected techniques creating explosions of heat and light that forced Jin Tian to constantly adjust his position.

But Jin Tian was not fighting to win.

He was fighting to remember.

Every technique Liu Feng used, every stance, every subtle shift of weight—Jin Tian catalogued them all, comparing them to the memories of his first life. And slowly, horrifyingly, a pattern began to emerge.

The way Liu Feng's left foot dragged slightly before he launched his strongest attacks. The way he favored his right side, leaving his left flank exposed for a fraction of a second. The way he breathed—controlled, measured, almost mechanical.

This was not a natural fighting style. This was something learned, something drilled into muscle memory through countless hours of repetition. It was the style of a man who had been trained to kill.

And Jin Tian recognized it.

It was the same style used by the assassin who had killed him in his first life. The same movements, the same patterns, the same deadly efficiency.

Liu Feng had been trained by the same master.

The realization hit Jin Tian like a physical blow, and in that moment of distraction, Liu Feng struck.

The blade of crimson crystal pierced through Jin Tian's defenses, slicing across his chest in a spray of blood. He stumbled back, pain lancing through his body, but he didn't fall. He didn't yield.

"What's wrong?" Liu Feng asked, his voice carrying amusement. "Did you see a ghost?"

"Something like that," Jin Tian replied, pressing his hand against the wound. Blood seeped through his fingers, but the cut was shallow. He had moved just in time. "Tell me, Liu Feng. Who trained you?"

The question hung in the air. Liu Feng's smile flickered, just for a moment.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Yes, you do." Jin Tian straightened, ignoring the pain. "The way you fight. The techniques you use. I've seen them before. I just can't remember where."

"You're delirious from blood loss." Liu Feng raised his sword again. "Let me end this."

He attacked again, faster this time. Fire exploded around them, the formation arrays straining to contain the power of the techniques. Jin Tian gave ground, letting the attacks flow past him, studying, analyzing, remembering.

And then it hit him.

The name.

The face.

The master who had trained the assassin who killed him.

It was Elder Zhou.

The same Elder Zhou who had presided over the tournament. The same Elder Zhou who had allowed Jin Tian to challenge Chen Wei. The same Elder Zhou who had smiled and praised his boldness.

Elder Zhou was the one who had trained Liu Feng. Elder Zhou was the one who had ordered his death.

And Elder Zhou was watching from the overseer's platform right now.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

"Liu Feng," he said, his voice barely audible over the roar of combat. "Elder Zhou sent you to kill me, didn't he?"

The flame-haired disciple's eyes went wide. For a single, frozen moment, the mask slipped, and Jin Tian saw the truth written plainly on his face.

"Yes," Liu Feng whispered. "But you weren't supposed to know."

He lunged.

The blade of crimson crystal drove forward, aimed directly at Jin Tian's heart. It was a killing blow, delivered with perfect technique and absolute intent. There was no way to dodge. No way to block. No way to survive.

Except Jin Tian had seen this attack before.

In his first life, Liu Feng had used the same technique to pierce his heart. The same movement, the same angle, the same killing intent. It had killed him once.

It would not kill him again.

Jin Tian twisted, letting the blade pass through the gap between his ribs and his arm. The edge sliced through his robes, drawing a thin line of blood, but it missed everything vital. At the same time, he brought his own sword up, driving it through Liu Feng's guard and into his shoulder.

Liu Feng screamed.

The crowd erupted in chaos.

Jin Tian pulled his blade free, stepping back as Liu Feng collapsed to his knees. Blood poured from the wound, soaking the jade platform in crimson. The formation arrays flickered, struggling to contain the damage.

"You shouldn't have told me," Jin Tian said softly. "Now I know."

Liu Feng looked up at him, his face pale, his eyes filled with something that might have been relief. "You don't understand. He's not the only one. There are others. More powerful. More dangerous. They're all watching you, Jin Tian. They've been watching you since the moment you were reborn."

The words struck Jin Tian like lightning.

"Reborn? What do you mean, reborn?"

But Liu Feng didn't answer. His eyes rolled back in his head, and he collapsed, unconscious.

The overseer rushed forward, his face ashen. "The match is over! Outer Disciple Jin Tian is the victor!"

The crowd's cheers were distant, muffled, unimportant. Jin Tian stood over Liu Feng's unconscious body, his mind racing.

They knew.

Someone in this sect knew about his regression. Someone had been watching him since the beginning. And if they knew about his regression, then they knew everything—his plans, his goals, his identity as the Sword God.

He looked up at the overseer's platform.

Elder Zhou was gone.

The seat was empty, the cushions still warm, a cup of tea half-drunk. He had vanished in the chaos of the match, slipping away like smoke.

Jin Tian's hands tightened on his sword.

The game had changed. He was no longer the hunter. He was the hunted, trapped in a sect that harbored enemies he didn't know, surrounded by allies who might be traitors, walking through a world that knew more about him than he knew about them.

And somewhere in the shadows, Elder Zhou was watching, waiting, planning.

The first move had been made.

Now it was Jin Tian's turn to respond.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Echo of a Broken Seal

Jin Tian stood in the shadow of the Heaven's Pillar Pavilion, the cold stone of the outer sect's meditation courtyard pressing against his back. The sun had begun its descent, painting the sky in shades of amber and blood—fitting colors for what was to come.

Three hours until his match against Liu Feng.

Three hours to prepare for a man who had killed him once already.

But as Jin Tian closed his eyes and reached into his spiritual sea, he found something he had not expected. A crack. A hairline fracture in the foundation of his cultivation base, running from his dantian to the first meridian node. It was small, barely noticeable unless one knew exactly where to look. And Jin Tian knew because he had seen this same crack three hundred years ago—in the body of a dead man.

The corpse of Elder Wu, the sect's former formation master, had been found with the same fracture when he was assassinated in the seventh year of Jin Tian's first life. The official report had called it a cultivation deviation. Jin Tian had known better even then. Someone had planted a time-delayed seal in Elder Wu's foundation, one that would shatter his cultivation from within at a precise moment.

Someone had done the same to him.

*When?* Jin Tian's mind raced. He had not sensed any foreign qi entering his body. He had not felt the touch of a formation or a seal. And yet there it was, growing with each passing breath, spreading like frost across a winter window.

He had three hours. Maybe less.

The crack would not fully break before his match. The seal was designed for a slower death, a week at most. But if he entered battle with a damaged foundation, his sword techniques would lack precision. His qi flow would stutter at critical moments. Against a disciple of Liu Feng's caliber, such flaws would be fatal.

And that was the point.

Someone wanted him to die in that arena. Not by assassination, not by poison, but by a blade that would be declared an honorable kill. No investigation, no questions, just another promising outer disciple who had overreached and paid the price.

Jin Tian's lips curled into a cold smile.

They had forgotten who they were dealing with.

He pressed his palm against his dantian and began to weave his qi into a reverse spiral, a technique he had developed in his first life to counter exactly this kind of sabotage. It would take time. It would drain his reserves. But it would grind the seal to dust.

He had two hours and forty-seven minutes.

---

Chen Wei watched from the shadow of the inner sect's eastern gate as Jin Tian entered the meditation courtyard. The little rat had taken the bait, had stepped into the arena that Chen Wei had so carefully constructed. It had been almost too easy.

The seal had been planted three days ago, during the night of the new moon. Chen Wei had slipped into the outer disciple dormitories while Jin Tian was in the middle of his cultivation session, deep in meditation. The sleeping formation had been simple to bypass, and the seal itself was a masterwork—a gift from his uncle, Elder Zhou's personal formation master.

Even Chen Wei did not fully understand how it worked. He only knew that it would ensure Jin Tian's qi channels would collapse at the most inopportune moment. Perhaps during the match. Perhaps during his next cultivation session. The timing was imprecise, but the result was guaranteed.

*Pity,* Chen Wei thought, not meaning it at all. *The boy had potential.*

But potential meant nothing against the established order. Jin Tian's rise had been too fast, too unexpected. If he were allowed to compete in the inner division tournament, he would draw attention. He would make connections. He would become a variable that could not be controlled.

And Chen Wei could not afford variables.

Not when his plans were so close to fruition.

He turned and walked away, his robes billowing behind him. Let the little rat train. Let him prepare. In the end, it would not matter. Liu Feng was a blade honed by years of combat, a weapon that the sect had forged with care and precision. Jin Tian was a sharpened stick, dangerous only to those who underestimated him.

Liu Feng would not underestimate him.

Chen Wei had made sure of that.

---

The meditation courtyard emptied as the hour of the match approached. Disciples filtered out, heading toward the Grand Arena where the inner division tournament was already underway. Jin Tian remained, seated in the center of the courtyard, sweat beading on his forehead as he pushed against the seal with everything he had.

The crack was shrinking.

Slowly, painfully, the reverse spiral was grinding away at the foreign qi, dissolving it strand by strand. But the process was leaving him hollow. His qi reserves had dropped by nearly half, and his spiritual sea felt like a drained well.

He opened his eyes.

One hour until the match.

He was not going to make it.

The seal had been too well-crafted. Whoever had planted it knew his cultivation base intimately, knew exactly where to strike for maximum effect. If he continued to fight it, he would enter the arena with barely a third of his strength. If he stopped, the seal would continue to spread, and he would die within the week.

Either way, he lost.

Jin Tian closed his eyes again, and for the first time since his rebirth, he felt the cold touch of despair.

*Think.*

He was the Sword God. He had lived three hundred years, had mastered techniques that the current sect could not even dream of. He had faced demon cultivators, ancient beasts, and the machinations of heavenly courts. He had died once, and he had clawed his way back from the abyss.

He would not be stopped by a child's seal.

*The seal is designed to attack qi channels,* he reasoned. *It targets the flow of energy, the pathways through which cultivation moves. If I redirect my qi to bypass the affected channels...*

But that would require restructuring his entire meridian network on the fly, a technique that even in his prime had taken months to master.

*Unless...*

An old memory surfaced. A technique from the Southern Wastes, taught to him by a mad hermit who had claimed to be the last descendant of the Primordial Serpent Clan. The Serpent's Shedding Technique—a method of temporarily abandoning one's cultivation base and regrowing it from scratch, stronger and more resilient than before.

It was dangerous. It was excruciatingly painful. And it would leave him at his absolute weakest for a full minute while the new foundation took root.

But it would also destroy the seal completely.

Jin Tian took a deep breath.

Then he shattered his own cultivation base.

---

The Grand Arena was packed. Thousands of disciples lined the stone bleachers, their voices rising in a cacophony of anticipation. The inner division tournament was the most anticipated event of the year, a showcase of the sect's rising talents and a proving ground for those who sought to ascend to the ranks of the core disciples.

Chen Wei sat in the VIP section, flanked by his uncle and several other elders. He wore a mask of polite interest, but inside, he was counting the seconds.

Liu Feng stood in the arena below, his fire-aspected qi wreathing his body in a corona of crimson light. He was a tall man, lean and sharp-featured, with eyes that burned like embers. His blade, Emberfang, was already drawn, its edge gleaming with heat.

The announcer's voice rang out across the arena. "Entering the arena from the outer division: Outer Disciple Jin Tian!"

The crowd's murmur shifted. A few scattered cheers, but mostly curiosity. Who was this boy who had dared to challenge the inner division's ranking?

The gate on the opposite side of the arena slid open.

Jin Tian walked through.

He looked... terrible. His robes were soaked with sweat, his face pale, his steps unsteady. His hand gripped his sword, but his knuckles were white, and his arm trembled slightly.

Chen Wei smiled.

*The seal is working perfectly.*

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. "You look like death, boy. Surrender now. I have no desire to kill a corpse."

Jin Tian raised his head. His eyes were hollow, but there was something in them that made Liu Feng pause. A flicker of cold light, like the gleam of a blade in darkness.

"One minute," Jin Tian said, his voice barely audible.

"What?"

"Give me one minute. Then I'll give you a fight worth remembering."

Liu Feng's lips curled in contempt. "One minute? I could kill you in ten seconds."

"Then do it."

The challenge hung in the air. Liu Feng's grip tightened on Emberfang. The crowd fell silent, sensing the shift in atmosphere.

And then Liu Feng moved.

He was fast, faster than any outer disciple should have been able to track. His blade became a streak of fire, aimed directly at Jin Tian's heart.

Jin Tian did not move.

The blade crossed the distance in less than a heartbeat.

And stopped.

Three inches from Jin Tian's chest.

Liu Feng's eyes widened. His arm was trembling, not from effort, but from resistance. Something was holding his blade in place, something invisible and immovable.

Jin Tian's hollow eyes met his.

"The seal is gone," he said softly. "And I've been waiting for this moment."

The air around Jin Tian exploded.

Qi erupted from his body in a torrent, shattering the stone beneath his feet and sending a shockwave across the arena. The crowd screamed, shields flickering to life as the elders scrambled to contain the blast. Chen Wei shot to his feet, his face draining of color.

*Impossible.*

Jin Tian's cultivation base had not just recovered. It had *transformed*. The qi pouring off him was denser, purer, suffused with an edge that cut through the air like a physical blade. His sword, a simple iron blade, began to hum, its surface glowing with a faint silver light.

Liu Feng stumbled back, his fire-aspected qi sputtering against the oppressive pressure of Jin Tian's aura. "What—what are you?"

Jin Tian raised his sword.

"I am the one who remembers."

He moved.

Not fast. *Instantly.*

One moment he was ten paces away. The next, his blade was pressed against Liu Feng's throat, the edge drawing a thin line of blood.

The crowd went silent.

Liu Feng's Emberfang clattered to the ground. His hands rose slowly, palms open.

"I yield," he whispered.

Jin Tian did not lower his sword. His eyes bored into Liu Feng's, searching for something.

"Tell me who planted the seal," he said, his voice soft and deadly. "Tell me, and I'll let you walk away."

Liu Feng's face went pale. His eyes darted toward the VIP section, toward Chen Wei.

Jin Tian followed his gaze.

Chen Wei was standing, his face a mask of controlled fury. Beside him, Elder Zhou's expression was unreadable.

"Interesting," Jin Tian murmured. "Very interesting."

He stepped back, sheathing his sword. The pressure in the arena lifted, and the crowd erupted in chaos.

But Jin Tian was not listening.

He was watching Chen Wei's hand, which had slipped into his sleeve. Watching the subtle gesture, the signal meant for someone in the crowd.

Watching as Liu Feng's body suddenly went rigid, his eyes rolling back in his head.

The inner disciple collapsed, foam bubbling from his lips.

Poison.

Someone had silenced him before he could talk.

Jin Tian turned to face the VIP section, his gaze locking onto Chen Wei.

"Next time," he said, loud enough for the elders to hear, "I won't need him to talk."

Chen Wei's smile returned, cold and sharp.

"We'll see, little rat. We'll see

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Unseen Hand

The arena buzzed with anticipation as Jin Tian stepped onto the stone platform. The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the packed earth, and the gathered disciples pressed against the barrier ropes, their voices a rising tide of speculation.

Liu Feng stood opposite him, a full head taller, his robes bearing the crimson trim of an inner disciple. A blade of pure obsidian rested at his hip, its edge gleaming with unnatural sharpness. His face was calm, almost bored, as if this match was beneath his attention.

Jin Tian knew better. He remembered that face—the cold precision, the effortless grace, the way Liu Feng had moved through his defenses like water through cracks in stone. In his first life, this man had been the final witness to his death. The blade that took his life had been meant for mercy, Liu Feng had claimed. A quick death was preferable to what the others had planned.

Now Jin Tian would learn the truth.

"Begin!" Elder Zhou's voice rang out.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian expected. A burst of flame erupted from his palm, transforming into a crescent of fire that screamed across the arena. Jin Tian sidestepped, feeling the heat kiss his cheek as the attack passed harmlessly by.

"Fast," Liu Feng observed, his voice carrying a hint of surprise. "But not fast enough."

He lunged, his obsidian blade tracing a diagonal arc aimed at Jin Tian's throat. Steel met steel as Jin Tian's sword intercepted, the impact sending sparks cascading through the air. The force of the blow drove Jin Tian back three steps, his feet carving grooves in the earth.

*He's stronger than I remember,* Jin Tian thought, adjusting his grip. *Or I'm weaker.*

But that would change. Already his muscles were remembering the flow of combat, the subtle shifts of weight and intent that marked a true swordsman. His cultivation base was still shallow, but his understanding was ocean-deep.

Liu Feng pressed his advantage, raining down blows with merciless precision. Each strike was calculated, each feint designed to draw Jin Tian into a trap. The older disciple's movements were fluid, almost hypnotic, and Jin Tian found himself being herded toward the arena's edge.

*He's not trying to defeat me,* Jin Tian realized. *He's trying to corner me.*

"Why?" Jin Tian asked, his voice low enough that only Liu Feng could hear.

The inner disciple's eyes flickered with something—recognition? Surprise?—before the mask of indifference returned. "Why what?"

"Why did you kill me?"

The question hung between them like a drawn blade. Liu Feng's next attack faltered, the rhythm broken. For a single heartbeat, Jin Tian saw confusion flash across the man's face.

"What are you talking about?" Liu Feng hissed, driving forward with renewed aggression.

Jin Tian deflected the blow, pivoting to create distance. "In another life. You don't remember, but I do. Your blade through my heart. The look on your face—it wasn't hatred. It was duty."

Liu Feng's composure cracked. His next attack was wild, uncontrolled, and Jin Tian saw his opening. He stepped inside the arc of the obsidian blade, his own sword tracing a line of crimson across Liu Feng's forearm.

The inner disciple stumbled back, blood dripping from the wound. His eyes were wide now, no longer bored, no longer indifferent. They held something that Jin Tian recognized intimately: fear.

"I don't know what game you're playing, outer disciple," Liu Feng said, his voice trembling slightly, "but you're wrong about everything."

"Am I?" Jin Tian advanced, his blade steady. "Then tell me. Who ordered you to kill me? Was it Chen Wei? The sect master? Someone else?"

Liu Feng's jaw tightened. His hand moved to his storage ring, and Jin Tian tensed, expecting a desperate attack or a retreat. Instead, the inner disciple pulled out a small jade token, its surface etched with symbols that made Jin Tian's blood run cold.

The same symbols that had been carved into the blade that killed him.

"I was told to watch you," Liu Feng said, his voice barely above a whisper. "To observe your growth and report back. The elimination was meant for later, after the tournament. But you..." He shook his head. "You're not supposed to know."

"Who told you?" Jin Tian demanded, closing the distance.
