Liu Feng's eyes met his, and in them, Jin Tian saw resignation. "I can't say. If I do, I'm dead. If I don't..." He laughed bitterly. "I'm dead anyway, aren't I? You won't stop until you find the truth."

"Then help me. Tell me who sent you, and I'll protect you."

"You can't protect me from them." Liu Feng raised the jade token, and Jin Tian noticed his hands were shaking. "They're already here. They've always been here."

The token began to glow, and Jin Tian lunged forward, but he was too late. A pulse of energy erupted from the jade, engulfing Liu Feng in a cocoon of white light. The inner disciple's body convulsed, his features contorting in agony as the light consumed him.

"No!" Jin Tian grabbed Liu Feng's shoulder, but the energy repelled him, sending him careening across the arena floor.

When he looked up, Liu Feng was gone. Where the inner disciple had stood, only a scorched circle remained, the obsidian blade lying abandoned on the stone.

The crowd erupted in confusion. Disciples shouted, elders rushed forward, and someone was screaming about divine punishment. But Jin Tian heard none of it. His eyes were fixed on the jade token, which had fallen to the ground, now cracked and inert.

He picked it up, turning it over in his hands. The symbols were familiar—ancient writing from before the sect's founding, a script that had been lost to time. But Jin Tian remembered it. He had seen it in his first life, carved into the walls of a forbidden chamber beneath the sect's main hall.

A chamber he had never been able to enter.

*They're already here,* Liu Feng had said. *They've always been here.*

The realization struck Jin Tian like a physical blow. The traitors weren't just among the disciples. They were among the elders. They had been part of the sect's foundation, hidden in plain sight for centuries.

And they knew he was coming.

"Jin Tian!" Elder Zhou's voice cut through the chaos. "What happened to Liu Feng? What did you do?"

"I didn't do anything," Jin Tian said, his voice flat. "He eliminated himself. The token was triggered remotely."

"Preposterous! No such token exists in the sect's records."

"Then your records are incomplete." Jin Tian held up the jade. "I suggest you examine this carefully, Elder. It bears markings that predate the sect's current era."

Elder Zhou's eyes narrowed. He took the token, studying it with growing unease. "Where did you get this?"

"Liu Feng dropped it. Along with some interesting information." Jin Tian paused, weighing his next words carefully. "He claimed he was ordered to watch me. To report on my progress. He said 'they' were already here."

A murmur ran through the gathered elders. Some exchanged glances, while others stared at Jin Tian with suspicion. But one face caught Jin Tian's attention—Elder Wei, a man he had trusted in his first life, a man who had stood beside him in countless battles.

Elder Wei's expression was unreadable, but his hand had moved to rest on his sword hilt. A protective gesture. Or a threatening one.

"This is a serious accusation, Jin Tian," Elder Zhou said slowly. "You're suggesting that forces within the sect are conspiring against you."

"I'm not suggesting anything. I'm stating facts." Jin Tian met the elder's gaze without flinching. "Liu Feng admitted it before he was silenced. Someone is afraid of what I might become. They've been watching me since I entered the sect."

"And why would anyone fear an outer disciple? Even one with your... unusual talents."

Jin Tian smiled, though there was no warmth in it. "That's what I intend to find out."

He turned and walked from the arena, ignoring the shouted questions and accusations that followed him. The jade token's symbols were burned into his memory, and he had three centuries of knowledge to draw upon. He knew where the forbidden chamber was. He knew how to access it.

And now he knew that the answers he sought were waiting for him beneath the sect's foundations.

But as he stepped off the arena platform, a hand grabbed his arm. He turned to find Elder Wei standing beside him, his face pale.

"Jin Tian," the elder whispered, his voice urgent. "You must not pursue this. Not now. Not yet."

"Why? What do you know?"

Elder Wei glanced around, ensuring no one was listening. "I know that Liu Feng was not the only one watching you. I know that the symbols on that token belong to a group that has existed since before the sect's founding. They call themselves the Hand of the First Emperor."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. The First Emperor. The founder of the Azure Sect. A figure of legend, said to have ascended to immortality ten thousand years ago.

"The Hand is still active?"

"They never stopped." Elder Wei's grip tightened. "And they know about you, Jin Tian. They've known since you awakened your memories. The only reason you're still alive is that they can't decide whether to recruit you or eliminate you."

"Then I'll give them my answer personally." Jin Tian pulled his arm free. "Where do I find them?"

"In the chamber beneath the main hall. But you'll never make it past the guardians."

"Watch me."

He left Elder Wei standing there, his face a mask of concern and resignation. Jin Tian had no time for caution. The game had changed. The enemy was no longer hidden in shadow—they had a name, a history, a purpose.

And Jin Tian intended to tear it all apart.

He made his way through the sect's winding paths, past training grounds and meditation halls, until he reached the main hall's rear entrance. A stone staircase spiraled downward into darkness, its steps worn smooth by centuries of use.

Jin Tian descended without hesitation, his hand resting on his sword. The air grew cold, heavy with the weight of ancient power. Torches flickered to life as he passed, their flames burning with an unnatural blue light.

At the bottom of the stairs, a massive iron door barred his path. Its surface was etched with the same symbols as the jade token, and in its center, a circular depression waited.

Jin Tian placed the broken token into the depression. For a moment, nothing happened. Then the door groaned, its hinges protesting centuries of disuse, and swung open.

Beyond it lay a chamber of impossible size. Pillars of black stone rose toward a ceiling lost in shadow, and at the chamber's center, a dais held a single object: a sword, suspended in midair, its blade made of crystal that pulsed with inner light.

But it was the figures standing around the dais that drew Jin Tian's attention.

Seven hooded figures, their faces hidden, their hands resting on identical swords. They turned as one as Jin Tian entered, and a voice emerged from the shadows—cold, ancient, and familiar.

"Welcome, Jin Tian. We've been expecting you."

The voice belonged to someone he had trusted in his first life. Someone who had stood beside him until the very end.

The voice of his first disciple.

Chen Wei's voice.

But that was impossible. Chen Wei was still an outer disciple, still years away from becoming Jin Tian's student.

Unless... unless the Hand of the First Emperor had been pulling strings all along. Unless Chen Wei

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Weight of Memory

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The other outer disciples had scattered to spread news of his shocking victory in the selection tournament, and the inner disciples wouldn't bother with this remote corner of the sect grounds. Perfect.

He needed space. He needed silence. And he needed to understand why his body was trembling.

Three hours until he faced Liu Feng. Three hours until he stood across from the man who had murdered him in his past life.

Jin Tian sat cross-legged beneath an ancient pine, its branches twisted by centuries of wind and lightning. The bark was scarred, the wood hardened by adversity. A fitting companion for what he needed to do.

He closed his eyes and reached inward.

The *Sword Heart Sutra* had always been his foundation, the technique that had carried him from outer disciple to Sword God. But in this young body, with its undeveloped meridians and shallow qi reserves, he couldn't simply rely on the advanced forms he'd mastered in his previous life. The body would shatter under the strain.

Instead, he needed precision. Efficiency. The kind of combat economy that came from a hundred years of life-or-death experience.

Liu Feng's technique was fire-aspected, built around overwhelming power and relentless pressure. In their first life, Jin Tian had matched that power with power, exchanging blows until one of them fell. It had been a close battle, decided by inches and heartbeats.

But Liu Feng had won. And then he had driven his blade through Jin Tian's heart.

*Why?*

The question burned. Liu Feng had no reason to kill him. They weren't enemies. They weren't even rivals, not really. Jin Tian had always been the better swordsman, the stronger cultivator. Liu Feng had acknowledged that, had even sought his advice on technique refinement.

And yet, when the moment came, Liu Feng's blade had been steady. His eyes had been clear. There had been no hesitation, no regret.

*Someone ordered him to do it.*

The realization crystallized in Jin Tian's mind like frost forming on a winter morning. Liu Feng hadn't acted alone. He had been a weapon, not a will. The true enemy was the hand that had wielded him.

And now Jin Tian had three hours to figure out who that hand belonged to.

He opened his eyes and began to move.

The first form of the *Sword Heart Sutra*—*Dawn Breaks the Horizon*. A simple horizontal slash, meant to clear the mind and warm the muscles. But Jin Tian modified it, shifting his weight slightly forward, angling the blade a fraction higher. It changed the technique's flow, redirecting the energy from a broad sweep to a focused thrust.

Against fire-aspected techniques, the key was not to block but to pierce. Fire consumed what it touched, but it could be extinguished by cutting off its source. If Liu Feng's power came from his qi circulation, then Jin Tian needed to target the meridians that fed the flames.

*Strike the hands. Break the channels. Kill the fire before it can burn.*

His sword whispered through the air, each movement precise, economical. He practiced the same form a hundred times, then a hundred more, refining the angle by millimeters, adjusting the timing by heartbeats. By the time he was satisfied, sweat plastered his robes to his skin and his muscles screamed for rest.

But rest was a luxury he couldn't afford.

The second form—*Mountain Stands Against Storm*. A defensive posture, meant to weather an onslaught. Jin Tian discarded it completely. Against Liu Feng's relentless assault, defense was death. You couldn't block fire; you could only redirect it, or better yet, extinguish it at the source.

He would need to be faster. Faster than Liu Feng remembered. Faster than the records showed.

Jin Tian reached into his storage ring and withdrew a small jade vial. The liquid inside was pale gold, shimmering with trapped starlight. Starfall Essence. A treasure he had discovered in his first life, hidden in a forgotten cave beneath the sect's meditation hall. In this life, he had retrieved it on his second day back, knowing it would be crucial.

One drop would amplify his qi circulation by three times for a single breath. One vial would give him ten breaths of overwhelming speed and power.

He had six drops left after today's tournament.

He would need all of them.

---

The inner division arena was grander than the outer division's training grounds. Carved from white jade and inlaid with formation arrays that shimmered with protective energy, it could withstand attacks that would level mountains. The stands were filled with disciples, elders, and sect officials, all eager to witness the spectacle.

Jin Tian stood at one end of the arena, his sword held loosely at his side. Across from him, Liu Feng was already in position.

The inner disciple was everything the records claimed—broad-shouldered, with a fighter's build and eyes that burned with orange light. His weapon was a blade of crimson crystal, forged from volcano heartstone and quenched in the blood of a flame-wyrm. It radiated heat that made the air shimmer.

"So you're the boy who took Chen Wei's place," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying easily across the arena. "I expected someone... bigger."

Jin Tian didn't respond. He was reading Liu Feng's stance, the way he held his weight, the subtle tension in his shoulders. The inner disciple was confident, relaxed. He saw this as an easy victory, a chance to humble an upstart.

Good. Let him believe that.

The referee elder raised his hand. "The match will begin on my signal. Both disciples, assume your starting positions."

Jin Tian settled into a standard opening stance—feet shoulder-width apart, sword held at waist height, tip angled slightly upward. It was the most basic form, the first stance every disciple learned.

Liu Feng's lip curled. "Is that all you have? I expected more from someone who defeated Chen Wei."

"You talk too much," Jin Tian said mildly.

The elder's hand dropped. "Begin!"

Liu Feng exploded forward, his blade leaving a trail of fire in the air. The heat washed over Jin Tian like a physical force, pressing against his skin, threatening to steal his breath. It was an opening attack meant to overwhelm, to end the match before it truly began.

In his first life, Jin Tian had met this attack with a rising slash, matching force with force.

This time, he stepped to the side.

The movement was minimal—a single step, barely a foot. But it took him out of the direct path of Liu Feng's charge while keeping him close enough to strike. His sword came up in a diagonal thrust, aimed at Liu Feng's exposed wrist.

The inner disciple's eyes widened. He twisted mid-strike, redirecting his blade to parry, but the angle was awkward, the movement forced. The clang of metal echoed across the arena as their swords met.

Jin Tian didn't press the advantage. Instead, he stepped back, resuming his basic stance.

Liu Feng's confidence had faded, replaced by wary respect. "You're faster than the reports said."

"Your reports are incomplete."

They clashed again, and this time Jin Tian showed more of his hand. He met Liu Feng's fire-aspected strikes with precise, economical movements, each parry and riposte designed to minimize energy expenditure while maximizing effect. He wasn't trying to win in a single exchange. He was testing, probing, searching for the weakness he knew existed.

Every technique had a flaw. Every cultivator had a blind spot. And Jin Tian had three centuries of experience finding them.

Liu Feng's fire technique relied on a specific qi circulation pattern—a spiral that began in the dantian and expanded outward through the meridians, gathering heat and power. If Jin Tian could disrupt that spiral at the right moment, the technique would collapse, leaving Liu Feng vulnerable.

But timing was everything. Too early, and Liu Feng could adjust. Too late, and the flames would consume him.

They exchanged a dozen more blows, the arena filling with sparks and the smell of scorched stone. The crowd was silent, watching with held breath. This was not the one-sided slaughter they had expected.

Jin Tian saw his opening.

Liu Feng launched into his signature technique—*Blazing Sunfall*, a descending strike that channeled all his fire qi into a single, devastating blow. The blade blazed white-hot, the air around it igniting into plasma. It was a technique that had killed countless opponents, a technique that had ended Jin Tian's first life.

But this time, Jin Tian was ready.

He didn't block. He didn't dodge. Instead, he stepped *into* the strike, his body moving with a speed that seemed to violate the laws of physics. One drop of Starfall Essence dissolved on his tongue, and his qi circulation exploded.

His sword came up in a thrust that was almost too fast to see, aimed not at Liu Feng's body but at the precise point where the *Blazing Sunfall* technique gathered its power—the center of the spiral, the nexus of fire qi that made the technique possible.

The tip of Jin Tian's blade met the heart of Liu Feng's attack.

For a single breath, the world held still.

Then the fire technique collapsed.

Liu Feng screamed as his own qi backlashed through his meridians, the fire he had summoned turning inward. His blade fell from nerveless fingers, clattering against the jade floor. He collapsed to his knees, smoke rising from his skin, his face twisted in agony.

The referee elder rushed forward, hands glowing with healing light. "The match is over! Jin Tian is the victor!"

The crowd erupted.

But Jin Tian wasn't looking at the crowd. He was looking at Liu Feng, at the transformation that had come over the inner disciple's face. The pain was fading, replaced by something else.

Recognition.

"You..." Liu Feng whispered, his voice barely audible over the shouting. "You fight like him. Like the Sword God."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

"Who?" he demanded, stepping closer. "Who told you to kill him?"

Liu Feng's eyes went wide. "How did you—"

The arrow came from nowhere.

It pierced Liu Feng's throat with surgical precision, punching through flesh and cartilage to emerge from the other side. The inner disciple's words died in a gurgle of blood. He toppled forward, dead before he hit the ground.

Jin Tian spun, his sword coming up, but the assassin was already gone. There was only the chaos of the crowd, the screaming of disciples, the shouting of elders.

And in the distance, barely visible against the setting sun, a figure in black robes disappearing into the shadow of the sect's main hall.

Jin Tian stood alone in the arena, Liu Feng's blood pooling at his feet, the weight of three centuries pressing down on his shoulders.

Someone in this sect had known about the Sword God.

Someone had killed to keep the secret.

And now that someone knew that Jin Tian was asking questions.

title: The Disciple Who Should Be Dead
content:
The training grounds had emptied by the time Jin Tian finished his preparations. Three hours until his match against Liu Feng. Three hours to uncover the truth that had haunted him through death and rebirth.

He sat cross-legged in the meditation chamber beneath the main hall, the cold stone grounding him in the present. His cultivation base was still shallow—Meridian Opening Stage, barely sufficient to channel Qi through a quarter of his channels. Against an inner disciple like Liu Feng, who had likely reached the Qi Condensation Stage, the gap was laughable.

But Jin Tian had never relied on raw power alone.

He closed his eyes and let his consciousness drift, not into meditation, but into memory. The moment of his death replayed with perfect clarity: Liu Feng's blade, black as obsidian, piercing through his chest from behind. The shock of betrayal. The confusion that had followed him into the darkness.

Why Liu Feng?

In his first life, they had never been close. Never enemies either. Liu Feng was the quiet type—ambitious but not cruel, competitive but not malicious. The kind of disciple who kept his head down and his blade sharp. The kind who rose through the ranks on merit alone.

Not the kind who assassinated sect elders.

Unless something had changed. Unless someone had changed him.

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open.

The chamber's entrance creaked, and a shadow fell across the floor. Jin Tian's hand moved to his sword, but he stopped when he recognized the figure.

Chen Wei stepped into the torchlight, his face unreadable. He was alone, his robes immaculate, his bearing that of a prince condescending to visit a peasant.

"I thought I'd find you here," Chen Wei said, his voice lacking the venom Jin Tian expected. "Hiding in the dark, plotting your little victories."

"I'm meditating."

"You're scheming. Don't insult my intelligence." Chen Wei walked closer, stopping just outside Jin Tian's reach. "I've been watching you, Jin Tian. For months now. Do you know what I've seen?"

Jin Tian said nothing.

"A boy who fights like he's lived a hundred lives. A boy who knows his opponents' weaknesses before they even draw their blades. A boy who should be nothing—a talentless orphan from the outer mountains—yet somehow keeps winning." Chen Wei's eyes narrowed. "It's not natural."

"Neither is losing to someone you consider beneath you."

Chen Wei's composure cracked, a flash of anger crossing his features. "You think this is a game? You think the inner division tournament is about proving who's stronger?" He laughed, bitter and sharp. "You have no idea what you've walked into, Jin Tian. No idea what waits for you in that arena."

"Then enlighten me."

For a long moment, Chen Wei said nothing. He studied Jin Tian with an intensity that bordered on desperate, as if searching for something he couldn't find.

"Liu Feng is going to kill you," Chen Wei said finally. "Not defeat you. Not embarrass you. Kill you. The tournament rules don't forbid lethal force in matches between inner and outer disciples. It's considered 'acceptable losses'—a way to cull the weak."

"I'm aware."

"No, you're not." Chen Wei stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "Liu Feng has been given orders. Specific orders. He's not fighting for rank or glory. He's fighting on someone else's behalf. Someone who wants you dead before you reach the inner sect."

The words hung in the air like smoke.

Jin Tian felt his blood run cold, but his face remained still. "Who?"

"If I knew that, I wouldn't be warning you. I'd be helping them." Chen Wei's lips curled into a bitter smile. "But I don't like being used as a pawn. And I don't like watching talented people die for reasons they don't understand."

"You're warning me because you respect talent?"

"I'm warning you because if you die in that arena, I'll never get the satisfaction of beating you myself." Chen Wei turned and walked toward the exit. At the threshold, he paused. "One more thing. That girl you've been training with—Lian Hua. She's been asking questions about you. About your past. About where you learned to fight."

Jin Tian's heart stopped.

"She's clever. Too clever for her own good." Chen Wei glanced back, his expression unreadable. "If I were you, I'd be careful who I trusted."

Then he was gone, leaving Jin Tian alone with the echoes of his warning.

---

The arena was packed when Jin Tian arrived.

Disciples lined the stone walls four deep, their faces a mix of anticipation and bloodlust. The inner division tournament was the highlight of the sect's calendar—a chance to see the strong devour the weak, to witness the forging of legends.

Jin Tian paid them no attention.

His eyes were fixed on the figure standing at the arena's center.

Liu Feng was taller than he remembered, his build lean and dangerous. He wore the black and crimson robes of an inner disciple, his long hair tied back in a warrior's knot. At his hip hung a blade that seemed to drink the light—the same blade that had ended Jin Tian's first life.

But it wasn't Liu Feng's appearance that made Jin Tian pause.

It was his eyes.

They were empty. Hollow. Like a doll's eyes, devoid of the spark that had defined the man Jin Tian once knew. This wasn't the Liu Feng he remembered—the quiet prodigy with dry humor and unwavering principles. This was a shell. A puppet.

And puppets had masters.

Elder Zhou stood on the raised platform overlooking the arena, his voice carrying across the crowd. "The first match of the inner division tournament will now begin. Outer Disciple Jin Tian versus Inner Disciple Liu Feng. The match ends when one combatant yields, is incapacitated, or—" he paused, his eyes lingering on Jin Tian, "—is unable to continue."

The gong sounded.

Liu Feng moved like water—smooth, fluid, inevitable. His blade cleared its sheath before Jin Tian could blink, a crescent of black Qi slicing through the air where Jin Tian had been standing.

Jin Tian rolled, feeling the heat of the attack pass inches from his face. He came up with his own sword drawn, the familiar weight settling into his hands.

"You're fast," Liu Feng said, his voice flat. "But speed won't save you."

He attacked again, his strikes coming in a relentless barrage. Fire-aspected Qi blazed along his blade, leaving trails of heat that made the air shimmer. Each strike was precise, calculated, deadly.

Jin Tian deflected, dodged, retreated. His body screamed in protest—he was pushing his Meridian Opening Stage to its absolute limit, channeling Qi through channels that were barely formed. One wrong move, and his cultivation base would shatter.

But he couldn't afford to hold back.

Because Liu Feng wasn't just fighting well. He was fighting perfectly. Every strike landed exactly where it should. Every feint was executed with flawless timing. There were no wasted movements, no openings, no weaknesses.

This wasn't the skill of a seventh-ranked inner disciple.

This was the skill of a master.

Jin Tian's mind raced. Liu Feng's cultivation base should be at the second level of Qi Condensation at most. But the power behind his strikes suggested something else—something darker. The Qi he used felt wrong, tainted, like oil mixed with wine.

"You feel it, don't you?" Liu Feng said, pressing his advantage. "The difference between us. You're fighting with borrowed knowledge. I'm fighting with true power."

"Borrowed knowledge?" Jin Tian deflected a strike aimed at his throat, the impact jarring his arm. "Is that what they told you?"

"I was told many things. About you. About your past. About the threat you pose to the sect."

"Who told you?"

Liu Feng's blade stopped an inch from Jin Tian's chest. The crowd held its breath.

"That's not important," Liu Feng said. "What matters is that you die here. Today. Before you can corrupt anyone else with your lies."

Jin Tian looked into those hollow eyes and saw the truth.

Liu Feng wasn't his enemy. Liu Feng was a victim—brainwashed, controlled, turned into a weapon by the same forces that had orchestrated Jin Tian's death in his first life. The same forces that had set his disciples against him. The same forces that had destroyed everything he loved.

And if Jin Tian killed Liu Feng now, he would never learn who those forces were.

"Liu Feng," Jin Tian said, lowering his sword slightly. "Do you remember the bamboo grove?"

Liu Feng's blade wavered.

"Three years ago. You were an outer disciple, like me. You had just lost your mother. You came to the grove to be alone, but I was already there, practicing my forms." Jin Tian met his eyes. "You didn't say anything. You just watched. And when I finished, you nodded once, and left."

Liu Feng's face flickered—confusion, recognition, pain. "How... how do you know that?"

"Because I was there. And because I remember you, Liu Feng. Not the puppet standing before me now. The real you. The one who loved his mother. The one who wanted to be a swordsman, not a killer."

"I am a swordsman."

"Then prove it." Jin Tian sheathed his sword. "Fight me with your own skill. Not the poison running through your veins. Not the commands whispering in your mind. Fight me as the Liu Feng I remember."

The arena was silent.

Liu Feng's hand trembled. His blade dipped, then rose, then dipped again. His eyes—those hollow, empty eyes—began to clear, as if a fog was lifting from his soul.

"I... I don't..."

A sharp whistle cut through the air.

Liu Feng's body went rigid. His eyes snapped wide, then glazed over, the fog returning thicker than before. When he spoke again, his voice was wrong—layered, as if two people were speaking at once.

"You should not have done that, Sword God."

Jin Tian's blood turned to ice.

No one called him Sword God. No one except—

"Is that you, Elder Zhou?" Jin Tian called out, not taking his eyes off Liu Feng. "Or should I address you by your true name?"

The voice laughed through Liu Feng's throat. "Clever boy. But cleverness won't save you. You've already lost, Jin Tian. You just don't know it yet."

Liu Feng's blade erupted with black fire.

And the true battle began.

title: The First Step on a New Path
content:
Jin Tian walked away from the tournament grounds with measured steps, his outer robes billowing slightly in the afternoon breeze. Behind him, the murmurs of disciples and elders blended into a dull roar, but he paid them no mind. His focus had narrowed to a single point—the three hours before his match against Liu Feng.

He needed a plan.

In his past life, he had fought Liu Feng dozens of times. He knew the man's patterns, his strengths, his tells. But knowing and defeating were two different things, especially when his cultivation base was still at the fourth stage of Qi Condensation while Liu Feng operated at the seventh. The gap in raw power was significant—three whole stages that normally would take years to bridge.

But Jin Tian had centuries of experience that Liu Feng didn't.

He found a secluded training ground behind the outer disciple dormitories, a small clearing surrounded by bamboo groves where the morning dew still clung to the leaves. The ground was packed earth, scarred by years of practice swords and countless footfalls. It would do.

He drew his practice sword—a simple iron blade, unadorned and unremarkable. In his past life, he would have scoffed at such a weapon. Now, he treated it with the respect it deserved. A sword was a sword, regardless of its make. The difference lay in the hand that held it.

Jin Tian closed his eyes and breathed.

Three hours. He needed to push his cultivation to the fifth stage of Qi Condensation before the match. It was an aggressive timeline—normally, breaking through a stage required weeks of careful meditation and qi gathering. But Jin Tian had done this before, and he knew shortcuts that others didn't.

He began to move, his sword tracing lazy arcs through the air. The movements were slow, almost meditative, but each one carried precise intent. He wasn't practicing forms—he was building pathways. His qi followed the sword's edge, carving channels through his meridians that had long since atrophied from disuse.

*Pain flared through his arms.* His body was young, but it had never been pushed this hard. The meridians protested, strained by the sudden influx of qi they weren't designed to handle. Jin Tian gritted his teeth and continued.

*"The body is a vessel," his master had taught him in his first life. "And like any vessel, it can be expanded. But expansion comes at a cost."*

The cost was pain. Agony that lanced through his limbs and settled in his dantian like a burning coal. Jin Tian welcomed it. Pain meant progress. Pain meant growth.

By the time the sun had shifted noticeably in the sky, he had broken through.

The fifth stage of Qi Condensation settled over him like a second skin, his meridians humming with newfound energy. He flexed his fingers, testing the flow of qi through his arms. It was rough, unrefined—a far cry from the mastery he had once commanded. But it would have to do.

He had two hours left.

Jin Tian sat cross-legged on the packed earth, placing his practice sword across his knees. His breathing slowed, deepened, as he entered a state of half-meditation. His mind drifted back to his past life, to the countless battles he had fought, the techniques he had mastered, the enemies he had slain.

Liu Feng's fighting style came into sharp focus.

The inner disciple favored fire-aspected techniques—explosive, aggressive, overwhelming. He would open with a burst of flame to disorient his opponent, then follow with a series of rapid strikes designed to break through their defenses. His weakness was endurance. Liu Feng burned bright, but he burned fast. After thirty exchanges, his qi reserves would begin to flag, his attacks losing their edge.

In a normal match, Jin Tian could simply weather the storm and counterattack when Liu Feng's energy waned. But Jin Tian wasn't playing the long game. He needed information. He needed to understand *why* Liu Feng had killed him in his past life.

That meant he couldn't just win. He had to make Liu Feng talk.

A shadow fell over him.

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open. Standing at the edge of the training ground was a figure he recognized immediately—Chen Wei, flanked by two other outer disciples. The boy's face had regained its composure, but his eyes still burned with suppressed fury.

"Impressive," Chen Wei said, his voice carrying a false lightness. "Breaking through to the fifth stage in just a few hours. I underestimated you, Jin Tian."

Jin Tian rose slowly, his practice sword still in hand. "Is there something you need, Chen Wei?"

"I came to offer my congratulations." Chen Wei stepped closer, his companions following like obedient shadows. "And perhaps a word of advice. Liu Feng is not like the opponents you've faced in the outer division. He's killed before. In the arena, during suppression missions. He doesn't hold back."

"I'm aware."

"Are you?" Chen Wei's smile turned sharp. "I've seen Liu Feng fight. He's brutal. Efficient. When he wins, he doesn't just defeat his opponent—he *breaks* them. Crippling injuries are common. Permanent damage is expected. The arena overseers look the other way because Liu Feng is valuable."

Jin Tian met Chen Wei's gaze evenly. "Is there a point to this warning, or are you simply trying to frighten me?"

Chen Wei laughed, but there was no humor in it. "I'm trying to give you an out. Withdraw from the match. Let me take your place. I'll handle Liu Feng, and you can go back to being the unnoticed outer disciple you've always been."

*Ah.* There it was. Chen Wei wasn't worried about Jin Tian's safety—he was worried about his own opportunities. The inner division tournament was a path to advancement, to resources, to power. Chen Wei had been willing to let Jin Tian take the slot earlier because he'd assumed Jin Tian would lose. But now, seeing Jin Tian's rapid progress, he was having second thoughts.

"I appreciate the concern," Jin Tian said, his voice flat. "But I'll be keeping my slot."

Chen Wei's expression hardened. "You're making a mistake."

"Perhaps." Jin Tian turned his back on them, a deliberate show of contempt. "But it's my mistake to make."

For a long moment, nothing happened. Jin Tian could feel Chen Wei's gaze boring into his back, could sense the tension in the air as the other disciples waited for a command. But no command came.

"Very well," Chen Wei said finally, his voice cold. "Don't say I didn't warn you."

The sound of retreating footsteps faded into the bamboo grove. Jin Tian let out a slow breath, releasing the tension that had coiled in his shoulders. Chen Wei was a problem for another day. Right now, he needed to focus on Liu Feng.

He checked the position of the sun. One hour remained.

Jin Tian spent the next sixty minutes drilling a single technique—*Flowing Water Stance*, a basic qi-manipulation method that allowed the user to redirect incoming force. It wasn't flashy, and it wouldn't win him any points for elegance. But against Liu Feng's explosive attacks, it would be invaluable.

By the time the arena overseer's voice echoed across the compound, summoning him to the main stage, Jin Tian was ready.

The arena was packed. Word of the match had spread quickly, and disciples from all three divisions had gathered to watch. Inner disciples stood in elevated viewing platforms, their expressions ranging from bored to mildly interested. Outer disciples crowded the ground level, craning their necks for a better view.

Liu Feng was already on the stage.

He was older than Jin Tian remembered—nineteen, with a lean, athletic build and close-cropped black hair. His robes were the deep crimson of the inner division, and at his waist hung a sword that radiated heat, its blade shimmering with barely contained fire qi. His face was impassive, his eyes flat and calculating.

Jin Tian stepped onto the stage, his simple practice sword feeling woefully inadequate. A ripple of laughter passed through the crowd at the sight of his weapon.

"Is that all he brought to a match against Liu Feng?"

"The outer disciples really are desperate."

"He won't last three exchanges."

Jin Tian ignored them. His focus was entirely on Liu Feng, on the subtle shifts in the man's stance, the way his fingers rested lightly on his sword's hilt. In his past life, he had seen Liu Feng make that same gesture thousands of times. It meant he was about to strike.

The overseer raised his hand. "The match will begin on my signal. Both disciples, prepare yourselves."

Liu Feng drew his sword, and the air around them grew thick with heat. The blade burned with crimson light, flames licking along its edge.

Jin Tian raised his practice sword, assuming the *Flowing Water Stance*.

"You're bold," Liu Feng said, his voice low enough that only Jin Tian could hear. "Coming here with an iron toy. Do you have a death wish?"

"I have questions," Jin Tian replied, equally quiet. "And you're going to answer them."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. "Is that so?"

The overseer's hand dropped.

Liu Feng exploded forward, his sword trailing fire like a comet's tail. The crowd roared as he closed the distance in a heartbeat, his blade arcing toward Jin Tian's neck.

Jin Tian didn't block.

He *redirected*.

His practice sword met Liu Feng's blade at an angle, shimmering with qi as he guided the strike past his shoulder instead of meeting it head-on. The flames licked at his robes but didn't catch. Liu Feng's momentum carried him forward, past Jin Tian's guard, and for a single, perfect moment, his back was exposed.

Jin Tian struck.

His blade connected with Liu Feng's ribs—not hard enough to wound, but enough to send a message. The crowd's roar faltered, replaced by gasps of disbelief. Liu Feng stumbled, catching himself before he fell, and turned to face Jin Tian with eyes that burned with fury.

"You—"

"I told you," Jin Tian said, settling back into his stance. "I have questions."

Liu Feng's grip on his sword tightened until his knuckles went white. The flames along his blade grew brighter, hotter, until the air around them shimmered with heat distortion.

"Then ask," Liu Feng snarled. "Because after I crush you, you won't have the breath to speak."

title: The Weight of Shadows
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived.

He had chosen this place deliberately—a secluded clearing behind the outer disciple dormitories, where the bamboo grew thick and the moonlight filtered through the leaves like scattered jade. In his first life, he had spent countless nights here, perfecting techniques that would later define his legend.

Now he was back, a boy of twelve with a god's memories and a child's body.

He sat cross-legged on the worn stone platform, closing his eyes. The ambient qi in the air was thin—barely a trickle compared to the rich spiritual energy of the inner sect—but it would have to suffice. He had three hours. Three hours to prepare for Liu Feng.

Liu Feng.

The name echoed in his mind, accompanied by the phantom sensation of cold steel piercing his chest. He had replayed that moment a thousand times in the void between death and rebirth. The blade had entered between his fourth and fifth ribs, angled upward to pierce his heart. A perfect strike. A killing blow delivered by someone who knew exactly where to cut.

But why?

Jin Tian pushed the question aside. Answers would come later. First, he needed to survive.

He began cycling his qi through his meridians, mapping the pathways that had atrophied in his young body. Three hundred years of cultivation had given him an intimate understanding of how energy flowed, how it pooled and surged and broke against obstacles. His current cultivation base was shallow—barely at the third level of Qi Condensation—but his control was absolute.

He could work with this.

The first hour passed in meditation. He reinforced his foundation, shoring up weaknesses that would have taken other disciples years to identify. The second hour, he practiced forms—not the simple techniques taught to outer disciples, but the fundamentals of the Heavenly Sword Dao, stripped of all ornamentation and reduced to pure efficiency.

By the third hour, he was ready.

Not strong. Not by the standards he had once commanded. But ready.

---

The inner division tournament was held in the Grand Assembly Hall, a massive structure of white stone and crimson pillars that could hold five thousand spectators. When Jin Tian arrived, the hall was already packed. Disciples lined the walls, elders occupied the raised platforms, and in the center, the combat arena gleamed under the light of formation-enhanced lamps.

The arena was a perfect circle, fifty meters in diameter, ringed by protective barriers that could withstand attacks from Nascent Soul cultivators. The floor was made of tempered spirit stone, scarred by countless battles but never damaged beyond repair.

Jin Tian stepped through the crowd, feeling eyes on him. Whispers followed in his wake.

"That's the outer disciple who challenged Chen Wei."

"Did you hear? He proved his efficiency score was higher. Bold move."

"Bold or stupid. He's facing Liu Feng. Seventh-ranked inner disciple. He'll be lucky to walk away intact."

Jin Tian ignored them. He had learned long ago that words were wind. Only steel mattered.

He reached the competitor's waiting area and found Liu Feng already there.

The inner disciple was older than Jin Tian remembered—nineteen, with sharp features and eyes that burned like embers. His robes were crimson and gold, marking him as a disciple of the Blazing Flame Peak. A sword hung at his hip, its scabbard etched with fire-aspected formations that pulsed with contained heat.

Liu Feng looked up as Jin Tian approached. His gaze was curious, but not hostile. Not yet.

"You're the one who took Chen Wei's slot," Liu Feng said. His voice was calm, measured. "I expected someone older."

"Age isn't everything," Jin Tian replied.

"No. It isn't." Liu Feng studied him for a long moment. "I've seen your matches. You fight efficiently. No wasted movement. No unnecessary flourishes."

"I prefer to save my energy for when it matters."

Liu Feng's lips quirked. "Then you'll need every scrap of it today. I don't hold back against opponents, regardless of their age or rank."

"I would be disappointed if you did."

The inner disciple laughed—a genuine sound, warm and unexpected. "I like you, outer disciple. What's your name?"

"Jin Tian."

"Jin Tian." Liu Feng tested the name on his tongue. "Remember it. After today, the sect will know it too. Whether as a cautionary tale or a rising star... that depends on you."

Before Jin Tian could respond, a gong sounded. The crowd fell silent.

Elder Zhou rose from his seat on the main platform, his voice amplified by qi to reach every corner of the hall. "The first match of the inner division tournament will now commence. Inner Disciple Liu Feng, rank seven, versus Outer Disciple Jin Tian, rank one hundred and twelve."

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. The gap in ranking was absurd. Humiliating.

Jin Tian didn't care.

He stepped into the arena.

---

The protective barriers flared to life as Liu Feng took his position across from him. The inner disciple drew his sword—a blade of crimson steel that seemed to drink the light, its edge shimmering with heat haze.

"I'll give you one piece of advice," Liu Feng said, assuming a ready stance. "Don't try to block my flames directly. They'll burn through your qi faster than you can replenish it."

"Noted."

The gong sounded a second time. The match began.

Liu Feng moved first—a blazing charge that left afterimages in the air. His sword arced toward Jin Tian's neck, wreathed in flames that roared like a living thing.

Jin Tian didn't block.

He sidestepped, his movement minimal, economical. The flaming blade passed inches from his face, the heat singing his skin but failing to connect. He pivoted on his heel, bringing his own sword up in a counter-strike aimed at Liu Feng's exposed ribs.

Liu Feng twisted, deflecting the blow with his sword's flat. Sparks flew. The impact sent vibrations through Jin Tian's arms, reminding him of the difference in their physical cultivation. Liu Feng was stronger. Faster.

But Jin Tian had three hundred years of combat instinct.

They exchanged a dozen blows in the span of ten seconds. Jin Tian gave ground, not because he was forced to, but because he was measuring. Testing. Liu Feng's style was aggressive, fire-aspected techniques designed to overwhelm. Each strike carried the weight of his cultivation base, each flame a declaration of dominance.

But there was a pattern.

Every third attack, Liu Feng's left shoulder dipped slightly before he launched a heavy strike. It was a tell so subtle that most opponents would never notice it. Jin Tian noticed.

When the pattern repeated, Jin Tian was ready.

Liu Feng's shoulder dipped. His sword came around in a blazing horizontal arc, aiming to cleave Jin Tian in half.

Jin Tian dropped.

He fell to one knee, the blade passing over his head, and drove his sword upward—not at Liu Feng's body, but at his wrist. The tip found the gap between his vambrace and gauntlet, piercing flesh.

Liu Feng hissed, pulling back. Blood dripped from his wrist, sizzling as it hit the spirit stone floor.

The crowd gasped.

"You cut me," Liu Feng said, eyes wide with something between surprise and respect.

"I told you. I prefer to save my energy."

Liu Feng's smile returned, sharper now. "Good. I was worried this would be boring."

He exploded forward, flames erupting from his body in a corona of heat. The temperature in the arena spiked. The protective barriers groaned. Liu Feng's sword became a blur of fire and death, each strike faster than the last, each blow carrying the full weight of his cultivation.

Jin Tian's world narrowed to a single point of focus.

He moved like water, flowing around each attack, deflecting what he couldn't dodge, redirecting force instead of meeting it head-on. His sword became an extension of his will, parrying and countering with precision that bordered on supernatural.

But Liu Feng was relentless.

A kick caught Jin Tian in the chest, sending him sliding back. He recovered, only to find Liu Feng already there, sword descending in a vertical slash that would split him from shoulder to hip.

Jin Tian raised his blade to block.

The impact drove him to one knee. The spirit stone beneath him cracked. His arms screamed in protest, the difference in cultivation base finally making itself felt. Liu Feng pressed down, flames licking at Jin Tian's guard, searing his skin.

"Yield," Liu Feng said, his voice strained with exertion. "There's no shame in admitting defeat to a superior opponent."

Jin Tian looked up at him.

In his mind, he saw a different scene. A dark night. A blade piercing his heart. Liu Feng's face, cold and empty, as he twisted the sword.

"You killed me," Jin Tian whispered.

Liu Feng's eyes widened. "What?"

"I'm not yielding."

With a surge of qi, Jin Tian twisted his blade, redirecting Liu Feng's sword past his shoulder. He rose, driving his shoulder into Liu Feng's chest, then brought his sword around in a horizontal slash that forced the inner disciple to leap back.

They faced each other across the arena, both breathing hard.

"You fight like someone who's already died," Liu Feng said, his voice low. "Like you have nothing to lose."

"I have everything to lose," Jin Tian replied. "That's why I can't afford to lose anything else."

Liu Feng's expression shifted. The competitive fire in his eyes flickered, replaced by something more complex. Recognition. Understanding.

"You know something," Liu Feng said slowly. "About me. About what's coming."

Jin Tian didn't answer.

"I don't know what you think you know," Liu Feng continued, "but I can promise you this: when the time comes, I won't be the one you need to worry about."

"What does that mean?"

Liu Feng opened his mouth to respond—

And then the arena shattered.

---

The protective barriers didn't break. They were obliterated.

A wave of force slammed into the arena, throwing Jin Tian and Liu Feng apart. The crowd screamed. Elders rose from their seats, hands going to their weapons. The Grand Assembly Hall shook, dust raining from the ceiling.

In the center of the arena, where the protective barriers had been, stood a figure wreathed in shadows.

It was a woman. Tall, with hair that flowed like liquid night and eyes that burned with violet light. Her robes were black, unadorned, but the aura she radiated was suffocating. She was a cultivator at the peak of the Nascent Soul realm—maybe higher.

She looked at Jin Tian.

And smiled.

"So," she said, her voice like velvet over steel, "you survived after all. I was beginning to think my information was wrong."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

He knew that voice.

In his first life, he had heard it only once. Whispered in his ear as he lay dying, his lifeblood pooling beneath him.

"Interesting," the woman said, stepping closer. "You're weaker than I expected. But the talent—" her eyes traced his meridians with obvious hunger, "—the talent is unmistakable."

Elder Zhou's voice thundered across the hall. "Intruder! Identify yourself or be destroyed!"

The woman laughed. It was a beautiful sound. Terrifying.

"Destroyed? By you?" She turned to face Elder Zhou, and Jin Tian saw the elder's face pale. "I've destroyed cultivators like you before you were born, old man. Don't test me."

She turned back to Jin Tian, her smile widening.

"I'm not here to fight. I'm here to deliver a message." She reached into her robes and pulled out a small jade slip, tossing it to Jin Tian's feet.

title: The Traitor's Shadow
content:
The arena for inner disciple matches was a different world entirely.

Jin Tian stood at the entrance, watching the stone platform rotate slowly in the air, suspended by formation arrays that hummed with power. Spectral barriers flickered at its edges, designed to contain even the most violent eruptions of qi. The outer disciple rings were dirt and desperation. This was marble and murder.

Three hours. He had used them well.

His fingers traced the wrapped haft of the practice blade he had convinced the quartermaster to lend him. Not his preferred weapon—nothing in the outer section could match what he had wielded in his past life—but it would serve. The weight was wrong, the balance off by a hair's breadth, but he had compensated with a subtle alteration to his grip. Small adjustments that would mean nothing to most observers and everything to the outcome.

The crowd had gathered. Inner disciples in their blue and silver robes lined the elevated viewing platforms, their faces a mix of curiosity and condescension. An outer disciple challenging in the inner tournament was unprecedented. An outer disciple *winning* the slot was scandalous.

And then there was Liu Feng.

He stood at the opposite end of the arena, already on the platform, his arms crossed and his expression unreadable. He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader in the shoulders, with a shock of red hair that matched the fire-aspected techniques he was famous for. His eyes were amber, almost golden, and they never blinked.

In Jin Tian's first life, those eyes had been the last thing he saw before the blade entered his heart.

*Focus.*

He stepped onto the platform. The moment his foot touched the stone, the formation activated, sealing them in. A low hum vibrated through his bones, and the air grew heavy with suppressed power.

Elder Zhou's voice carried from the highest viewing platform, amplified by qi. "This match will follow standard inner tournament rules. Combat ends upon submission, incapacitation, or when one party is thrown from the platform. Killing your opponent is forbidden, though accidents do happen." A pause. "Begin."

Liu Feng didn't move.

Jin Tian didn't either.

They stood across from each other, thirty feet of polished stone between them, and the silence stretched.

"You're interesting," Liu Feng said finally. His voice was rough, like stones grinding together. "I watched your match against Chen Wei. You moved like you knew what he was going to do before he did it."

"I studied him."

"No." Liu Feng shook his head slowly. "That wasn't study. That was prediction. You knew his patterns before he showed them. You knew his weaknesses before he revealed them." He tilted his head, and his amber eyes narrowed. "That's not something an outer disciple should be capable of."

Jin Tian's grip tightened on his blade. "Maybe I'm just talented."

"Maybe." Liu Feng's hand drifted to his own sword—a curved blade, wicked and hungry, its edge gleaming with a faint red light. "Or maybe you're something else entirely."

He attacked.

The movement was so fast that Jin Tian's mortal eyes barely tracked it. In his past life, he would have been dead already, his throat opened before his brain registered the threat. But his soul remembered. His instincts screamed.

He sidestepped.

The curved blade whistled past his ear, close enough to slice a few strands of hair. Jin Tian didn't counterattack. He flowed backward, creating distance, his eyes fixed on Liu Feng's stance.

*Fast. Faster than the records suggested. And he's holding back.*

Liu Feng pressed forward, his sword a blur of crimson light. Each strike was precise, economical, designed to kill with minimal movement. He didn't waste energy on flourishes or feints. Every attack aimed for a vital point—throat, heart, kidneys, femoral artery.

Jin Tian dodged. Blocked. Redirected.

He didn't strike back.

The crowd murmured. They saw an outer disciple barely surviving, stumbling away from attacks he couldn't hope to match. They saw a mismatch, a boy out of his depth against a true prodigy.

They were wrong.

Jin Tian was reading Liu Feng. Not his techniques—those were standard, predictable, exactly what the inner sect taught. But his *tells*. The micro-expressions that crossed his face before each attack. The slight shift in his weight before he committed to a movement. The way his eyes flickered to specific targets before his blade followed.

Liu Feng was a killer. He had been trained as one, honed as one, and in another life, he had succeeded. But training created patterns, and patterns could be exploited.

"You're not attacking," Liu Feng said, his blade cutting through empty air where Jin Tian's chest had been a moment before.

"I'm learning."

"Learning what?"

Jin Tian's lips curved into a thin smile. "Why you killed me."

Liu Feng froze.

It was only for a fraction of a second—a hesitation so brief that no one in the audience noticed, that no recording formation would register. But Jin Tian saw it. He felt the shift in Liu Feng's qi, the sudden spike of alarm that his disciplined breathing couldn't quite suppress.

*Interesting.*

"What did you say?" Liu Feng's voice had dropped, losing its rough edges, becoming something colder.

"I said I'm learning why you're so eager to prove yourself against an outer disciple." Jin Tian kept his voice calm, conversational. "A ranked inner disciple should have nothing to prove against someone like me. Yet here you are. Fighting like your life depends on it."

Liu Feng's jaw tightened. "You don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't I?"

Jin Tian attacked.

It was the first time he had gone on the offensive, and the change in tempo caught Liu Feng off guard. His blade—borrowed, unbalanced, wrong—still sang through the air with a precision that belied its flaws. He struck high, then low, then mid, each strike flowing into the next like water carving through stone.

Liu Feng retreated.

The crowd gasped.

*That's right. Run. Show them what you really are.*

Jin Tian pressed his advantage, driving Liu Feng toward the edge of the platform. His attacks weren't aimed to kill—they were aimed to *reveal*. Each strike forced Liu Feng into a specific response, exposing his footwork, his recovery time, the subtle weakness in his left guard that he compensated for with raw aggression.

"Why did you do it?" Jin Tian asked, his voice low enough that only Liu Feng could hear. "We weren't enemies. We were rivals. Competitors. You had nothing to gain from my death."

Liu Feng's eyes widened. "You're insane. I've never—"

"The blade entered just below my sternum," Jin Tian continued, his voice flat, emotionless, as if reciting a historical fact. "Angled upward, through the heart. A killing stroke, not a combat wound. You meant to execute me, not defeat me."

"You're *mad*." Liu Feng's voice cracked, just slightly.

"Am I?" Jin Tian's blade slipped past Liu Feng's guard, drawing a thin line of blood across his forearm. "Then why are you afraid?"

Liu Feng exploded.

Fire erupted from his body, a corona of crimson and gold that forced Jin Tian back. The temperature on the platform spiked, the stone beneath their feet cracking from the heat. Liu Feng's eyes blazed, his qi surging like a volcano preparing to erupt.

"You want to see what I really am?" Liu Feng's voice had changed again. It was deeper now, rougher, layered with something that wasn't quite human. "Fine. But don't blame me if you burn."

He moved.

Jin Tian barely got his blade up in time. The impact sent shockwaves through his arms, numbing his fingers. Liu Feng's strength had tripled, his speed doubled. Every strike carried the weight of a collapsing mountain, the heat of a forge's heart.

*This is it. This is what killed me before.*

But Jin Tian was not the same man he had been.

In his past life, he had been caught off guard by Liu Feng's true power. He had underestimated his enemy, assumed that the ranked inner disciple was merely talented rather than something more. He had paid for that mistake with his life.

This time, he had come prepared.

Jin Tian's blade shifted. The grip changed, the angle adjusted, the weight distribution altered by a fraction of a millimeter. It was a modification he had developed in his first life, a technique designed specifically for fighting opponents who drew power from external sources.

It was called the Severing Strike.

Liu Feng's blade came down, wreathed in flames that could melt steel. Jin Tian met it with a single, precise cut—not against the blade, but against the qi flowing through it.

The fire died.

Liu Feng stumbled, his connection to his power severed for a single, crucial moment. His eyes went wide, his balance compromised, his guard open.

Jin Tian's blade touched his throat.

"Yield."

The word hung in the air, absolute, undeniable. The crowd was silent, stunned. Elder Zhou rose from his seat, his weathered face showing something that might have been shock.

Liu Feng stared at the blade at his throat. His hands trembled. His qi flickered, unstable, desperate to reassert itself.

"You should kill me," he whispered.

"Why?"

"Because if you don't, I'll come for you again. They'll make me." Liu Feng's voice was barely audible, broken. "I don't have a choice. I never had a choice."

Jin Tian's eyes narrowed. "Who?"

But before Liu Feng could answer, a scream tore through the arena.

It came from the viewing platforms—high, shrill, cut off abruptly. Heads turned. Disciples scrambled. And Jin Tian saw, through the chaos, a figure in white robes standing where Elder Zhou had been moments before.

The figure held a bloodied dagger.

Elder Zhou lay at his feet, his throat opened, his eyes still wide with shock.

The figure turned.

Chen Wei smiled.

"Surprise, senior brother," he said, his voice carrying across the frozen arena. "Did you think I'd let you win?"

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Truth Beneath the Flames

Jin Tian stood in the shadow of the arena's eastern wall, his back pressed against cold stone. Three hours. He had three hours to prepare for a fight against a man who had killed him in his previous life, and every instinct screamed that this was no coincidence.

The tournament grounds buzzed with activity around him. Outer disciples rushed past carrying supplies. Servants arranged seating for the elders. Merchants set up stalls selling elixirs and talismans, capitalizing on the gathered crowd. But Jin Tian saw none of it. His mind was elsewhere, replaying that final moment from three hundred years ago.

Liu Feng's blade. The fire. The betrayal.

"I heard you caused quite a stir."

Jin Tian didn't turn. He had felt the presence approaching, recognized the measured footsteps. "I merely stated facts, Senior Sister Lian."

Lian Mei stepped beside him, her arms crossed. She was an inner disciple now, ranked twelfth, but she had started as an outer disciple just like him. In his first life, she had died during a demon beast outbreak five years from now. He had never known her well.

Now he wondered if he should.

"I also heard Chen Wei has been making inquiries about you," she said, her voice low. "He's visiting the inner disciple halls today. Meeting with several senior brothers."

"Let him."

"You're not taking this seriously." Lian Mei turned to face him fully. "Chen Wei's family controls the southern medicine trade. His father has donated enough spirit stones to the sect to buy favor with half the elder council. You embarrassed him publicly. That's not something his family forgets."

Jin Tian finally met her eyes. They were brown, sharp, calculating. "Are you warning me or testing me?"

A flicker of surprise crossed her face before she masked it. "Both. The tournament is tomorrow. But your match against Liu Feng happens today, in three hours. If I were you, I'd wonder why."

The words hung in the air.

"Why what?"

"Why the schedule was changed. Why an outer disciple's exhibition match was moved before the main tournament. Why Liu Feng was chosen as your opponent specifically." She leaned closer. "I've been an inner disciple for three years. I know how the administration thinks. This isn't standard procedure, Jin Tian. Someone wants to see you humiliated. Or worse."

She turned and walked away, leaving him alone with his thoughts.

Jin Tian watched her disappear into the crowd. In his first life, he had dismissed Lian Mei as irrelevant, a minor figure in the vast tapestry of the sect. But he was beginning to realize that his previous life had been filled with blind spots. He had been so focused on his own advancement, his own power, that he had failed to see the webs being woven around him.

Not this time.

He pushed off from the wall and began walking toward the inner disciple training grounds. He had three hours. That was enough time to gather intelligence, to observe his enemy, to prepare.

The training grounds were restricted to inner disciples, but Jin Tian knew the patrol schedules, the blind spots, the hidden pathways. Three hundred years of memories gave him an advantage that no amount of spirit stones could buy.

He found a vantage point atop a storage building, hidden behind stacks of training dummies. Below him, inner disciples practiced their techniques. A girl with ice-aspected qi froze a waterfall mid-flow. A young man shattered boulders with a single palm strike. And there, in the center of the grounds, sat Liu Feng.

He was meditating, cross-legged on a raised platform, his blade resting across his knees. The sword was a thing of beauty—black steel with crimson runes carved into the blade, pulsing with barely contained fire. Jin Tian remembered that sword well. He remembered the heat of it piercing his chest, the smell of his own flesh burning.

But now, watching Liu Feng's face, Jin Tian noticed something he hadn't in his first life. The man's brow was furrowed. His breathing was uneven. His hands, resting on the sword, trembled slightly.

Liu Feng was nervous.

Why would a ranked inner disciple be nervous about fighting an outer disciple?

Unless he knew something Jin Tian didn't.

Unless he had been ordered to do something he didn't want to do.

Jin Tian watched for another hour, memorizing every detail. Liu Feng's meditation posture revealed a slight favoring of his left side—an old injury, perhaps, or a hidden weakness. His breathing pattern suggested he was cycling qi through a blocked meridian, likely in his right shoulder. His sword, for all its beauty, had a hairline crack near the hilt that would make it unstable under extreme heat.

These were the details that mattered. These were the cracks that could be exploited.

When the hour bell rang, Jin Tian descended from his perch and made his way to the arena.

The crowd had gathered in force. Thousands of disciples, hundreds of elders, even a few visitors from neighboring sects had come to watch. The exhibition match between an outer disciple and an inner disciple was unusual enough to draw attention. The fact that it was Jin Tian, the boy who had publicly challenged Chen Wei, made it a spectacle.

Elder Zhou stood at the center of the arena, his voice amplified by qi. "Disciples, welcome. Today's exhibition match is between Outer Disciple Jin Tian and Inner Disciple Liu Feng. Standard rules apply: no lethal strikes, no forbidden techniques, and surrender is always an option."

Jin Tian stepped into the arena. The stone beneath his feet was warm, heated by the afternoon sun. Across from him, Liu Feng emerged from the opposite entrance, his black sword already drawn.

Their eyes met.

And in that moment, Jin Tian saw something that made his blood run cold.

Liu Feng's eyes weren't filled with the confidence of a superior fighter. They weren't filled with the nervousness he had shown during meditation. They were filled with fear.

Not fear of losing.

Fear of *him*.

"Begin!" Elder Zhou's voice rang out.

Liu Feng moved first, as expected. Fire erupted from his blade, a wave of crimson heat that swept across the arena. Jin Tian sidestepped, feeling the heat singe his robes. The crowd gasped at the display of power.

But Jin Tian had seen this attack before. In his first life, Liu Feng had opened every fight with the same technique—a wide, sweeping flame that forced opponents to dodge, giving him time to close the distance. It was predictable. It was exploitable.

Jin Tian didn't dodge the next attack. He stepped *into* it.

The crowd screamed in alarm as he disappeared into the flames. Liu Feng's eyes widened, his stance faltering for just a fraction of a second. That was all Jin Tian needed.

He emerged from the fire directly in front of Liu Feng, his wooden practice sword already swinging. The strike was aimed not at Liu Feng's body, but at the crack in his blade. The impact was precise, calculated, devastating.

The black sword shattered.

Liu Feng stumbled backward, shock written across his face. The runes on the broken blade flickered and died. For a moment, the arena was silent.

Then Jin Tian spoke, his voice carrying across the stunned crowd.

"Who sent you to kill me?"

The question was so direct, so unexpected, that Liu Feng's composure shattered completely. His face went pale. His hands trembled. And in that moment of vulnerability, Jin Tian saw the truth.

"I don't—" Liu Feng started.

"Don't lie to me." Jin Tian took a step closer, his voice dropping to a whisper that only Liu Feng could hear. "I know what you were ordered to do. I know what happens if you succeed. And I know what happens if you fail."

Liu Feng's breath caught. "How—"

"I know many things." Jin Tian's eyes bored into him. "I know about the meeting in the southern pavilion three days ago. I know about the poison that was supposed to be in my evening meal. I know about the payment that was delivered to your family compound this morning."

He didn't know any of this. He was bluffing, drawing on fragments of memory and logic. But the look on Liu Feng's face told him he had hit the mark.

"You're being used, Liu Feng. Just like I was." Jin Tian's voice softened. "Tell me who sent you, and I'll help you break free."

For a long moment, Liu Feng said nothing. The crowd watched in confusion, unable to hear their exchange. Elder Zhou frowned, clearly uncertain how to proceed.

Then Liu Feng spoke, his voice barely audible.

"It was Elder Wu."

The name hit Jin Tian like a physical blow. Elder Wu. Wu Chen. The head of the sect's disciplinary council. A man revered for his wisdom, his fairness, his unwavering commitment to justice.

In Jin Tian's first life, Elder Wu had been one of the few people he had trusted completely.

"He controls the southern medicine trade," Liu Feng continued, his words tumbling out now. "He owns Chen Wei's family. He's been planning something for years—something involving the tournament, involving the sect, involving—"

A sound cut through the air. A sharp, whistling noise that Jin Tian recognized immediately.

Arrow.

He grabbed Liu Feng and threw them both to the ground. The arrow streaked past, embedding itself in the stone where Liu Feng had been standing. The tip glowed with a sickly green light.

Poison.

The crowd erupted into chaos. Disciples screamed. Elders shouted. And in the confusion, Jin Tian saw a figure retreating from the eastern stands—a hooded figure that moved with practiced stealth.

He wanted to give chase. He needed to know who had fired that arrow, who was trying to silence Liu Feng before he could reveal more.

But when he looked down at the inner disciple, he knew it was too late.

The arrow hadn't struck Liu Feng. But the poison had been meant for him, and in his panic, Liu Feng had inhaled something—a mist, a powder, something that had been released when the arrow shattered against the stone.

His face was already turning blue. His breathing was ragged. His eyes, locked on Jin Tian's, held a desperate plea.

"Tell... my sister..." Liu Feng gasped. "She's in... the eastern compound... he'll go after her next..."

"Who?" Jin Tian demanded. "Who else is involved?"

But Liu Feng's eyes had already glazed over. His body went limp.

Jin Tian knelt there, surrounded by chaos, holding the body of a man who had killed him in another life, and realized the truth with chilling clarity.

The conspiracy he had uncovered in his first life—the betrayal of his seven disciples—had been only the surface. Beneath it, stretching back centuries, was something far larger. Something that involved the sect's highest authorities. Something that had already begun to move.

And they had just tried to kill him. Again.

He looked up at the stands, scanning the faces of the elders. Elder Zhou was shouting orders. Other elders were calming the crowd. But one face was conspicuously absent.

Elder Wu's seat was empty.

Jin Tian rose to his feet, his wooden sword still in hand. The tournament was supposed to begin tomorrow. The exhibition match was supposed to be a minor event.

Now everything had changed.

And somewhere in the shadows, the ones who had orchestrated his death once before were already planning their next move.

But this time, Jin Tian had a name.

And he would not stop until he had all of them.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Debt of Murders

The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived.

Most disciples were preparing for the evening meal or retreating to their quarters. The sun hung low, casting long shadows across the stone courtyard. It was perfect. No witnesses. No questions.

He needed to test something.

Jin Tian drew his practice blade—a cheap iron sword with a chipped edge, standard issue for outer disciples. In his previous life, he would have laughed at anyone who called this a weapon. Now, it was all he had.

He closed his eyes and reached for the qi within him.

It was thin. Pathetic. A trickle compared to the ocean he once commanded. But it was there, and more importantly, it remembered.

*The First Form: Heaven Cleaving Strike.*

His body moved before his mind caught up. The blade arced upward, trailing a whisper of silver light. It was clumsy. Slow. A child's imitation of the technique that had once split mountains.

But it was *possible*.

Jin Tian's eyes snapped open. In his previous life, he hadn't mastered Heaven Cleaving Strike until he was a Core Formation cultivator. The technique required immense qi reserves, perfect meridians, and years of practice.

Yet here he was, a Qi Condensation outer disciple, and the form had responded to him.

*The memories,* he realized. *My body remembers what it once knew. The pathways are there, even if the power isn't.*

It was an advantage he hadn't considered. His cultivation might be reset, but his understanding remained. Techniques that should have been impossible for a disciple of his level were now within reach—if he could supply enough qi.

He spent the next hour running through forms. Not the basic ones taught to outer disciples, but the advanced techniques from his previous life. Each one was slower, weaker, barely more than a shadow of its true power.

But each one worked.

By the time the bell rang for his match, Jin Tian had confirmed something crucial: he wasn't starting from zero. He was starting from the peak, buried under years of regression. If he could dig deep enough, fast enough, he might uncover power that no one expected.

---

The inner tournament arena was packed.

Unlike the outer division matches, which drew only a handful of observers, the inner division tournament was a major event. Elders, inner disciples, and even a few sect elders from allied factions had come to watch.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the arena, his iron sword at his side. Across from him, Liu Feng was already in position.

The inner disciple was everything Jin Tian remembered. Tall, broad-shouldered, with hair the color of dying embers. His cultivation was at the sixth level of Qi Condensation—three levels above Jin Tian. He wore the red-trimmed robes of an inner disciple, and at his waist hung a blade that shimmered with heat distortion.

A fire-aspected sword. Rare and expensive.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the arena. "I must admit, I was surprised when I heard my opponent. An outer disciple replacing Chen Wei? The selection committee must have seen something exceptional."

The words were polite. The tone was not.

Jin Tian didn't respond. He was too busy watching Liu Feng's stance, the way his weight shifted, the slight tension in his shoulders. These were tells he hadn't noticed in his previous life. He had been too focused on power levels and technique rankings.

Now he saw the truth.

Liu Feng was nervous.

Not afraid—the inner disciple had no reason to fear an outer disciple three levels below him. But there was a tightness in his movements, a carefully controlled energy that suggested he was holding something back.

*Interesting.*

The referee, an elder from the combat division, raised his hand. "The match will begin on my signal. No killing blows. Surrender is accepted. Do you understand?"

Both fighters nodded.

"Then..."

The hand dropped.

Liu Feng moved first.

Flames erupted from his blade as he closed the distance, leaving a trail of scorched stone behind him. The attack was direct, almost arrogant—a statement of power meant to overwhelm.

In his previous life, Jin Tian would have met it head-on. He would have matched fire with steel, strength with strength, and lost.

Now he did something different.

He stepped *inside* the arc of Liu Feng's swing.

It was a movement that defied logic. The space between them should have been filled with flame and steel, but Jin Tian slipped through it like water through cracks. His iron blade came up not to block, but to redirect.

The edge of his sword caught Liu Feng's wrist, deflecting the strike just enough that the flames passed harmlessly over Jin Tian's shoulder.

Liu Feng's eyes widened.

Jin Tian didn't give him time to recover. His blade snapped forward, not at Liu Feng's body, but at his sword hand. The flat of the blade slammed against the inner disciple's knuckles.

Liu Feng's grip loosened.

For a single heartbeat, his fire sword wobbled.

Then Jin Tian's foot connected with Liu Feng's knee, and the inner disciple stumbled backward, scrambling to regain his balance.

The crowd went silent.

"He disarmed him," someone whispered. "An outer disciple disarmed an inner disciple."

Liu Feng's face flushed red—not with embarrassment, but with something darker. His hand tightened on his sword, and the flames along its length grew hotter, brighter.

"You got lucky," he snarled.

Jin Tian said nothing. He simply raised his blade and waited.

Liu Feng attacked again.

This time, there was nothing controlled about his movements. Fire exploded from his blade in waves, turning the arena into a furnace. The stone beneath their feet cracked from the heat, and the spectators closest to the ring scrambled backward.

Jin Tian moved through it like a ghost.

He didn't block. He didn't parry. He flowed around each attack, his iron blade a constant presence at the edges of Liu Feng's awareness. Every time the inner disciple thought he had an opening, Jin Tian was already somewhere else.

And every time Jin Tian struck, it was against Liu Feng's hands, his wrists, his fingers—always targeting the grip, never the body.

After the fourth exchange, Liu Feng's sword clattered to the ground.

The inner disciple stared at his empty hand, then at Jin Tian. His expression cycled through disbelief, fury, and finally, cold recognition.

"You're not an outer disciple," he said, his voice low enough that only Jin Tian could hear. "Who are you?"

Jin Tian met his gaze. "Someone who wants answers."

"Answers to what?"

"To a murder that hasn't happened yet."

The confusion on Liu Feng's face was genuine. He didn't understand. He didn't remember.

But Jin Tian remembered. He remembered the blade through his heart, the flames that had consumed his final moments, the face of the man who had delivered the killing stroke.

And he remembered the words Liu Feng had whispered as he died.

*"Forgive me, Sword God. I have no choice."*

"What do you mean, 'no choice'?" Jin Tian asked, his voice barely audible.

Liu Feng's face went pale.

For a long moment, neither of them moved. The crowd watched, confused by the sudden stillness. The referee stepped forward, ready to intervene.

Then Liu Feng did something that made Jin Tian's blood run cold.

He smiled.

It was a broken thing, full of pain and resignation. "You know," he said. "You actually know."

"Know what?"

"Who sent me."

The referee's hand came down. "Match concluded! Winner: Outer Disciple Jin Tian!"

The crowd erupted in chaos. Outer disciples were cheering, inner disciples were muttering in disbelief, and several elders were already converging on the arena, eager to question the boy who had just humiliated a sixth-level Qi Condensation cultivator.

But Jin Tian didn't hear any of it.

He was still staring at Liu Feng, watching the inner disciple's smile fade into something else. Something desperate.

"Find me tonight," Liu Feng whispered as he turned away. "Behind the sect archives. Come alone."

Then he was gone, swallowed by the crowd, leaving Jin Tian standing in the center of the arena with a thousand questions burning in his mind.

---

The night air was cold.

Jin Tian stood in the shadow of the sect archives, his hand resting on his iron sword. The building loomed above him, ancient and silent, its walls covered in protective formations that pulsed with faint blue light.

He had come alone, as Liu Feng had asked.

He wasn't foolish enough to come unprepared.

Footsteps echoed from the darkness. Liu Feng emerged, his face tired, his fire sword conspicuously absent.

"You came," he said.

"I want answers."
