"And I want forgiveness." Liu Feng stopped a few meters away, his hands raised in a gesture of peace. "I don't have much time. They'll notice I'm gone soon."

"Who will?"

Liu Feng's jaw tightened. "The same people who ordered me to kill you in your past life."

Jin Tian's hand tightened on his sword. "Explain."

"I can't. Not fully. I don't even know who's really behind it." Liu Feng's voice cracked. "But three years from now, I'll receive an order. A command that I can't refuse. And I'll be sent to kill the strongest cultivator in the sect."

"The Sword God."

"Yes." Liu Feng met his eyes. "You."

Silence stretched between them.

"Why are you telling me this?" Jin Tian asked. "If you're going to be ordered to kill me, why warn me now?"

"Because I don't want to do it." Liu Feng's voice broke. "Because in your past life, I failed. I killed you, and it destroyed me. I spent the rest of my days trying to undo what I had done, but I couldn't. The cycle repeated. Over and over again."

Jin Tian's blood went cold. "What do you mean, 'over and over again'?"

Liu Feng's eyes filled with tears. "This isn't your first regression, Sword God. It's your *seventh*."

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow.

*Seven. Seven lives. Seven deaths.*

"And every time," Liu Feng continued, his voice barely a whisper, "you get a little further. A little stronger. But you never make it past the betrayal."

"Because you kill me."

"Because I have no choice!" Liu Feng's hands were shaking. "They have something on me. Something that controls me. I can't fight it. I've tried. In every life, I've tried."

"Then tell me who they are."

"I can't."

"Tell me!"

"I *can't*!" Liu Feng's eyes blazed with sudden fire. "If I say their name, they'll know. And then they'll accelerate the timeline. You're not ready yet. You need more time."

Jin Tian stepped forward, his blade half-drawn. "Then what good are you to me?"

Liu Feng met his gaze, and in that moment, Jin Tian saw something he hadn't expected.

Hope.

"I can give you the one thing you've never had in any of your lives," Liu Feng said. "I can show you who to trust."

He reached into his robe and pulled out a jade slip, its surface covered in ancient characters.

"This is a list," he said, holding it out. "The names of your seven disciples. The ones who will betray you."

Jin Tian's hand trembled as he took it.

"Read it," Liu Feng said. "And then burn it. Because once you know the truth, nothing will ever be the same."

Jin Tian raised the jade slip to the moonlight.

The first name made his heart stop.

Then the second.

By the time he reached the

title: The Price of Victory
content:
The arena stones still held the warmth of the morning sun as Jin Tian stepped onto their surface. Around him, the inner division tournament grounds buzzed with spectators—inner disciples, elders, and visiting cultivators from allied sects. The air smelled of incense and anticipation.

Across from him stood Liu Feng.

The inner disciple was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader in the shoulders, with flame-red hair that seemed to flicker even without wind. His cultivation base radiated at the fifth level of Qi Condensation—a full three levels above Jin Tian's current state. In their first life, the gap had been insurmountable.

Now Jin Tian saw the cracks in Liu Feng's foundation. The fire-aspected techniques had been pushed too fast, too aggressively. The meridians in his left arm were slightly scorched, the spiritual energy flowing through them turbulent rather than smooth. A flaw that would be invisible to anyone who hadn't spent three hundred years studying the human body as a weapon.

"Outer Disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying the practiced confidence of someone who had never known defeat. "I'll try not to hurt you too badly."

"I appreciate the consideration," Jin Tian replied, drawing his sword. The blade was standard-issue iron, unremarkable in every way. "But I wouldn't worry about me if I were you."

Liu Feng's eyes narrowed. "Arrogant words for someone who's about to be taught a lesson."

The overseer's hand fell. "Begin!"

Liu Feng exploded forward, flames wreathing his fists as he closed the distance in three heartbeats. His opening strike was a blazing palm aimed at Jin Tian's chest—a technique designed to overwhelm and intimidate.

Jin Tian stepped left.

Not back, not to the side in a defensive dodge, but forward and left, angling his body so that Liu Feng's palm passed inches from his ribs. His own sword came up in a horizontal arc, not aimed at Liu Feng's body but at the space where his arm would be if he committed fully to the attack.

Liu Feng pulled back, surprise flickering across his features. "Lucky."

Jin Tian didn't respond. He pivoted on his heel, sweeping his blade low toward Liu Feng's ankles. The inner disciple leaped over it, launching a kick mid-air that Jin Tian caught on his sword's flat. The impact sent vibrations through his arm, but he absorbed them, letting the momentum carry him into a spin.

In that spin, his blade found Liu Feng's ribcage.

Not a deep cut—the sword barely drew blood—but it was enough. The crowd gasped as Liu Feng staggered back, his hand pressed to his side. Blood seeped between his fingers, bright red against his robes.

"You cut me," Liu Feng said, his voice dropping to something dangerous. "You actually cut me."

"I noticed."

Liu Feng's eyes ignited. Literally. A corona of flame erupted around him, forcing the nearest spectators to shield their faces from the heat. The arena stones beneath his feet began to crack from the temperature.

Jin Tian had expected this. In their first life, Liu Feng had used this technique against a demon beast, incinerating it in seconds. The flames consumed spiritual energy at an alarming rate, but they also made Liu Feng nearly untouchable.

Nearly.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword.

The crowd erupted in confusion. Even Liu Feng paused, his flames flickering uncertainly. "What are you doing?"

"I'm not going to beat you with a sword," Jin Tian said calmly. "Not like this. My cultivation is too low, my weapon too weak. If I try to trade blows, I'll lose every time."

"Then you admit defeat?"

"No." Jin Tian lowered his stance, his hands resting on his sheathed blade. "I'm going to beat you with your own weakness."

Liu Feng laughed, but there was no humor in it. "And what weakness is that?"

"Speed." Jin Tian met his gaze. "Your techniques are powerful, but they're heavy. You've trained for overwhelming force, not efficiency. Every movement you make is designed to crush, not to flow. And that means you're predictable."

"Predictable?" Liu Feng's flames surged higher. "I'll show you predictable!"

He charged again, faster this time, his fists wreathed in fire that left trails of heat in the air. A combination strike—left jab, right hook, knee to the midsection, all in less than two seconds.

Jin Tian saw it all before it happened.

He dropped. Not dodging, not blocking—just dropping to one knee, letting Liu Feng's attacks sail over his head. As the inner disciple's momentum carried him forward, Jin Tian drew his sword in a razor-thin arc, the blade catching Liu Feng's trailing leg just behind the knee.

A tendon, severed.

Liu Feng screamed, collapsing sideways as his leg gave out. The flames around him guttered and died as his concentration shattered. He hit the arena stones hard, rolling once before coming to a stop on his back, clutching his leg.

The arena was silent.

"I warned you," Jin Tian said, rising slowly. He didn't sheath his sword. "Your left leg absorbs most of your weight when you attack. It's a bad habit. One that takes years to correct."

Liu Feng's face was contorted with pain and rage. "You... you crippled me!"

"Temporarily. It will heal in a month with proper treatment." Jin Tian walked closer, stopping a few feet away. "I want to ask you something."

"Go to hell!"

"Why did you kill me?"

The words hung in the air. Liu Feng's expression shifted from rage to confusion to something else—something that made Jin Tian's blood run cold.

"Kill you?" Liu Feng laughed bitterly. "I don't even know who you are. I've never seen you before today."

"In another life, you did. You put a blade through my heart." Jin Tian's voice was flat. "I want to know why."

Liu Feng stared at him, and for a moment, something flickered in his eyes. Recognition. Not of Jin Tian's face, but of his name. His voice.

"Jin Tian," Liu Feng whispered. "That's your name."

"Yes."

"I..." Liu Feng's face drained of color. "I've heard that name before. The sect master mentioned it. In a private meeting, three days ago."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. "The sect master?"

"He said..." Liu Feng swallowed hard. "He said you were a threat. That you needed to be... contained. Eliminated if necessary." His eyes met Jin Tian's. "I didn't know who you were. I thought he meant someone else. Some enemy of the sect."

"What did he say exactly?"

"He said—" Liu Feng stopped, his eyes widening. "Behind you!"

Jin Tian spun, his sword coming up—

But there was nothing there. Only empty air and confused spectators.

When he turned back, Liu Feng was dead.

A thin needle of ice protruded from his throat, the frozen metal already beginning to melt in the residual heat of the arena. His eyes were still open, still locked on Jin Tian's face.

The crowd erupted into chaos.

Jin Tian dropped to his knees beside the body, his mind racing. The needle had come from somewhere in the crowd. Someone had killed Liu Feng to silence him. Someone who didn't want Jin Tian to know the truth.

Elder Zhou appeared at the edge of the arena, his face thunderous. "What happened?"

"He was killed," Jin Tian said, pulling the needle from Liu Feng's throat. It dissolved in his fingers, leaving only a faint chill. "Someone silenced him."

"Silenced him from what?"

Jin Tian looked up at the elder, and for the first time since his regression, he felt genuine fear. Not for himself, but for what he was about to uncover.

"He was about to tell me something about the sect master."

The crowd's murmurs became gasps. Elder Zhou's expression hardened. "You're accusing the sect master of conspiracy?"

"I'm not accusing anyone of anything," Jin Tian said carefully. "I'm stating a fact. Liu Feng received an order from the sect master to eliminate me. And now he's dead because he started to reveal that information."

Elder Zhou's hand moved to his sword. "This is a serious accusation, boy."

"I know."

"One that could get you executed."

"I know."

"Yet you make it anyway?"

Jin Tian rose, meeting the elder's gaze without flinching. "I've been dead before, Elder Zhou. I'm not afraid to die again. But I won't die ignorant. Not this time."

A heavy silence fell over the arena. Elder Zhou studied him for a long moment, his hand never leaving his sword.

Then, slowly, he nodded.

"Come with me. We need to speak privately."

As Jin Tian followed the elder from the arena, he caught sight of Chen Wei standing among the spectators. The other boy's face was pale, his eyes fixed on Liu Feng's body.

But when Chen Wei noticed Jin Tian looking, he smiled.

It was the smile of someone who knew exactly what was coming next.

And who was looking forward to it.

title: The Fire That Burns Within
content:
The training grounds were empty when Jin Tian arrived. The sun had begun its descent, casting long shadows across the stone courtyard where inner disciples honed their techniques. He had chosen this place deliberately—far from prying eyes, close to the sect's eastern wall where the ambient qi was thin enough to go unnoticed.

Three hours. One hundred and eighty minutes to prepare for a battle that would define his second life.

He sat cross-legged on the cold stone, closing his eyes. The memories of his first life flooded back unbidden—Liu Feng's blade, the burning sensation as fire qi devoured his meridians, the look of cold satisfaction on his killer's face. But Jin Tian had learned something in those final moments. Something he had never understood until now.

Liu Feng's attack had been perfectly timed. Not a second early, not a second late. The strike had come when Jin Tian's qi was at its lowest ebb, when his guard was down, when he was most vulnerable. It was the kind of precision that came from intimate knowledge of an opponent's patterns.

Someone had told Liu Feng exactly when to strike.

"Interesting," Jin Tian murmured to himself. "So the betrayal began earlier than I thought."

He pushed the thoughts aside. Dwelling on the past would not help him survive the next three hours. Instead, he focused inward, cycling his qi through his meridians in a pattern he had perfected over three hundred years of cultivation. The technique was called the Verdant Lotus Breathing Method—a foundational technique that most outer disciples abandoned once they advanced to the inner sect. They thought it was too basic, too weak.

They were wrong.

The Verdant Lotus was one of the few techniques that strengthened the foundation of a cultivator's qi pathways rather than simply increasing their power. In his first life, Jin Tian had discarded it at the first opportunity. In his second, he had clung to it like a lifeline.

His meridians began to glow with a faint green light, visible even through his robes. The qi flowed smoothly, unobstructed, pure. He pushed harder, forcing the energy through channels that had been blocked since his regression. Pain lanced through his chest, but he did not stop.

A cultivator's strength came not from their techniques, but from their will to endure.

"Impressive."

The voice came from behind him, soft and amused. Jin Tian's eyes snapped open, his hand already reaching for his sword. But he stopped when he saw who it was.

A woman stood at the edge of the training grounds, leaning against a stone pillar with her arms crossed. She was older than him by perhaps five years, with sharp features and eyes that held a knowing glint. Her robes were those of an inner disciple, but the silver embroidery at her collar marked her as something more—a direct disciple of an elder.

"Forgive my intrusion," she said, pushing off from the pillar. "I was passing by and felt an interesting fluctuation in qi. Most outer disciples would not recognize the Verdant Lotus, let alone practice it."

Jin Tian remained seated, his expression carefully neutral. "And who might you be?"

"Me?" She smiled, a thin, dangerous curve of her lips. "I am Ling Yue, third direct disciple of Elder Zhou. I was curious about the boy who dared to challenge Chen Wei's position."

So this was Ling Yue. In his first life, she had been one of the few inner disciples who had shown him kindness—and one of the first to die when the traitors struck. He remembered finding her body in the sect library, her throat slit from ear to ear, a look of surprise frozen on her face.

"You're not what I expected," she said, walking closer. "I expected either a fool or a prodigy. Instead, I find someone who understands the value of foundations."

"Both can be true," Jin Tian replied carefully. "A fool can be a prodigy, and a prodigy can be a fool."

Ling Yue laughed, a genuine sound that echoed across the empty courtyard. "You're clever too. That's dangerous in this sect."

She stopped a few feet away, studying him with an intensity that made Jin Tian's instincts scream. He had learned to trust those instincts over centuries of combat. She was not here to help him.

"I came to warn you," she said, her voice dropping. "Liu Feng has been given a special pill before your match. A Flameheart Elixir."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold. The Flameheart Elixir was a forbidden substance, one that temporarily boosted a cultivator's fire qi by burning through their life force. It was dangerous, addictive, and strictly prohibited by sect rules.

"How do you know this?"

"Because I was the one who was asked to acquire it," Ling Yue said, her smile turning bitter. "By Chen Wei. He paid me handsomely and promised more if I kept quiet. I took his money, but I made no promise about my silence."

She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a small jade vial, tossing it to Jin Tian. He caught it instinctively.

"What is this?"

"An antidote. Of sorts." She turned to leave, her robes swirling around her. "It won't stop the Flameheart, but it will buy you time. Use it wisely."

"Wait." Jin Tian stood, the vial clutched in his hand. "Why are you helping me? You don't know me. We've never met before today."

Ling Yue paused, her back still to him. "Because I knew your mother."

The words hit him like a physical blow. His mother had died when he was six years old, her death shrouded in mystery and whispered rumors. The sect had told him she died in a training accident, but he had always known that was a lie.

"What do you know about my mother?"

Ling Yue glanced over her shoulder, her expression unreadable. "More than I can say. And less than I should." She began walking away, her voice carrying back to him. "Survive your match against Liu Feng, Jin Tian. Survive, and I will tell you everything."

She vanished around a corner, leaving him alone in the fading light.

Jin Tian stared at the vial in his hand, his mind racing. His mother. The woman who had taught him his first sword technique, who had held him when he cried, who had whispered secrets of cultivation that he had never understood until now.

She had been hiding something. And Ling Yue knew what it was.

He pocketed the vial and returned to his training, but his concentration was shattered. The memories of his mother overwhelmed him—her warm smile, her gentle hands, the way she would hum old songs when she thought no one was listening.

And the night she died. The screams. The blood. The figure standing over her body, sword in hand.

Jin Tian had always assumed it was a demon beast that had killed her. The sect had told him so, had shown him the claw marks on her body. But now, with the knowledge of his regression and the weight of his past lives pressing down on him, he began to question everything.

What if his mother had been murdered? What if her death was connected to the betrayal that would claim his own life?

He shook his head, forcing himself to focus. There would be time for questions later. Now, he had a match to prepare for.

He opened the vial Ling Yue had given him and sniffed the contents. A sharp, acrid smell filled his nostrils, burning slightly. He recognized the ingredients—Nightshade Root, Frozen Lotus Petal, and something he couldn't identify. It was a crude antidote at best, but it would have to do.

He drank it in one gulp, grimacing at the bitter taste.

The sun had fully set by the time he finished his preparations. He stood, stretching his limbs, feeling the qi flowing smoothly through his meridians. The antidote had settled into his system, a cool presence that counteracted the heat of his own cultivation.

He was as ready as he would ever be.

The arena was packed when he arrived. Hundreds of disciples had gathered to watch the first match of the inner division tournament—an outer disciple challenging a ranked inner disciple. The odds were heavily against him, and the betting pools reflected it. Jin Tian ignored the crowd, his eyes fixed on the figure standing at the center of the arena.

Liu Feng.

The inner disciple was tall and broad-shouldered, with red hair that seemed to flicker like flames in the torchlight. His face was handsome in a rough, brutal way, and his eyes held the cold confidence of someone who had never known defeat.

He was exactly as Jin Tian remembered him.

"So," Liu Feng said, his voice carrying across the arena, "the little mouse has come to play." He cracked his neck, rolling his shoulders. "I was told you were bold. Let's see if you have the strength to back it up."

Jin Tian stepped onto the arena floor, drawing his sword. The blade was simple, unadorned, but it felt right in his hands. "Strength is not the only path to victory."

"No." Liu Feng's grin widened. "But it's the path I prefer."

He exploded forward, his speed shocking for someone of his size. Fire qi erupted from his body, coating his fists in orange flames. He didn't bother drawing his weapon—to him, Jin Tian was not worth the effort.

Jin Tian sidestepped the first punch, feeling the heat singe his robes. He didn't counterattack. He watched.

In his first life, Liu Feng had been a whirlwind of destruction, overwhelming his opponents with raw power. But raw power had weaknesses. It was predictable. It followed patterns.

The second punch came faster, aimed at his head. Jin Tian ducked, feeling the flames pass inches above him. Still, he didn't attack.

"What's wrong, mouse?" Liu Feng taunted, his fists blurring. "Too scared to fight back?"

Jin Tian remained silent, his eyes tracking every movement, every shift of weight, every tell. Liu Feng was fast, but he was also arrogant. He left openings, expecting his power to compensate.

The third punch was a feint. Jin Tian saw it coming a mile away. He didn't dodge.

Instead, he moved forward.

The crowd gasped as Jin Tian stepped inside Liu Feng's guard, his shoulder slamming into the larger man's chest. The impact was minimal, but it disrupted Liu Feng's balance, forcing him to take a step back.

For the first time, Liu Feng's eyes showed surprise.

Jin Tian didn't give him time to recover. His sword lashed out, a precise strike aimed at the meridian point just below Liu Feng's ribs. The tip of the blade connected, sending a jolt of qi through Liu Feng's system.

The Flameheart Elixir reacted instantly.

Liu Feng's eyes widened as the pill's energy went haywire, forcing his qi to burn through his meridians at triple speed. He let out a roar of pain, his body convulsing as flames erupted uncontrollably from his skin.

"What did you do?" he screamed, his voice cracking.

Jin Tian stepped back, his sword held steady. "I gave you what you wanted. Power."

The crowd erupted into chaos as Liu Feng collapsed, his body wreathed in flames that he could no longer control. The antidote Ling Yue had given Jin Tian had done its work—not by neutralizing the Flameheart, but by making it unstable, unpredictable.

It was a cruel trick, but Jin Tian had no room for mercy. Not against a man who had killed him once before.

The overseers rushed forward, trying to extinguish the flames, but it was too late. Liu Feng's cultivation base had been shattered, his meridians burned beyond repair. He would live, but he would never wield qi again.

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and turned to leave.

"Wait

title: The Flames of Truth
content:
Jin Tian did not return to his quarters.

Instead, he walked the empty training grounds of the outer sect, his bare feet pressing into the cold stone as the afternoon sun cast long shadows across the courtyard. The air smelled of dust and dried blood—remnants of the qualifiers that had ended only hours ago.

Three hours until he faced Liu Feng.

Three hours until he stood across from the man who had ended his first life.

The name echoed in his skull like a wound that refused to heal. Liu Feng. Ranked seventh among the inner disciples. A practitioner of the Blazing Sun Arts, a fire-aspected cultivation method that allowed him to weapons channel searing qi through his blade. In his first life, Jin Tian had considered Liu Feng a worthy rival—someone to push against, to grow from.

He had never considered him an enemy.

But enemies didn't stab you in the heart while you were distracted. Enemies didn't watch your blood pool beneath you with empty, calculating eyes. And enemies certainly didn't whisper your name as if it were a prayer of gratitude.

*"Jin Tian... thank you."*

Those had been Liu Feng's last words to him in his first life. The memory surfaced now with crystalline clarity—the way Liu Feng's voice had cracked with something that sounded almost like relief. Like a burden being lifted.

Jin Tian stopped walking.

A breeze stirred the dust around him, carrying the distant sound of clashing weapons from the inner sect training grounds. He closed his eyes and reached into his dantian, feeling the familiar pulse of his cultivation base. Meridian Opening, Peak Stage. Not enough. Not nearly enough.

Liu Feng was at the Qi Condensation stage—a full realm above him. The gap between Meridian Opening and Qi Condensation was not merely a difference in power; it was a difference in kind. Qi Condensation cultivators could project their qi externally, forming shields, launching attacks, enhancing their weapons with elemental properties. A Meridian Opening cultivator could only circulate qi internally.

The gap was supposed to be unbridgeable.

But Jin Tian had bridged it before. In his first life, he had defeated Liu Feng in the inner division tournament's semi-finals, using a technique he had developed over decades of cultivation—the Shadowless Sword Art. It was a style that relied on speed and deception, on striking where the opponent least expected it.

The problem was that technique required a foundation of Qi Condensation to execute properly.

Jin Tian opened his eyes. His hand drifted to the hilt of his practice sword—a cheap, unremarkable blade that had been issued to him on his first day as an outer disciple. The metal was poorly refined, the edge already chipped from months of use.

He drew the blade and assumed a stance.

The Shadowless Sword Art had seven forms. The first three could be performed at Meridian Opening, though they would lack the full power of the later techniques. But Jin Tian didn't need power. He needed precision. He needed to understand Liu Feng—not the Liu Feng of three hundred years ago, but the Liu Feng who existed now.

In his first life, they had met in the tournament's final stage. Now, they would meet in the first round. The timeline had shifted.

*Why?*

The question gnawed at him. He had changed nothing of significance—only his performance in the qualifiers. But that single change had ripple effects, altering the bracket, forcing new matchups, creating a path that led directly to Liu Feng.

Was this fate? Or was something else at work?

Jin Tian dismissed the thought. He didn't believe in fate. Fate was an excuse for the weak and the complacent. He had been given a second chance, and he would carve his own destiny with the edge of his sword.

He began to move.

The first form of the Shadowless Sword Art was called "Falling Leaf." It was a feinting strike designed to draw the opponent's guard high, leaving their lower body exposed for a follow-up attack. Jin Tian executed it slowly, feeling the way his qi flowed through his meridians, the way the blade responded to his intent.

Too slow. Too weak.

He tried again, increasing his speed. The blade whistled through the air, tracing an arc of silver light. Better, but still not enough. The technique required an explosive burst of qi at the moment of contact—a burst he couldn't generate at his current cultivation level.

He would have to adapt.

Jin Tian lowered his sword and began to pace. His mind raced through possibilities, discarding each one as quickly as it formed. He couldn't match Liu Feng's power. He couldn't match his speed. He couldn't match his qi reserves.

But he could match his experience.

In his first life, Jin Tian had studied Liu Feng's techniques for years. He knew the Blazing Sun Arts inside and out—their strengths, their weaknesses, their tells. He knew that Liu Feng favored a right-handed overhead strike, that he always led with fire before following with steel, that his defensive posture left a gap under his left arm when he was preparing to unleash his signature technique.

The question was whether that knowledge still held true. Three hundred years had passed in Jin Tian's mind, but only days had passed in this timeline. Liu Feng was still young, still developing, still learning the techniques that would define him as a cultivator.

Which meant he was also still making mistakes.

A smile touched Jin Tian's lips. Not a warm smile, but the smile of a hunter who had found the crack in his prey's armor.

"You seem pleased."

The voice came from behind him, soft and familiar. Jin Tian turned, his blade rising instinctively before he recognized the speaker.

Liang Xue stood at the edge of the training ground, her arms crossed, her expression unreadable. She was dressed in the blue robes of an inner disciple, her hair bound in a simple ponytail that accentuated the sharp lines of her face.

"I didn't expect to find you here," she said, stepping onto the stone courtyard. "I thought you'd be resting before your match."

"I don't need rest."

"Everyone needs rest." She stopped a few feet away, her eyes dropping to his blade. "You're practicing. Before the biggest match of your life."

"I'm preparing."

"For what? You're facing an inner disciple. A Qi Condensation cultivator." Liang Xue's voice carried no mockery, only a strange note of concern. "Do you even understand what you're walking into?"

Jin Tian considered his answer carefully. Liang Xue had been an ally in his first life—a trusted friend, a fierce warrior, one of the few people he had genuinely respected. But she had also been absent during his final moments. He had never learned where she was, what she was doing, why she hadn't been there to help him.

He still didn't trust her completely.

"I understand," he said simply. "I understand that Liu Feng has a tell when he's about to use the Blazing Sun's Wrath. I understand that his left side is weaker than his right. I understand that he always spends the first three exchanges testing his opponent's strength before committing to an attack."

Liang Xue's eyes widened. "How do you—"

"I've been watching him. Studying him." The lie came easily. "Every match he's fought in the inner sect has been recorded. I've watched them all."

"That's..." She paused, her expression shifting to something like respect. "That's thorough. Most outer disciples don't bother learning about their opponents. They just charge in and hope for the best."

"Most outer disciples die young."

A silence stretched between them. Liang Xue studied him with renewed intensity, as if seeing him for the first time. Her hand drifted to the hilt of her own sword—a beautiful blade wrapped in leather, the guard shaped like a crescent moon.

"You're not what I expected," she said finally.

"And what did you expect?"

"I don't know. Someone smaller. Someone more afraid." She shook her head. "You're neither. You're standing here, practicing your forms, planning your strategy, as if facing an inner disciple is just another match."

"It is just another match."

"No, it's not." Liang Xue stepped closer, her voice dropping to a whisper. "Liu Feng is not like the other inner disciples. He's connected. His family has ties to the sect elders, to the council, to people who can make problems disappear. If you beat him, you'll make enemies. Powerful ones."

Jin Tian met her gaze. "And if I lose?"

"Then you'll be forgotten. Just another outer disciple who reached too high and fell."

"That's not much of a choice."

"No," Liang Xue agreed. "It's not." She turned to leave, pausing at the edge of the courtyard. "One more thing. I heard something interesting in the inner sect this morning. A rumor."

"What kind of rumor?"

"There's been talk of a traitor in the outer sect. Someone feeding information to a rival sect. The elders have been investigating for weeks, and they've narrowed it down to three suspects." She glanced back at him, her eyes unreadable. "One of them is you."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

"The evidence is circumstantial," she continued. "But it's enough to warrant an inquiry. If you lose your match tonight, the inquiry will proceed. If you win, it might buy you enough time to clear your name."

"How do you know this?"

"Because I'm the one who found the evidence." Liang Xue's voice hardened. "And I know it's a fabrication. Someone is setting you up, Jin Tian. Someone wants you gone. Not just defeated—gone."

She left without another word, her footsteps fading into the evening shadows.

Jin Tian stood alone in the courtyard, his sword hanging loosely at his side. The weight of her words pressed down on him, heavier than any blade.

Someone was setting him up.

Someone wanted him dead.

And in three hours, he would step into the arena against a man who had killed him once before—while a knife waited for him in the dark, ready to strike if he failed.

He looked up at the sky, where the first stars were beginning to emerge.

*So this is how it begins,* he thought. *Not with a blade, but with a lie.*

Jin Tian sheathed his sword and began walking toward the arena. He had three hours to prepare for a battle that would decide more than just his rank.

He had three hours to become the man who would survive the night.

title: The Price of Victory
content:
The arena bells had not yet rung, but Jin Tian could already hear the blood pounding in his ears.

Three hours. He had three hours to prepare for a confrontation with a man who had ended his life in a past existence. Three hours to bridge the gap between a twelve-year-old outer disciple's body and the techniques of a Sword God reborn.

He slipped away from the gathering crowd, weaving through the stone corridors of the sect's inner compound. His robes still bore the scent of incense from the judgment hall, and his hands trembled—not from fear, but from the raw, searing anger that threatened to consume him.

*Focus.* He forced his breathing to slow. *Anger is a blade without a handle. It cuts the wielder first.*

He found an empty training pavilion hidden behind the main hall, its wooden floor worn smooth by generations of disciples. The afternoon sun cast long shadows through the lattice windows, painting stripes of gold and darkness across the practice room.

Jin Tian closed his eyes and reached inward.

His cultivation base was still pitifully small. In his first life, he had been at the sixth level of Qi Condensation by this age, a prodigy among prodigies. Now he barely scraped the third level, his meridians still clogged with impurities that would take months to clear.

But cultivation base was not everything. Not when you had mastered sixteen sword forms across three hundred years of bitter training.

He drew the practice sword from the rack—a simple wooden blade, unremarkable in every way. To the outside observer, it was identical to the hundreds of other practice swords in the sect. But Jin Tian felt its weight, its balance, its subtle imperfections, and adjusted his grip accordingly.

"Liu Feng," he murmured to the empty room. "Ranked seventh among inner disciples. Known for the Crimson Lotus Flame technique. Aggressive, relentless, but predictable in his patterns."

He had watched Liu Feng fight dozens of times in his first life. Had studied his movements, his tells, the way he shifted his weight before launching a fire-aspected strike. They had been rivals once, equals who pushed each other to greater heights.

Until the day Liu Feng's blade found his heart.

*Why?* The question burned in Jin Tian's chest like a brand. *What did they promise you? What could possibly justify—*

He stopped himself. Answers would come. But first, he had to survive.

Jin Tian began to move through the forms, starting slow, building speed. The wooden sword sang through the air, tracing arcs that seemed to bend the light around them. He was not practicing the sect's standard techniques—those were too rigid, too predictable. Instead, he wove together fragments of advanced sword styles from his past life, adapting them to his current body's limitations.

The first form: Falling Petal. A defensive technique that created the illusion of multiple blade trajectories, forcing the opponent to hesitate.

The second form: Hidden Current. A strike that flowed beneath the opponent's guard, targeting the gaps in their qi circulation.

The third form: *Thousand Reflections*—but his body screamed in protest. His meridians were too weak, his cultivation too shallow. The technique fizzled, sending a spike of pain through his arm.

Jin Tian stumbled, catching himself against a pillar. Blood beaded on his lip where he had bitten through the skin.

*Fool,* he cursed himself. *You're trying to run before you can walk.*

He needed a different approach. Liu Feng was stronger, faster, and more experienced in this life. Direct confrontation would end in disaster. But Liu Feng also had a weakness—a flaw that Jin Tian had discovered in their many spars during his first lifetime.

Liu Feng's Crimson Lotus technique required precise control of fire qi through his right meridian. If that meridian was disrupted, even briefly, his offensive would collapse.

Jin Tian's mind raced through possibilities. He had no cultivation techniques capable of disrupting meridians at his current level. But he didn't need qi techniques. Not when he knew exactly where to strike.

The human body had pressure points—junctures where qi flowed close to the surface. A precise strike, delivered with enough force, could momentarily stun a meridian. Against a weaker opponent, it could paralyze an entire limb.

Against Liu Feng, it might buy Jin Tian the opening he needed.

He practiced the strike a hundred times. A hundred more. His arm ached, his fingers blistered, but he did not stop. The sun crawled across the sky, and the bells of the arena began to toll.

Jin Tian lowered his sword, breathing hard. Sweat dripped from his brow, and his muscles screamed for rest. But something else stirred within him—a cold certainty that had nothing to do with confidence and everything to do with purpose.

He would face Liu Feng. He would survive. And he would learn the truth.

---

The inner division arena was a different world from the outer disciple training grounds.

Carved from white jade and reinforced with formation arrays that hummed with barely contained power, the arena could withstand attacks that would level mountains. Seating rose in tiered rings around the combat space, packed with inner disciples, elders, and visiting dignitaries.

Jin Tian stood at the entrance, feeling the weight of hundreds of eyes upon him. Whispers rippled through the crowd.

"Is that the outer disciple who challenged Chen Wei?"

"He's just a child. What is Elder Zhou thinking?"

"I heard the boy's efficiency score was higher than Chen Wei's. Must be a fluke."

Jin Tian ignored them. His attention was fixed on the figure standing at the far end of the arena.

Liu Feng was tall for his age, with broad shoulders and a shock of red hair that matched his fiery temperament. He wore the crimson robes of an inner disciple, and a blade of remarkable quality hung at his waist—a spirit weapon, if Jin Tian's eyes did not deceive him. The sword's edge shimmered with a faint orange glow, heat distortion rising from the metal.

Their eyes met across the arena floor. Liu Feng's expression was unreadable, but Jin Tian saw something flicker in his gaze. Recognition? No, that was impossible. They had never met in this life.

But there was something else. A tension in Liu Feng's shoulders, a tightness around his eyes that spoke of discomfort. He did not want to be here.

*Interesting.*

Elder Zhou rose from his seat, his voice carrying across the arena without effort. "The first match of the inner division tournament: Outer Disciple Jin Tian versus Inner Disciple Liu Feng. The match will continue until one party yields, is unable to continue, or is thrown from the arena. No lethal strikes are permitted."

A murmur of disappointment rippled through the crowd. No lethal strikes meant no bloodshed—a restriction that made the match less entertaining for the spectators.

Jin Tian stepped onto the jade floor. The formation arrays hummed beneath his feet, and he felt his qi respond, drawn upward by the ambient energy of the arena. It was a small advantage, but he would take it.

"Begin!" Elder Zhou's voice rang out.

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. He exploded forward, his crimson robes trailing fire as he drew his blade in a single, fluid motion. The spirit weapon screamed through the air, leaving a trail of heat that distorted the space around it.

Jin Tian did not block. He could not block. Instead, he sidestepped, letting the strike pass within inches of his chest. The heat washed over him, searing his robes, but he was already moving, circling around Liu Feng's extended guard.

The inner disciple recovered quickly, spinning to face him. "Fast," he said, his voice carrying a hint of surprise. "But speed won't save you."

He pressed the attack, his blade becoming a blur of fire and steel. Each strike was precise, powerful, and backed by the full weight of his cultivation base. Jin Tian dodged, weaved, and parried only when absolutely necessary, each deflection sending shocks through his arms.

*He's holding back,* Jin Tian realized. *He's not using his full power.*

Why? The question gnawed at him as he continued to evade. Liu Feng had every reason to crush him quickly, to demonstrate the gap between inner and outer disciples. Instead, he was fighting cautiously, almost reluctantly.

Then Jin Tian saw it.

A flicker of movement in the stands. A figure in white robes, partially obscured by shadow. The figure was watching the match with an intensity that bordered on obsession, their gaze fixed not on the combat, but on Liu Feng.

And Liu Feng knew it. He kept glancing toward the figure, his movements becoming more erratic, more aggressive. He was trying to prove something—or someone was forcing him to.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

He recognized the robes. The bearing. The way the figure sat, perfectly still, like a predator waiting to strike.

*Chen Wei's master,* Jin Tian realized. *Elder Bai.*

The elder was a master of the sect's internal affairs division, a man of considerable power and influence. He was also, in Jin Tian's first life, one of the architects of his downfall. Elder Bai had been the one who orchestrated the betrayal, who had promised the seven disciples rewards beyond their wildest dreams in exchange for Jin Tian's death.

And Elder Bai was here, watching Liu Feng fight a twelve-year-old outer disciple.

The pieces clicked into place with terrible clarity.

Liu Feng had not killed Jin Tian out of ambition or hatred. He had been coerced. Blackmailed. Perhaps his family was threatened, or he owed a debt he could not repay. Elder Bai had been pulling the strings all along.

But why? What had Jin Tian done in his first life to warrant such elaborate betrayal?

He didn't have time to think. Liu Feng's blade came screaming toward his neck, and Jin Tian threw himself backward, the edge grazing his skin. Blood welled from a shallow cut, staining his collar red.

"You're distracted," Liu Feng said, his voice tight. "Focus on the fight, boy. Or I'll end it right now."

There was a warning in his words—not a threat, but a plea. *Don't make me do this. Don't force my hand.*

Jin Tian understood.

He changed his stance, dropping into a low guard that exposed his right side. It was a deliberate weakness, an invitation that only someone who knew his fighting style would recognize.

Liu Feng's eyes widened for a fraction of a second. Then he attacked, lunging forward with a thrust aimed at Jin Tian's exposed flank.

It was exactly what Jin Tian had hoped for.

He twisted, not away from the blade, but toward it. The tip of Liu Feng's sword sliced through his robes, skimming his ribs, but Jin Tian was already inside the inner disciple's guard. His wooden sword came up, striking the pressure point on Liu Feng's right arm with surgical precision.

The effect was immediate. Liu Feng's arm went numb, his grip on the spirit weapon faltering. Fire qi erupted from the disrupted meridian, scorching his own robes and sending him stumbling backward.

The crowd gasped.

Jin Tian did not press the advantage. He stepped back, lowering his sword, his eyes locked on Liu Feng's.

"Yield," he said quietly. "You don't have to do this."

For a long moment, Liu Feng stared at him, his expression cycling through shock, confusion, and something that looked almost like hope. Then his gaze flickered toward the stands, toward Elder Bai's shadowed figure.

The hope died.

"I can't," Liu Feng whispered. He raised his sword again, his hand still trembling from the meridian strike. "I'm sorry."

He charged, fire blazing along his blade, his speed doubled by desperation. The attack was wild, unrefined, but backed by the full force of his cultivation base

title: The Weight of a Name
content:
Jin Tian stood at the edge of the training grounds, the afternoon sun casting long shadows across the packed earth. His hands remained steady at his sides, but inside, the storm raged.

Liu Feng.

The name echoed through his memories like a funeral bell. He had spent the past three hours in meditation, cycling his qi through pathways that most cultivators his age couldn't even sense. He had reviewed every technique, every counter, every weakness Liu Feng had ever displayed in their countless sparring matches across Jin Tian's first life.

But something was wrong.

In his memories, Liu Feng had been a straightforward fighter. Fire-aspected techniques, aggressive pursuit, little patience for defense. A brute with talent. But the more Jin Tian thought about it, the more gaps appeared in his recollection.

Why had Liu Feng been at the assassination? He wasn't one of the seven disciples. He had no reason to hate Jin Tian. They had been rivals, yes, but rivals respected each other. They had shared drinks after tournaments. They had saved each other's lives in the Shadow Realm Incursion.

And then Liu Feng's blade had found his heart.

"You're early."

The voice came from behind him, smooth and measured. Jin Tian turned slowly, his hand instinctively drifting toward the wooden sword at his hip.

Liu Feng stood twenty paces away, arms crossed, his crimson robes billowing gently in the breeze. He was older than Jin Tian by four years, already fifteen, with a lean build that spoke of speed rather than raw power. His face was handsome in a way that seemed almost forgettable—the kind of face that could disappear into a crowd.

But his eyes were anything but forgettable.

They were black. Deep, endless black, like wells that had no bottom. In Jin Tian's memories, those eyes had been brown. Warm brown, the color of earth and hearth fires.

"So you're the one who stole Chen Wei's slot," Liu Feng said, his tone carrying no hostility. If anything, he sounded amused. "I expected someone taller."

"Size doesn't determine victory," Jin Tian replied, keeping his voice neutral.

"No, it doesn't." Liu Feng uncrossed his arms and began walking toward him, each step measured and deliberate. "But experience does. And talent. And preparation." He stopped five paces away, close enough that Jin Tian could smell the faint scent of smoke clinging to his robes. "I've been training for this tournament for three years. I've memorized the techniques of every potential opponent. I've studied the terrain of the arena, the placement of the arrays, the timing of the qi fluctuations."

He smiled, and it didn't reach his eyes.

"And you? You've been an outer disciple for what, two months?"

Jin Tian met his gaze without flinching. "Long enough."

Liu Feng laughed, a sound that was supposed to be light but carried an undercurrent of something darker. "I like you, Jin Tian. You have guts. But guts won't save you in the arena." He leaned in, lowering his voice. "I'm going to give you some advice, free of charge. Withdraw from the match. Claim illness. Tell them you're not ready."

"And why would I do that?"

"Because I've been ordered to hurt you." Liu Feng's smile vanished, replaced by something cold and businesslike. "Not defeat you. Not embarrass you. Hurt you. Permanently, if possible. The kind of injury that takes months to heal, if it heals at all."

Jin Tian's blood ran cold, but he kept his expression neutral. "Ordered by whom?"

Liu Feng's eyes flickered to the side, toward the administrative pavilion where Elder Zhou and the other overseers were gathered. Then he looked back at Jin Tian, and for just a moment, his mask slipped.

There was fear in those black eyes.

"You already know the answer to that," Liu Feng said quietly. "You're smarter than you look. That's why they want you crippled." He stepped back, his voice returning to its normal volume. "Consider my advice, little brother. Some battles aren't worth fighting."

He turned and walked away, his crimson robes trailing behind him like a bloodstain.

Jin Tian watched him go, his mind racing. Liu Feng had been ordered to hurt him. By whom? Chen Wei's family? The Heavenly Cloud Sect's elders? Someone higher?

And why had Liu Feng warned him?

In his first life, Liu Feng had been his enemy. The man who killed him. But this Liu Feng was different. Younger. More uncertain. And clearly operating under duress.

Was the assassination ordered from the very beginning? Had someone been pulling the strings all along, using Jin Tian's disciples as pawns and Liu Feng as the executioner?

The implications were staggering.

He was still processing when a hand landed on his shoulder. He spun, his hand going for his sword, but stopped when he saw Wei Zhen's concerned face.

"You look like you've seen a ghost," Wei Zhen said. "What did Liu Feng say to you?"

Jin Tian hesitated. Wei Zhen was his friend, but trust was a luxury he couldn't afford. Not yet. "He told me to withdraw."

"Withdraw?" Wei Zhen's eyes widened. "Why would he—never mind, I know why. He's ranked seventh. He probably thinks you're beneath him." He squeezed Jin Tian's shoulder. "Don't listen to him. You earned that slot. You beat Chen Wei's score fair and square."

"Did I?"

Wei Zhen blinked. "What do you mean?"

Jin Tian looked past him, toward the training grounds where disciples were gathering for the afternoon announcements. Chen Wei stood among them, surrounded by his usual entourage, but his eyes were fixed on Jin Tian with an intensity that bordered on obsessive.

"I mean," Jin Tian said slowly, "that I'm starting to wonder if the tournament was rigged from the start. If my score wasn't supposed to be higher. If Chen Wei wasn't supposed to lose."

Wei Zhen's face paled. "That's... that's a serious accusation, Jin Tian."

"I know."

"If you're wrong, they'll expel you. Or worse."

"I know."

"And if you're right..." Wei Zhen swallowed hard. "If you're right, then the people who rigged it are the same people who run the sect. The same people who decide who lives and dies."

Jin Tian turned to face his friend fully. "Wei Zhen, I need you to listen carefully. In three hours, I'm going to fight Liu Feng. If I win, I'll have to fight again tomorrow, and the day after that, and the day after that. I'll be climbing a ladder that someone else built, and every step will bring me closer to whoever wants me dead."

He paused, letting the words sink in.

"But if I lose... if I withdraw... I'll be exactly where they want me. Weak. Powerless. Forgettable."

Wei Zhen's hands were shaking. "What are you going to do?"

Jin Tian looked up at the sky, where clouds were gathering on the horizon. A storm was coming. He could feel it in the air, in the qi, in the way the world seemed to hold its breath.

"I'm going to fight," he said. "And I'm going to win. But first, I need to know something." He met Wei Zhen's eyes. "Can I trust you?"

Wei Zhen's face went through a dozen emotions in the span of a heartbeat. Fear. Uncertainty. Loyalty. And finally, something that looked almost like resolve.

"Yes," he said. "You can trust me."

Jin Tian nodded. "Then I need you to do something for me. Something dangerous."

"Name it."

"I need you to find out who Chen Wei's family has been meeting with. Not the public meetings—the private ones. The ones that don't appear on any official records."

Wei Zhen's eyes widened. "That's impossible. Those records are sealed. Only the inner elders have access."

"Which is why you're not going to look for records." Jin Tian reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small jade token, the same one that had allowed him access to the library archives. "You're going to find a disciple named Mei Ling. She works in the administrative pavilion. She's quiet, forgettable, and she notices everything."

"How do you know that?"

Jin Tian allowed himself a thin smile. "Because in another life, she saved my life three times. She was the best spy the sect never knew it had."

Wei Zhen stared at the token, then at Jin Tian. "Who are you, Jin Tian?"

The question hung in the air between them, heavy with meaning.

"I'm someone who's running out of time," Jin Tian said. "Take the token. Find Mei Ling. Tell her that the man who returned the stolen jade book needs her help."

"What jade book?"

"She'll know." Jin Tian pressed the token into Wei Zhen's hand. "Go now. Before anyone notices you're gone."

Wei Zhen hesitated for only a moment, then nodded and disappeared into the crowd, moving with a purpose that Jin Tian hadn't seen in him before.

Jin Tian watched him go, then turned back toward the arena where his match against Liu Feng would take place. The sun had begun to set, painting the sky in shades of orange and red.

Soon, it would be time.

He had three hours to prepare for a battle against a man who had been ordered to kill him. Three hours to figure out why Liu Feng had warned him. Three hours to decide whether he could trust a man who, in another life, had been his murderer.

And somewhere in the shadows, the person who had given that order was watching, waiting, calculating.

Jin Tian closed his eyes and reached for his qi, feeling it flow through his meridians like a river of light. He was only twelve years old, trapped in a body that hadn't yet grown into its potential. But inside, he carried the memories of three hundred years of cultivation. The techniques. The strategies. The understanding of sword intent that most cultivators spent a lifetime pursuing.

It would have to be enough.

Because if he lost this match, he wouldn't just lose the tournament.

He would lose everything.

The bells chimed in the distance, signaling the start of the evening assembly. Jin Tian opened his eyes and began walking toward the arena, his wooden sword at his side, his heart steady as a drum.

Behind him, in the shadow of the administrative pavilion, a figure watched him go.

The figure smiled, and their eyes glowed with the faintest hint of crimson.

"So," the figure whispered, their voice carrying on the wind like a forgotten melody, "the Sword God awakens at last. How delightful."

They turned and vanished into the darkness, leaving no trace of their presence.

And somewhere in the depths of the sect's archives, a jade book that had been hidden for three hundred years began to glow with a faint, pulsing light.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Shadow in the Flame

The arena was smaller than Jin Tian remembered. In his first life, the inner division tournament had been held in the Grand Coliseum, a massive structure capable of holding ten thousand spectators. But this was merely a training ring, cordoned off by formation flags and surrounded by a handful of elder disciples who had gathered to watch.

It made sense. The tournament wouldn't begin for another three days. This was just an exhibition match, a chance for the outer sect's prodigy to test himself against an established inner disciple.

Jin Tian stood at the edge of the ring, his wooden practice sword resting across his shoulders. The weapon felt wrong in his hands—too light, too brittle. In his prime, he had wielded the Heaven Sunder, a blade forged from a fallen star and quenched in the blood of a thousand demons. Now he held a stick.

But a stick in the hands of a master was still a weapon.

Across from him, Liu Feng entered the ring with the casual confidence of a predator surveying prey. He was taller than Jin Tian remembered, broader in the shoulders, his face carrying that same arrogant smirk that had haunted Jin Tian's dying moments. He carried a real sword—standard inner disciple issue, steel with a copper core, but sharp enough to kill.

"Outer disciple Jin Tian," Liu Feng said, the words dripping with condescension. "I heard you caused quite a stir in the selection hall. Chen Wei must be humiliated."

"Chen Wei will recover," Jin Tian replied evenly. "His pride is durable."

Liu Feng laughed, a harsh sound that echoed off the surrounding buildings. "You've got teeth. I'll give you that. Let's see if you've got anything else."

Elder Zhou raised his hand from the observation platform. "This is a sparring match to first blood or submission. No killing. No crippling techniques. Begin."

Liu Feng moved before the word finished echoing.

Fire exploded from his palm, a lance of crimson qi that streaked toward Jin Tian's chest. In his first life, this opening strike had caught him off guard, searing through his defensive stance and leaving him vulnerable for the follow-up.

Now he simply wasn't there.

He had shifted three inches to the left, letting the fire lance pass so close it singed the fabric of his robe. The heat was intense, but pain was an old friend. He had been burned before. He had been burned alive, once, in a cave-in during his fifth century of cultivation. This was nothing.

Liu Feng's eyes widened slightly, but he recovered quickly, pivoting into a low sweep aimed at Jin Tian's knees. The sword cut through the air with a whistle, but Jin Tian had already read the movement in the shift of his opponent's shoulders, the subtle adjustment of his hips.

He jumped, planted his foot on Liu Feng's blade, and launched himself into a spinning kick aimed at the inner disciple's temple.

Liu Feng blocked with his forearm, the impact sending a shockwave through the arena. He stumbled back, his smirk replaced by genuine surprise.

"You're fast," he admitted, shaking out his arm. "But speed isn't everything."

He planted his feet and raised his sword above his head. Qi gathered around him like a storm, heat shimmering off his skin until the air itself seemed to warp. The technique was familiar—too familiar.

*Flame Dragon Ascension.*

In Jin Tian's first life, Liu Feng had used this technique to kill a rogue cultivator who had been terrorizing a village. It was one of his signature moves, a powerful area-of-effect attack that consumed everything within thirty feet.

But here, in this training ring, with spectators barely ten feet away?

*He's not trying to win,* Jin Tian realized. *He's trying to kill me.*

The fire exploded outward.

Jin Tian moved, not away from the blast, but into it. His wooden sword extended before him, qi flowing through the grain in a pattern he had perfected over three hundred years. The *Void Piercer*—a technique that used the opponent's own energy to create a path through their attack.

The flames parted around him like water around a stone. He emerged on the other side, his wooden sword pressed against Liu Feng's throat.

Silence.

The elder disciples stared, their mouths hanging open. Elder Zhou had risen from his seat, his eyes wide with incredulity.

Liu Feng's sword clattered to the ground. His hands were shaking, not from fear, but from something else. Something Jin Tian couldn't read.

"First blood goes to the outer disciple," Elder Zhou announced, his voice carrying a hint of wonder. "The match is over."

Jin Tian lowered his sword and stepped back. But as he did, he noticed something. A mark on Liu Feng's wrist, barely visible beneath the sleeve of his robe. A tattoo, small and intricate, depicting a serpent coiled around a crescent moon.

The Mark of the Moon Serpent.

Jin Tian's blood ran cold.

He knew that mark. He had seen it before, in the aftermath of his death. Not on Liu Feng's body—he had never seen Liu Feng's body after the betrayal. No, he had seen it on the leader of the assassins who had hunted him through the Shadow Realm during his final days.

The Moon Serpent Sect. A shadow organization that operated in the spaces between the great sects, selling their services to the highest bidder. They had been the ones who provided the poison that weakened his qi. They had been the ones who tracked his movements, reported his weaknesses, engineered his downfall.

And Liu Feng, the man who had delivered the killing blow, bore their mark.

"You don't remember me, do you?" Liu Feng whispered, so quietly that only Jin Tian could hear.

Jin Tian froze.

"I thought not. You've changed too much. But I remember you, Sword God. I remember what you did to my village."

The words hit Jin Tian like a physical blow. He staggered back, his mind racing through centuries of memories, searching for a village, for a face, for anything that could explain the hatred burning in Liu Feng's eyes.

"I don't know what you're talking about," he said, his voice steady despite the chaos in his mind.

Liu Feng smiled, a cold, broken thing. "You will. Before this tournament is over, you will remember. And when you do, I'll be there to finish what I started."

He turned and walked away, leaving Jin Tian standing alone in the center of the ring, the wooden sword hanging limp in his hand.

The crowd was cheering, but Jin Tian didn't hear them. He was lost in a labyrinth of memories, searching for a truth he had never known.

*What did I do? What village? What crime could justify three hundred years of hatred?*

He looked down at his hands. They were still the hands of a twelve-year-old boy, soft and unmarked by the decades of battle that awaited him. But they were also the hands of the Sword God, soaked in the blood of thousands.

*How many villages had he destroyed? How many families had he torn apart?*

In his pursuit of power, in his rise to the peak of cultivation, he had left a trail of bodies behind him. He had always justified it as necessary, as the cost of progress in a world where the strong devoured the weak.

But now, staring at the mark of the Moon Serpent, he wondered if the cost had been too high.

Elder Zhou approached him, his expression unreadable. "Impressive display, Jin Tian. You've proven yourself capable beyond your years. The inner division will be honored to have you."

Jin Tian bowed mechanically. "Thank you, Elder."

"I have to ask, though. That technique you used—the one that parted the flames. I've never seen anything like it. Where did you learn it?"

The question was innocent, but Jin Tian felt the trap beneath it. If he answered honestly, he would reveal his secret. If he lied, he would invite suspicion.

*Think. You've been here before. You know how to navigate these waters.*

"I read about it in a scroll," he said, choosing his words carefully. "In the library. The *Void Piercer* technique was developed by an ancient cultivator who specialized in countering fire-aspected attacks."

Elder Zhou's eyes narrowed. "I've spent forty years in the sect's library. I've never seen such a scroll."

"It was in the restricted section," Jin Tian said, letting a hint of embarrassment creep into his voice. "I may have... borrowed it without permission."

For a long moment, Elder Zhou stared at him. Then he laughed, a booming sound that echoed across the arena. "A rule-breaker with talent. You'll fit right in with the inner disciples. Just try not to get caught next time."

He clapped Jin Tian on the shoulder and walked away, still chuckling to himself.

Jin Tian let out a breath he hadn't realized he was holding. The lie had been accepted, but it would only hold for so long. Sooner or later, someone would start asking questions he couldn't answer.

He looked at the mark on his wrist, the same mark he had seen on Liu Feng. He had been so focused on his seven disciples, on the betrayal that had ended his first life, that he had never considered the possibility that his enemies extended beyond them.

The Moon Serpent Sect had been involved. And if they had been involved in his death, they had likely been involved in the events that led to it.

*Who hired them? Who wanted me dead badly enough to pay the Moon Serpent's price?*

The answer, he suspected, lay in the memories he had buried. The faces of the dead. The villages he had burned. The families he had destroyed.

For the first time in three hundred years, Jin Tian was afraid of what he might find when he looked into his own past.

He turned and walked away from the arena, the weight of a forgotten crime pressing down on his shoulders. The tournament would begin in three days, and with it, the first steps toward uncovering the truth.

But as he walked, he couldn't shake the feeling that he was being watched. That somewhere, in the shadows between the buildings, eyes were tracking his every move.

The Moon Serpent had found him once.

They would find him again.

And this time, he would be ready.

Or so he told himself.

But as he disappeared into the maze of the sect's corridors, a single thought echoed in his mind, growing louder with every step:

*What did I do?*

title: The Serpent's True Face
content:
The tournament grounds had transformed.

Where once stood simple bamboo platforms and wooden observation decks, now rose a grand arena of white jade, its surface etched with formation arrays that pulsed with dormant power. Seven floating platforms surrounded the central stage, each one large enough to hold a small army. Above them, crystalline screens shimmered, ready to broadcast every moment of combat to the assembled disciples.

Jin Tian stood in the waiting chamber beneath the arena, running his finger along the edge of his sword. The blade was standard issue—forged iron, decent balance, nothing special. In his first life, he would have scoffed at using such a weapon. Now, he understood that the sword mattered less than the hand that wielded it.

"You're the one who stole Chen Wei's spot."

The voice came from the shadows. Jin Tian didn't turn.

"I didn't steal anything. I earned it."

"Earned it." A laugh, low and mocking. "You think the tournament is about earning? You think merit means anything to people like us?"

Jin Tian finally turned. A figure emerged from the darkness—tall, lean, with eyes that burned like embers. Liu Feng. He wore the red-trimmed robes of an inner disciple, but there was something different about him. Something that hadn't been there in Jin Tian's first life.

Or perhaps Jin Tian simply hadn't noticed before.

"You're speaking as if we're the same," Jin Tian said. "We're not."

"No?" Liu Feng circled him slowly, his footsteps echoing in the empty chamber. "You know what I find interesting, Jin Tian? The way you move. The way you hold yourself. You fight like someone who's been doing this for decades, not years." He stopped, tilting his head. "I've been watching you. Since your first match."

Jin Tian's blood went cold. "You've been spying on outer disciples? That's beneath someone of your rank."

"Is it?" Liu Feng's smile didn't reach his eyes. "Or is it exactly where I need to be? After all, someone told me to keep an eye out for anomalies. Disciples who improved too fast. Disciples who knew too much." He stepped closer, his voice dropping to a whisper. "Disciples who might not be who they appear to be."

The words hung in the air like poison.

Jin Tian forced his expression to remain neutral. "You're paranoid."

"Am I?" Liu Feng produced a small jade slip from his sleeve, holding it up so the light caught its surface. "This arrived three days ago. From the Heavenly Observation Pavilion. It contains a list of names—disciples whose spiritual signatures don't match their recorded histories." He paused. "Your name is on that list."

The world tilted.

The Heavenly Observation Pavilion was one of the most ancient organizations in the cultivation world. They maintained records of every cultivator's spiritual signature from the moment they first awakened their Qi. No one could fake a signature. No one could change it.

But Jin Tian wasn't the same person who had awakened his Qi thirty years ago. He was the same soul, yes, but his spiritual signature had been reforged when he traveled back through time. It was a subtle difference—one he had hoped would go unnoticed.

He had been wrong.

"If you had evidence," Jin Tian said carefully, "you would have already presented it to the Elders."

"True." Liu Feng pocketed the jade slip. "But I'm curious. I want to see what you're capable of. I want to see if you're worth reporting, or if the Pavilion made a mistake." He drew his own sword—a blade of crimson crystal that seemed to drink the light. "So here's what's going to happen. We're going to fight. You're going to show me everything you have. And if you impress me enough, I might just keep your secret."

"And if I don't?"

"Then I'll have a very interesting conversation with Elder Zhou about infiltrators from the Demon Sects."

The chamber fell silent. Jin Tian's mind raced through possibilities. He could kill Liu Feng here, now, before the match began. But the formation arrays would detect violence outside the arena. He could flee, but the sect would hunt him down. He could try to explain, but what explanation could possibly account for a mismatched spiritual signature?

There was only one path forward.

"Fine," Jin Tian said. "I'll show you everything. But when I do, you'll wish you had never asked."

Liu Feng's grin widened. "That's the spirit."

---

The arena was deafening.

Thousands of disciples packed the stands, their voices merging into a roar that shook the very air. The inner division tournament was the highlight of the year, and the first match had drawn the largest crowd in recent memory—an unknown outer disciple challenging the seventh-ranked inner disciple.

Jin Tian walked onto the jade platform, feeling the weight of a thousand eyes upon him. The formation arrays hummed beneath his feet, ready to absorb stray attacks and protect the spectators. Above him, the crystalline screens magnified his image, showing every detail to the crowd.

Across the arena, Liu Feng took his position. He moved with practiced grace, his crimson sword already drawn. The blade seemed to pulse with its own heartbeat, casting eerie red light across his face.

"Disciples!" Elder Zhou's voice boomed from the central observation deck. "This match will determine the final slot in the inner division rankings. Standard rules apply—no lethal strikes, no forbidden techniques, no external assistance. The match ends when one combatant yields, is incapacitated, or is thrown from the platform."

Jin Tian tuned out the rest. He was already reading Liu Feng's stance, analyzing his breathing, tracking the subtle shifts in his Qi. In his first life, he had fought Liu Feng dozens of times. He knew every technique, every pattern, every weakness.

But that had been a different Liu Feng. A Liu Feng who hadn't been watching him. A Liu Feng who hadn't received secret messages from the Heavenly Observation Pavilion.

"Begin!"

Liu Feng moved first, as Jin Tian had expected. He was aggressive by nature, preferring to seize the initiative and never let go. His crimson blade traced a burning arc through the air, leaving trails of fire that hung like afterimages.

Jin Tian sidestepped. Once. Twice. Three times.

Each dodge was precise, calculated, designed to frustrate rather than evade. He wanted Liu Feng to commit. He wanted him to overextend.

"Sword of Burning Heaven—First Form!"

Liu Feng's blade exploded with flames, the heat so intense that the jade platform began to glow. He thrust forward, and the fire coalesced into a spear of pure energy, aimed directly at Jin Tian's heart.

This was the technique that had killed him in his first life.

Jin Tian didn't dodge.

He stepped forward.

The fire spear passed through empty air as Jin Tian's body twisted at an impossible angle. His sword came up not to block, but to guide. The blade traced a perfect circle, redirecting the residual heat around him like water around a stone. And then he was inside Liu Feng's guard, his sword pressing against the inner disciple's throat.

The crowd went silent.

"Your technique hasn't changed," Jin Tian said quietly, so only Liu Feng could hear. "But you have. You're hesitating. You're holding back. Why?"

Liu Feng's eyes widened. "How did you—"

"I know you, Liu Feng. I know every technique you've ever learned. Every weakness you've ever had. I know that you favor your right side when you're nervous, and that you always, always telegraph your killing strikes with a slight shift in your left foot."

He pressed the blade harder, drawing a thin line of blood.

"So I'll ask you again. Who told you to watch me?"

Liu Feng's composure cracked. For a moment, Jin Tian saw genuine fear in those burning eyes. And then the fear was replaced by something else. Something like relief.

"You really are different," Liu Feng whispered. "The Pavillion was right. You're not supposed to exist."

"Answer my question."

"I can't." Liu Feng's voice dropped to barely a whisper. "Because if I tell you, she'll kill me. Just like she killed the others."

She?

Jin Tian's mind seized. In his first life, there had been no "she." There had been seven disciples, seven traitors, seven faces that had watched him die. But now, in this new timeline, there was someone else. Someone pulling strings from the shadows.

"Who is she?" Jin Tian demanded.

But Liu Feng was already moving.

The inner disciple exploded backward, abandoning his sword to create distance. His hands came together in a complicated seal, and the formation arrays beneath their feet flickered. Not the protective arrays—a different set. One that had been hidden.

"You want to know who she is?" Liu Feng's voice echoed across the arena. "Then let me show you."

The hidden formation activated.

A pulse of dark energy erupted from beneath Jin Tian's feet, and the world dissolved into shadows.

---

When the light returned, Jin Tian was no longer in the arena.

He stood in a vast, empty space—a chamber of black stone with no walls, no ceiling, no floor. The only illumination came from a single figure standing before him.

She was beautiful. That was the first thing Jin Tian noticed. Her features were like carved jade, her hair like a waterfall of silver moonlight, her eyes like pools of liquid amethyst. She wore robes of pure white, unadorned, unblemished, untouched by the world's filth.

She was also impossible.

Because Jin Tian knew this woman. He had known her in his first life. Had loved her. Had trusted her above all others.

"Li Hua," he breathed.

The woman smiled, and it was the most terrifying thing Jin Tian had ever seen.

"Hello, Master," she said. "I've been waiting for you."

Jin Tian's sword clattered to the ground.

Li Hua. His first disciple. The one he had taken in as an orphan, raised from childhood, taught everything he knew. The one who had been at his side for two hundred years. The one who had been the first to drive a blade through his heart.

She had been dead for fifty years before his betrayal. Killed in a demon attack that had nearly destroyed the sect. He had mourned her. Had built a shrine in her memory. Had wept at her grave.

But she was standing here. Alive. Smiling.

"You're supposed to be dead," Jin Tian said, his voice hollow.

"Dead?" Li Hua laughed, and the sound was like breaking glass. "Oh, Master. I've never been more alive." She spread her arms, and the shadows around her twisted and writhed. "I simply chose to stop pretending. To stop playing the role of the loyal disciple. To pursue my own path."

"Your own path?" Jin Tian's hands trembled. "You were my most trusted—"

"Your most trusted what? Tool? Weapon? Asset?" Li Hua's smile turned sharp. "You never saw me as anything more than a reflection of your own glory. I was Jin Tian's first disciple. I was Jin Tian's greatest achievement. I was never Li Hua."

"That's not true—"

"Isn't it?" She stepped closer, and with each step, the shadows grew darker. "When I came to you, I was nothing. A street rat with a spark of talent. You refined that spark into a flame. You gave me purpose. You gave me power." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "But you never gave me freedom."

Jin Tian's mind reeled. This was wrong. All wrong. In his first life, Li Hua had been dead before the betrayal. She couldn't have

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Weight of Shadows

Jin Tian stood alone in the preparation chamber beneath the tournament arena, the muffled roar of the crowd seeping through the stone walls. The jade lanterns cast pale light across his face, illuminating the dust motes that danced in lazy spirals around him. He had three hours. Three hours to prepare for a confrontation that, in his first life, had ended with a blade through his chest.

But preparation was not the same as training. He had already trained. For three centuries, he had trained. What he needed now was information.

He pressed his palm against the wall, feeling the faint pulse of the formation seals embedded in the stone. The inner division tournament was a spectacle, designed to showcase strength, but it was also a carefully controlled environment. Every match was recorded, every technique analyzed, every outcome predicted by the sect elders. They thought they understood combat. They thought they understood power.

They had never faced a sword god.

Jin Tian closed his eyes and let the memories surface. Liu Feng. Seventh-ranked inner disciple. Twenty-three years old. Fire-aspected cultivation, specializing in the *Blazing Sun Palm* technique and a mid-grade earth-ranked sword art called *Crimson River*. His style was aggressive, overwhelming, designed to crush opponents before they could establish rhythm. He favored high-pressure tactics, forcing enemies to react rather than act.

And in Jin Tian's first life, Liu Feng had been the one who betrayed him.

Not with words. Not with poison. With steel.

The memory burned: the tournament finals, the crowd roaring, the clash of blades echoing across the arena. Liu Feng had been losing—badly. Jin Tian had been at the peak of his power then, a sword cultivator who had surpassed the limitations of the mortal realm. Liu Feng's fire techniques had been nothing more than sparks against a hurricane.

But then something had changed.

Liu Feng's eyes had gone hollow. His movements had shifted, becoming jerky, unnatural. His cultivation base had surged, breaking through its limits in a single, impossible moment. And when his blade had struck, it had been guided by something beyond skill, beyond technique, beyond the mortal understanding of combat.

Jin Tian had seen that strike coming. He had seen the opening, the weakness, the counter. But his body had refused to move. Not from fear. Not from surprise. His own qi had turned against him, freezing his meridians, locking his limbs in place as if his own cultivation had betrayed him.

And then the blade had pierced his heart.

Jin Tian opened his eyes. The memory still stung, but it no longer burned with the same intensity. He had spent three centuries in the void between lives, dissecting that moment, analyzing every variable. He had come to a single conclusion.

Someone had controlled Liu Feng.

Not through puppetry or possession—those left traces, disturbances in the qi flow that any experienced cultivator could detect. This had been something subtler. A seal planted deep within Liu Feng's cultivation base, triggered at the perfect moment. A command that overwrote his will, his techniques, his very soul.
